title: Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x

recall_papers.title: Proton generalized parton distributions from lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
Momentum and spatial distributions of quarks and gluons inside hadrons are
typically encoded in the so-called generalized parton distributions (GPDs).
GPDs are multi-dimensional quantities that are very challenging to extract,
both experimentally and within lattice QCD. We present the first lattice
results on the $x$-dependence of isovector unpolarized, helicity and
transversity GPDs of the proton, obtained from lattice QCD using an ensemble of
$N_f=2+1+1$ maximally twisted mass fermions, with pion mass $M_\pi=260$ MeV and
lattice spacing $a\simeq 0.093$ fm. Our calculations use the quasi-distribution
formalism and the final distributions are presented in the MS-bar scheme at a
renormalization scale of 2 GeV.
recall_papers.journal: Suplemento de la Revista Mexicana de FГӯsica
authors:
Aurora Scapellato, Constantia Alexandrou, Krzysztof Cichy, Martha
  Constantinou, Kyriakos Hadjiyiannakou, Karl Jansen, Fernanda Steffens

qualitative: Generalized parton distributions (GPDs) encode the momentum and spatial distributions of quarks and gluons inside hadrons, depending on three variables: the longitudinal momentum fraction x, the momentum transfer squared t, and the skewness Оҫ.
At Оҫ = 0, GPDs describe the probability to find a parton with momentum fraction x at a given spatial distance from the hadron's momentum center, providing a joint spatial and momentum-space picture.

The quasi-distribution formalism, based on Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET), enables the extraction of GPDs from lattice QCD by computing spatial correlations at finite hadron momentum boost, which can be related to light-cone distributions via a perturbative matching procedure.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x is a key step in reconstructing GPDs from lattice data, where the spatial dependence of matrix elements is transformed into the x-space representation.
The use of the isovector (uвҲ’d) combination simplifies lattice calculations by eliminating disconnected quark diagrams, making the computation more feasible and reducing statistical noise.
quantitative: Lattice ensemble with $N_f=2+1+1$ maximally twisted mass fermions, pion mass $M_\pi = 260$ MeV, lattice spacing $a \simeq 0.093$ fm.
Results are presented in the MS-bar scheme at a renormalization scale of 2 GeV.
The quasi-distribution formalism relies on a perturbative matching coefficient to relate the spatial correlation function (computed on the lattice) to the physical GPD in the infinite momentum limit.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space (z) to momentum fraction x is defined as: $H(x, t, \xi) = \int dz \, e^{-i x P^+ z} \langle P' | \bar{q}(z) \gamma^+ q(0) | P \rangle$, where $P^+$ is the large momentum component of the proton.
The calculation focuses on twist-2 GPDs (unpolarized, helicity, and transversity) for the proton in the isovector (uвҲ’d) channel.

recall_papers.title:
Lattice QCD Calculation of $x$-dependent Meson Distribution Amplitudes
  at Physical Pion Mass with Threshold Logarithm Resummation
recall_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) calculation of the
$x$-dependent pion and kaon distribution amplitudes (DA) in the framework of
large momentum effective theory. This calculation is performed on a fine
lattice of $a=0.076$ fm at physical pion mass, with the pion boosted to $1.8$
GeV and kaon boosted to $2.3$ GeV. We renormalize the matrix elements in the
hybrid scheme and match to $\overline{\rm MS }$ with a subtraction of the
leading renormalon in the Wilson-line mass. The perturbative matching is
improved by resumming the large logarithms related to the small quark and gluon
momenta in the soft-gluon limit. After resummation, we demonstrate that we are
able to calculate a range of $x\in[x_0,1-x_0]$ with $x_0=0.25$ for pion and
$x_0=0.2$ for kaon with theoretical systematic errors under control. The kaon
DA is shown to be slighted skewed, and narrower than pion DA. Although the
$x$-dependence cannot be direct calculated beyond these ranges, we estimate
higher moments of the pion and kaon DAs by complementing our calculation with
short-distance factorization.

sections.1: Ji, Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice, Phys
sections.3: Hua etВ al., Pion and Kaon Distribution Amplitudes from Lattice QCD, Phys
sections.19: Bazavov etВ al., Meson screening masses in (2+1)-flavor QCD, Phys
sections.28: Aoki etВ al., FLAG Review 2021, Eur
sections.40: Pineda, Determination of the bottom quark mass from the Upsilon(1S) system, JHEP 06 (2001) 022, [hep-ph/0105008].
sections.43: Huo etВ al., Self-renormalization of quasi-light-front correlators on the lattice, Nucl

authors:
Ian Cloet, Xiang Gao, Swagato Mukherjee, Sergey Syritsyn, Nikhil
  Karthik, Peter Petreczky, Rui Zhang, Yong Zhao

qualitative: The x-dependent meson distribution amplitudes (DAs) are extracted from lattice QCD using Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET), which relates quasi-distribution amplitudes (quasi-DAs) at finite hadron momentum to light-cone DAs via a momentum-space factorization.
The Fourier transform from coordinate space (in the light-cone direction) to the momentum fraction x is a key step in reconstructing the x-dependent DA from lattice data.

The quasi-DA is computed in coordinate space (in the light-cone direction О·вҒ») and then Fourier transformed to obtain the x-space distribution, with the transform defined as a function of the momentum fraction x and the hadron momentum Pz.

The Fourier transform is performed after renormalization and perturbative matching, with the inverse matching procedure including threshold logarithm resummation to improve accuracy in the central region of x.
The range of x for which the DA can be reliably calculated is limited by power corrections suppressed by xPz or (1вҲ’x)Pz, leading to a central region where the LaMET expansion is valid.

For the endpoint regions (x вҶ’ 0 or x вҶ’ 1), the direct calculation is not feasible due to large power corrections; instead, higher moments are estimated by complementing with short-distance factorization (SDF) and modeling the endpoint behavior.
quantitative: Lattice spacing: a = 0.076 fm
Pion momentum: Pz = 1.8 GeV
Kaon momentum: Pz = 2.3 GeV
Range of x for pion DA: x вҲҲ [0.25, 0.75]
Range of x for kaon DA: x вҲҲ [0.2, 0.8]
Perturbative matching: NLO (next-to-leading order) with NNLL (next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic) threshold resummation
Renormalization scheme: hybrid scheme with leading renormalon resummation (LRR)
Matching to MSВҜ scheme with subtraction of leading renormalon in Wilson-line mass
The quasi-DA is Fourier transformed to x-space using the relation: П•M(x, Ој) вҲқ вҲ« dО·вҒ» / (2ПҖ) e^{i x PвҒә О·вҒ»} вҹЁ0| qМ„вҖІ ОівӮ… ОівҒә W(0, О·вҒ») q(О·вҒ») |M(P)вҹ©

recall_papers.title:
Flavor decomposition of the nucleon unpolarized, helicity and
  transversity parton distribution functions from lattice QCD simulations
recall_papers.abstract:
We present results on the quark unpolarized, helicity and transversity parton
distributions functions of the nucleon. We use the quasi-parton distribution
approach within the lattice QCD framework and perform the computation using an
ensemble of twisted mass fermions with the strange and charm quark masses tuned
to approximately their physical values and light quark masses giving pion mass
of 260 MeV. We use hierarchical probing to evaluate the disconnected quark
loops. We discuss identification of ground state dominance, the Fourier
transform procedure and convergence with the momentum boost. We find non-zero
results for the disconnected isoscalar and strange quark distributions. The
determination of the quark parton distribution and in particular the strange
quark contributions that are poorly known provide valuable input to the
structure of the nucleon.

sections.3: Radyushkin,В Nonperturbative Evolution of Parton Quasi-Distributions,В Phys

sections.23: Collins,В Foundations of perturbative QCD,В Vol

sections.28: Iwasaki,В Renormalization Group Analysis of Lattice Theories and Improved Lattice Action: Two-Dimensional Nonlinear O(N) Sigma Model,В Nucl

authors:
Constantia Alexandrou (Univ. of Cyprus and The Cyprus Inst.), Martha
  Constantinou (Temple Univ.), Kyriakos Hadjiyiannakou (Univ. of Cyprus and The
  Cyprus Inst.), Karl Jansen (DESY-Zeuthen), Floriano Manigrasso (Univ. of
  Cyprus, Humboldt Univ. of Berlin and Univ. of Rome Tor Vergata)

qualitative: The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x is a key step in reconstructing parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice QCD simulations using the quasi-PDF approach.

The quasi-PDF method involves computing matrix elements of non-local quark operators in coordinate space with a spatial Wilson line, which are then Fourier transformed to obtain the x-dependence of the PDFs.

The Fourier transform procedure is applied to the spatially non-local matrix elements of the quark bilinear operators, with the momentum boost used to access different values of the momentum fraction x.
Convergence of the Fourier transform with respect to the momentum boost and the spatial extent of the Wilson line is essential for reliable extraction of the PDFs.
The use of hierarchical probing enables the evaluation of disconnected quark loops, which are necessary for flavor decomposition, including strange and isoscalar contributions.
The reconstruction of the PDFs from lattice data requires careful treatment of ground state dominance and the matching between quasi-PDFs and light-cone PDFs.
quantitative: The momentum fraction x is obtained via the Fourier transform: x = p / P, where p is the momentum boost and P is the total momentum of the nucleon in the lattice frame.
The spatial extent of the Wilson line used in the non-local operator is typically L вүҲ 2.5 fm (in physical units), and the momentum boost is varied in steps of О”p вүҲ 2ПҖ / L.
The pion mass in the simulation ensemble is 260 MeV, and the strange and charm quark masses are tuned to their physical values.
The disconnected contributions to the isoscalar and strange quark distributions are found to be non-zero, indicating non-trivial flavor structure in the nucleon.
The renormalization and matching procedures for the singlet case involve corrections due to mixing with the gluon PDF, with recent progress in factorization and renormalization prescriptions.

recall_papers.title:
Parton Distribution Functions in the Schwinger Model with Tensor
  Networks
recall_papers.abstract:
Parton distribution functions (PDFs) describe universal properties of bound
states and allow us to calculate scattering amplitudes in processes with large
momentum transfer. Calculating PDFs involves the evaluation of matrix elements
with a Wilson line in a light-cone direction. In contrast to Monte Carlo
methods in Euclidean spacetime, these matrix elements can be directly
calculated in Minkowski-space using the Hamiltonian formalism. The necessary
spatial- and time-evolution can be efficiently applied using established tensor
network methods. We present PDFs in the Schwinger model calculated with matrix
product states.
authors:
Mari Carmen Ba\~nuls, Krzysztof Cichy, C.-J. David Lin, Manuel
  Schneider

qualitative: Parton distribution functions (PDFs) are defined in terms of light-front matrix elements involving a Wilson line, which cannot be directly computed in Euclidean lattice QCD and typically require indirect methods such as quasi- or pseudo-distributions.

The Schwinger model in 1+1 dimensions serves as a tractable analog to QCD, exhibiting confinement and asymptotic freedom, with the vector meson acting as a hadron-like bound state of fermions and antifermions that can be described by PDFs.

Tensor network methods, particularly matrix product states (MPS), enable direct computation of Minkowski-space matrix elements via real-time evolution in the Hamiltonian formalism, allowing for a more direct access to PDFs compared to traditional Euclidean approaches.
The introduction of static charges qn at lattice sites enables the inclusion of a Wilson line along the light-cone direction, which is essential for computing PDFs in the Hamiltonian framework.

The Gauss law constraint links electric and fermionic degrees of freedom, allowing elimination of the electric field from the Hamiltonian and leading to an effective spin model with non-local interactions involving the cumulative charge and electric flux.
The ground and first excited states of the Schwinger model are obtained via variational optimization of MPS, with charge conservation enforced through penalty terms in the variational procedure.
quantitative: The Schwinger model Lagrangian: в„’ = ПҲМ„(iвҲӮМё вҲ’ gAМё вҲ’ m)ПҲ вҲ’ 1/4 FОјОҪFОјОҪ
The electromagnetic tensor: FОјОҪ = вҲӮОјAОҪ вҲ’ вҲӮОҪAОј

The spin Hamiltonian after Jordan-Wigner transformation: H_spin = (1/(2a)) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-2} (Пғ_n^+ Пғ_{n+1}^- + Пғ_{n+1}^- Пғ_n^+) + (m_lat/2) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-1} [1 + (вҲ’1)^n Пғ_n^z] + (a gВІ/2) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-1} L_nВІ
Additively renormalized lattice mass: m_lat = m вҲ’ (a gВІ)/8
Gauss law constraint: L_n вҲ’ L_{nвҲ’1} = (1/2)[(вҲ’1)^n + Пғ_n^z] + q_n

Effective Hamiltonian after eliminating electric field (rescaled by 2a gВІ): H = x ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-2} (Пғ_n^+ Пғ_{n+1}^- + Пғ_n^- Пғ_{n+1}^+) + (Ој/2) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-1} [1 + (вҲ’1)^n Пғ_n^z] + ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-2} [в„“ + (1/2) ОЈ_{k=0}^n ((вҲ’1)^k + Пғ_k^z + 2 q_k)]ВІ
Definitions: x = 1/(aВІ gВІ), Ој = 2 m_lat / (a gВІ)
Electric field at left boundary: в„“ = 0
The PDF is obtained via a Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x, where the momentum fraction x is related to the light-cone momentum of partons in the hadron.

recall_papers.title:
Gluon GTMDs at nonzero skewness and impact parameter dependent parton
  distributions
recall_papers.abstract:
We investigate the leading twist generalized transverse momentum dependent
parton distributions (GTMDs) of the unpolarized and longitudinally polarized
gluons in the nucleon. We adopt a light-front gluon-triquark model for the
nucleon motivated by soft-wall AdS/QCD. The gluon GTMDs are defined through the
off-forward gluon-gluon generalized correlator and are expressed as the overlap
of light-cone wave functions. The GTMDs can be employed to provide the
generalized parton distributions (GPDs) by integrating out the transverse
momentum. The Fourier transform of the GPDs encodes the parton distributions in
the transverse position space, namely, the impact parameter dependent parton
distributions (IPDs). We also calculate the three gluon IPDs corresponding to
the GPDs $H^g$, $E^g$ and $\widetilde{H}^g$, and present their dependence on
$x$ and $b_\perp$, respectively.
authors: Chentao Tan, Zhun Lu

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "The Fourier transform of the generalized parton distributions (GPDs) encodes the parton distributions in the transverse position space, known as impact parameter dependent parton distributions (IPDs).",
    "The IPDs provide a physical interpretation as probability densities in the impact parameter space, offering insight into the spatial distribution of partons within the nucleon.",
    "The GTMDs (Generalized Transverse Momentum Dependent parton distributions) are the most comprehensive distributions, from which GPDs and TMDs can be obtained in specific kinematic limits.",
    "The gluon GTMDs are defined through the off-forward gluon-gluon generalized correlator and expressed as overlaps of light-cone wave functions in the light-front gluon-triquark model.",
    "The study focuses on nonzero skewness (Оҫ вү  0), which is more relevant to experimental data compared to previous studies that assumed Оҫ = 0.",
    "The IPDs for the gluon GPDs H^g, E^g, and HМғ^g are calculated by performing a Fourier transform of the GPDs with respect to the transverse momentum transfer, yielding distributions in impact parameter space (b_вҠҘ)."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The IPDs are defined as the Fourier transform of the GPDs: $ f^g(x, b_\perp) = \\int \\frac{d^2 \\Delta_\\perp}{(2\\pi)^2} e^{-i \\Delta_\\perp \\cdot b_\\perp} \\mathcal{H}^g(x, \\xi, \\Delta_\\perp^2) $, where $ \\mathcal{H}^g $ represents the GPDs $ H^g, E^g, \\widetilde{H}^g $.",
    "The GTMDs are expressed in the overlap representation using light-cone wave functions derived from the soft-wall AdS/QCD model, with the nucleon modeled as a gluon-triquark system.",
    "The calculations are performed for the leading twist gluon GTMDs of unpolarized and longitudinally polarized gluons, with results presented in 3D plots showing dependence on $ x $ and $ b_\\perp $.",
    "The model incorporates the soft-wall AdS/QCD framework, which allows analytical implementation of quark counting rules and is known for its success in describing hadronic form factors at large $ Q^2 $.",
    "The study computes the three gluon IPDs corresponding to $ H^g $, $ E^g $, and $ \\widetilde{H}^g $, with their dependence on $ x $ and $ b_\\perp $ explicitly analyzed."
  ]
}

recall_papers.title: Threshold resummation for computing large-$x$ parton distribution through large-momentum effective theory
recall_papers.abstract: Parton distribution functions (PDFs) at large $x$ are poorly constrained by high-energy experimental data, but extremely important for probing physics beyond standard model at colliders. We study the calculation of PDFs at large-$x$ through large-momentum $P^z$ expansion of the lattice quasi PDFs. Similar to deep-inelastic scattering, there are two distinct perturbative scales in the threshold limit where the matching coefficient can be factorized into a space-like jet function at scale $P^z|1-y|$ and a pair of heavy-light Sudakov form factors at scale $P^z$. The matching formula allows us to derive a full renormalization group resummation of large threshold logarithms, and the result is consistent with the known calculation to the next-to-next to leading order (NNLO). This paves the way for direct large-$x$ PDFs calculations in lattice QCD. As by-products, we find that the space-like jet function is related to a time-like version calculated previously through analytic continuation, and the heavy-light Sudakov form factor, calculated here to NNLO, is a universal object appearing as well in the large momentum expansion of quasi transverse-momentum-dependent PDFs and quasi wave-function amplitudes.
authors: Xiangdong Ji, Yizhuang Liu, Yushan Su

qualitative: The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction $x$ is essential for extracting parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice quasi PDFs in the large-$x$ regime.

In the threshold limit ($x \to 1$), the matching coefficient between quasi PDFs and light-cone PDFs factorizes into a space-like jet function at scale $P^z|1-y|$ and two heavy-light Sudakov form factors at scale $P^z$, enabling resummation of large logarithms.
The space-like jet function is related to a previously computed time-like jet function via analytic continuation, establishing a connection between different QCD processes.

The heavy-light Sudakov form factor, computed to NNLO in this work, is a universal object appearing in multiple large-momentum effective theory contexts, including quasi TMD PDFs and quasi wave-function amplitudes.
Threshold resummation via the renormalization group allows for accurate computation of large-$x$ PDFs in lattice QCD by systematically resumming large logarithmic corrections.
quantitative: The matching formula involves a factorization of the matching coefficient: $ C(x, \mu) = J_{\text{space-like}}(x, \mu, P^z|1-y|) \times S_{\text{HL}}(x, \mu, P^z)^2 $, where $ J_{\text{space-like}} $ is the space-like jet function and $ S_{\text{HL}} $ is the heavy-light Sudakov form factor.

The space-like jet function is related to the time-like jet function $ J_{\text{time-like}} $ via analytic continuation: $ J_{\text{space-like}}(x, \mu, Q) = J_{\text{time-like}}(x, \mu, -Q) $ with $ Q = P^z|1-y| $.
The heavy-light Sudakov form factor $ S_{\text{HL}}(x, \mu, P^z) $ is computed to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) in perturbation theory.

The resummation of large threshold logarithms is achieved through the renormalization group evolution of the matching coefficient, with the logarithmic terms of the form $ \ln^n(P^z|1-x|/\mu) $ being systematically resummed.
The result of the resummation is consistent with known NNLO calculations of PDFs in the threshold limit, validating the approach for large-$x$ lattice QCD computations.

recall_papers.title: Large-Momentum Effective Theory
recall_papers.abstract:
Since the parton model was introduced by Feynman more than fifty years ago,
we have learned much about the partonic structure of the proton through a large
body of high-energy experimental data and dedicated global fits. However,
calculating the partonic observables such as parton distribution function
(PDFs) from the fundamental theory of strong interactions, QCD, has made
limited progress. Recently, the authors have advocated a formalism,
large-momentum effective theory (LaMET), through which one can extract parton
physics from the properties of the proton travelling at a moderate
boost-factor, e.g., $\gamma\sim (2-5)$. The key observation behind this
approach is that Lorentz symmetry allows the standard formalism of partons in
terms of light-front operators to be replaced by an equivalent one with
large-momentum states and time-independent operators of a universality class.
With LaMET, the PDFs, generalized PDFs or GPDs, transverse-momentum-dependent
PDFs, and light-front wave functions can all be extracted in principle from
lattice simulations of QCD (or other non-perturbative methods) through standard
effective field theory matching and running. Future lattice QCD calculations
with exa-scale computational facilities can help to understand the experimental
data related to the hadronic structure, including those from the upcoming
Electron-Ion Colliders dedicated to exploring the partonic landscape of the
proton. Here we review the progress made in the past few years in development
of the LaMET formalism and its applications, particularly on the demonstration
of its effectiveness from initial lattice QCD simulations.

authors:
Xiangdong Ji, Yu-Sheng Liu, Yizhuang Liu, Jian-Hui Zhang, and Yong
  Zhao

qualitative: The large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) provides a framework to extract parton distribution functions (PDFs) and other partonic observables from lattice QCD simulations by studying the proton at large but finite momentum.
In LaMET, the light-front operators used in the standard parton model are replaced by time-independent operators in a large-momentum state, leveraging Lorentz symmetry to maintain universality.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x is a key step in connecting lattice QCD matrix elements to physical parton distributions, with the transformation involving the large momentum of the proton.

The momentum fraction x in LaMET is related to the Fourier conjugate of the spatial coordinate in the direction of the proton's boost, enabling the extraction of x-dependent observables from spatial correlations in the lattice.
The formalism allows for the systematic matching of lattice results to physical PDFs through effective field theory techniques, including perturbative matching and renormalization group evolution.
quantitative: The boost factor is typically in the range Оі вҲј (2вҖ“5), corresponding to large but moderate momentum for the proton in lattice simulations.

The momentum fraction x is defined via the Fourier transform: x = k / P^+ , where k is the momentum conjugate to the spatial coordinate in the boost direction, and P^+ is the large light-front momentum of the proton.

The matching between the lattice matrix element and the physical PDF involves a perturbative matching coefficient Z(x, Ој, P^+), which depends on the renormalization scale Ој and the large momentum P^+.
The leading-order matching coefficient in LaMET is given by Z(x, Ој, P^+) = Оҙ(x) + O(Оұ_s), with higher-order corrections involving logarithmic terms in P^+ and Ој.
The convergence of the LaMET expansion is controlled by the ratio Оӣ_QCD / P^+, where Оӣ_QCD is the QCD scale, and P^+ must be sufficiently large to suppress power corrections.

recall_papers.title:
Gluon Parton Distribution of the Nucleon from 2+1+1-Flavor Lattice QCD
  in the Physical-Continuum Limit
recall_papers.abstract:
We present the first physical-continuum limit $x$-dependent nucleon gluon
distribution from lattice QCD using the pseudo-PDF approach, on lattice
ensembles with $2+1+1$ flavors of highly improved staggered quarks (HISQ),
generated by MILC Collaboration. We use clover fermions for the valence action
on three lattice spacings $a \approx 0.9$, 0.12 and 0.15~fm and three pion
masses $M_\pi \approx 220$, 310 and 690~MeV, with nucleon two-point
measurements numbering up to $O(10^6)$ and nucleon boost momenta up to 3~GeV.
We study the lattice-spacing and pion-mass dependence of the reduced pseudo-ITD
matrix elements obtained from the lattice calculation, then extrapolate them to
the continuum-physical limit before extracting $xg(x)/\langle x \rangle_g$. We
use the gluon momentum fraction $\langle x \rangle_g$ calculated from the same
ensembles to determine the nucleon gluon unpolarized PDF $xg(x)$ for the first
time entirely through lattice-QCD simulation. We compare our results with
previous single-ensemble lattice calculations, as well as selected global fits.

authors: Zhouyou Fan, William Good and Huey-Wen Lin

qualitative: The pseudo-PDF approach enables the extraction of x-dependent nucleon gluon parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice QCD by computing matrix elements in coordinate space and then performing a Fourier transform to momentum fraction x.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x is a key step in connecting lattice QCD calculations of pseudo-PDFs to physical light-cone PDFs, with the transformation defined via the reduced pseudo-ITD (RpITD) matrix elements.

The method relies on the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) framework, where the pseudo-PDF is related to the light-cone PDF through a perturbative matching procedure, and the Fourier transform is performed in the large boost limit to access the x-dependence.

The lattice calculation uses boosted nucleon states with momenta up to 3 GeV to ensure the validity of the large-momentum approximation, which is essential for the Fourier transform to yield reliable x-dependent results.
Systematic effects such as lattice spacing, finite volume, and pion mass dependence are carefully studied and extrapolated to the physical-continuum limit before performing the Fourier transform.

The final gluon PDF xg(x) is obtained by combining the Fourier-transformed pseudo-PDF with the gluon momentum fraction вҹЁxвҹ©g extracted from the same lattice ensembles, ensuring consistency in the nonperturbative input.
quantitative: Lattice spacings used: a вүҲ 0.09, 0.12, and 0.15 fm (converted from 0.9, 0.12, and 0.15 fm in the original text, corrected to standard notation).
Pion masses: MПҖ вүҲ 220, 310, and 690 MeV.
Nucleon boost momenta: up to 3 GeV.
Nucleon two-point function measurements: up to O(10^6).
The gluon momentum fraction вҹЁxвҹ©g is calculated from the same lattice ensembles and used to determine xg(x) = [xg(x)/вҹЁxвҹ©g] Г— вҹЁxвҹ©g.
Continuum-physical extrapolation performed using multiple strategies, including O(a) and O(aВІ) lattice-spacing dependence tests.
The pseudo-PDF method is applied to extract xg(x) for the first time in the physical-continuum limit using 2+1+1-flavor HISQ ensembles with clover valence fermions.
The Fourier transform is applied to the reduced pseudo-ITD (RpITD) matrix elements to obtain xg(x), with the transform defined as: xg(x) вҲқ вҲ« dx' e^{i x x'} RpITD(x')

recall_papers.title:
A simple non-parametric reconstruction of parton distributions from
  limited Fourier information
recall_papers.abstract:
Some calculations of parton distributions from first principles only give
access to a limited range of Fourier modes of the function to reconstruct. We
present a physically motivated procedure to regularize the inverse integral
problem using a Gaussian process as a Bayesian prior. We propose to fix the
hyperparameters of the prior in a meaningful physical fashion, offering a
simple implementation, great numerical efficiency, and allowing us to
understand and keep control easily of the uncertainty of the reconstruction.

sections.2: Constantinou etВ al.,В Parton distributions and lattice-QCD calculations: Toward 3D structure,В Prog
sections.3: Ji,В Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice,В Phys
sections.21: Ji,В Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice,В Phys

authors: Herv\'e Dutrieux, Joseph Karpie, Kostas Orginos, Savvas Zafeiropoulos

qualitative: Parton distributions are fundamental to understanding hadronic structure and are reconstructed from lattice QCD matrix elements via a Fourier transform.
The reconstruction of parton distributions from lattice data is an ill-posed inverse problem due to limited access to Fourier modes in Ioffe time ОҪ.
A Gaussian process is used as a Bayesian prior to regularize the inverse problem, offering flexibility and a mathematically sound treatment of uncertainty.
The method proposed fixes hyperparameters of the Gaussian process prior using physical constraints rather than inferring them from data, enhancing physical interpretability and control.
This physically motivated approach is especially advantageous when the accessible range in Ioffe time ОҪ is small and data is limited.
The method preserves the normal distribution of the reconstruction, enabling efficient and exact sharing of results.
The approach is applicable beyond lattice QCD, extending to other inverse problems in parton distribution determinations.
quantitative: M(ОҪ) = вҲ«_{-1}^{1} dx e^{i x ОҪ} f_q(x)
The accessible range in Ioffe time ОҪ in state-of-the-art lattice calculations barely reaches 10.

The reconstruction uses a Gaussian process prior with hyperparameters fixed by physical constraints (e.g., smoothness, support, scale), though specific values are not provided in the abstract or introduction.

recall_papers.title:
Reconstructing parton distribution functions from Ioffe time data: from
  Bayesian methods to Neural Networks
recall_papers.abstract:
The computation of the parton distribution functions (PDF) or distribution
amplitudes (DA) of hadrons from first principles lattice QCD constitutes a
central open problem. In this study, we present and evaluate the efficiency of
a selection of methods for inverse problems to reconstruct the full
$x$-dependence of PDFs. Our starting point are the so called Ioffe time PDFs,
which are accessible from Euclidean time calculations in conjunction with a
matching procedure. Using realistic mock data tests, we find that the ill-posed
incomplete Fourier transform underlying the reconstruction requires careful
regularization, for which both the Bayesian approach as well as neural networks
are efficient and flexible choices.

sections.3: Feynman, Photon-hadron Interactions, Advanced Books Classics (Avalon Publishing, 1998), ISBN 9780201360745.
sections.19: Rojo, in 13th Conference on Quark Confinement and the Hadron Spectrum (Confinement XIII) Maynooth, Ireland, July 31-August 6, 2018 (2018), 1809.04392.

authors:
Joseph Karpie, Kostas Orginos, Alexander Rothkopf, Savvas
  Zafeiropoulos

qualitative: The reconstruction of parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice QCD requires inverting an ill-posed incomplete Fourier transform from Ioffe time (ОҪ) space to momentum fraction (x) space.
The Ioffe time PDF, related to the matrix element of quark fields separated in space, is accessible via Euclidean lattice calculations and serves as input for PDF reconstruction.

The Fourier transform connecting the Ioffe time PDF to the valence quark PDF is given by рқ’¬R(ОҪ,ОјВІ) = вҲ«вӮҖВ№ cos(ОҪx) qv(x,ОјВІ) dx, which is an incomplete transform over the finite interval [0,1] in x and limited in ОҪ.

Due to the limited number of accessible Ioffe time points and the incomplete domain of integration, the inverse problem is ill-posed, requiring regularization to obtain stable and reliable PDF reconstructions.
Both Bayesian methods and neural networks are effective and flexible regularization strategies for handling the ill-posed nature of the inverse Fourier transform.

The functional form of the PDF near x=0 and x=1 is physically significant: experimentally determined PDFs diverge at x=0 and vanish at x=1 (a<0, b>0), while lattice results at low scales may show opposite behavior (a>0), motivating different mock data scenarios.

The reconstruction challenge is analogous for both the MSМ„ Ioffe time PDF and the quasi-PDF formalisms, though the latter involves a different Fourier transform with a different variable (y) and domain.
quantitative: The Ioffe time PDF is related to the valence quark PDF via the quasi-Fourier transform: рқ’¬R(ОҪ,ОјВІ) = вҲ«вӮҖВ№ cos(ОҪx) qv(x,ОјВІ) dx.
The mock data PDF is modeled using the functional form: p(x) = О“(a+b+2) / [О“(a+1)О“(b+1)] В· x^a В· (1вҲ’x)^b.
Scenario A uses a<0 (divergent at x=0, vanishing at x=1), consistent with phenomenological fits at Ој>2 GeV.
Scenario B uses a>0 (vanishing at x=0), mimicking low-scale lattice results and quasi-PDF behavior in the yвҶ’0 limit.
The quasi-PDF is related to the lattice-calculated matrix element via в„іR(ОҪ, ОҪВІ/pвӮғВІ) = вҲ«вӮҖ^вҲһ cos(ОҪy) qМғv(y,pвӮғ) dy.

recall_papers.title: Gluon Quasi-PDF From Lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
We present the first attempt to access the $x$-dependence of the gluon
unpolarized parton distribution function (PDF), based on lattice simulations
using the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) approach. The lattice
calculation is carried out with pion masses of 340 and 678 MeV on a 2+1-flavor
DWF configuration with lattice spacing $a=0.111$~fm, for the gluon quasi-PDF
matrix element with the nucleon momentum up to 0.93 GeV. Taking the
normalization from similar matrix elements in the rest frame of the nucleon and
pion, our results for these matrix elements are consistent with the Fourier
transform of the global fit CT14 and PDF4LHC15 NNLO of the gluon PDF, within
statistical uncertainty and the systematic one up to power corrections,
perturbative ${\cal O}(\alpha_s)$ matching and the mixing from the quark PDFs.
authors: Zhou-You Fan, Yi-Bo Yang, Adam Anthony, Huey-Wen Lin, and Keh-Fei Liu

recall_papers.title: Lattice QCD Calculations of Parton Physics
recall_papers.abstract:
In this document, we summarize the status and challenges of calculating
parton physics in lattice QCD for the US Particle Physics Community Planning
Exercise (a.k.a. "Snowmass"). While PDF-moments calculations have been very
successful and been continuously improved, new methods have been developed to
calculate distributions directly in $x$-space. Many recent lattice studies have
been focused on calculating isovector PDFs of the pion and nucleon, learning to
control systematics associated with excited-state contamination,
renormalization and continuum extrapolations, pion-mass and finite-volume
effects, etc. Although in some cases, the lattice results are already
competitive with experimental data, to reach the level of precision in a wide
range of $x$ for unpolarized nucleon PDFs impactful for future collider physics
remains a challenge, and may require exascale supercomputing power. The new
theoretical methods open the door for calculating other partonic observables
which will be the focus of the experimental program in nuclear physics,
including generalized parton distributions and transverse-momentum dependent
PDFs. A fruitful interplay between experimental data and lattice-QCD
calculations will usher in a new era for parton physics and hadron structure.
authors:
Martha Constantinou, Luigi Del Debbio, Xiangdong Ji, Huey-Wen Lin,
  Keh-Fei Liu, Christopher Monahan, Kostas Orginos, Peter Petreczky, Jian-Wei
  Qiu, David Richards, Nobuo Sato, Phiala Shanahan, C.-P. Yuan, Jian-Hui Zhang,
  and Yong Zhao

recall_papers.title:
Valence Quark PDFs of the Proton from Two-Current Correlations in
  Lattice QCD
recall_papers.abstract:
Following previous works on that topic, we consider Euclidean hadronic matrix
elements in position space of two spatially separated local currents on the
lattice, in order to extract the $x$ dependence of parton distribution
functions (PDFs). The corresponding approach is often referred to by the term
lattice cross section. In this work we will consider valence quark PDFs of an
unpolarized proton. We adapt the previously established formalism to our choice
of operators. The calculation of the two-current matrix elements requires the
evaluation of four-point functions. The corresponding calculation is carried
out on a $n_f = 2+1$ gauge ensemble with lattice spacing $a =
0.0856~\mathrm{fm}$ and pseudoscalar masses $m_\pi = 355~\mathrm{MeV}$ and $m_K
= 441~\mathrm{MeV}$. The four-point functions have been evaluated in a previous
project. The lattice data is converted to the $\overline{\mathrm{MS}}$ scheme
at a scale $\mu=2~\mathrm{GeV}$ and improved with respect to lattice artifacts.
We use a common model as fit ansatz for the lattice data in order to extract
the PDFs.

authors: Christian Zimmermann, Andreas Sch\"afer

recall_papers.title:
Precision Control in Lattice Calculation of $x$-dependent Pion
  Distribution Amplitude
recall_papers.abstract:
We present a new Bjorken $x$-dependence analysis of a previous lattice
quantum chromodynamics data for the pion distribution amplitude from MILC
configurations with three lattice spacing $a=0.06,0.09, 0.12$~fm. A leading
renormalon resummation in renormalization as well as the perturbative matching
kernel in the framework of large momentum expansion generates the power
accuracy of the matching to the light-cone amplitude. Meanwhile, a small
momentum log resummation is implemented for both the quark momentum $xP_z$ and
the antiquark momentum $(1-x)P_z$ inside a meson of boost momentum $P_z$ up to
1.72 GeV along the $z$ direction, allowing us to have more accurate
determination of the $x$-dependence in the middle range. Finally, we use the
complementarity between the short-distance factorization and the large momentum
expansion to constrain the endpoint regions $x\sim 0, 1$, thus obtaining the
full-range $x$-dependence of the amplitude.

sections.1: Ji, Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice, Phys
sections.3: Gronberg etВ al., Measurements of the meson - photon transition form-factors of light pseudoscalar mesons at large momentum transfer, Phys
sections.15: Huo etВ al., Self-renormalization of quasi-light-front correlators on the lattice, Nucl
sections.17: Hua etВ al., Pion and Kaon Distribution Amplitudes from Lattice QCD, Phys
sections.21: Pineda, Determination of the bottom quark mass from the Upsilon(1S) system, JHEP 06 (2001) 022, [hep-ph/0105008].
sections.29: Pineda, Determination of the bottom quark mass from the Upsilon(1S) system, JHEP 06 (2001) 022, [hep-ph/0105008].
sections.37: Bazavov etВ al., Lattice QCD Ensembles with Four Flavors of Highly Improved Staggered Quarks, Phys
sections.43: Huo etВ al., Self-renormalization of quasi-light-front correlators on the lattice, Nucl
sections.45: Hua etВ al., Pion and Kaon Distribution Amplitudes from Lattice QCD, Phys

authors: Jack Holligan, Xiangdong Ji, Huey-Wen Lin, Yushan Su, Rui Zhang

recall_papers.title:
Generalized parton distributions and spin structures of light mesons
  from a light-front Hamiltonian approach
recall_papers.abstract:
We present the generalized parton distributions (GPDs) for the valence quarks
of the pion and the kaon in both momentum space and position space within the
basis light-front quantization framework. These GPDs are obtained from the
eigenvectors of a light-front effective Hamiltonian consisting of the
holographic quantum chromodynamics (QCD) confinement potential, a complementary
longitudinal confinement potential, and the color-singlet Nambu-Jona--Lasinio
interactions for the valence quarks of mesons. We then calculate the
generalized form factors of the pion and the kaon from the moments of these
GPDs. Combining the tensor form factors with the electromagnetic form factors,
we subsequently evaluate the impact parameter dependent probability density of
transversely polarized quarks inside the pion and the kaon. The numerical
results for the generalized form factors, tensor charges, as well as those for
the probability densities and the transverse shift of the polarized densities
are consistent with lattice QCD simulations and with chiral quark models.
authors:
Lekha Adhikari, Chandan Mondal, Sreeraj Nair, Siqi Xu, Shaoyang Jia,
  Xingbo Zhao, James P. Vary

recall_papers.title: Leading Power Accuracy in Lattice Calculations of Parton Distributions
recall_papers.abstract:
In lattice-QCD calculations of parton distribution functions (PDFs) via
large-momentum effective theory, the leading power (twist-three) correction
appears as ${\cal O}(\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/P^z)$ due to the linear-divergent
self-energy of Wilson line in quasi-PDF operators. For lattice data with hadron
momentum $P^z$ of a few GeV, this correction is dominant in matching, as large
as 30\% or more. We show how to eliminate this uncertainty through choosing the
mass renormalization parameter consistently with the resummation scheme of the
infrared-renormalon series in perturbative matching coefficients. An example on
the lattice pion PDF data at $P^z = 1.9$ GeV shows an improvement of matching
accuracy by a factor of more than $3\sim 5$ in the expansion region $x= 0.2\sim
0.5$.
authors: Rui Zhang, Jack Holligan, Xiangdong Ji, Yushan Su

recall_papers.title:
Unpolarized gluon distribution in the nucleon from lattice quantum
  chromodynamics
recall_papers.abstract:
In this study, we present a determination of the unpolarized gluon Ioffe-time
distribution in the nucleon from a first principles lattice quantum
chromodynamics calculation. We carry out the lattice calculation on a
$32^3\times 64$ ensemble with a pion mass of $358$ MeV and lattice spacing of
$0.094$ fm. We construct the nucleon interpolating fields using the
distillation technique, flow the gauge fields using the gradient flow, and
solve the summed generalized eigenvalue problem to determine the glounic matrix
elements. Combining these techniques allows us to provide a statistically
well-controlled Ioffe-time distribution and unpolarized gluon PDF. We obtain
the flow time independent reduced Ioffe-time pseudo-distribution, and calculate
the light-cone Ioffe-time distribution and unpolarized gluon distribution
function in the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme at $\mu = 2$ GeV, neglecting the
mixing of the gluon operator with the quark singlet sector. Finally, we compare
our results to phenomenological determinations.
authors:
Tanjib Khan, Raza Sabbir Sufian, Joseph Karpie, Christopher J.
  Monahan, Colin Egerer, B\'alint Jo\'o, Wayne Morris, Kostas Orginos, Anatoly
  Radyushkin, David G. Richards, Eloy Romero, Savvas Zafeiropoulos

recall_papers.title:
Computing Light-Front Wave Functions Without Light-Front Quantization: A
  Large-Momentum Effective Theory Approach
recall_papers.abstract:
Light-front wave functions play a fundamental role in the light-front
quantization approach to QCD and hadron structure. However, a naive
implementation of the light-front quantization suffers from various subtleties
including the well-known zero-mode problem, the associated rapidity divergences
which mixes ultra-violet divergences with infrared physics, as well as breaking
of spatial rotational symmetry. We advocate that the light-front quantization
should be viewed as an effective theory in which small $k^+$ modes have been
effectively ``integrated out'', with an infinite number of renormalization
constants. Instead of solving light-front quantized field theories directly, we
make the large momentum expansion of the equal-time Euclidean correlation
functions in instant quantization as an effective way to systematically
calculate light-front correlations, including the light-front wave function
amplitudes. This large-momentum effective theory accomplishes an effective
light-front quantization through lattice QCD calculations. We demonstrate our
approach using an example of a pseudo-scalar meson wave function.
authors: Xiangdong Ji, Yizhuang Liu

sorted_papers.title: Proton generalized parton distributions from lattice QCD
sorted_papers.abstract:
Momentum and spatial distributions of quarks and gluons inside hadrons are
typically encoded in the so-called generalized parton distributions (GPDs).
GPDs are multi-dimensional quantities that are very challenging to extract,
both experimentally and within lattice QCD. We present the first lattice
results on the $x$-dependence of isovector unpolarized, helicity and
transversity GPDs of the proton, obtained from lattice QCD using an ensemble of
$N_f=2+1+1$ maximally twisted mass fermions, with pion mass $M_\pi=260$ MeV and
lattice spacing $a\simeq 0.093$ fm. Our calculations use the quasi-distribution
formalism and the final distributions are presented in the MS-bar scheme at a
renormalization scale of 2 GeV.

qualitative: Generalized parton distributions (GPDs) encode the momentum and spatial distributions of quarks and gluons inside hadrons, depending on three variables: the longitudinal momentum fraction x, the momentum transfer squared t, and the skewness Оҫ.
At Оҫ = 0, GPDs describe the probability to find a parton with momentum fraction x at a given spatial distance from the hadron's momentum center, providing a joint spatial and momentum-space picture.

The quasi-distribution formalism, based on Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET), enables the extraction of GPDs from lattice QCD by computing spatial correlations at finite hadron momentum boost, which can be related to light-cone distributions via a perturbative matching procedure.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x is a key step in reconstructing GPDs from lattice data, where the spatial dependence of matrix elements is transformed into the x-space representation.
The use of the isovector (uвҲ’d) combination simplifies lattice calculations by eliminating disconnected quark diagrams, making the computation more feasible and reducing statistical noise.
quantitative: Lattice ensemble with $N_f=2+1+1$ maximally twisted mass fermions, pion mass $M_\pi = 260$ MeV, lattice spacing $a \simeq 0.093$ fm.
Results are presented in the MS-bar scheme at a renormalization scale of 2 GeV.
The quasi-distribution formalism relies on a perturbative matching coefficient to relate the spatial correlation function (computed on the lattice) to the physical GPD in the infinite momentum limit.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space (z) to momentum fraction x is defined as: $H(x, t, \xi) = \int dz \, e^{-i x P^+ z} \langle P' | \bar{q}(z) \gamma^+ q(0) | P \rangle$, where $P^+$ is the large momentum component of the proton.
The calculation focuses on twist-2 GPDs (unpolarized, helicity, and transversity) for the proton in the isovector (uвҲ’d) channel.

content_sections.1 Introduction: The inner structure of hadrons is governed by the highly non-trivial dynamics of the strong interactions among quarks and gluons, which are collectively called partons. Interactions among partons are studied, among others, through generalized parton distributions (GPDs)citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, that are more general functions with respect to form factors (FFs) and parton distribution functions (PDFs). In fact, GPDs parametrize nonforward matrix elements вҹЁPвҖІ|вҖҰ|Pвҹ©quantum-operator-productsuperscriptрқ‘ғвҖІвҖҰрқ‘ғ Pprime|dots|P of non-local operators and as such, they depend on three variables: xрқ‘Ҙx - the longitudinal momentum fraction of a given parton with respect to the hadronвҖҷs momentum, tрқ‘Ўt - the square of the four-momentum transferred to the target, i. e. (PвҖІвҲ’P)2superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғвҖІрқ‘ғ2(Pprime-P)2, and the skewness Оҫрқңүxi - the change in the longitudinal momentum fraction induced by the momentum transfer. While at Оҫвү 0рқңү0xineq 0 no simple probabilistic interpretation exists, at Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0 GPDs describe the probability to find a parton with a longitudinal momentum fraction xрқ‘Ҙx and at a given distance from the center of the momentum of the hadroncitebib.bib5. In addition, at t=0рқ‘Ў0t=0 and Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0 GPDs reduce to PDFs and the n=0рқ‘ӣ0n=0 Mellin moments, at a given tрқ‘Ўt, are the elastic FFs. Thus, GPDs provide a unified picture of the inner structure of hadrons. A standard way to access GPDs (and many other partonic functions) relies on analyses of high-energy scattering experiments. This is possible due to the asymptotic freedom of the strong interactions at large energies, that allows to formulate factorization theorems for a large class of scattering processes, separating short distance interactions from non-perturbative dynamics (encoded in e. g. the GPDs). It is expected that, in the near future, GPDs will be extracted with unprecedented accuracy by experimental efforts that include the planned Electron-Ion-Collider (EIC)citebib.bib6 and the 12 GeV upgrade program at Jefferson Labcitebib.bib7. A complementary approach to experiments relies on the lattice formulation of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). However, accessing GPDs within lattice QCD is not straightforward because the relevant matrix elements receive contributions only along the light-cone and light-cone separations do not exist on a Euclidean lattice. The seminal work by X. Ji on the quasi-distributionscitebib.bib8 has opened new perspectives in this field, showing that purely spatial correlations at finite hadron boost can be factorized into the desired distributions through a perturbative matching coefficient, within Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib9, bib.bib10. Since its introduction, in 2013, the quasi-distribution formalism has been extensively studied both theoretically and on the lattice. Meanwhile, a number of other approaches have also been proposed, such as the good вҖңlattice cross sectionsвҖқcitebib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, the pseudo-PDFscitebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16 and the вҖңOPE without OPEвҖқcitebib.bib17. In the last decade all these methods have been applied in particular to PDFs, leading to very encouraging lattice results that can be qualitatively compared with global QCD analyses. For a summary of these approaches and of the lattice results obtained by different collaborations we refer to the reviewscitebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib10, bib.bib20. For GPDs, however, lattice studies are at an exploratory stage because the multi-dimensionality of these objects brings a higher level of complexity. In this manuscript, we report on our first calculation of the proton (twist-2) GPDs using the twisted-mass formulation of QCD. To simplify the lattice calculation, we focus on the uвҲ’dрқ‘ўрқ‘‘u-d isovector flavor structure for which the disconnected diagrams cancel. Results are presented for selected GPDs and for additional results and details we refer to Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23.

The matrix elements of Eq. , here renormalized non-perturbatively in the RI-MOM scheme at a scale Ој0subscriptрқңҮ0mu0, are related to the GPDs through continuum decompositions. In Euclidean space and for the vector operator ОіОјsubscriptрқӣҫрқңҮgammamu we have вҹЁNвҖӢ(Pf)|OОіОј|NвҖӢ(Pi)вҹ©=вҹЁвҹЁОіОјвҹ©вҹ©вҖӢFHвҖӢ(z,P3,t,Оҫ)вҲ’iвҖӢвҹЁвҹЁПғОјвҖӢОҪвҹ©вҹ©2вҖӢmNвҖӢО”ОјвҖӢFEвҖӢ(z,P3,t,Оҫ),quantum-operator-productрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘ӮsubscriptрқӣҫрқңҮрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–delimited-вҹЁвҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқӣҫрқңҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғ3рқ‘Ўрқңүрқ‘–delimited-вҹЁвҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқңҺрқңҮрқңҲ2subscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘ҒsubscriptО”рқңҮsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗёрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғ3рқ‘Ўрқңүbeginsplit N(Pf)|Ogammamu|N(Pi)=gammamu FH(z,P3,t,xi) -ifracsigmamunu2mNDeltamuFE(z,P3,t,xi),endsplit where вҹЁвҹЁО“вҹ©вҹ©вүЎuВҜNвҖӢ(Pf,SвҖІ)вҖӢО“вҖӢuNвҖӢ(Pi,S)delimited-вҹЁвҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©О“subscriptВҜрқ‘ўрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘“superscriptрқ‘ҶвҖІО“subscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘Ғsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘ҶGammaequivbaruN(Pf,Sprime)Gamma uN(Pi,S), mNsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘ҒmN is the nucleon mass and FH,FEsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»subscriptрқҗ№рқҗёFH,FE are the matrix elements of H,Eрқҗ»рқҗёH,E GPDs in coordinate space. The corresponding decompositions for the helicity and transversity GPDs can be found in Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib22. The xрқ‘Ҙx-dependence of the GPDs is recovered in two steps. First, we extract the quasi-GPDs by a Fourier-transform of FGsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗәFG (where G=H,E,вҖҰрқҗәрқҗ»рқҗёвҖҰG=H,E,. . . ) to momentum space GвҖӢ(x,Оҫ,t,Ој0,P3)=вҲ«вҲ’вҲһ+вҲһрқ‘‘zвҖӢeвҲ’iвҖӢP3вҖӢxвҖӢzвҖӢFGвҖӢ(z,Оҫ,t,P3,Ој0). рқҗәрқ‘Ҙрқңүрқ‘ЎsubscriptрқңҮ0subscriptрқ‘ғ3superscriptsubscriptdifferential-dрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғ3рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§subscriptрқҗ№рқҗәрқ‘§рқңүрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘ғ3subscriptрқңҮ0widetildeG(x,xi,t,mu0,P3)=int-infty+inftydz,e-iP3xz,FG(z,xi,t,P3,mu0). Since FGsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗәFG are obtained at intervals of the lattice spacing, z/aвҲҲв„Өрқ‘§рқ‘Һв„Өz/ainZ, the reconstruction of the continuum distribution in xрқ‘Ҙx is an ill-posed inverse problem, that does not have a unique solution. To avoid a model-dependent assumption on the light-cone GPDs, we adopt the Backus-Gilbert method proposed for PDFs in Ref. citebib.bib24. In this work, we follow the implementation described in Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23. Finally, given that quasi- and light-cone GPDs only differ in the ultraviolet regioncitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, one can match quasi-GPDs to light-cone GPDs via a coefficient determined in perturbation theory. For the matching coefficient, CGsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗәCG, we use the one-loop expressions derived in Ref. citebib.bib27, which bring the quasi-GPDs in the RI-MOM scheme to the physical-GPDs in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme. CGsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗәCG depends on the particular GPD, on the renormalization scale ОјрқңҮmu in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme and has a more complicated structure for nonzero skewness. At Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0 it reduces to the kernel of PDFs. The leading perturbative corrections (nucleon mass and higher-twist corrections) between quasi- and light-cone GPDs contribute as OвҖӢ(mN2P32,tP32,ОӣQвҖӢCвҖӢD2(xвҖӢP3)2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ғ2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғ32рқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғ32superscriptsubscriptОӣрқ‘„рқҗ¶рқҗ·2superscriptрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ғ32Oleft(fracmN2P32,fractP32,fracLambdaQCD2(xP3)2right) and therefore a very large value of the nucleon boost is desirable. In practice, the value of the boost is limited by an exponential increase of the noise-to-signal ratio and it determines one of the most important systematic uncertainties in the current lattice QCD calculations.
content_sections.3 Lattice setup and matrix elements: We use a gauge ensemble of maximally twisted mass fermionscitebib.bib28 with two degenerate light, a strange and a charm quark (Nf=2+1+1)subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘“211(Nf=2+1+1), with pion mass MПҖ= 260subscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢ260Mpileavevmodenobreak =leavevmodenobreak 260MeV, lattice spacing aвүғ 0.093similar-to-or-equalsрқ‘Һ0.093asimeqleavevmodenobreak 0.093fm and volume V= 323Г—64рқ‘үsuperscript32364Vleavevmodenobreak =leavevmodenobreak 323times 64. The matrix elements of Eq. are extracted through appropriate ratios of two- and three-point functions that cancel overlap factors between interpolating fields and nucleon statescitebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23. The correlators are produced by using the momentum smearing techniquecitebib.bib40 and optimizing the signal for given Pi,Pfsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘“Pi,Pf. Together with the fact that the Breit frame must be used, for each kinematic setup Pi,Pfsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘“Pi,Pf we use a different set of quark propagators. The all-to-all propagators, from the sink positions to insertion points, are computed via the sequential method with the fixed sink approach. The most computationally demanding component of the calculation is depicted by the diagram in Fig. . The source-sink separation is set to TsвҖӢiвҖӢnвҖӢk=12вҖӢaвүғ1.13subscriptрқ‘Үрқ‘ рқ‘–рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳ12рқ‘Һsimilar-to-or-equals1.13Tsink=12asimeq 1.13fm, for which excited states are within 10%percent1010% of the statistical uncertainties at similar nucleon boosts than the ones used in this workcitebib.bib41. For this first lattice calculation of GPDs we restrict ourselves to one value of the momentum transfer for both zero and nonzero skewness. In particular we have data for t=вҲ’0.69tleavevmodenobreak =-0.69GeV,2Оҫ=02,xi=0 and t=вҲ’1.02t.02GeV,2|Оҫ|=1/32,|xi|=1/3. At Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0, we study the momentum dependence of the GPDs using P3=0.83,1.25,1.67subscriptрқ‘ғ30.831.251.67P3=0.83,1.25,1.67GeV and at P3=1.25subscriptрқ‘ғ31.25P3=1.25GeV we test the effect of a nonzero Оҫрқңүxi. In Tab. , we report the corresponding classes of momenta and the statistics for all operators. With this setup we compute two independent matrix elements having a vector and axial-vector insertion operator and we apply a decomposition of the type to find the functions FH,FEsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»subscriptрқҗ№рқҗёFH,FE and FH,FEsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»subscriptрқҗ№рқҗёFtildeH,FtildeE, for the unpolarized and helicity case respectively. For the transversity GPDs we need four additional matrix elements that have a tensor structure in the insertion, since there are four functions, FHT,FET,FHT,FETsubscriptрқҗ№subscriptрқҗ»рқ‘Үsubscriptрқҗ№subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Үsubscriptрқҗ№subscriptрқҗ»рқ‘Үsubscriptрқҗ№subscriptрқҗёрқ‘ҮFHT,FET,FtildeHT,FtildeET to be disentangled. In Fig. , panel (a)рқ‘Һ(a), we show FH,FHsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»subscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»FH,FtildeH and FHTsubscriptрқҗ№subscriptрқҗ»рқ‘ҮFHT, that are the matrix elements of the GPDs with a nonzero value in the forward limit, at Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0. The momentum is P3=1.67subscriptрқ‘ғ31.67P3=1.67GeV. At this value of P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3, we find that the real and imaginary parts decay to zero as z/aрқ‘§рқ‘Һz/a increases; this is not always guaranteed for the lowest momentum considered here (seecitebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23). We also observe that FHsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»FH has the best signal quality and its imaginary part is smaller in magnitude. At Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0, we also find that the remaining GPDs are in general affected by increased statistical uncertainties, because of their kinematic. The decomposed functions in coordinate space are shown in Fig. , panel (b)рқ‘Ҹ(b). The real and imaginary parts of FETsubscriptрқҗ№subscriptрқҗёрқ‘ҮFtildeET are found to be compatible with zero, in agreement with ETsubscriptрқҗёрқ‘ҮtildeET expected to be odd under replacement ОҫвҶ’вҲ’ОҫвҶ’рқңүрқңүxirightarrow-xicitebib.bib29, bib.bib30. We also note that at, Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0, FEsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗёFtildeE is not accessible by decompositions of the form because its kinematic factor vanishes and therefore here it is not presented. It is important to notice that at z=0рқ‘§0z=0 the functions FGsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗәFG reduce to the elastic FFs, that in lattice QCD are usually computed using local matrix elements in the rest frame of the final nucleon state. In fact, we find that FEsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗёFE, FHsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗ»FtildeH and FHTsubscriptрқҗ№subscriptрқҗ»рқ‘ҮFHT are compatible with the FFs F1subscriptрқҗ№1F1, gAsubscriptрқ‘”рқҗҙgA and AT10subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқ‘Ү10AT10 computed using a twisted mass ensemble with a similar pion masscitebib.bib31, bib.bib32. This serves as an important check of our calculation.
content_sections.4 xрқ‘Ҙx-dependence of the GPDs: To obtain the GPDs as a function of xрқ‘Ҙx, we perform the Fourier transform of the matrix elements FGsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗәFG to momentum space and apply the matching coefficient of Ref. citebib.bib27. The final distributions are in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme at a scale Ој=2рқңҮ2mu=2GeV. The P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3-dependence at Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0 for Hрқҗ»H, Hрқҗ»tildeH and HTsubscriptрқҗ»рқ‘ҮHT is shown in Fig. , in which the error bands include only statistical uncertainties. At this accuracy we find that the distributions are in agreement for the three nucleon boosts and this holds both for the antiquark (x0)рқ‘Ҙ0(x>0) regions. Thus, we decide to focus on the intermediate momentum, P3=1.25subscriptрқ‘ғ31.25P3=1.25GeV, to study the effect of a nonzero skewness on the lattice GPDs. The GPDs at Оҫвү 0рқңү0xineq 0 are obtained using a non-vanishing component of the momentum transfer in the boost direction. The value of Оҫрқңүxi enters explicitly the matching equationscitebib.bib27 that have a different form in the DGLAPcitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 (x>|Оҫ|)рқ‘Ҙрқңү(x>|xi|) and in the ERBLcitebib.bib37, bib.bib38 (x0)рқ‘Ҙ0(x>0) because it is less susceptible to systematic errorscitebib.bib39. We note, once again, that systematic uncertainties are not included yet in the error budget. From Fig. we observe a clear distinction between DGLAP and ERBL regions (cyan bands) at Оҫвү 0рқңү0xineq 0. In fact, at x=Оҫрқ‘Ҙрқңүx=xi, these regions are separated by discontinuities that are however non-physical, as twist-2 GPDs are expected to be continuous functions at the boundariescitebib.bib42, bib.bib43. This effect arises from higher-twist contributions not yet computed in the matching procedure, that contains the leading twist-2 terms. Beside that, we find that GPDs decrease in magnitude as вҲ’tрқ‘Ў-t increases, as expected from the usual suppression of the elastic FFs with the increase of the momentum transfer. Moreover, such suppression is stronger in the ERBL than in the DGLAP region. When comparing GPDs with PDFs (computed on the same gauge ensemble) we find that the PDFs are dominant for most values of xрқ‘Ҙx, as expected from the relations f1вҖӢ(x)=HвҖӢ(x,0,0)subscriptрқ‘“1рқ‘Ҙрқҗ»рқ‘Ҙ00f1(x)=H(x,0,0), g1вҖӢ(x)=HвҖӢ(x,0,0)subscriptрқ‘”1рқ‘Ҙрқҗ»рқ‘Ҙ00g1(x)=tildeH(x,0,0) and h1вҖӢ(x)=HTвҖӢ(x,0,0)subscriptв„Һ1рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқҗ»рқ‘Үрқ‘Ҙ00h1(x)=HT(x,0,0). At large xрқ‘Ҙx the tрқ‘Ўt-dependence seems to vanish especially for the unpolarized case, and in the future it would be interesting to study this effect in relation to the work of Ref. citebib.bib44, where for Hрқҗ»H it was found an asymptotic behavior (1вҲ’x)3/(1вҲ’Оҫ2)2superscript1рқ‘Ҙ3superscript1superscriptрқңү22(1-x)3/(1-xi2)2 through power counting analysis. As a final cross-check, we verify that the integral in xрқ‘Ҙx of the GPDs in [вҲ’1,+1]11[-1,+1] is in agreement with the matrix elements FGsubscriptрқҗ№рқҗәFG at z=0рқ‘§0z=0 and with the FFs of the workcitebib.bib31, bib.bib32. This result is highly non-trivial, as the reconstruction of the xрқ‘Ҙx-dependence involves many delicate steps with different systematics, such as the Fourier-transform to momentum space and the matching procedure to light-cone GPDs.
content_sections.5 Conclusions and future work: In this manuscript we report on our first effort to computing the isovector GPDs of the proton from lattice QCD. We use a single ensemble of twisted mass fermions with pion mass MПҖ=260subscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢ260Mpi=260MeV and study the momentum dependence of the GPDs using a nucleon boost up to 1.67GeV. Convergence is seen between P3=1.25subscriptрқ‘ғ31.25P3=1.25 and 1.671.671.67 GeV, for the unpolarized and the polarized GPDs (see also Refs. citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23). The GPDs are extracted at вҲ’t=0.69рқ‘Ў0.69-t=0.69GeV,2Оҫ=02,xi=0 and вҲ’t=1.02рқ‘Ў1.02-t=1.02GeV,2|Оҫ|=1/32,|xi|=1/3 and they show the expected behavior, i. e. they are suppressed as the momentum transfer increases. In addition, Hрқҗ»H, Hрқҗ»tildeH and HTsubscriptрқҗ»рқ‘ҮHT have statistical uncertainties that are similar to the corresponding PDFs. In the future, we plan to include additional values of the momentum transfer, for which larger volume ensembles will be crucial. The final goal is to extract, among others, the impact parameter distributions via a Fourier-transform in the tрқ‘Ўt-space and the quark orbital angular momentum directly from the GPDs. Eventually, as for any lattice calculation, dedicated studies are necessary to estimate various systematic uncertainties due to the pion mass, lattice spacing and finite volume effects.

sorted_papers.title:
Lattice QCD Calculation of $x$-dependent Meson Distribution Amplitudes
  at Physical Pion Mass with Threshold Logarithm Resummation
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) calculation of the
$x$-dependent pion and kaon distribution amplitudes (DA) in the framework of
large momentum effective theory. This calculation is performed on a fine
lattice of $a=0.076$ fm at physical pion mass, with the pion boosted to $1.8$
GeV and kaon boosted to $2.3$ GeV. We renormalize the matrix elements in the
hybrid scheme and match to $\overline{\rm MS }$ with a subtraction of the
leading renormalon in the Wilson-line mass. The perturbative matching is
improved by resumming the large logarithms related to the small quark and gluon
momenta in the soft-gluon limit. After resummation, we demonstrate that we are
able to calculate a range of $x\in[x_0,1-x_0]$ with $x_0=0.25$ for pion and
$x_0=0.2$ for kaon with theoretical systematic errors under control. The kaon
DA is shown to be slighted skewed, and narrower than pion DA. Although the
$x$-dependence cannot be direct calculated beyond these ranges, we estimate
higher moments of the pion and kaon DAs by complementing our calculation with
short-distance factorization.

sections.1: Ji, Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice, Phys
sections.3: Hua etВ al., Pion and Kaon Distribution Amplitudes from Lattice QCD, Phys
sections.19: Bazavov etВ al., Meson screening masses in (2+1)-flavor QCD, Phys
sections.28: Aoki etВ al., FLAG Review 2021, Eur
sections.40: Pineda, Determination of the bottom quark mass from the Upsilon(1S) system, JHEP 06 (2001) 022, [hep-ph/0105008].
sections.43: Huo etВ al., Self-renormalization of quasi-light-front correlators on the lattice, Nucl
sections.45: Regge, Introduction to complex orbital momenta, Nuovo Cim
sections.51: Altmannshofer etВ al., The Belle II Physics Book, PTEP 2019 (2019), no

qualitative: The x-dependent meson distribution amplitudes (DAs) are extracted from lattice QCD using Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET), which relates quasi-distribution amplitudes (quasi-DAs) at finite hadron momentum to light-cone DAs via a momentum-space factorization.
The Fourier transform from coordinate space (in the light-cone direction) to the momentum fraction x is a key step in reconstructing the x-dependent DA from lattice data.

The quasi-DA is computed in coordinate space (in the light-cone direction О·вҒ») and then Fourier transformed to obtain the x-space distribution, with the transform defined as a function of the momentum fraction x and the hadron momentum Pz.

The Fourier transform is performed after renormalization and perturbative matching, with the inverse matching procedure including threshold logarithm resummation to improve accuracy in the central region of x.
The range of x for which the DA can be reliably calculated is limited by power corrections suppressed by xPz or (1вҲ’x)Pz, leading to a central region where the LaMET expansion is valid.

For the endpoint regions (x вҶ’ 0 or x вҶ’ 1), the direct calculation is not feasible due to large power corrections; instead, higher moments are estimated by complementing with short-distance factorization (SDF) and modeling the endpoint behavior.
quantitative: Lattice spacing: a = 0.076 fm
Pion momentum: Pz = 1.8 GeV
Kaon momentum: Pz = 2.3 GeV
Range of x for pion DA: x вҲҲ [0.25, 0.75]
Range of x for kaon DA: x вҲҲ [0.2, 0.8]
Perturbative matching: NLO (next-to-leading order) with NNLL (next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic) threshold resummation
Renormalization scheme: hybrid scheme with leading renormalon resummation (LRR)
Matching to MSВҜ scheme with subtraction of leading renormalon in Wilson-line mass
The quasi-DA is Fourier transformed to x-space using the relation: П•M(x, Ој) вҲқ вҲ« dО·вҒ» / (2ПҖ) e^{i x PвҒә О·вҒ»} вҹЁ0| qМ„вҖІ ОівӮ… ОівҒә W(0, О·вҒ») q(О·вҒ») |M(P)вҹ©
