content_sections.III Lattice setup III.1 Numerical method: To improve the overlap between the states generated by the interpolating field NОұвҖӢ(x)=ОөaвҖӢbвҖӢcвҖӢuОұaвҖӢ(x)вҖӢ(dbвҖӢTвҖӢ(x)вҖӢрқ’һвҖӢОі5вҖӢucвҖӢ(x))subscriptрқ‘Ғрқӣјрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҖрқ‘Һрқ‘Ҹрқ‘җsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ўрқӣјрқ‘Һрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ҹрқ‘Үрқ‘Ҙрқ’һsuperscriptрқӣҫ5superscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘җрқ‘ҘNalpha(x)=varepsilonabcualphaa(x)left(dbT(x)Cgamma5uc(x)right) and the proton ground state we employ Gaussian smearingcitebib.bib65, bib.bib66. In addition, we use APE smearing for the gauge links that enter the Gaussian smearing. The optimal parameters for the Gaussian and APE smearing techniques, determined in Ref. citebib.bib67, are (ОұG,NG)=(4.0,50)subscriptрқӣјGsubscriptрқ‘ҒG4.050(alpharm G,Nrm G)=(4.0,50) and (ОұAPE,NAPE)=(0.5,50)subscriptрқӣјAPEsubscriptрқ‘ҒAPE0.550(alpharm APE,Nrm APE)=(0.5,50), respectively. Moreover, to further improve the overlap with the boosted proton ground state, we use momentum smearingcitebib.bib68, as it has been proven to drastically reduce the statistical noise in the matrix elements of boosted hadronscitebib.bib69. In Ref. citebib.bib70 and in this work, the momentum smearing parameter has been tuned to Оҫ=0.6рқңү0.6xi=0.6, which minimizes the statistical errors. The momentum smearing operator рқ’®рқ’®S on a quark field ПҲвҖӢ(x)рқң“рқ‘Ҙpsi(x) reads рқ’®вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(x)=11+6вҖӢОұGвҖӢ(ПҲвҖӢ(x)+ОұGвҖӢвҲ‘j=13UjвҖӢ(x)вҖӢeiвҖӢОҫвҖӢPвҶ’вӢ…jвҖӢПҲвҖӢ(x+j)),рқ’®рқң“рқ‘Ҙ116subscriptрқӣјрқҗәрқң“рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқӣјрқҗәsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘—13subscriptрқ‘Ҳрқ‘—рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘’вӢ…рқ‘–рқңүвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘—рқң“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘—Spsi(x)=frac11+6alphaGleft(psi(x)+alphaGsumlimitsj=13Uj(x)eixivecPcdothatjpsi(x+hatj)right), where Оҫрқңүxi is the momentum smearing parameter and jрқ‘—j runs over the spatial directions, with UjвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘Ҳрқ‘—рқ‘ҘUj(x) being the link in the spatial jрқ‘—j-direction. To evaluate the connected contributions to the three-point functions, we employ the sequential methodcitebib.bib71 through the sink. Moreover, to further increase the number of measurements, we compute the three-point functions with Nsrcsubscriptрқ‘ҒsrcNrm src different source positions on each configuration and we boost the nucleon along all the spatial directions and orientations, i. e. Вұx,Вұy,Вұzplus-or-minusрқ‘Ҙplus-or-minusрқ‘Ұplus-or-minusрқ‘§pm x,pm y,pm z. Indeed, in Ref. citebib.bib41 it was found that the statistical uncertainty decreases as 1/NsrcвҖӢNdirs1subscriptрқ‘Ғsrcsubscriptрқ‘Ғdirs1/sqrtNrm srcNrm dirs for all the operators under consideration, with Ndirs=6subscriptрқ‘Ғdirs6Nrm dirs=6 being the number of directions of the nucleon boost. The number of source positions employed depends on the nucleon boost, and is Nsrc=8subscriptрқ‘Ғsrc8Nrm src=8 for the two lowest values of the momentum and Nsrc=14subscriptрқ‘Ғsrc14Nrm src=14 at P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,operatornamerm GeV for the third. The source-sink separation is ts=0.94вҖӢfmsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 0.94fmts=0.94,operatornamerm fm for the lowest momentum value, and ts=1.13вҖӢfmsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 1.13fmts=1.13,operatornamerm fm for the two highest ones. Eq. is a correlation of two parts: the nucleon two-point function and the quark loop with a Wilson line. The latter can be written as в„’u+dвҖӢ(П„;z;О“)=вҲ‘yвҶ’TrвҒЎ[(рқ’ўuвҖӢ(y;y+z)+рқ’ўdвҖӢ(y;y+z))вҖӢО“вҖӢWвҖӢ(z)]=вҲ‘yвҶ’TrвҒЎ[ПҲВҜвҖӢ(y+z)вҖӢО“вҖӢWвҖӢ(z)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(y)],superscriptв„’udрқңҸрқ‘§О“subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҰTrsubscriptрқ’ўрқ‘ўрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұрқ‘§subscriptрқ’ўрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұрқ‘§О“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҰTrВҜрқң“рқ‘Ұрқ‘§О“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§рқң“рқ‘ҰbeginsplitLrm u+d(tau;z;Gamma)=sumlimitsvecyoperatornameTrleft[left(Gu(y;y+z)+Gd(y;y+z)right)Gamma W(z)right] =sumlimitsvecyoperatornameTrleft[overlinepsi(y+z)Gamma W(z)psi(y)right],,endsplit where the trace in the second line is intended over volume, spin and flavor indices. Due to the presence of the all-to-all propagator рқ’ўfвҖӢ(xвҶ’,tx;yвҶ’,ty)subscriptрқ’ўрқ‘“вҶ’рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҘвҶ’рқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҰGf(vecx,tx;vecy,ty), the exact evaluation of the disconnected contribution in Eq. would require вүҲ107absentsuperscript107approx 107 inversions of the Dirac operator per configuration for the lattice that we are considering. In contrast, stochastic techniques allow to carry out the computation of the disconnected loops with a feasible but yet high computational cost compared to the currently available resources. Stochastic techniques employ noise sources ОҫrвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқңүрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ҙxir(x) that obey two properties 1NrвҖӢвҲ‘rОҫrвҖӢ(x)=0+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1Nr)1NrвҖӢвҲ‘rОҫrвҖӢ(x)вҠ—ОҫrвҲ—вҖӢ(y)=ОҙвҖӢ(x,y)вҖӢОҙОұвҖӢОІвҖӢОҙaвҖӢb+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1Nr),1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқңүрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ҙ0рқ’Ә1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹ1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқ‘ҹtensor-productsubscriptрқңүрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ҙsubscriptsuperscriptрқңүрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ұрқӣҝрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰsubscriptрқӣҝрқӣјрқӣҪsubscriptрқӣҝрқ‘Һрқ‘Ҹрқ’Ә1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹbeginsplitfrac1Nrsumlimitsrxir(x)=0+Oleft(frac1sqrtNrright) frac1Nrsumlimitsrxir(x)otimesxi*r(y)=delta(x,y)deltaalphabetadeltaab+Oleft(frac1sqrtNrright),,endsplit where the product between source vector has to be intended as a tensor product in volume, spin and color subspaces. Given the set of vectors ОҫrвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқңүрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ҙxir(x), the all-to-all propagator can be constructed by solving the equation MвҖӢ(x,y)вҖӢП•rвҖӢ(y)=ОҫrвҖӢ(x),рқ‘Җрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұsubscriptitalic-П•рқ‘ҹрқ‘Ұsubscriptрқңүрқ‘ҹрқ‘ҘM(x,y)phir(y)=xir(x),, MвҖӢ(x,y)рқ‘Җрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰM(x,y) being the Dirac twisted-mass operator. Having the set of solutions П•rвҖӢ(x)subscriptitalic-П•рқ‘ҹрқ‘Ҙphir(x), the all-to-all propagator can be estimated via рқ’ўвҖӢ(x;y)=1NrвҖӢвҲ‘rП•rвҖӢ(x)вҖӢОҫrвҖ вҖӢ(y)+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1Nr). рқ’ўрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұ1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқ‘ҹsubscriptitalic-П•рқ‘ҹрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscriptрқңүрқ‘ҹвҖ рқ‘Ұрқ’Ә1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ҹG(x;y)=frac1Nrsumlimitsrphir(x)xirdagger(y)+Oleft(frac1sqrtNrright),. The number of stochastic vectors required so that the stochastic error becomes comparable to the gauge error should be much smaller than calculating the all-to-all propagator exactly. In addition, exploiting a property of the twisted mass operator, it is possible to design a stochastic algorithm that further reduce the computational cost. Recalling the transformation of Eq. , the insertion operator О“О“Gamma in twisted basis reads О“tmвүЎeiвҖӢОұ2вҖӢОі5вҖӢП„3вҖӢО“вҖӢeiвҖӢОұ2вҖӢОі5вҖӢП„3,superscriptО“tmsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқӣј2superscriptрқӣҫ5superscriptрқңҸ3О“superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқӣј2superscriptрқӣҫ5superscriptрқңҸ3Gammarm tmequiv eifracalpha2gamma5tau3Gamma eifracalpha2gamma5tau3,, with Оұ=ПҖ/2рқӣјрқңӢ2alpha=pi/2 at maximal-twist. Depending on the operator О“О“Gamma it is possible to exploit two properties of the twisted-mass operator to evaluate the loop of Eq. with a stochastic techniquecitebib.bib72, bib.bib73: 1. If [О“tm,Оі5]=0superscriptО“tmsuperscriptрқӣҫ50[Gammarm tm,gamma5]=0 for a particular О“О“Gamma, then П„3superscriptрқңҸ3tau3 appears in the loop of Eq. when expressed in the twisted basis в„’u+dвҖӢ(П„;z;О“)=в„’tmu+dвҖӢ(П„;z;iвҖӢОі5вҖӢП„3вҖӢО“)=вҲ‘yвҶ’TrвҒЎ[(рқ’ўutmвҖӢ(y;y+z)вҲ’рқ’ўdtmвҖӢ(y;y+z))вҖӢiвҖӢОі5вҖӢО“вҖӢWвҖӢ(z,0)]. superscriptв„’рқ‘ўрқ‘‘рқңҸрқ‘§О“subscriptsuperscriptв„’рқ‘ўрқ‘‘tmрқңҸрқ‘§рқ‘–superscriptрқӣҫ5superscriptрқңҸ3О“subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҰTrsuperscriptsubscriptрқ’ўрқ‘ўtmрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ’ўtmрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұрқ‘§рқ‘–superscriptрқӣҫ5О“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§0beginsplitLu+d(tau;z;Gamma)=Lu+drm tm(tau;z;igamma5tau3Gamma) =sumlimitsvecyoperatornameTrleft[left(Gurm tm(y;y+z)-Grm tmd(y;y+z)right)igamma5Gamma W(z,0)right],. endsplit In this case, we apply the standard one-end trick, that exploit the following property of the twisted mass operator рқ’ўutmвҲ’рқ’ўdtm=вҲ’2вҖӢiвҖӢОјвҖӢ(в„іuвҖ вҖӢв„іu)вҲ’1вҖӢОі5. superscriptsubscriptрқ’ўрқ‘ўtmsuperscriptsubscriptрқ’ўрқ‘‘tm2рқ‘–рқңҮsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptв„ірқ‘ўвҖ subscriptв„ірқ‘ў1superscriptрқӣҫ5Gurm tm-Gdrm tm=-2imu(MudaggerMu)-1gamma5,. The transversity operator О“=Пғ3вҖӢjО“subscriptрқңҺ3рқ‘—Gamma=sigma3j belongs in this category. 2. If О“tm,Оі5=0superscriptО“tmsuperscriptрқӣҫ50Gammarm tm,gamma5=0, then the loop in twisted basis possess the same analytical form as in the physical basis в„’u+dвҖӢ(П„;z;О“)=в„’tmu+dвҖӢ(П„;z;О“)=вҲ‘yвҶ’TrвҒЎ[(рқ’ўutmвҖӢ(y;y+z)+рқ’ўdtmвҖӢ(y;y+z))вҖӢО“вҖӢWвҖӢ(z,0)]. superscriptв„’рқ‘ўрқ‘‘рқңҸрқ‘§О“subscriptsuperscriptв„’рқ‘ўрқ‘‘tmрқңҸрқ‘§О“subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҰTrsuperscriptsubscriptрқ’ўрқ‘ўtmрқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ’ўtmрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұрқ‘§О“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§0beginsplitLu+d(tau;z;Gamma)=Lu+drm tm(tau;z;Gamma) =sumlimitsvecyoperatornameTrleft[left(Gurm tm(y;y+z)+Grm tmd(y;y+z)right)Gamma W(z,0)right],. endsplit The quantity of interest can be computed with the generalized one-end trick, exploiting the following property рқ’ўutm+рқ’ўdtm=2вҖӢОі5вҖӢDWвҖӢ(в„іuвҖ вҖӢв„іu)вҲ’1вҖӢОі5,superscriptsubscriptрқ’ўрқ‘ўtmsuperscriptsubscriptрқ’ўрқ‘‘tm2superscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқҗ·рқ‘Ҡsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptв„ірқ‘ўвҖ subscriptв„ірқ‘ў1superscriptрқӣҫ5Gurm tm+Gdrm tm=2gamma5DW(MudaggerMu)-1gamma5,, where DWsubscriptрқҗ·рқ‘ҠDW is the massless Wilson-Dirac clover operator. Note that if the twisted mass parameter becomes very small (close to the physical point) this type of one-end trick is approaching the standard definition in Eq. . The helicity operator О“=Оі5вҖӢОі3О“superscriptрқӣҫ5superscriptрқӣҫ3Gamma=gamma5gamma3 and the unpolarized О“=Оі0О“superscriptрқӣҫ0Gamma=gamma0 belong to this category. One of the technical aspects of the calculation is the evaluation of the traces in Eqs. - . With small quark masses, the contribution to the loops coming from the low modes of the spectrum of the Dirac operator may be sizeable, and contributes significantly to the stochastic noisecitebib.bib74. Therefore, we compute the first NeвҖӢv=200subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘’рқ‘Ј200Nev=200 eigen-pairs О»j,|vjвҹ©subscriptрқңҶрқ‘—ketsubscriptрқ‘Јрқ‘—lambdaj,ketvj of the squared Dirac operator в„іuвҖӢв„іuвҖ subscriptв„ірқ‘ўsuperscriptsubscriptв„ірқ‘ўвҖ MuMudagger, that allow to reconstruct exactly the low-mode contribution to the disconnected quark loops. At this stage, stochastic techniques can be employed with the deflated operator to evaluate the high-modes contribution to the traces. To reduce the stochastic noise, we use the hierarchical probing algorithmcitebib.bib25, that allows to reduce the contamination to the trace coming from off-diagonal terms up to a distance 2ksuperscript2рқ‘ҳ2k. This improvement is achieved by partitioning the lattice with 2dвҖӢ(kвҲ’1)+1superscript2рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳ112d(k-1)+1 Hadamard vectors, where d=4рқ‘‘4d=4 is the number of dimensions of the lattice. Finally, to remove the contamination from off-diagonal terms in spin-color subspaces, we apply full dilutioncitebib.bib75. The algorithm employed in the present work has been successfully used in other studies involving the evaluation of disconnected contributionscitebib.bib18, bib.bib20, bib.bib19, bib.bib17. For each value of the proton boost P3=0.41,0.83,1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ30.410.831.24GeVP3=0.41,0.83,1.24,operatornamerm GeV, we evaluated the two-point functions contributing to the disconnected diagram of Eq. with NsвҖӢrвҖӢcвҖӢs=200subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ рқ‘ҹрқ‘җрқ‘ 200Nsrcs=200 source positions (see Sec. ). In addition, apart from averaging over all possible directions and orientations of the nucleon boost, we also average over forward and backward projections. In Table, we report the total statistics collected for the disconnected three point correlators. We note that, for the second largest value of the momentum used, namely P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,operatornamerm GeV we use вүҲ106absentsuperscript106approx 106 measurements. In addition, we also compute the matrix elements for P3=1.65вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.65GeVP3=1.65,operatornamerm GeV and all О“О“Gamma using approximately the same statistics as the previous smaller boost. While this number of statistics is not sufficient to obtain the same statistical accuracy as lower momenta, it allows us to check whether convergence with P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 is reached.
content_sections.III Lattice setup III.2 Two-point functi: The two-point functions enter the calculation through the ratio of Eq. , but also contribute to the evaluation of the disconnected diagram, as shown in Eq. . For this reason, to obtain a significant amount of measurements for the disconnected contributions we compute the two-point functions with a large number of source positions, Nsrc2вҖӢpвҖӢt=200superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ғsrc2pt200Nrm srcrm 2pt=200, and consider boosts of the nucleon along the different spatial directions and orientations. This procedure allowed us to considerably reduce the statistical error in the disconnected contributions at small computational cost because the same loops is combined with all 200 two-point functions on the same configurations. Given that the computational cost of the two-points function is considerably lower compared to the one required to evaluate the disconnected quark loops, using multiple source positions is highly beneficial. In Table we report the number of measurements of the two-point function performed at each nucleon boost P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3. The two-point function can be written as C2вҖӢpвҖӢtвҖӢ(PвҶ’;t,0)=вҲ‘n|вҹЁО©|NвҖӢ(0вҶ’,0)|nвҹ©|2вҖӢeвҲ’tвҖӢEnвҖӢ(P),subscriptрқҗ¶2рқ‘қрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘Ў0subscriptрқ‘ӣsuperscriptquantum-operator-productО©рқ‘ҒвҶ’00рқ‘ӣ2superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗёрқ‘ӣрқ‘ғC2pt(vecP;t,0)=sumlimitsn|braketOmegaN(vec0,0)n|2e-tEn(P),, with |nвҹ©ketрқ‘ӣketn being the nthsuperscriptрқ‘ӣthnrm th energy state of the interpolator NОұвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘Ғрқӣјрқ‘ҘNalpha(x) and EnвҖӢ(P)subscriptрқҗёрқ‘ӣрқ‘ғEn(P) its energy. We performed the analysis by keeping up to two terms in the expansion of Eq. . In particular, the two-state fit function of the two-point correlator consists of C2вҖӢpвҖӢtвҖӢ(PвҶ’;t,0)=c0вҖӢeвҲ’tвҖӢE0+c1вҖӢeвҲ’tвҖӢE1=c0вҖӢeвҲ’tвҖӢE0вҖӢ(1+c1c0вҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢt),subscriptрқҗ¶2рқ‘қрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘Ў0subscriptрқ‘җ0superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗё0subscriptрқ‘җ1superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗё1subscriptрқ‘җ0superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқҗё01subscriptрқ‘җ1subscriptрқ‘җ0superscriptрқ‘’О”рқҗёрқ‘ЎbeginsplitC2pt(vecP;t,0)=c0e-tE0+c1e-tE1 =c0e-tE0left(1+fracc1c0e-Delta Etright),,endsplit while the effective energy reads EEffвҖӢ(PвҶ’;t,0)вүЎlogвҒЎ(C2вҖӢpвҖӢtвҖӢ(PвҶ’;t,0)C2вҖӢpвҖӢtвҖӢ(PвҶ’;t+1,0))=E0+logвҒЎ(1+BвҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢt1+BвҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢ(t+1)),subscriptрқҗёEffвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘Ў0subscriptрқҗ¶2рқ‘қрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘Ў0subscriptрқҗ¶2рқ‘қрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘Ў10subscriptрқҗё01рқҗөsuperscriptрқ‘’О”рқҗёрқ‘Ў1рқҗөsuperscriptрқ‘’О”рқҗёрқ‘Ў1Erm Eff(vecP;t,0)equivlogleft(fracC2pt(vecP;t,0)C2pt(vecP;t+1,0)right)=E0+logleft(frac1+Be-Delta Et1+Be-Delta E(t+1)right),, with О”вҖӢE=(E1вҲ’E0)О”рқҗёsubscriptрқҗё1subscriptрқҗё0Delta E=(E1-E0) and B=c1/c0рқҗөsubscriptрқ‘җ1subscriptрқ‘җ0B=c1/c0. In Fig. , we show the results of the two-state fits of the correlator and the effective energy for the parameters E0,О”вҖӢE,c0subscriptрқҗё0О”рқҗёsubscriptрқ‘җ0E0,,Delta E,,c0 and c1/c0subscriptрқ‘җ1subscriptрқ‘җ0c1/c0, varying the low-end of the fit interval tminsubscriptрқ‘Ўmintrm min. The results show that the fits on the correlator or the effective energy lead to the same ground state energy. Furthermore, the plateau and two-state fits converge at ts/a=9subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ9ts/a=9 for momentum 1.24 GeV. In Table we report the parameters extracted using one- and two-state fits. The results for E0subscriptрқҗё0E0 are obtained with the plateau fit of the effective energy, and they are compatible with the values extracted using two-state fit results. In Fig. we show the effective energy for the second largest momentum P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,operatornamerm GeV, together with the plateau fit and two-state fit results. By iterating the fit procedure described above over the data for the different nucleon boosts, we reconstructed the dispersion relation a2вҖӢE2=a2вҖӢmN2вҖӢc4+a2вҖӢPвҶ’2вҖӢc2,superscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptрқҗё2superscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘җ4superscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptвҶ’рқ‘ғ2superscriptрқ‘җ2a2E2=a2mN2c4+a2vecP2c2, with mNsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘ҒmN being the nucleon mass. In Fig. we show the observed trend of the energy with the nucleon boost P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 together with a linear fit performed with the function of Eq. , giving a2вҖӢmN2вҖӢc4=0.2678вҖӢsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ғ2superscriptрқ‘җ40.26788a2mN2c4=0.2678 and c2=1.003вҖӢsuperscriptрқ‘җ21.0037c2=1.003. As can be seen, the lattice data are fully compatible with the dispersion relation for all values of P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3.
content_sections.IV Disconnected matrix elements: Obtaining the disconnected contributions to the up-, down- and strange-quark PDFs is the central goal, and most laborious aspect of this work. We use the techniques outlined in Section to extract the matrix elements and study systematic uncertainties, such as excited-states contamination.
content_sections.IV Disconnected matrix elements IV.1 Exc: To extract reliably the ground-state contribution to the matrix elements, we evaluate the ratio between the three- and two-point functions at seven source-sink separations, ranging from ts=0.563вҖӢfmsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 0.563fmts=0.563,rm fm to ts=1.126вҖӢfmsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 1.126fmts=1.126,rm fm in steps of a=0.0938вҖӢfmрқ‘Һ0.0938fma=0.0938,operatornamerm fm. For disconnected contributions, the evaluation of different source-sink separations does not require new inversions. This allowed us to study the excited-states contamination to the matrix elements using several tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts values and three analysis methods: plateau fit, two-state fit and summation method. We briefly summarize these methods. 1. Two-state fit. In Sec. , the two-point correlator is expanded up to the first excited state. Likewise, we can expand the three-point correlator keeping terms up to the first excited state. This gives four terms, that is C3вҖӢpвҖӢtвҖӢ(PвҶ’;ts,П„)=рқ’ң0,0вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢeвҲ’E0вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢts+рқ’ң0,1вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢeвҲ’E0вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢtsвҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢП„+рқ’ң1,0вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢeвҲ’E1вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢtsвҖӢeО”вҖӢEвҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢП„+рқ’ң1,1вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢeвҲ’E1вҖӢ(PвҶ’)вҖӢts. subscriptрқҗ¶3рқ‘қрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқңҸsubscriptрқ’ң00вҶ’рқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗё0вҶ’рқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ subscriptрқ’ң01вҶ’рқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗё0вҶ’рқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ superscriptрқ‘’О”рқҗёвҶ’рқ‘ғрқңҸsubscriptрқ’ң10вҶ’рқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗё1вҶ’рқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ superscriptрқ‘’О”рқҗёвҶ’рқ‘ғрқңҸsubscriptрқ’ң11вҶ’рқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗё1вҶ’рқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ beginsplitC3pt(vecP;ts,tau)=A0,0(vecP)e-E0(vecP)ts +A0,1(vecP)e-E0(vecP)tse-Delta E(vecP)tau +A1,0(vecP)e-E1(vecP)tseDelta E(vecP)tau +A1,1(vecP)e-E1(vecP)ts,. endsplit Being interested in the forward kinematic limit allows one to reduce the number of independent parameters, since рқ’ң0,1=рқ’ң1,0subscriptрқ’ң01subscriptрқ’ң10A0,1=A1,0. We performed a fit of the ratio of Eq. with the function вҹЁC3вҖӢpвҖӢt(PвҶ’;t;ts,0вҹ©вҹЁC2вҖӢpвҖӢtвҖӢ(PвҶ’;t,0)вҹ©=рқ’ң0,0c0вҖӢ[1+(рқ’ң0,1/рқ’ң0,0)вҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢП„+(рқ’ң0,1/рқ’ң0,0)вҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢ(tsвҲ’П„)+(рқ’ң1,1/рқ’ң0,0)вҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢts][1+c1c0вҖӢeвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢts],fracbraketC3pt(vecP;t;ts,0braketC2pt(vecP;t,0)=fracA0,0c0fracleft[1+left(A0,1/A0,0right)e-Delta Etau+left(A0,1/A0,0right)e-Delta E(ts-tau)+left(A1,1/A0,0right)e-Delta Etsright]left[1+fracc1c0e-Delta Etsright],, where the parameters c1/c0subscriptрқ‘җ1subscriptрқ‘җ0c1/c0 and О”вҖӢEО”рқҗёDelta E are determined through the effective energy fit and the results are reported in Table. Thus, the parameters determined by fitting the ratio of three- and two-point functions are рқ’ң0,0/c0subscriptрқ’ң00subscriptрқ‘җ0A0,0/c0, рқ’ң0,1/рқ’ң0,0subscriptрқ’ң01subscriptрқ’ң00A0,1/A0,0 and рқ’ң1,1/рқ’ң0,0subscriptрқ’ң11subscriptрқ’ң00A1,1/A0,0. рқ’ң0,0/c0subscriptрқ’ң00subscriptрқ‘җ0A0,0/c0 corresponds to the matrix element we are interested in. Such fits are weighted by the statistical errors, and therefore the fit is driven by the most accurate data. Since the statistics for the disconnected contributions are independent of th tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts value, we repeat the two-state fits modifying, each time, the starting value of tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts entering the fit (tslowsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ lowtsrm low). The results from the two-state fit method, allows us to verify ground-state dominance by comparing the matrix elements from the individual tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts values. 2. Plateau fit. For 0вүӘП„вүӘtsmuch-less-than0рқңҸmuch-less-thansubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 0lltaull ts and О”вҖӢEвҖӢtsвү«0much-greater-thanО”рқҗёsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 0Delta E,tsgg 0, the first term in the ratio of Eq. dominates. Thus, the matrix elements can be extracted by performing a constant fit on the ratio of Eq. in the region defined 0вүӘП„вүӘtsmuch-less-than0рқңҸmuch-less-thansubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 0lltaull ts, with large enough source-sink separation. We exclude from the fit range three points from left and right, i. e. we evaluate the weighted average in the interval П„вҲҲ[3,tsвҲ’3]рқңҸ3subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 3tauin[3,ts-3]. While the excited-states contamination decreases with tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts, at the same time the statistical uncertainty exponentially increases. For this reason, the determination of the ground state of the matrix elements with the plateau fit method is a challenging task, and the results need to be compared with other analysis techniques. 3. Summation method. Summing over the insertion time П„рқңҸtau of the ratio of the three- and two-point functions we findcitebib.bib76, bib.bib77 SвҖӢ(ts)=вҲ‘П„=2П„=tsвҲ’2вҹЁC3вҖӢpвҖӢt(PвҶ’;t;ts,0вҹ©вҹЁC2вҖӢpвҖӢtвҖӢ(PвҶ’;t,0)вҹ©=tsвҖӢA0,0c0+c+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(eвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢts). S(ts)=sumlimitstau=2tau=ts-2fracbraketC3pt(vecP;t;ts,0braketC2pt(vecP;t,0)=tsfracA0,0c0+c+Oleft(e-Delta Etsright). Thus, the matrix elements corresponds with the slope of the straight line SвҖӢ(ts)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ S(ts), and can be measured by performing a linear regression. Using the three aforementioned approaches we analyze the excited-states effects on the matrix elements for the unpolarized, helicity and transversity PDFs. In the next three subsections, we present the analysis of the real and imaginary parts of the matrix elements at P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,operatornamerm GeV, as a representative example.We start by discussing the analysis of the unpolarized isoscalar u+dрқ‘ўрқ‘‘u+d disconnected matrix elements. In Fig. we show the ratio of three- and two-point functions at P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,operatornamerm GeV for the unpolarized operator for z/a=3рқ‘§рқ‘Һ3z/a=3 and we compare the results obtained with the three analysis methods reported in Sec. . The real part of the matrix elements shows no substantial dependence on the source-sink separation, and the plateau fit results obtained at different tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts give all compatible results. The dependence of the two-state fit on the lowest source-sink separation tslowsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ lowtsrm low, included in the fit shows a constant trend, which is also compatible with the results obtained with the summation method. The reduced chi-square ПҮ2/d. o. f=0.96formulae-sequencesuperscriptрқң’2dof0.96chi2/rm d. o. f=0.96 suggests that the function of Eq. provides a good description of the data. To extract the matrix elements, we compute the constant correlated fit of the plateau fit results starting from tslow/a=9superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ lowрқ‘Һ9tsrm low/a=9. In contrast to the real part, the imaginary part shows a large effect due to the excited-states contamination. However, the two-state fit is compatible with the plateau value using ts/a=11subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ11ts/a=11. Also, the results obtained at different tslowsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ lowtsrm low using the summation method are compatible with the other methods within uncertainties. As final results for the unpolarized matrix element, we report the ones from the plateau fit for ts/a=11subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ11ts/a=11, which is compatible with the results obtained with the two-state fit and summation method.The disconnected contributions to the helicity isoscalar matrix elements is purely real and exhibits a non-negligible dependence on the source-sink separation. We observe a decreasing behavior as tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts increases, which is a behavior also observed in the axial chargecitebib.bib18. In addition, the plateau fit for ts/a>10subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ10ts/a>10 are compatible with the two-state fit. Therefore, we use the plateau fit for ts/a=10subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ10ts/a=10 as our final results, so that statistical uncertainties are controlled.The ratio of the three- and two-point functions for the disconnected contributions to the isoscalar transversity matrix elements does not shows dependence on the source-sink separation for both the real and imaginary parts (see Fig. ). Thus, the matrix elements are computed from the plateau fit for ts/a=9subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ9ts/a=9, both for the real and the imaginary parts. Using the criterion adopted for selecting the final results for each PDF case we compared the extracted matrix elements in Fig. for all values of zрқ‘§z. In summary, the plateau fits are evaluated at ts/a=9, 10, 9subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ9109ts/a=9,,10,,9 (ts/a=11, 10, 9subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ11109ts/a=11,,10,,9) for the real (imaginary) part of the unpolarized, helicity and transversity, respectively. For the summation method, we employ all tssubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ ts values available, except for the imaginary part of the unpolarized and the real part of the helicity matrix elements, where ts/a=6subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ6ts/a=6 is excluded as explained above. The two-state fit is performed in the range ts/aвҲҲ[6,12]subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ612ts/ain[6,12] in all cases, except for the imaginary part of the unpolarized and transversity matrix elements, where the ts/a=6subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ6ts/a=6 value, as explained, is not included in the regression. Our conclusions for the isoscalar disconnected matrix elements apply also to the strange matrix elements, with the excited-states contamination showing similar effects.
content_sections.IV Disconnected matrix elements IV.2 Mom: As explained in Sec. , the matrix elements and the quasi-PDFs have a dependence on the nucleon boost P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3, which also enters the matching formula leading to the PDFs. An important aspect of the study is the investigation of the momentum dependence of the matrix elements, which affects the convergence to the light-cone PDFs. In Fig. , we present the results for the renormalized strange and isoscalar disconnected matrix elements as a function of the momentum boost. For the unpolarized case, the real part decreases in magnitude as the P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 increases, and becomes compatible with zero. In contrast, its imaginary part is non-zero and shows convergence for the two largest values of P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3. We find that the isoscalar disconnected matrix elements share the same qualitative behavior as the strange-quark ones. First results on the helicity distribution appeared in Ref. citebib.bib26. Here we show results with increased statistics, and with the addition of P3=1.65вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.65GeVP3=1.65,operatornamerm GeV. The matrix elements show a mild residual dependence on the momentum. The imaginary part of the renormalized matrix elements arises entirely from the complex multiplication with the renormalization function and the bare matrix elements (see Eq. ). Indeed, as mentioned already in Sec. , the disconnected contribution to the bare matrix element for the helicity distribution is purely real. The real part of the matrix elements for the transversity distribution exhibit a strong dependence on the nucleon boost, changing dramatically as we increase P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 from 0.83вҖӢGeV0.83GeV0.83,operatornamerm GeV to 1.24вҖӢGeV1.24GeV1.24,operatornamerm GeV. However, results obtained for P3=1.65вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.65GeVP3=1.65,operatornamerm GeV show agreement with those for P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,operatornamerm GeV albeit the large uncertainty. From the current results it is still unclear if convergence is reached. However, in order to fully check this would require a larger momentum and much more measurements to reach the required accuracy. This is beyond the current study and will be tested in a followup work. We thus, construct the PDFs using the results for P3=1.24subscriptрқ‘ғ31.24P3=1.24GeV. In Sec. , we comment on the region of xрқ‘Ҙx affected by the gap observed in the real part of the matrix elements as momentum increases. In contrast, the imaginary part is fully compatible with zero for the two lowest momenta, while it is slightly non-zero at large zрқ‘§z at the highest momentum.
content_sections.V Connected matrix elements: The evaluation of the connected matrix elements contributing to the three types of PDFs has been studied in our previous works. In particular, we refer the reader to the study of Ref. citebib.bib41, where several sources of systematic uncertainties were discussed in great detail. For completeness, we briefly discuss here the connected contributions which are needed for the flavor decomposition. In Fig. , we show the momentum dependence of the bare connected contributions to the isoscalar and isovector matrix elements for the three types of PDFs. In all cases, as the nucleon boost increases, the real part of the matrix elements decay to zero faster, and the magnitude for the imaginary part increases in the region z/aвүІ9less-than-or-similar-toрқ‘§рқ‘Һ9z/alesssim 9. The unpolarized matrix elements show convergence with P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 while the imaginary parts of the helicity and transversity distributions increase in magnitude. We note that in order to compute the connected contributions for a fourth larger boost would require new inversions and large number of measurements to reduce the errors sufficiently enough to check convergence. Thus for the current work we opt to use the results for P3=1.24subscriptрқ‘ғ31.24P3=1.24GeV for the connected parts since the focus of this work is the evaluation of the disconnected contributions. The uncertainty on the unpolarized distribution is smaller as compared to the other two distributions. This behavior is due to the fact that both the three- and two-point functions share the same projector, (1+Оі0)/21superscriptрқӣҫ02(1+gamma0)/2, which increases the correlation between the two quantities and, as a result, drastically decreases the noise-to-signal ratio. We note also that the transversity distribution reported here is the average over the two insertions Пғ3вҖӢjsubscriptрқңҺ3рқ‘—sigma3j with j=1,2рқ‘—12j=1,2.
content_sections.VI Nucleon charges: The nucleon charges are usually extracted from the nucleon matrix elements of local operators. This limit is obtained from the matrix elements of non-local operators at z=0рқ‘§0z=0. Since the charges are frame independent, any value of P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 may be used. Indeed, in Sec. we demonstrate that the z=0рқ‘§0z=0 have little dependence on P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 For the disconnected contributions, we have the matrix elements at P3=0subscriptрқ‘ғ30P3=0 (rest frame). For the connected contributions to the charges, we use the lowest momentum, so we control statistical uncertainties. In what follows, we will show the results obtained for the isovector uвҲ’dрқ‘ўрқ‘‘u-d, isoscalar u+dрқ‘ўрқ‘‘u+d and strange-quark vector, axial and tensor charges, gVsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘үgV, gAsubscriptрқ‘”рқҗҙgA and gTsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘ҮgT. First, we describe our results for the disconnected contributions, whose total number of measurements in the rest frame is Nmeas=66вӢ…103subscriptрқ‘ҒmeasвӢ…66superscript103Nrm meas=66cdot 103. In Fig. we show our results for the renormalized disconnected vector, axial and tensor charges. The integral over the volume (i. e. Fourier transform at zero momentum transfer) of the trace of the vector current ПҲВҜвҖӢ(x)вҖӢОі0вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(x)ВҜрқң“рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқӣҫ0рқң“рқ‘Ҙbarpsi(x)gamma0psi(x) is zero because quark and antiquark loops contributions cancel each other. Thus, the disconnected contribution to the unpolarized isoscalar and strange matrix elements in the absence of the Wilson line and at P3=0subscriptрқ‘ғ30P3=0 expected to be zero is verified and this constitutes a consistency check of our computations. Due to excited-states contamination, the disconnected isoscalar and strange vector charges gVu+dвҖӢ(disc)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘үuddiscgVrm u+d,(disc) and gVssuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘үрқ‘ gVs obtained with the two-state and plateau fits are not compatible with zero at small source-sink separation. In particular, the two-state fit results become compatible with zero when tlowвүҘ10subscriptрқ‘Ўlow10trm lowgeq 10. The results obtained with the summation method have the largest uncertainties and are compatible with zero. The axial charge shows the larger contamination from excited states. In particular, the plateau fit results show a decreasing trend with the tlowsubscriptрқ‘Ўlowtrm low, converging to a constant value for tlow/aвүҘ11subscriptрқ‘Ўlowрқ‘Һ11trm low/ageq 11 for the isoscalar and tlow/aвүҘ8subscriptрқ‘Ўlowрқ‘Һ8trm low/ageq 8 for the strange charges, which are selected as our final values. In contrast, both the results obtained with two-state fit and summation are constant and compatible with the selected plateau fit results. We find gAu+dвҖӢ(disc)=вҲ’0.104вҖӢ,gAs=вҲ’0.0320вҖӢ. formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқҗҙuddisc0.10410superscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқҗҙрқ‘ 0.032028gArm u+d,(disc)=-0.104,quad gAs=-0.0320,. The results on the disconnected contributions of the tensor charge show very mild excited states effects. We use the plateau value extracted by fitting the ratio to a constant for ts/aвүҘ9subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ9ts/ageq 9 for the isoscalar connected tensor charge gTu+dвҖӢ(disc)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘ҮuddiscgTrm u+d(disc) and for ts/aвүҘ8subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ8ts/ageq 8 for gTssuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Үрқ‘ gTs. Our final results for these two quantities are gTu+dвҖӢ(disc)=вҲ’0.00818вҖӢ,gTs=вҲ’0.00265вҖӢ. formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Үuddisc0.0081891superscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Үрқ‘ 0.0026560gTrm u+d,(disc)=-0.00818,quad gTs=-0.00265,. We stress that despite the agreement of the strange tensor charge with the value extracted using local operatorscitebib.bib78, bib.bib12, a direct comparison is not meaningful since we are using gauge ensembles simulated with heavier than physical pion mass. It thus comes with no surprise that the disconnected isoscalar tensor charge differs from the value obtained at the physical pion mass. The connected contributions to the nuclear charges are computed for the smallest momentum P3=0.41вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ30.41GeVP3=0.41,operatornamerm GeV. Using these results we can extract the values for each quark flavor for the vector, axial an tensor charges. The details on the computation of the connected isoscalar and isovector contributions are given in Sec. . The connected contributions used to extract the charges are obtained from plateau fits with ts/a=12subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ рқ‘Һ12ts/a=12. The nucleon axial and tensor charges are given in Table. We note that for the vector charge gVsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘үgV we find results that are consistent with charge conservation.
content_sections.VII Parton distribution functions VII.1: In Fig. we show the momentum dependence of the total renormalized isoscalar and isovector matrix elements, including disconnected contributions. The renormalized matrix elements are reported as a function of zвҖӢP3рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғ3zP3. We renormalize and apply the matching procedure independently for the isoscalar and isovector distributions, allowing us to obtain the individual up and down quark PDFs. The source-sink separation used is ts=0.94subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 0.94ts=0.94fm for the lowest momentum and ts=1.13вҖӢfmsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 1.13fmts=1.13,operatornamerm fm for P3=0.83subscriptрқ‘ғ30.83P3=0.83 and 1.24вҖӢGeV1.24GeV1.24,operatornamerm GeV. From previous studies of the isovector distributions (see, e. g. Ref. citebib.bib41) and nucleon chargescitebib.bib18, we expect that excited-states contamination is more significant for the nucleon three-point correlators of the axial and tensor currents as compared to the vector. However, the statistical uncertainty is larger for these quantities and within our current errors the source-sink separation employed at the highest momentum is sufficient to suppress excited states to this level of accuracycitebib.bib41. A summary of the results for the connected matrix elements in the absence of the Wilson line are reported in Table. The momentum dependence of all the matrix elements analyzed is negligible for z=0рқ‘§0z=0 as expected For example, the isoscalar connected matrix elements for the unpolarized distribution, hu+dвҖӢ(z=0)superscriptв„Һudрқ‘§0hrm u+d(z=0), for the largest momentum differs from the others by less then 1%percent11%. The isovector unpolarized matrix elements at z=0рқ‘§0z=0 is independent of the momentum boost, and equal to 1, as expected from charge conservation. Regarding the isoscalar helicity case, we still find agreement for different P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 within uncertainties, but with larger fluctuations of the mean values, as the disconnected contribution is about вҲј17%similar-toabsentpercent17sim 17% of the connected part. We note that the О”вҖӢhuвҲ’dвҖӢ(z=0)О”superscriptв„Һрқ‘ўрқ‘‘рқ‘§0Delta hu-d(z=0) is compatible with the experimental value gAuвҲ’d=1.27641вҖӢsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘”рқҗҙud1.2764156gArm u-d=1.27641citebib.bib79. Insensitivity to the momentum boost is also observed in the transversity case.
content_sections.VII Parton distribution functions VII.2: In order to construct the x-dependence of PDFs we need to take the Fourier transform. Since the matrix elements are determined for discrete finite number of zрқ‘§z values, we study the dependence on the cutoff zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max to understand systematic effects related to the reconstruction. In particular, for all types of distributions we verify that a zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max exists such that, addition of information for z>zmaxрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘§maxz>zrm max in the Fourier transform leaves the PDF unchanged within statistical uncertainties. This value of zрқ‘§z is selected as the maximum value zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max included in the Fourier transform and, typically, the matrix elements at this value has a vanishing real part. Note that the latter is just a qualitative criterion and in practice we always check by increasing zрқ‘§z for convergence. In Fig. we show the dependence on the cutoff zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max for the isoscalar and isovector distributions at P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,rm GeV. We compare the results obtained with the discrete Fourier transform of Eq. with the results from the Bayes-Gauss-Fourier transform (BGFT)citebib.bib80. The latter is an advanced reconstruction technique based on Gaussian process regression, which allows to obtain an improved estimate of quasi-PDF for continuous values of xрқ‘Ҙx, starting from a discrete set of data obtained with lattice QCD computations. The chosen values of zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max for each quark flavor and each operator are given in Table. . From Fig. it is clear that increase of the cutoff beyond the reported values of Table, does not affect the results for the PDFs. We also find compatible of the discreet Fourier transform with the results using BGFT.
content_sections.VII Parton distribution functions VII.3: The isoscalar and isovector PDFs are extracted from the corresponding renormalized matrix elements shown in Fig. . For the isoscalar combination, we add both the connected and disconnected contributions. We plot the matrix elements against zвҖӢP3рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғ3zP3, which is the argument of the exponential in the Fourier transform. In all cases, we find that the matrix elements for the lowest momentum P3=0.41subscriptрқ‘ғ30.41P3=0.41 GeV do not decayed to zero for large zрқ‘§z, demonstrating, as expected, that the momentum is not large enough. By increasing the momentum to P3=1.24subscriptрқ‘ғ31.24P3=1.24GeV, the matrix elements become consistent with zero within their uncertainties. While the imaginary parts show a residual momentum dependence, the convergence must be checked at the level of the reconstructed PDF. This is due to the fact that P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 enters the matching kernel and affects the convergence. Therefore, to address the momentum convergence as we increase P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3, we show in Fig. the momentum dependence of the isoscalar and isovector PDFs. We use the standard Fourier transform, with the values of zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max given in Table, as discussed in Sec. . As can be seen in Fig. , the overall dependence on the two largest values of the momentum is relatively small. Dependence on P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 is observed in the unpolarized isoscalar PDF. In general, the PDFs for the smallest momentum, do not show convergence, and exhibit non-physical oscillations due to the presence of systematic effects in the reconstruction of the xрқ‘Ҙx-dependence. However, such oscillations are suppressed for the higher values of P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3. The isoscalar and isovector helicity distributions have a similar magnitude and exhibit milder dependence on the boost as compared to the unpolarized. In particular, both isoscalar and isovector helicity distributions are consistent for P3=0.83вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ30.83GeVP3=0.83,operatornamerm GeV and P3=1.24вҖӢGeVsubscriptрқ‘ғ31.24GeVP3=1.24,operatornamerm GeV. Finally, the isoscalar and isovector transversity distributions also show nice convergence with P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3 for the two largest values. These distributions will be used for the flavor decomposition presented in Sec. together with comparison of our data with phenomenology.
content_sections.VII Parton distribution functions VII.4: Our results on the isoscalar and isovector distributions presented in Sec. allow us to extract the up and down quark contributions for the unpolarized, helicity and transversity distributions. The disconnected contributions are taken into account in all cases. We stress that the comparison with phenomenology can only be qualitative for a number of reasons: i) We use an ensemble with larger than physical pion mass. We know from previous studies that there is a non-negligible pion mass dependence on the PDFs; ii) lattice systematics, such as cut-off effects, are not taken into account; iii) the renormalization ignores mixing present in the case of the unpolarized and helicity singlet PDFs; and iv) errors are still sizable and may hide systematics, such as convergence with the boost. However, it is still interesting to compare with phenomenology keeping these caveats in mind. The results for the unpolarized PDF at the largest momentum are compared with data by NNPDF3вҖӢ.1NNPDF3.1rm NNPDF3.1citebib.bib81, while the helicity distribution is compared with JAM17citebib.bib82 and NNPDFPOL1вҖӢ.1subscriptNNPDFPOL1.1rm NNPDFrm POL1.1citebib.bib2. Finally, the quark transversity distribution obtained in this study is compared against the SIDIS datacitebib.bib83 and SIDIS data constrained by the value of tensor charge gTsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘ҮgT computed in lattice QCDcitebib.bib83. For the anti-quark region for the NNPDF3вҖӢ.1NNPDF3.1rm NNPDF3.1 data, we include the crossing relations of Eq. , such that we show the antiquark distributions in the negative-xрқ‘Ҙx region. The light-quark contributions to the unpolarized PDF show good agreement with phenomenology in the region xвүі0.2greater-than-or-equivalent-toрқ‘Ҙ0.2xgtrsim 0.2. Also, the region xвүІвҲ’0.2less-than-or-similar-toрқ‘Ҙ0.2xlesssim-0.2 both estimates are compatible with zero. Note that lattice results for the small-xрқ‘Ҙx region (|x|вүІ0.15less-than-or-similar-toрқ‘Ҙ0.15|x|lesssim 0.15) suffer from uncontrolled uncertainties due to the reconstruction of the PDFs and the values of the lattice spacing used. The case of the helicity distributions is very interesting, as it has non-negligible contribution from the disconnected diagram. Our results for the up quark helicity show similar features as the NNPDF data, but are have higher values. The down quark distribution gives compatible results both with NNPDFPOL1вҖӢ.1subscriptNNPDFPOL1.1rm NNPDFrm POL1.1 and JAM17 data for all xрқ‘Ҙx in the physical region [вҲ’1,1]11[-1,1]. The transversity distribution is the least known collinear PDF and it is not well-constrained by SIDIS data. As a result, global fits for the light quark ОҙвҖӢqвҖӢ(x)рқӣҝрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙdelta q(x) carry large relative error of вүҲ50вҲ’100%absent50percent100approx 50-100% citebib.bib83. A more precise phenomenological estimate of the transversity PDFs can be obtained by constraining the distributions with the value of the tensor charge gTsubscriptрқ‘”рқ‘ҮgT computed within lattice QCDcitebib.bib83. A comparison with the latter, reveals a similar agreement as for the helicity PDFs. We would like to stress that the overall qualitative agreement is very promising, as this computation is done using simulations with heavier than physical pions.The strange distributions presented here are computed using the renormalized matrix elements shown in Fig. . The values of zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max employed in the Fourier transform defining the quasi-PDF are reported in Table. The criterion adopted to select zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max is to analyze the dependence of the PDF as zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max is increased, as discussed in the previous section. In Fig. we show the unpolarized, helicity and transversity PDFs. The antiquark distribution reported here takes into account the crossing relations in Eq. , showing the anti-quark distributions in the negative xрқ‘Ҙx region. Although the unpolarized PDFs extracted from the matrix element using the two largest momenta tend towards the phenomenological result, there is still some residual dependence, which points to the need to increase the momentum boost to check the independence on P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3. Due to the simultaneous suppression of the real part of the matrix elements and the enhancement of the imaginary part, sВҜвҖӢ(x)ВҜрқ‘ рқ‘Ҙbars(x) becomes symmetrical with respect to x=0рқ‘Ҙ0x=0 as the momentum boost increases. This symmetry feature is exploited in the global fits. The results for the helicity distribution are approximately symmetric in the quark and antiquark regions, and are compatible with the results from the NNPDFPOL1вҖӢ.1subscriptNNPDFPOL1.1rm NNPDFrm POL1.1citebib.bib2 and with JAM17 global fits analysis both of which have larger uncertainties. Our results, thus, provide valuable input for phenomenological studies. In fact, this is more evident for the strange transversity distribution where experimental results are lacking. We obtained results on the transversity PDF with small uncertainties that show no residual momentum dependence for the two largest momentum values. Besides the individual sвҖӢ(x)рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙs(x) and sВҜвҖӢ(x)ВҜрқ‘ рқ‘Ҙbars(x) distributions, there is also an interest on the strange-quark asymmetry. This is partly due to the fact that there is no symmetry to suggest that the two distributions have to be the same. The strange and anti-strange asymmetry has been discussed within chiral effective theorycitebib.bib85, bib.bib86, perturbative evolution of QCDcitebib.bib87, and a physical model for parton momentacitebib.bib88. Here, we study the asymmetry using our data for P3=0.41, 0.83, 1.24subscriptрқ‘ғ30.410.831.24P3=0.41,,0.83,,1.24 GeV, and the results are shown in Fig. . In contrast to the individual sвҖӢ(x)рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙs(x) and sВҜвҖӢ(x)ВҜрқ‘ рқ‘Ҙbars(x) distributions, here we find that there is no momentum dependence in the strange-quark asymmetry. We also note that the difference between sвҖӢ(x)рқ‘ рқ‘Ҙs(x) and sВҜвҖӢ(x)ВҜрқ‘ рқ‘Ҙbars(x) is a non-singlet combination and, thus, does not mix with the gluon PDFs. Focusing on the most accurate results at P3subscriptрқ‘ғ3P3, we find that the asymmetry vanishes at xвүі0.2greater-than-or-equivalent-toрқ‘Ҙ0.2xgtrsim 0.2 and is small but non negligible in the small-xрқ‘Ҙx region. This conclusion is, at present stage, qualitative, and an investigation of systematic effects is needed before drawing quantitative conclusions.
content_sections.VII Parton distribution functions VII.5: In this section, we calculate the moments вҹЁxnвҹ©expectationsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣbraketxn of the three PDFs considering n=0,вҖҰ,3рқ‘ӣ0вҖҰ3n=0,. . . ,3. The nвҲ’limit-fromрқ‘ӣn-th moment of the unpolarized, helicity and transversity distributions are defined as вҹЁxnвҹ©q=вҲ«01xnвҖӢ[qвҖӢ(x)+(вҲ’1)n+1вҖӢqВҜвҖӢ(x)]вҖӢрқ‘‘x=вҲ«вҲ’11xnвҖӢqвҖӢ(x)вҖӢрқ‘‘x,вҹЁxnвҹ©О”вҖӢq=вҲ«01xnвҖӢ[О”вҖӢqвҖӢ(x)+(вҲ’1)nвҖӢО”вҖӢqВҜвҖӢ(x)]вҖӢрқ‘‘x=вҲ«вҲ’11xnвҖӢО”вҖӢqвҖӢ(x)вҖӢрқ‘‘x,вҹЁxnвҹ©ОҙвҖӢq=вҲ«01xnвҖӢ[ОҙвҖӢqвҖӢ(x)+(вҲ’1)n+1вҖӢОҙвҖӢqВҜвҖӢ(x)]вҖӢрқ‘‘x=вҲ«вҲ’11xnвҖӢОҙвҖӢqвҖӢ(x)вҖӢрқ‘‘x,formulae-sequencesubscriptexpectationsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘һsuperscriptsubscript01superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣdelimited-рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙsuperscript1рқ‘ӣ1ВҜрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙdifferential-dрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscript11superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙdifferential-dрқ‘Ҙsubscriptexpectationsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣО”рқ‘һsuperscriptsubscript01superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣdelimited-О”рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙsuperscript1рқ‘ӣО”ВҜрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙdifferential-dрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscript11superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣО”рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙdifferential-dрқ‘Ҙsubscriptexpectationsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқӣҝрқ‘һsuperscriptsubscript01superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣdelimited-рқӣҝрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙsuperscript1рқ‘ӣ1рқӣҝВҜрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙdifferential-dрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscript11superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣрқӣҝрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙdifferential-dрқ‘Ҙbeginsplitbraketxnq=int01xn,left[q(x)+(-1)n+1barq(x)right],dx=int-11xn,q(x),dx, braketxnDelta q=int01xn,left[Delta q(x)+(-1)nDeltabarq(x)right],dx=int-11xn,Delta q(x),dx, braketxndelta q=int01xn,left[delta q(x)+(-1)n+1deltabarq(x)right],dx=int-11xn,delta q(x),dx,endsplit where we employed the crossing relations of Eq. , to write the moments as a function of the quark distributions only. In Table we report the results for the isovector, isoscalar and flavor diagonal moments. The zero-th moments are compatible with the nucleon charges reported in Tab. This is a non-trivial check, as the calculation of the charges follows a totally different procedure and undergoes a Fourier transform and matching.
content_sections.VIII Conclusions: In this work we present a study of the xрқ‘Ҙx-dependence of proton collinear quark PDFs from lattice QCD considering both connected and disconnected diagrams. These contributions are necessary to determine the individual-flavor contributions to PDFs. We present results for the up, down and strange quark for the unpolarized, helicity and transversity PDFs. This work extends our first calculation for the flavor decomposition of the helicity PDFscitebib.bib26; here we increase the statistics by about a factor of two, and include results on the unpolarized and transversity PDFs. The main goal of this work is to explore the feasibility of the calculation of disconnected quark loops with non-local operators. To this end, we provide the necessary details on technical and theoretical aspects, as well as the examination of some sources of systematic uncertainties. The calculation is carried out using one ensemble of Nf=2+1+1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘“211Nf=2+1+1 twisted mass fermions simulated with quark mass value that produces a pion mass of 260 MeV. Using a single ensemble, we can address excited-states contamination, reconstruction of the xрқ‘Ҙx dependence, and the convergence with increasing the momentum boost in the final PDFs. The matrix elements contain non-local operators with the length of the Wilson line extending up to half the spatial extend of the lattice. The proton states are boosted with momentum using three values, namely P3=0.41, 0.83, 1.24subscriptрқ‘ғ30.410.831.24P3=0.41,,0.83,,1.24 GeV. Several values of the source-sink time separation are considered. As we increase the boost we also increase the source-sink time separation in order to investigate the effect of excited states; we employ up to ts=1.13subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘ 1.13ts=1.13 fm for the highest momentum (see Figs. - ). Both the isovector and isoscalar flavor combinations are calculated, with the latter receiving contributions from the connected and disconnected diagrams. All matrix elements are renormalized multiplicatively using the RI scheme and evolved to the modified-MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextMS scheme at a scale of 2 GeV (unpolarized and helicity) or 22sqrt2 GeV (transversity). The renormalization is followed by the transform to the momentum space, xрқ‘Ҙx, which produces the quasi-PDFs. We apply the standard Fourier transform that is confirmed using the Bayes-Gauss-Fourier transform, finding compatible results (see Fig. ). The quasi-PDFs are matched to the light-cone PDFs using one-loop perturbation theory. The matching kernel contains information on the renormalization scheme and scale for the quasi-PDFs (modified-MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextMS scheme at 2 or 22sqrt2 GeV), which are then matched to the light-cone PDFs in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextMS scheme at the same scale. In this proof-of-principle study we neglect the mixing with the gluon PDFs for the unpolarized and helicity case. The extraction of the latter has its own challenges and will be included in our future studies. To test the influence of systematic uncertainties, we calculate the charges by integrating the PDFs (Table), and compare with the values obtained directly from the matrix elements (Table). We find good consistence among the results. We find that the light-quark disconnected contributions have the most impact for the helicity PDF, while the transversity disconnected contribution is very small. Regardless, a clear non-zero signal is found in all cases. The strange-quark PDFs are nonzero up to xвҲј0.5similar-toрқ‘Ҙ0.5xsim 0.5, with the unpolarized and helicity having a similar magnitude, and the transversity being an order of magnitude smaller, as can be seen in Fig. . These distributions are very challenging to extract from experimental data due to the lack of sensitivity to the strange-quark. In a qualitative comparison of our results with phenomelogically extracted PDFs we find: i) our results on the unpolarized have a statistical precision which is similar to the NNPDF data; ii) the helicity strange-quark PDF is significantly more accurate than the JAM and NNPDF results and iii) our results for the strange-quark transversity PDF serve as a prediction. There are a number of improvements that one can do to quantify and eliminate systematic uncertainties. These include, but not limited to, pion mass dependence, mixing under matching with the gluon PDFs for the unpolarized and helicity case, and address the inverse problem using, e. g. , Bayesian reconstruction methods. One can also address finite-volume and discretization effects, which require extracting the PDFs using multiple ensembles. However, this work clearly demonstrates the great potential in the extraction of the x-dependence of individual quark PDFs from lattice QCD. Acknowledgements. We would like to thank all members of ETMC for their constant and pleasant collaboration. We also thank Fernanda Steffens and Jeremy Green for useful discussions. M. C. acknowledge financial support by the U. S. Department of Energy Early Career Award under Grant No. DE-SC0020405. K. H. is supported financially by the Cyprus Research and Innovation Foundation under contract number POST-DOC/0718/0100. This project has received funding from the Marie SkЕӮodowska-Curie European Joint Doctorate program STIMULATE of the European Commission under grant agreement No 765048; F. M. is funded under this program. This research includes calculations carried out on HPC resources of the Cyprus Institute (the Cyclone and Cyclamen machines) and of Temple University, supported in part by the National Science Foundation through major research instrumentation grant number 1625061 and by the US Army Research Laboratory under contract number W911NF-16-2-0189. Computations for this work were carried out in part on facilities of the USQCD Collaboration, which are funded by the Office of Science of the U. S. Department of Energy. This research used resources of the Oak Ridge Leadership Computing Facility, which is a DOE Office of Science User Facility supported under Contract DE-AC05-00OR22725. The gauge configurations have been generated by the Extended Twisted Mass Collaboration on the KNL (A2) Partition of Marconi at CINECA, through the Prace project Pra133304 вҖқSIMPHYSвҖқ.

sorted_papers.title:
Parton Distribution Functions in the Schwinger Model with Tensor
  Networks
sorted_papers.abstract:
Parton distribution functions (PDFs) describe universal properties of bound
states and allow us to calculate scattering amplitudes in processes with large
momentum transfer. Calculating PDFs involves the evaluation of matrix elements
with a Wilson line in a light-cone direction. In contrast to Monte Carlo
methods in Euclidean spacetime, these matrix elements can be directly
calculated in Minkowski-space using the Hamiltonian formalism. The necessary
spatial- and time-evolution can be efficiently applied using established tensor
network methods. We present PDFs in the Schwinger model calculated with matrix
product states.

qualitative: Parton distribution functions (PDFs) are defined in terms of light-front matrix elements involving a Wilson line, which cannot be directly computed in Euclidean lattice QCD and typically require indirect methods such as quasi- or pseudo-distributions.

The Schwinger model in 1+1 dimensions serves as a tractable analog to QCD, exhibiting confinement and asymptotic freedom, with the vector meson acting as a hadron-like bound state of fermions and antifermions that can be described by PDFs.

Tensor network methods, particularly matrix product states (MPS), enable direct computation of Minkowski-space matrix elements via real-time evolution in the Hamiltonian formalism, allowing for a more direct access to PDFs compared to traditional Euclidean approaches.
The introduction of static charges qn at lattice sites enables the inclusion of a Wilson line along the light-cone direction, which is essential for computing PDFs in the Hamiltonian framework.

The Gauss law constraint links electric and fermionic degrees of freedom, allowing elimination of the electric field from the Hamiltonian and leading to an effective spin model with non-local interactions involving the cumulative charge and electric flux.
The ground and first excited states of the Schwinger model are obtained via variational optimization of MPS, with charge conservation enforced through penalty terms in the variational procedure.
quantitative: The Schwinger model Lagrangian: в„’ = ПҲМ„(iвҲӮМё вҲ’ gAМё вҲ’ m)ПҲ вҲ’ 1/4 FОјОҪFОјОҪ
The electromagnetic tensor: FОјОҪ = вҲӮОјAОҪ вҲ’ вҲӮОҪAОј

The spin Hamiltonian after Jordan-Wigner transformation: H_spin = (1/(2a)) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-2} (Пғ_n^+ Пғ_{n+1}^- + Пғ_{n+1}^- Пғ_n^+) + (m_lat/2) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-1} [1 + (вҲ’1)^n Пғ_n^z] + (a gВІ/2) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-1} L_nВІ
Additively renormalized lattice mass: m_lat = m вҲ’ (a gВІ)/8
Gauss law constraint: L_n вҲ’ L_{nвҲ’1} = (1/2)[(вҲ’1)^n + Пғ_n^z] + q_n

Effective Hamiltonian after eliminating electric field (rescaled by 2a gВІ): H = x ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-2} (Пғ_n^+ Пғ_{n+1}^- + Пғ_n^- Пғ_{n+1}^+) + (Ој/2) ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-1} [1 + (вҲ’1)^n Пғ_n^z] + ОЈ_{n=0}^{N-2} [в„“ + (1/2) ОЈ_{k=0}^n ((вҲ’1)^k + Пғ_k^z + 2 q_k)]ВІ
Definitions: x = 1/(aВІ gВІ), Ој = 2 m_lat / (a gВІ)
Electric field at left boundary: в„“ = 0
The PDF is obtained via a Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x, where the momentum fraction x is related to the light-cone momentum of partons in the hadron.

authors:
Mari Carmen Ba\~nuls, Krzysztof Cichy, C.-J. David Lin, Manuel
  Schneider

content_sections.1 Introduction: One of the central aims of particle physics is to achieve detailed understanding of the inner structure of hadrons, such as the nucleon or the pion. This structure is governed by the strong interaction and described by quantum chromodynamics (QCD). Due to the non-Abelian nature of QCD and the running of the strong coupling, the structure of hadrons is very intricate and can be quantified in terms of different kinds of partonic functions. The simplest of these are parton distribution functions (PDFs), which represent the fraction of momentum carried by the hadronвҖҷs constituents, quarks and gluonscitebib.bib1. PDFs are given in terms of light-front matrix elements and thus, they cannot be directly accessed in Euclidean lattice QCD. Instead, they are being computed indirectly, using factorization-based approaches. The most widely used of these are quasi-distributions citebib.bib2, bib.bib3 and pseudo-distributions citebib.bib4. However, other approaches also exist, such as the heavy-quark operator product expansion citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, current-current correlators citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, the hadronic tensor citebib.bib9 and the Compton amplitude citebib.bib10. For a review about the recent progress, see Refs. citebib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16. However, it would be desirable to extract partonic functions more directly from Minkowski-space matrix elements. This can in principle be achieved using tensor network state (TNS) techniques in the Hamiltonian framework. TNSs are efficient ansatzes for the wave function, which can be subjected to real-time evolution, thus giving access to light-front matrix elements. TNS methods are particularly successful in low dimensions, where the approach of matrix product states (MPS) citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19 is applicable and their suitability for equilibrium states of lattice gauge theories (LGTs) has been systematically established (see e. g. citebib.bib20 and references therein). Here we present an exploratory study of this possibility, focused on the Schwinger model citebib.bib21, i. e. quantum electrodynamics (QED) in 1+1 dimensions. The model shares several non-trivial properties with QCD, such as confinement and asymptotic freedom. Originally, it was introduced as a model of non-perturbative generation of a mass gap. The lightest composite state of this model, the vector meson, can be thought of as an analogue of a hadron in QCD. The vector meson is a bound state of fermions and antifermions, which can be considered as partons that are described by PDFscitebib.bib22. Whereas earlier calculations of Schwinger model PDFs in the Hamiltonian formalism exist, they were limited to exact diagonalization in a small momentum latticecitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25 or numerical integrations in the approximation of small fermion numberscitebib.bib26. In contrast, the TNS approach, which we apply to PDFs for the first time, allows treating very large systems, and a systematic improvement for convergence.
content_sections.2 The Schwinger Model: The massive Schwinger model is described by the Lagrangian в„’=ПҲВҜвҖӢ(iвҖӢвҲӮМёвҲ’gвҖӢAМёвҲ’m)вҖӢПҲвҲ’14вҖӢFОјвҖӢОҪвҖӢFОјвҖӢОҪ,в„’ВҜрқң“рқ‘–not-partial-differentialрқ‘”italic-AМёрқ‘ҡрқң“14subscriptрқҗ№рқңҮрқңҲsuperscriptрқҗ№рқңҮрқңҲcalL=overlinepsi(inotpartial-gnotA-m)psi-frac14FmunuFmunu, where ПҲрқң“psi are Dirac fermions coupled to the gauge field AрқҗҙA with a coupling constant gрқ‘”g, and mрқ‘ҡm is the fermion mass. The electromagnetic tensor is defined as FОјвҖӢОҪ=вҲӮОјAОҪвҲ’вҲӮОҪAОјsubscriptрқҗ№рқңҮрқңҲsubscriptрқңҮsubscriptрқҗҙрқңҲsubscriptрқңҲsubscriptрқҗҙрқңҮFmunu=partialmuAnu-partialnuAmu. The Hamiltonian can be obtained by a Legendre transformationcitebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30. We work in the temporal gauge, A0=0subscriptрқҗҙ00A0=0. The model is evaluated on a lattice with lattice spacing aрқ‘Һa and with open boundary conditions. We use Kogut-Susskind staggered fermions, where the upper (lower) components of the Dirac fermions are placed on even (odd) sites of the latticecitebib.bib31. A Jordan-Wigner transformation maps the fermionic degrees of freedom to spin variables. The Hamiltonian for an Nрқ‘ҒN-site system becomes Hspin=12вҖӢaвҖӢвҲ‘n=0NвҲ’2(Пғn+вҖӢПғn+1вҲ’+Пғn+1вҲ’вҖӢПғn+)+mlat2вҖӢвҲ‘n=0NвҲ’1[1+(вҲ’1)nвҖӢПғnz]+aвҖӢg22вҖӢвҲ‘n=0NвҲ’1Ln2. subscriptрқҗ»spin12рқ‘Һsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣ0рқ‘Ғ2subscriptsuperscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣsubscriptsuperscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣ1subscriptsuperscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣ1subscriptsuperscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘ҡlat2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣ0рқ‘Ғ1delimited-1superscript1рқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘”22superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣ0рқ‘Ғ1superscriptsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘ӣ2Htextspin=frac12asumn=0N-2left(sigma+nsigma-n+1+sigma-n+1sigma+nright)+fracmtextlat2sumn=0N-1left[1+(-1)nsigmanzright]+fracag22sumn=0N-1Ln2. The electric field is described in a ladder space and the electric field operator Lnsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘ӣLn counts the electric flux on a link between sites nрқ‘ӣn and n+1рқ‘ӣ1n+1citebib.bib27. The additively-renormalized lattice mass mlat=mвҲ’aвҖӢg28subscriptрқ‘ҡlatрқ‘ҡрқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘”28mtextlat=m-fracag28 ensures fast convergence to the continuum limitcitebib.bib32, bib.bib33. Physical states in the Hamiltonian approach need to fulfill the Gauss law, which in spin language reads LnвҲ’LnвҲ’1=12вҖӢ[(вҲ’1)n+Пғnz]+qn. subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘ӣsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘ӣ112delimited-superscript1рқ‘ӣsubscriptsuperscriptрқңҺрқ‘§рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘ӣLn-Ln-1=frac12left[(-1)n+sigmaznright]+qn. Notice that we introduced here a static charge qnsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘ӣqn at site nрқ‘ӣn, which was not part of the original model. This will help us in to calculate matrix elements with a Wilson line along the light-cone. The static charge does not have a kinetic term and enters the model only by changing the charge locally. The electric and fermionic fields are not independent degrees of freedom because of the Gauss law. In 1+1 dimensions and with open boundary conditions, this can be used to eliminate the electric field from the Hamiltonian, which (after rescaling by a factor 2aвҖӢg22рқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘”2frac2ag2) becomes H=xвҖӢвҲ‘n=0NвҲ’2[Пғn+вҖӢПғn+1вҲ’+ПғnвҲ’вҖӢПғn+1+]+Ој2вҖӢвҲ‘n=0NвҲ’1[1+(вҲ’1)nвҖӢПғnz]+вҲ‘n=0NвҲ’2[в„“+12вҖӢвҲ‘k=0n((вҲ’1)k+Пғkz+2вҖӢqk)]2. рқҗ»рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣ0рқ‘Ғ2delimited-superscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣ1superscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣ1рқңҮ2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣ0рқ‘Ғ1delimited-1superscript1рқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣрқ‘§superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣ0рқ‘Ғ2superscriptdelimited-в„“12superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҳ0рқ‘ӣsuperscript1рқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ҳрқ‘§2subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘ҳ2H=xsumn=0N-2left[sigman+sigman+1-+sigman-sigman+1+right]+fracmu2sumn=0N-1left[1+(-1)nsigmanzright]+sumn=0N-2left[ell+frac12sumk=0nleft((-1)k+sigmakz+2qkright)right]2. We defined x=1a2вҖӢg2рқ‘Ҙ1superscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptрқ‘”2x=frac1a2g2 and Ој=2вҖӢmlataвҖӢg2рқңҮ2subscriptрқ‘ҡlatрқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘”2mu=frac2mtextlatag2. The electric field at the left boundary lрқ‘ҷl is set to zero in all our calculations. We furthermore restrict ourselves to states with zero total charge. The ground state of the Schwinger model is calculated using a variational optimization of the MPScitebib.bib19, bib.bib29. Subsequently, the ground state is projected out by a penalty term, and a second penalty term ensures that the next state belongs to the zero charge sector. In this way, we can find the first excited state as well by a variational minimization of the energycitebib.bib29.

Even though momentum is not well-defined for open boundary conditions, the first excited state becomes the vector boson at rest in the thermodynamic and continuum limits. We can, thus, calculate PDFs from lattices with open boundary conditions for large enough volumes and fine enough lattices. The subscript cрқ‘җc on the expectation value in indicates that vacuum expectation values should be subtracted from matrix elementscitebib.bib1. The remaining two matrix elements connect one even and one odd site. The upper and lower parts of the fermionic operator ПҲрқң“psi each have the form вҲҸk<n(iвҖӢПғkz)вҖӢПғnвҲ’subscriptproductрқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptsubscriptрқңҺрқ‘ӣprodk<nleft(isigmakzright)sigman- in the spin language, a Jordan-Wigner string and an annihilation operator. The Wilson line is path-dependentcitebib.bib34 and needs to be evolved along a light-cone direction. This is not directly possible with a spatial lattice. We therefore replace the Wilson line by a step-wise evolution as sketched in for the в„і(o,o)subscriptв„і(o,o)Mtext(o,o) matrix element. After applying the initial operators at the initial site, the state is evolved for a time that corresponds to two lattice spacings ОҙвҖӢt=2вҖӢaрқӣҝрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Һdelta t=2a (with the speed of light c=1рқ‘җ1c=1). Next, we evolve the electric field by two lattice sites in the spatial direction. This is followed by a time evolution again and repeated recursively until we reach the final site. The parallel transporter in time-direction becomes a time evolution of the state with the correspondingly evolved electric fieldcitebib.bib35. For the calculation of matrix elements in the negative direction, d<0рқ‘‘0d<0, we start with a spatial evolution in the negative direction and then evolve by a negative time ОҙвҖӢt=вҲ’2вҖӢaрқӣҝрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Һdelta t=-2a (see ). Our procedure approximates the light-cone in the continuum limit. We are working in a formulation with spin-degrees of freedom only, the gauge fields are integrated out. Therefore, we cannot evolve the electric field directly by applying operators to the state. Instead, we introduce static charges that change Gauss law locally, or, equivalently, contribute to the electric field by their charge. Whenever we apply a Пғ+superscriptрқңҺsigma+ or ПғвҲ’superscriptрқңҺsigma- operator, we insert a static charge at the same position with the corresponding charge, such that it cancels the effect of the ПғрқңҺsigma operator on the electric fields. The fermion number can, thus, be changed without modifying the electric flux. Then, we move the location of the static charge, which has the same effect as the application of a parallel transporter in spatial direction: changing the electric flux between two sites by one unit. Note that the static charges only enter the matrix elements by modifying the electric part of the Hamiltonian during the time evolution. Our approach shares similarities with the ancillary fermion-antifermion pairs incitebib.bib35. The initial state |hвҹ©ketв„ҺKeth is an eigenstate of the Hamiltonian with eigenvalue Ehsubscriptрқҗёв„ҺEh, such that вҹЁh|вҖӢeiвҖӢHвҖӢtdbraв„Һsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқҗ»subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘‘BraheiHtd = вҹЁh|вҖӢeiвҖӢtdвҖӢEhbraв„Һsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘‘subscriptрқҗёв„ҺBraheitdEh with td=d2вӢ…ОҙвҖӢtsubscriptрқ‘Ўрқ‘‘вӢ…рқ‘‘2рқӣҝрқ‘Ўtd=fracd2cdotdelta t. Matrix elements in negative light-cone direction can be evaluated in an analogous way. Using a second order Suzuki-Trotter decompositioncitebib.bib36, bib.bib37 of the time evolution operator, we find a time-dependent MPS (tMPS) approximation to the evolved state. In this work, the bond dimension of the MPS is fixed to be D=80рқҗ·80D=80 both for the initial state and after the truncation in each time-evolving block-decimation (TEBD)citebib.bib38 step. We follow the approach incitebib.bib39 with a Trotter time step ОҙвҖӢП„=ОҙвҖӢt100рқӣҝрқңҸрқӣҝрқ‘Ў100deltatau=fracdelta t100 and truncate the electric fields to |Ln|вүӨ10subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘ӣ10left|Lnright|leq 10. Finally, fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright) can be computed by a discrete Fourier transform of the matrix elements.
content_sections.4 Numerical Results: We calculate the matrix elements from the four contributions in for different distances to the origin. An example is given in for the first excited state (vector meson at rest) in the zero charge sector. The real part of the matrix element is found to be symmetric with respect to the axis d=0рқ‘‘0d=0, while the imaginary part is antisymmetric. These symmetries make the PDF a real function as required. The PDF is related to the Fourier transform of the matrix elements, which is shown in for the ground state (vacuum) and the first excited state (vector meson). The vacuum matrix element is a background that needs to be subtracted to obtain physical PDFscitebib.bib1. We find that the excited state coincides with the vacuum contribution for a large range of Fourier modes kрқ‘ҳk, but a region with positive kрқ‘ҳk shows some excess, while a region for negative kрқ‘ҳk shows a lower value than the vacuum. In , we plot the matrix element of the first excited state with the vacuum contribution subtracted for each point. Different lattice spacings and physical volumes are considered. The real part in vanishes and the imaginary part follows a damped oscillation. The periodicity and form of the matrix element do not change significantly once the lattice spacing is chosen small enough and the physical volume is large enough. This corresponds to sufficiently large xрқ‘Ҙx and Lxрқҗҝрқ‘ҘfracLx. Only the amplitude of the lattice matrix elements, as defined in , and the resolution vary. Finally, we obtain the PDF from the subtracted matrix elements by rescaling from kрқ‘ҳk to Оҫрқңүxi by a factor of xmрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҡfracxm, where m=EhвҲ’E0рқ‘ҡsubscriptрқҗёв„Һsubscriptрқҗё0m=Eh-E0 is the energy difference between the excited state and the ground state. The function fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright) obtained from the matrix elements in is shown in . A peak around ОҫвүҲ0.5рқңү0.5xiapprox 0.5 is present and is approximately symmetric with respect to the central value. The different lattice spacings and physical volumes lead to very similar results, which indicates that we are close enough to the continuum and thermodynamic limit. The systematic errors will be studied in more details in a future publication. While the PDF is only defined for ОҫвҲҲ[0,1]рқңү01xiin[0,1], we can calculate fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright) outside this support as well. We find that fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright) vanishes for |Оҫ|>1рқңү1|xi|>1 as expected. Furthermore, fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright) is antisymmetric with respect to the axis Оҫ=0рқңү0xi=0, which is a consequence of the vanishing real part of the subtracted matrix elements (see ). This leads to a negative peak at around Оҫ=вҲ’0.5рқңү0.5xi=-0.5. Since fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright) for negative Оҫрқңүxi is related to the PDF of the antifermion by the relation fПҲвҖӢ(вҲ’Оҫ)=вҲ’fПҲВҜвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүsubscriptрқ‘“ВҜрқң“рқңүfpsi(-xi)=-foverlinepsileft(xiright)citebib.bib1, the anti-symmetry means that fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)вүЎfПҲВҜвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүsubscriptрқ‘“ВҜрқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright)equiv foverlinepsileft(xiright), i. e. the PDFs for the fermion and anti-fermion are identical. These numerical results confirm the physical picture of the vector boson to be a meson, with symmetric roles of the fermion and the anti-fermion, i. e. both of these particles carry one half of the total meson momentum on averagecitebib.bib22. We note that the distributions are rather narrow in Оҫрқңүxi-space, because of the large fermion mass. We further calculate fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңүfpsileft(xiright) for different fermion masses. Examples are shown in for half and double the mass presented in . We observe that the overall picture remains the same, but the peak widens (narrows) for lower (higher) mass. This is in good agreement withcitebib.bib24, bib.bib25 and is expected as the distribution function becomes wider with decreasing mass. The limit of vanishing mass is known to be fПҲвҖӢ(Оҫ)вүЎ1subscriptрқ‘“рқң“рқңү1fpsileft(xiright)equiv 1 for ОҫвҲҲ[0,1]рқңү01xiin[0,1], so the peak is widened maximallycitebib.bib22. In the opposite limit, mвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ҡmrightarrowinfty, the PDF should become a delta-function at Оҫ=0.5рқңү0.5xi=0.5, so maximally narrow.
content_sections.5 Conclusion and Outlook: We demonstrated that it is possible to calculate PDFs directly from light-cone correlators with tensor networks. On the lattice, the light-cone direction can be replaced by a zigzag-like evolution of the Wilson line in space and time. The time evolution is possible with a Suzuki-Trotter decomposition and standard MPS-techniques like TEBD. For the spatial evolution, we introduced static charges that can be moved in order to evolve the electric flux step-wise. The PDF can be obtained by a Fourier transform after subtracting the ground state matrix element. While a detailed study of systematic errors will be published elsewhere, the results show good convergence with the lattice spacing and physical volume, indicating the feasibility of a continuum limit extrapolation. Even though TNS methods can be applied in higher dimensions as well, the numerical costs increase. Moreover, the applicability of TNS methods to dynamical problems is limited by the entanglement growth. But the same strategy used for TNS calculations in the Hamiltonian formalism could in principle be implemented in quantum computers or simulators, which could potentially overcome these limitations. Indeed, first attempts have already been made to calculate Wilson lines in space and time in the context of quantum computing and with exact diagonalization for small systemscitebib.bib40, bib.bib35, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43. In this work, we presented, for the first time, a full calculation of PDFs in the Schwinger model from light-cone correlators. We observed the correct symmetry properties and conclude that the fermion- and antifermion-PDF are identical for the vector boson, as expected for a meson. We further calculated the PDF for different fermion masses and found the expected broadening of the PDF with decreasing mass. These results demonstrate the applicability of TNS methods for more general LGT problems, beyond their previously established success for equilibrium states.

sorted_papers.title:
Gluon GTMDs at nonzero skewness and impact parameter dependent parton
  distributions
sorted_papers.abstract:
We investigate the leading twist generalized transverse momentum dependent
parton distributions (GTMDs) of the unpolarized and longitudinally polarized
gluons in the nucleon. We adopt a light-front gluon-triquark model for the
nucleon motivated by soft-wall AdS/QCD. The gluon GTMDs are defined through the
off-forward gluon-gluon generalized correlator and are expressed as the overlap
of light-cone wave functions. The GTMDs can be employed to provide the
generalized parton distributions (GPDs) by integrating out the transverse
momentum. The Fourier transform of the GPDs encodes the parton distributions in
the transverse position space, namely, the impact parameter dependent parton
distributions (IPDs). We also calculate the three gluon IPDs corresponding to
the GPDs $H^g$, $E^g$ and $\widetilde{H}^g$, and present their dependence on
$x$ and $b_\perp$, respectively.
