content_sections.V Transverse-Momentum Dependent PDFs V.2: Before LaMET, there had been efforts to access TMD physics from lattice QCD by calculating the ratios of the xрқ‘Ҙx-moments of TMDPDFscitebib.bib222, bib.bib375, bib.bib374, bib.bib191, bib.bib449, which are free from complications associated with the soft function and can be compared to certain experimental observables. In LaMET, we are more interested in obtaining the full xрқ‘Ҙx and kвҶ’вҹӮsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toveckperp dependence of the TMDPDFscitebib.bib279, bib.bib271, bib.bib176, bib.bib177, bib.bib273, bib.bib272. Therefore, a proper treatment of the soft function subtraction and matching is essential. The earliest suggestion of a bent soft function incitebib.bib279 and the follow-up workcitebib.bib177 has the correct IR logarithms at one-loop order, but this is expected to break down at higher-loop orderscitebib.bib273, thus not allowing for a perturbative matching. Another suggestion which uses a naive rectangle-shaped Wilson loopcitebib.bib271, bib.bib177 does not possess the correct IR physics, either. Nevertheless, incitebib.bib176 important progress was made for calculating the nonperturbative Collins-Soper kernel KвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)рқҗҫsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮK(bperp,mu) from the ratio of quasi-TMDPDFs at two different large momenta. Recently, some of the authors showedcitebib.bib273, bib.bib272 that the quasi-TMDPDF combined with a reduced soft function capture the correct IR physics to all-orders and thus allow for a perturbative matching to the physical TMDPDF. To construct such quasi-TMDPDFs, the collinear part can be treated in a way similar to the collinear PDFs, while the soft piece is more challenging. Our starting point is that the physical fTMDsuperscriptрқ‘“TMDfrm TMD is independent of the rapidity regulator, so one can use a scheme in which the gauge-links in both fрқ‘“f and Sрқ‘ҶS are off the light-cone, such as that used incitebib.bib132. In this case, one can use Lorentz symmetry to boost the staple-shaped gauge-link рқ’Іnsubscriptрқ’Ірқ‘ӣcal Wn in fрқ‘“f to a purely space-like staple with no time dependence. However, one can only use this trick for one of the staples in Sрқ‘ҶS, say рқ’Іnsubscriptрқ’Ірқ‘ӣcal Wn, whereas the other one рқ’Іpsubscriptрқ’Ірқ‘қcal Wp is still time-dependent. In other words, there is no way to get rid of the time dependence in Sрқ‘ҶS entirely with Lorentz boost alone. This is natural because Sрқ‘ҶS in fact represents the square of an Sрқ‘ҶS-matrix, which appears to be intrinsically Minkowskian. However, using the LaMET principle that time dependence of an operator can be simulated through external physical states at large momentum, we find that Sрқ‘ҶS can indeed be calculated on the lattice in the off-light-cone scheme as a form factor. A detailed discussion will be given in the next subsection. Here we assume that this is true, and discuss the matching between quasi- and physical TMDPDFs. First, we define the quasi-TMDPDF with a stapleвҖ“shaped gauge-link along the zрқ‘§z directioncitebib.bib279, bib.bib271, bib.bib177, bib.bib272 as fвҖӢ(О»,bвҹӮ,Ој,О¶z)рқ‘“рқңҶsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮsubscriptрқңҒрқ‘§displaystyletildef(lambda,bperp,mu,zetaz) =limLвҶ’вҲһвҹЁP|ПҲВҜвҖӢ(О»вҖӢnz2+bвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢОіzвҖӢрқ’ІzвҖӢ(О»вҖӢnz2+bвҶ’вҹӮ;L)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(вҲ’О»вҖӢnz2)|Pвҹ©ZEвҖӢ(2вҖӢL,bвҹӮ,Ој),absentsubscriptвҶ’рқҗҝquantum-operator-productрқ‘ғВҜрқң“рқңҶsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2subscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘§subscriptрқ’Ірқ‘§рқңҶsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2subscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқҗҝрқң“рқңҶsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§2рқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘Қрқҗё2рқҗҝsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮdisplaystyle=!limLrightarrowinftyfrac P|barpsibig(fraclambda nz2!+!vecbperpbig)gammazcal Wz(fraclambda nz2!+!vecbperp;L)psibig(!-!fraclambda nz2big)|PsqrtZE(2L,bperp,mu) , where the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS renormalization is implied, and рқ’ІzвҖӢ(Оҫ;L)=WzвҖ вҖӢ(Оҫ;L)вҖӢWвҹӮвҖӢWzвҖӢ(вҲ’ОҫzвҖӢnz;L),subscriptрқ’Ірқ‘§рқңүрқҗҝsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҠвҖ рқ‘§рқңүрқҗҝsubscriptрқ‘Ҡperpendicular-tosubscriptрқ‘Ҡрқ‘§superscriptрқңүрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқҗҝdisplaystylecal Wz(xi;L)=Wdaggerz(xi;L)WperpWz(-xiznz;L) , WzвҖӢ(Оҫ;L)=рқ’«вҖӢexpвҖӢ[вҲ’iвҖӢgвҖӢвҲ«ОҫzLрқ‘‘О»вҖӢnzвӢ…AвҖӢ(ОҫвҶ’вҹӮ+nzвҖӢО»)]. subscriptрқ‘Ҡрқ‘§рқңүрқҗҝрқ’«expdelimited-рқ‘–рқ‘”superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptрқңүрқ‘§рқҗҝвӢ…differential-dрқңҶsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқҗҙsubscriptвҶ’рқңүperpendicular-tosubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқңҶdisplaystyle Wz(xi;L)=cal Prm expBig[-igintxizLdlambda nzcdot A(vecxiperp!+!nzlambda)Big] . Again, Оіzsuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘§gammaz can be replaced by Оіtsuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўgammat as in the collinear quasi-PDF. For a depiction of fрқ‘“tildef and ZEsubscriptрқ‘ҚрқҗёZE see Fig. . The purpose of the factor ZEsubscriptрқ‘ҚрқҗёZE is as follows. At large LрқҗҝL, the naive quasi-TMD correlator in the numerator of Eq. contains divergences that go as eвҲ’LвҖӢEвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)superscriptрқ‘’рқҗҝрқҗёsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮe-LE(bperp,mu) where EвҖӢ(bвҹӮ)рқҗёsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toE(bperp) is the ground state energy of a pair of static heavy-quarks. EвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)=2вҖӢОҙвҖӢm+VвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)рқҗёsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮ2рқӣҝрқ‘ҡрқ‘үsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮE(bperp,mu)=2delta m+V(bperp,mu) contains both the linear divergent mass corrections 2вҖӢОҙвҖӢm2рқӣҝрқ‘ҡ2delta m and the heavy-quark potential VвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)рқ‘үsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮV(bperp,mu) due to mutual interactions. The introduction of ZEsubscriptрқ‘ҚрқҗёsqrtZE also removes additional contributions from the transverse gauge link. An alternative approach to avoid the pinch-pole singularity was proposed incitebib.bib321. We should mention that although the ZEsubscriptрқ‘ҚрқҗёsqrtZE subtraction removes all the linear divergences, the logarithmic UV divergences are still present. Note that the Sivers function f1вҖӢTвҹӮsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘“perpendicular-to1рқ‘Үfperp1Tcitebib.bib415 and the Boer-Mulders function h1вҹӮsubscriptsuperscriptв„Һperpendicular-to1hperp1citebib.bib73 are Tрқ‘ҮT-odd. The orientation of the gauge-link have important effects on these two functionscitebib.bib137, bib.bib132, such that they change sign between the DY and SIDIS processes. In the light-cone gauge, these contributions arise from the transversal gauge-link at infinitiescitebib.bib53. They are related to the phenomenologically interesting single transverse-spin asymmetrycitebib.bib73, bib.bib185, bib.bib130, bib.bib129.
content_sections.V Transverse-Momentum Dependent PDFs V.3: In previous subsections, the soft function has been introduced to define rapidity-scheme-independent TMDPDFs. The major motivation of introducing the soft function is to capture nonperturbative effects due to soft-gluon radiations from fast moving color-charges. For many inclusive processes the soft radiations cancel in the total cross section, but for certain processes where a small transverse momentum is measured, such cancellation can be incomplete and result in measurable consequences. In such cases, the TMD soft function is introduced to account for the soft-gluon effects and appears in factorization theorems for the Drell-Yan (DY) processcitebib.bib146, bib.bib147 and semi-inclusive DIS (SIDIS)citebib.bib264, bib.bib263. To calculate the TMD physics nonperturbatively, formulating a Euclidean version of the soft function is critical. Since the soft function in fact is a cross section and hence real and positive, it satisfies the necessary condition for a Monte Carlo simulation. In this subsection, we present an approach to calculate it in heavy-quark effective theory (HQET)citebib.bib273. There is also another method proposed to extract the reduced soft function Srsubscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҹSr from a light-meson form factorcitebib.bib273, where many subtleties of HQET can be avoided. The first lattice calculation of the reduced soft function based on the light-meson formalism has been performed in the recent workcitebib.bib455. Due to the different space-time pictures of the DY and SIDIS processes, the soft functions for the two processes also differ from each other as shown in Fig. . To define the soft function, one also needs to specify a time-ordering prescription. Since it is a cross section, it involves a time order and an anti-time order (or cut diagrams). However, in the light-cone limit, the time order does not matter. What really matters is the rapidity regularization scheme. It has been proven for the Оҙрқӣҝdelta regulator in citebib.bib429 that the time ordering is not quite relevant up to overall phase factors, and the soft functions for the two processes are equal. The method therein can be modified to apply to the off-light-cone scheme too. Therefore, our first step is to convert the cut-diagrams into Feynman diagrams by imposing just the single time order. In this way, the soft function can be viewed as a scattering amplitude. In the off-light-cone scheme, there are further complications caused by the space-like or time-like choices for off-light-cone vectors. Fortunately, one can show that in the light-cone limit, the space-like and time-like choices are equivalent up to overall phase factorscitebib.bib274. Thus we will use the notation SвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІS(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) to denote a generic off-light-cone soft function that satisfies our demands. With these in mind, we show that the off-light-cone soft function SвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІS(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) is equivalent to an equal-time form factor of fast-moving color sources and can be formulated on the Euclidean lattice. From the matching formula Eq. in the last subsection, once the off-light-cone soft function is known, we can combine it with the lattice calculated quasi-TMDPDF to obtain the physical TMDPDF. Therefore, the cross section of DY processes in the low transverse-momentum regioncitebib.bib146 becomes predictable from first principlescitebib.bib273. To begin with, we define the scattering amplitude of a Wilson loop as shown in Fig. : WвҖӢ(t,tвҖІ,bвҹӮ,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІdisplaystyle W(t,tprime,bperp,Y,Yprime) =1NcвҖӢTrвҖӢвҹЁ0|рқ’ҜвҖӢ[рқ’ІvвҖІвҖ вҖӢ(bвҶ’вҹӮ,tвҖІ)вҖӢрқ’ІvвҖӢ(bвҶ’вҹӮ,t)]|0вҹ©absent1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җTrquantum-operator-product0рқ’Ҝdelimited-subscriptsuperscriptрқ’ІвҖ superscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ’Ірқ‘ЈsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘Ў0displaystyle=frac1Ncrm Tr, 0|cal Tleft[cal Wdaggervprime(vecbperp,tprime)cal Wv(vecbperp,t)right]|0 where |0вҹ©ket0|0 is the QCD vacuum state and Ncsubscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җNc is number of colors and TrTrrm Tr is the color-trace. Timelike four-vectors vОј=ОівҖӢ(1,ОІ,0вҶ’вҹӮ)superscriptрқ‘ЈрқңҮрқӣҫ1рқӣҪsubscriptвҶ’0perpendicular-tovmu=gamma(1,beta,vec0perp) and vвҖІвҒЈОј=ОівҖІвҖӢ(1,вҲ’ОІвҖІ,0вҶ’вҹӮ)superscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІрқңҮsuperscriptрқӣҫвҖІ1superscriptрқӣҪвҖІsubscriptвҶ’0perpendicular-tovprimemu=gammaprime(1,-betaprime,vec0perp) approach the lightcone as ОІрқӣҪbeta and ОІвҖІвҶ’1вҶ’superscriptрқӣҪвҖІ1betaprimeto 1. The rapidity Yрқ‘ҢY and the speed ОІрқӣҪbeta are related through ОІ=tanhвҖӢYрқӣҪtanhрқ‘Ңbeta=rm tanhY, in terms of the light-cone vectors pрқ‘қp and nрқ‘ӣn, the velocities read v=eY2вҖӢ(pp++eвҲ’2вҖӢYвҖӢp+вҖӢn)рқ‘Јsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘Ң2рқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘’2рқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣv=fraceYsqrt2left(fracpp++e-2Yp+nright) and vвҖІ=eY2вҖӢ(eвҲ’2вҖӢYвҖӢpp++p+вҖӢn)superscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘Ң2superscriptрқ‘’2рқ‘Ңрқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ӣvprime=fraceYsqrt2left(e-2Yfracpp++p+nright). The рқ’ІvвҖӢ(bвҶ’вҹӮ,t)subscriptрқ’Ірқ‘ЈsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘Ўcal Wv(vecbperp,t) is a staple-shaped gauge-link along vрқ‘Јv direction similar to those defined in Eqs. and . tрқ‘Ўt and tвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІtprime are the lengths of the tрқ‘Ўt-components of the staples. The single time-order prescription for Sрқ‘ҶS allows physical interpretation as a chronological process. Similar to the quasi-TMDPDF, the Wilson-loop in Eq. contains pinch-pole singularities associated to time evolution of initial and final states at large tрқ‘Ўt and tвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІtprime. Therefore, we need to subtract them out in Eq. with rectangular Wilson-loopscitebib.bib131, bib.bib271. This leads to an off-light-cone realization of the soft function: SвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІdisplaystyle S(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) =limtвҶ’вҲһtвҖІвҶ’вҲһWвҖӢ(t,tвҖІ,bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)ZвҖӢ(2вҖӢt,bвҹӮ,Ој,Y)вҖӢZвҖӢ(2вҖӢtвҖІ,bвҹӮ,Ој,YвҖІ),absentsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ЎвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІрқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІрқ‘Қ2рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңрқ‘Қ2superscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІdisplaystyle=limbeginsubarraycttoinfty tprimetoinftyendsubarrayfracW(t,tprime,bperp,mu,Y,Yprime)sqrtZ(2t,bperp,mu,Y)Z(2tprime,bperp,mu,Yprime) , where ZвҖӢ(2вҖӢt,bвҹӮ,Y)рқ‘Қ2рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘ҢZ(2t,bperp,Y) is the vacuum expectation value of rectangular Wilson loop which is similar to Wрқ‘ҠW by setting vвҖІ=vsuperscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІрқ‘Јvprime=v and tвҖІ=tsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІрқ‘Ўtprime=t, i. e. ZвҖӢ(2вҖӢt,bвҹӮ,Y)=WвҖӢ(t,t,bвҹӮ,Y,вҲ’Y)рқ‘Қ2рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘Ңрқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘Ңрқ‘ҢZ(2t,bperp,Y)=W(t,t,bperp,Y,-Y). The factor Zрқ‘ҚZ has a clear physical interpretation: It can be viewed as the wave function renormalization for incoming or outgoing color sources. After the subtraction through Zрқ‘ҚZ, the only remaining UV divergences for SвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІS(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) are the cusp divergences with hyperbolic angle Y+YвҖІрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІY+Yprime. We should mention that a more common definition of the soft function SDYвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)subscriptрқ‘ҶDYsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІSrm DY(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) for the DY process was proposed incitebib.bib133, bib.bib132. The space-like vectors uОј=ОівҖӢ(ОІ,1,0,0)superscriptрқ‘ўрқңҮрқӣҫрқӣҪ100umu=gamma(beta,1,0,0) and uвҖІвҒЈОј=ОівҖІвҖӢ(вҲ’ОІвҖІ,1,0,0)superscriptрқ‘ўвҖІрқңҮsuperscriptрқӣҫвҖІsuperscriptрқӣҪвҖІ100uprimemu=gammaprime(-betaprime,1,0,0) were chosen instead of time-like vрқ‘Јv and vвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІvprime to define the soft function for the DY process. This soft function has already been defined in the last subsection in Eq. . uрқ‘ўu and uвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ўвҖІuprime are equal to pYsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘ҢpY, nYвҖІsubscriptрқ‘ӣsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІnYprime up to overall normalization factors. While Sрқ‘ҶS and SDYsubscriptрқ‘ҶDYSrm DY are defined differently, we can show that SвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)=SDYвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІsubscriptрқ‘ҶDYsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІdisplaystyle S(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime)=Srm DY(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) using analyticity propertycitebib.bib274. Here we focus on Sрқ‘ҶS in Eq. , which has a simple Euclidean realization. After defining the soft function Sрқ‘ҶS, we now show that it is equal to a form factor. In HQET, the propagator of a color source is equivalent to a gauge-link along its moving direction. Thus WвҖӢ(t,tвҖІ,bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІW(t,tprime,bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) can be expressed by fields in HQET with the Lagrangian в„’HQET=QvвҖ вҖӢ(x)вҖӢ(iвҖӢvвӢ…D)вҖӢQvвҖӢ(x)+QВҜvвҖ вҖӢ(x)вҖӢ(iвҖӢvвӢ…D)вҖӢQВҜvвҖӢ(x),subscriptв„’HQETsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘„рқ‘ЈвҖ рқ‘ҘвӢ…рқ‘–рқ‘Јрқҗ·subscriptрқ‘„рқ‘Јрқ‘ҘsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқ‘„рқ‘ЈвҖ рқ‘ҘвӢ…рқ‘–рқ‘Јрқҗ·subscriptВҜрқ‘„рқ‘Јрқ‘Ҙdisplaystylecal Lrm HQET=Qvdagger(x)(ivcdot D)Qv(x)+barQvdagger(x)(ivcdot D)barQv(x) , where Qvsubscriptрқ‘„рқ‘ЈQv and QВҜvsubscriptВҜрқ‘„рқ‘ЈbarQv are quark and anti-quark in the fundamental and anti-fundamental representations, respectively; vОј=ОівҖӢ(1,ОІ,0вҶ’вҹӮ)superscriptрқ‘ЈрқңҮрқӣҫ1рқӣҪsubscriptвҶ’0perpendicular-tovmu=gamma(1,beta,vec0perp) is the four velocity; Dрқҗ·D is the covariant derivative. Note that quarks in HQET can be viewed as color sources. If the gluon soft function is considered, the heavy quarks should be in the adjoint representation. In HQET, a color-singlet heavy-quark pair separated by bвҶ’вҶ’рқ‘Ҹvecb generates a heavy quark potential VвҖӢ(|bвҶ’|)рқ‘үвҶ’рқ‘ҸV(|vecb|) in the ground state, and the spectrum includes a gapped continuum above it. The state can also have a residual momentum ОҙвҖӢPвҶ’рқӣҝвҶ’рқ‘ғdeltavecP, which is arbitrary due to reparameterization invariancecitebib.bib345, bib.bib355, and for simplicity we always consider ОҙвҖӢPвҶ’=0рқӣҝвҶ’рқ‘ғ0deltavecP=0. When the sources move with a velocity vрқ‘Јv, the ground state can be labeled by |QВҜвҖӢQ,bвҶ’,ОҙвҖӢPвҶ’вҹ©vsubscriptketВҜрқ‘„рқ‘„вҶ’рқ‘ҸрқӣҝвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘Ј|overlineQQ,vecb,deltavecPv, where the residue momentum ОҙвҖӢPвҶ’=PвҶ’totalвҲ’2вҖӢmQвҖӢОівҖӢОІвҶ’рқӣҝвҶ’рқ‘ғsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ғtotal2subscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘„рқӣҫвҶ’рқӣҪdeltavecP=vecPrm total-2mQgammavecbeta is the difference between the total momentum PвҶ’totalsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ғtotalvecPrm total and the kinetic momentum of the heavy-quarks. The residual energy of the state is E=ОівҲ’1вҖӢVвҖӢ(|bвҶ’вҹӮ|)+ОІвҶ’вӢ…ОҙвҖӢPвҶ’рқҗёsuperscriptрқӣҫ1рқ‘үsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toвӢ…вҶ’рқӣҪрқӣҝвҶ’рқ‘ғE=gamma-1V(|vecbperp|)+vecbetacdotdeltavecP. Consider a process with incoming and outgoing states being heavy-quark pairs separated by bвҶ’вҹӮsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tovecbperp and at velocity vрқ‘Јv and vвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІvprime, respectively. Such a state is created by the interpolating fields рқ’ӘvвҖӢ(t,bвҶ’вҹӮ)=вҲ«d3вҖӢrвҶ’вҖӢQvвҖ вҖӢ(t,rвҶ’)вҖӢрқ’°вҖӢ(rвҶ’,rвҶ’вҖІ,t)вҖӢQВҜvвҖ вҖӢ(t,rвҶ’вҖІ),subscriptрқ’Әрқ‘Јрқ‘ЎsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosuperscriptрқ‘‘3вҶ’рқ‘ҹsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘„рқ‘ЈвҖ рқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ҹрқ’°вҶ’рқ‘ҹsuperscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҹвҖІрқ‘ЎsuperscriptsubscriptВҜрқ‘„рқ‘ЈвҖ рқ‘ЎsuperscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҹвҖІdisplaystylecal Ov(t,vecbperp)=int d3vecr,Qvdagger(t,vecr,)cal U(vecr,vecr,prime,t)barQvdagger(t,vecr,prime) , where rвҶ’вҖІ=rвҶ’+bвҶ’вҹӮsuperscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҹвҖІвҶ’рқ‘ҹsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tovecr,prime=vecr+vecbperp and рқ’°вҖӢ(rвҶ’,rвҶ’вҖІ,t)рқ’°вҶ’рқ‘ҹsuperscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҹвҖІрқ‘Ўcal U(vecr,vecr,prime,t) is a gauge-link connecting rвҶ’вҖІsuperscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҹвҖІvecr,prime to rвҶ’вҶ’рқ‘ҹvecr at time tрқ‘Ўt. The heavy-quark pair created by рқ’Әvsubscriptрқ’Әрқ‘Јcal Ov is forced to be at relative separation bвҶ’вҹӮsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tovecbperp and to have vanishing residual momentum ОҙвҖӢPвҶ’=0рқӣҝвҶ’рқ‘ғ0deltavecP=0. Between the incoming and outgoing states, a product of two local equal-time operators JвҖӢ(v,vвҖІ,bвҶ’вҹӮ)=QВҜvвҖІвҖ вҖӢ(bвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢQВҜvвҖӢ(bвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢQvвҖІвҖ вҖӢвҖӢQvвҖӢрқҗҪрқ‘Јsuperscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosubscriptsuperscriptВҜрқ‘„вҖ superscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosubscriptВҜрқ‘„рқ‘ЈsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘„superscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІвҖ 0subscriptрқ‘„рқ‘Ј0displaystyle J(v,vprime,vecbperp)=barQdaggervprime(vecbperp)barQv(vecbperp)QvprimedaggerQv is inserted at t=0рқ‘Ў0t=0. Then Wрқ‘ҠW can be expressed in terms of HQET propagators which are gauge-links in the v,vвҖІрқ‘Јsuperscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІv,vprime directions. After integrating out the heavy-quark fields, we obtain up to an overall volume factorcitebib.bib273 WвҖӢ(t,tвҖІ,bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҡрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІdisplaystyle W(t,tprime,bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) =1NcвҖӢвҹЁ0|рқ’ӘvвҖІвҖ вҖӢ(tвҖІ,bвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢJвҖӢ(v,vвҖІ,bвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢрқ’ӘvвҖӢ(вҲ’t,bвҶ’вҹӮ)|0вҹ©absent1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җquantum-operator-product0subscriptsuperscriptрқ’ӘвҖ superscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқҗҪрқ‘Јsuperscriptрқ‘ЈвҖІsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosubscriptрқ’Әрқ‘Јрқ‘ЎsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-to0displaystyle=frac1Nc 0|cal Odaggervprime(tprime,vecbperp)J(v,vprime,vecbperp)cal Ov(-t,vecbperp)|0 вҶ’tвҶ’вҲһtвҖІвҶ’вҲһ1NcвҖӢОҰвҖ вҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)вҖӢSвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)вҖӢОҰвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)вҖӢeвҲ’iвҖӢEвҖІвҖӢtвҖІвҲ’iвҖӢEвҖӢt,вҶ’рқ‘ЎвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІabsentвҶ’absent1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җsuperscriptОҰвҖ subscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІОҰsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–superscriptрқҗёвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ЎвҖІрқ‘–рқҗёрқ‘Ўdisplaystylexrightarrow[beginsubarraycttoinfty tprimetoinftyendsubarray]frac1NcPhidagger(bperp,mu)S(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime)Phi(bperp,mu)e-iEprimetprime-iEt , where ОҰвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)ОҰsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮdisplaystylePhi(bperp,mu) =limTвҶ’вҲһвҹЁQВҜQ,bвҶ’вҹӮ|рқ’Әv(T,bвҶ’вҹӮ)|0вҹ©v,displaystyle=limTtoinftyvoverlineQQ,vecbperp|cal Ov(T,vecbperp)|0,, SвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Y,YвҖІ)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқ‘Ңsuperscriptрқ‘ҢвҖІdisplaystyle S(bperp,mu,Y,Yprime) =вҹЁQВҜQ,bвҶ’вҹӮ|J(v,vвҖІ,bвҶ’вҹӮ)|QВҜQ,bвҶ’вҹӮвҹ©vvвҖІ. displaystyle=vprimeoverlineQQ,vecbperp|J(v,vprime,vecbperp)|overlineQQ,vecbperpv . In the last line of Eq. , we have inserted a complete set of heavy-quark pair states before and after JрқҗҪJ. We have explicitly verified Eq. in Euclidean perturbation theory to the one-loop order. The soft function cannot be calculated on the lattice by simply replacing the Minkowskian gauge-links in Eq. with a finite number of Euclidean gauge-links. Through HQET, we find a time-independent formulation of the soft function, which opens up the possibility of direct lattice calculations.
content_sections.V Transverse-Momentum Dependent PDFs V.4: Light-front quantization (LFQ) or formalism is a natural language for parton physics in which partons are made manifest at all stages of calculations. It favors a Hamiltonian approach to QCD like for a non-relativistic quantum mechanical system, i. e. , to diagonalize the Hamiltonian PвҲ’вҖӢ|ОЁnвҹ©=Mn2+PвҶ’вҹӮ22вҖӢP+вҖӢ|ОЁnвҹ©,superscriptрқ‘ғketsubscriptОЁрқ‘ӣsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Җ2рқ‘ӣsuperscriptsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ғperpendicular-to22superscriptрқ‘ғketsubscriptОЁрқ‘ӣhatP-|Psin=fracM2n+vecPperp22P+|Psin , to obtain wave functions for the QCD bound statescitebib.bib93. The light-front wave functions (LFWFs) thus obtained can, in principle, be used to calculate all the partonic densities and correlations functions. Moreover, like in condensed matter systems, knowing quantum many-body wave-functions allows one to understand interesting aspects of quantum coherence and entanglement, as well as the fundamental nature of quantum systems. Therefore, a practical realization of light-front quantization program clearly would be a big step forward in understanding the fundamental structure of the proton. However, from a field theory point of view, wave functions are not the most natural objects to consider due to the non-trivial vacuum, UV divergences as well as the requirement of Lorentz symmetry, according to which the space and time should be treated on equal footing. The proton or other hadrons are excitations of the QCD vacuum which by itself is very complicated because of the well-known phenomena of chiral symmetry breaking and color confinement. To build a proton on top of this vacuum, one naturally has a question of what part of the wave-function reflects the property of the proton and what reflects the vacuum: It is the difference that yields the properties of the proton that are experimentally measurable. There is no clean way to make this separation unless one builds the proton out of elementary excitations or quasi-particles that do not exist in the vacuum, as often done in condensed matter systems. The partons in the IMF avoids the above problems to a certain extent. In fact, due to the kinematic effects, in the IMF all partons in the vacuum have longitudinal momentum k+=0superscriptрқ‘ҳ0k+=0, and to some degree of accuracy, the proton is made of partons with k+вү 0superscriptрқ‘ҳ0k+neq 0. This natural separation of degrees of freedom (DOF) is particularly welcome, making a wave-function description of the proton more natural and interesting in IMF than in any other frame. To implement the above DOF separation, one possibility is to assume triviality of the light-front vacuum. The question that to what extent this holds has been continuously debated over the years. One knows a priori that in relativistic QFT, the vacuum state is boost invariant and frame-independent. In fact, it was proven incitebib.bib377, bib.bib378 that not only the vacuum can not be trivial, even the GreenвҖҷs functions of the full theory cannot pose generic meaningful restrictions to the null-planes Оҫ+=csuperscriptрқңүрқ‘җxi+=c. In fact, the vacuum zero modes do contain non-trivial dynamics and contribute to the properties of the protoncitebib.bib270. Nevertheless, one can adopt an effective theory point of view to simply cut off the zero-modes and relegate their physics to renormalization constants. In some simple cases, these zero-modes can be treated explicitlycitebib.bib233, bib.bib447. By imposing an IR cut-off on the k+вүҘПөsuperscriptрқ‘ҳitalic-Пөk+geqepsilon in the effective Hilbert space, all physics below k+=Пөsuperscriptрқ‘ҳitalic-Пөk+=epsilon are taken into account through renormalization constants. We then obtain an effective LF theory with trivial vacuum, akвҖӢО»вҖӢ|0вҹ©=bpвҖӢПғвҖӢ|0вҹ©=dpвҖӢПғвҖӢ|0вҹ©=0. subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ҳрқңҶket0subscriptрқ‘Ҹрқ‘қрқңҺket0subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘қрқңҺket00aklambda|0=bpsigma|0=dpsigma|0=0 . where |0вҹ©ket0|0 is the vacuum of LFQ. Therefore, the proton can be expanded in terms of the superposition of Fock states in the LF gauge A+=0superscriptрқҗҙ0A+=0citebib.bib93, |Pвҹ©=вҲ‘n=1вҲһвҲ«рқ‘‘О“nвҖӢПҲn0вҖӢ(xi,kвҶ’iвҹӮ)вҖӢвҲҸaiвҖ вҖӢ(xi,kвҶ’iвҹӮ)вҖӢ|0вҹ©. ketрқ‘ғsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣ1differential-dsubscriptО“рқ‘ӣsubscriptsuperscriptрқң“0рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toрқ‘–absentproductsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҺвҖ рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toрқ‘–absentket0displaystyle|P=sumn=1inftyint dGammanpsi0n(xi,veckiperp)prod adaggeri(xi,veckiperp)|0 . where aвҖ superscriptрқ‘ҺвҖ adagger are generic quarks and gluon quanta on the light-front, the phase-space integral reads dвҖӢО“n=вҲҸdвҖӢk+вҖӢd2вҖӢkвҹӮ2вҖӢk+вҖӢ(2вҖӢПҖ)3рқ‘‘subscriptО“рқ‘ӣproductрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқ‘‘2subscriptрқ‘ҳperpendicular-to2superscriptрқ‘ҳsuperscript2рқңӢ3dGamman=prodfracdk+d2kperp2k+(2pi)3. The ПҲnвҖӢ(xi,kвҶ’iвҹӮ)subscriptрқң“рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toрқ‘–absentpsin(xi,veckiperp) are LFWF amplitudes or simply WF amplitudes, where xisubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–xi to denote the set of momentum fractions from x1subscriptрқ‘Ҙ1x1 to xnsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣxn. They are a complete set of non-perturbative quantities which describe the partonic landscape of the proton. The above amplitudes can in principle be calculated through Hamiltonian diagonalization. However, as explained in Sec. , a direct systematic solution in LFQ is impractical. LaMET offers an alternate route to calculate these WF amplitudes. Thanks to the triviality of the vacuum after the truncation k+вүҘПөsuperscriptрқ‘ҳitalic-Пөk+geqepsilon, they can then be written in terms of the invariant matrix elements by inverting the above expansion, ПҲn0вҖӢ(xi,kвҶ’iвҹӮ)=вҹЁ0|вҲҸaiвҖӢ(xi,kвҶ’iвҹӮ)|Pвҹ©. subscriptsuperscriptрқң“0рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toрқ‘–absentquantum-operator-product0productsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘–subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toрқ‘–absentрқ‘ғdisplaystylepsi0n(xi,veckiperp)= 0|prod ai(xi,veckiperp)|P . After properly restoring gauge-invariance and imposing regularizations, they become the matrix elements of light-cone correlators, the same type as those in the TMDPDFs. Therefore, the LaMET method applies to them, which allows one to effectively obtain the results of light-front quantization through instant quantization in a large momentum frame. To realize the goal, the LFWF amplitudes also need a rapidity renormalization, as in the case of TMDPDFs. In this section, we explain how the reduced soft function Srsubscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҹSr can be obtained by combining the LFWF amplitudes and a special light-meson form factor, instead of as the form factor in HQET discussed in the previous section. A lattice calculation based on the light-meson framework has been performed incitebib.bib455. Let us consider the following form factor of a pseudoscalar light-meson state with constituents ПҲВҜвҖӢО·ВҜрқң“рқңӮoverlinepsieta, FвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,P,PвҖІ,Ој)=вҹЁPвҖІ|О·ВҜвҖӢ(bвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢО“вҖІвҖӢО·вҖӢ(bвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢО“вҖӢПҲвҖӢ|Pвҹ©рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘ғвҖІрқңҮquantum-operator-productsuperscriptрқ‘ғвҖІВҜрқңӮsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosuperscriptО“вҖІрқңӮsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toВҜрқң“0О“рқң“0рқ‘ғdisplaystyle F(bperp,P,Pprime,mu)= Pprime|overlineeta(vecbperp)Gammaprimeeta(vecbperp)overlinepsiGammapsi|P where ПҲрқң“psi and О·рқңӮeta are light quark fields of different flavors; PОј=(Pt,0,0,Pz)superscriptрқ‘ғрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘Ў00superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pmu=(Pt,0,0,Pz) and PвҖІвҒЈОј=(Pt,0,0,вҲ’Pz)superscriptрқ‘ғвҖІрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘Ў00superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pprimemu=(Pt,0,0,-Pz) are two large momenta which approach two opposite light-like directions in the limit PzвҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pztoinfty; О“О“Gamma and О“вҖІsuperscriptО“вҖІGammaprime are Dirac gamma matrices, which can be chosen as О“=О“вҖІ=1О“superscriptО“вҖІ1Gamma=Gammaprime=1, Оі5subscriptрқӣҫ5gamma5 or ОівҹӮsubscriptрқӣҫperpendicular-togammaperp and ОівҹӮвҖӢОі5subscriptрқӣҫperpendicular-tosubscriptрқӣҫ5gammaperpgamma5, so that the quark fields have leading components on the respective light-cones. At large momentum, the form factor factorizes through TMD factorization into LFWF amplitudes. To motivate the factorization, we need to consider the leading region of IR divergences in a similar way for SIDIS and Drell-Yancitebib.bib264, bib.bib133, and the result is shown in Fig. . There are two collinear sub-diagrams responsible for collinear modes in ++ and вҲ’- directions, and a soft sub-diagram responsible for soft contributions. Besides, there are two IR-free hard cores localized around (0,0,0,0)0000(0,0,0,0) and (0,bвҶ’вҹӮ,0)0subscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҹperpendicular-to0(0,vecbperp,0). In the covariant gauge, there are arbitrary numbers of longitudinally-polarized collinear and soft gluons that can connect to the hard and collinear sub-diagrams. Based on the region decomposition, we now follow the standard procedure to make factorization into LF quantitiescitebib.bib133. We first factorize the soft divergences. This can be done with the soft function SвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој,Оҙ+,ОҙвҲ’)рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮsuperscriptрқӣҝsuperscriptрқӣҝS(bperp,mu,delta+,delta-). It re-sums the soft gluon radiations from fast-moving color-charges. Intuitively, soft gluons have no impact on the velocity of the fast-moving color charged partons, and the propagators of partons eikonalize to straight gauge links along their moving trajectory. We then factorize the collinear divergences. For the incoming direction, the collinear divergences is captured by the LFWF amplitude for the incoming parton ПҲqВҜвҖӢqвҖӢ(x,bвҹӮ,Ој,ОҙвҲ’вҖІ)psibarqq(x,bperp,mu,deltaprime-) defined with future-pointing gauge-links. Therefore, with non-perturbative quantities Fрқҗ№F and ПҲ+superscriptрқң“psi+, we obtain the reduced soft function, SrвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,Ој)=FвҖӢ(bвҹӮ,P,PвҖІ,Ој)вҲ«рқ‘‘xвҖӢрқ‘‘xвҖІвҖӢHвҖӢ(x,xвҖІ)вҖӢПҲqВҜвҖӢqвҖ вҖӢ(xвҖІ,bвҹӮ)вҖӢПҲqВҜвҖӢqвҖӢ(x,bвҹӮ),subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҹsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқңҮрқҗ№subscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘ғвҖІрқңҮdifferential-dрқ‘Ҙdifferential-dsuperscriptрқ‘ҘвҖІрқҗ»рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ҘвҖІsuperscriptsubscriptрқң“ВҜрқ‘һрқ‘һвҖ superscriptрқ‘ҘвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tosubscriptрқң“ВҜрқ‘һрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-toSr(bperp,mu)=fracF(bperp,P,Pprime,mu)int dxdxprimeH(x,xprime)tildepsibarqqdagger(xprime,bperp)tildepsibarqq(x,bperp) , where Hрқҗ»H can be obtained perturbatively. Based on the one-loop results for the form factor, the quasi-WF amplitudes and the reduced soft function, the one-loop matching kernel for the vector current can be extracted as: HвҖӢ(О¶z,О¶zвҖІ,О¶ВҜz,О¶ВҜzвҖІ,Ој2)=1+ОұsвҖӢCF2вҖӢiвҖӢlnвҒЎО¶zвҖӢО¶ВҜzО¶zвҖІвҖӢО¶ВҜzвҖІрқҗ»subscriptрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§subscriptВҜрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptВҜрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§superscriptрқңҮ21subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқ‘–subscriptрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptВҜрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptВҜрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§displaystyle H(zetaz,zetaprimez,barzetaz,barzetaprimez,mu2)=1+fracalphasCF2ilnfracsqrtzetazbarzetazsqrtzetaprimezbarzetaprimez +ОұsвҖӢCF4вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(вҲ’8+ln2вҒЎО¶zО¶zвҖІ+lnвҒЎО¶zвҖӢО¶zвҖІОј2+(О¶вҶ’О¶ВҜ)),subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№4рқңӢ8superscript2subscriptрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§subscriptрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§superscriptрқңҮ2вҶ’рқңҒВҜрқңҒdisplaystyle+fracalphasCF4pileft(-8+ln2fracsqrtzetazsqrtzetaprimez+lnfracsqrtzetazzetaprimezmu2+(zetarightarrowbarzeta)right) , and the renormalization group equation for Hрқҗ»H reads: Ој2вҖӢddвҖӢОј2вҖӢlnвҒЎHвҖӢ(О¶z,О¶zвҖІ,О¶ВҜz,О¶ВҜzвҖІ,Ој2)=вҲ’2вҖӢОіFвҖӢ(Оұs)вҲ’О“SвҖӢ(Оұs),superscriptрқңҮ2рқ‘‘рқ‘‘superscriptрқңҮ2рқҗ»subscriptрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§subscriptВҜрқңҒрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptВҜрқңҒвҖІрқ‘§superscriptрқңҮ22subscriptрқӣҫрқҗ№subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptО“рқ‘Ҷsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ displaystylemu2fracddmu2ln H(zetaz,zetaprimez,barzetaz,barzetaprimez,mu2)=-2gammaF(alphas)-GammaS(alphas) , where ОіFsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗ№gammaF and О“SsubscriptО“рқ‘ҶGammaS have been defined before. Here we briefly comment on the end-point behavior. As xвҲј0similar-toрқ‘Ҙ0xsim 0, the hard kernel diverges logarithmically near the end point as 1+ОұsвҖӢln2вҒЎx1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ superscript2рқ‘Ҙ1+alphasln2x, but the quasi-WF amplitudes approach zero at large or small xрқ‘Ҙx linearly, thus the end point regions behave as xвҖӢln2вҒЎxрқ‘Ҙsuperscript2рқ‘Ҙxln2x, which is free from those problems for the kTsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘ҮkT factorization for electromagnetic form factorcitebib.bib322. Moreover, we can fix the zрқ‘§z-component momentum transfer at each of the vertices to be Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, which indicating that x+xвҖІ=1рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ҘвҖІ1x+xprime=1. In this case the end-point behavior is improved to x2вҖӢln2вҒЎxsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙ2superscript2рқ‘Ҙx2ln2x.
content_sections.VI Lattice Parton Physics with LaMET: Lattice gauge theory simulates continuum QCD in imaginary time on a discretized 4D Euclidean lattice. The method is characterized by the finite lattice spacing aрқ‘Һa and volume L1Г—L2Г—L3Г—Tsubscriptрқҗҝ1subscriptрқҗҝ2subscriptрқҗҝ3рқ‘ҮL1times L2times L3times T, and input parameters such as the strong coupling and quark masses. To calculate physical quantities, one usually expects to take the continuum (aвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘Һ0ato 0) and infinite volume Li,TвҶ’вҲһвҶ’subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘–рқ‘ҮLi,Ttoinfty limits, as well as tuning the quark masses so that observables such as the pion mass mПҖsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқңӢmpi agrees with the physical value of вҲј140similar-toabsent140sim 140 MeV. There are different methods to implement the fermions on the latticecitebib.bib403, which leads to different properties of the lattice action such as chiral symmetry breaking for Wilson fermions. In the lattice calculation of hadron matrix elements, the initial and final states are generated by acting the source and sink interpolation operators on the vacuum, and the ground-state contributions are filtered out by propagating over a sufficiently large Euclidean time. A boosted hadron state can be obtained by inserting momentum into the source and sink operators through Fourier transform in the 3D spatial coordinates. The lattice QCD calculations of parton physics using LaMET started with the exploratory studies on the simplest PDFs and the gluon helicitycitebib.bib327, bib.bib22, bib.bib448, which yielded fairly encouraging results, demonstrating that LaMET is a viable approach. In subsequent studies, more attention has been paid to the systematics, including establishing a proper renormalization and matching procedure, simulating at the physical pion mass, removing the excited-state contamination, etc. Such studies have greatly improved the precision of the calculations, with the latest results exhibiting a reasonable agreement with phenomenological PDFscitebib.bib20, bib.bib329, bib.bib21, bib.bib248, bib.bib206. In the meantime, explorations have also been made on similar large momentum data using the coordinate-space factorization methods including the pseudo-PDFcitebib.bib381, bib.bib295, bib.bib294, bib.bib296 and current-current correlationcitebib.bib33, bib.bib422, bib.bib421. Nevertheless, dedicated large-scale efforts with the state-of-art resources are yet to be seen. Lattice parton physics with LaMET is just at its dawn. With EIC in the US going forward, a new era of lattice calculations is to come. In this section, we summarize the current status of lattice calculations using LaMET and discuss future prospects. We will begin with a general discussion on what kind of lattice setups are best suited for LaMET calculations, and then briefly summarize relevant lattice techniques that facilitate such calculations. After that, we review the lattice calculations that have been carried out so far and point out future improvements. A nice complementary discussion about lattice calculations has been made incitebib.bib127. Other reviews that summarize the recent developments in the lattice calculation of PDFs can be found incitebib.bib368, bib.bib459, bib.bib148.
In this subsection, we discuss the challenges for lattice calculations in LaMET, and estimate the required lattice requirements by taking the collinear PDFs as an example.In addition to common challenges with other lattice calculations, such as taking the continuum and infinite volume limits, simulating at or extrapolating to the physical pion mass, etc. , LaMET applications require generating large-momentum hadron on lattice. For LaMET expansion, 1/(xвҖӢPz)1рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§1/(xPz) is the expansion parameter, and for the coordinate-space factorizations, large quasi-light-cone distance О»рқңҶlambda requires even bigger hadron momentum. However, realizing this faces a number of practical challenges. First, it has been difficult to generate large-momentum hadron states on the lattice, until the technique of momentum smearingcitebib.bib36 was proposed. The conventional smearing method in coordinate space is designed to increase the overlap with ground state hadron at rest. Thus, it is not surprising that such a smearing is not efficient when the hadron has a large momentum. The momentum smearing technique introduces an extra phase factor eiвҖӢkвҶ’вӢ…zвҶ’superscriptрқ‘’вӢ…рқ‘–вҶ’рқ‘ҳвҶ’рқ‘§eiveckcdotvecz to the quark field, such that it is peaked at nonzero momentum kвҶ’вҶ’рқ‘ҳveck in Fourier space, as shown inFig. . In this way, the overlap with high momentum state is vastly increased after Euclidean time evolution. Recently, the momentum smearing technique has been incorporated into the framework of distillationcitebib.bib187 to improve the extraction of ground-state energy and matrix elements at momentum вүІless-than-or-similar-tolesssim 3 GeV. Although there are other proposed methods to generate large momentumcitebib.bib442, the momentum smearing has become a standard technique in LaMET applications. Second, the proton size is frame-dependent and changes with its momentum. In the protonвҖҷs rest frame, simulating its structure requires that the lattice spacing be much smaller than the QCD confinement scale, i. e. aвүӘОӣQCDвҲ’1much-less-thanрқ‘ҺsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD1allLambdarm QCD-1. When the proton is moving fast, it undergoes Lorentz contraction by a boost factor Оірқӣҫgamma in the momentum direction, thus a finer lattice spacing aвүӘ(ОівҖӢОӣQCD)вҲ’1much-less-thanрқ‘ҺsuperscriptрқӣҫsubscriptОӣQCD1all(gammaLambdarm QCD)-1 is needed. If aвүӨ0.2рқ‘Һ0.2aleq 0.2 fm is the minimum requirement to investigate a static proton, one will need at least aвүӨ0.04рқ‘Һ0.04aleq 0.04 fm to have the same resolution for a proton at 5 GeV. A smaller lattice spacing is difficult to achieve with current computing resources, for it suffers from the well-known critical slowing down problem, i. e. , the auto-correlation times of observables such as the topological charge increase when approaching the continuum limitcitebib.bib406, which can be much longer than the Monte-Carlo simulation times. A lattice with open (Neumann) boundary condition on gauge fields in the Euclidean time directioncitebib.bib348, which allows topological charge to flow in and out at boundaries of time, may overcome this problem. Third, the gaps between the ground state and the excited state energies become smaller because of the time dilation effect. In the protonвҖҷs rest frame, the excited state contamination exponentially decays with the mass gap О”вҖӢMО”рқ‘ҖDelta M and evolution time П„рқңҸtau in the form of eвҲ’О”вҖӢMвҖӢП„superscriptрқ‘’О”рқ‘ҖрқңҸe-Delta Mtau. In the boosted frame, the mass gap О”вҖӢMО”рқ‘ҖDelta M in the decay factor is replaced by the energy gap О”вҖӢEвҲјО”вҖӢM/Оіsimilar-toО”рқҗёО”рқ‘ҖрқӣҫDelta EsimDelta M/gamma, and the decay changes like eвҲ’О”вҖӢMвҖӢП„вҶ’eвҲ’О”вҖӢEвҖӢП„=eвҲ’О”вҖӢMвҖӢП„/ОівҶ’superscriptрқ‘’О”рқ‘ҖрқңҸsuperscriptрқ‘’О”рқҗёрқңҸsuperscriptрқ‘’О”рқ‘ҖрқңҸрқӣҫe-Delta Mtauto e-Delta Etau=e-Delta Mtau/gamma under Euclidean time evolution. Therefore, with a boosted state, a longer time evolution (source-sink separation) is needed. For example, if a source-sink separation of 1 fm is needed to separate the excited state of proton with 2 GeV momentum, a proton with 5 GeV momentum will require a source-sink separation of 2.5 fm. Even if the two-state fit technique is used, a longer time evolution is still required so that only the ground and first excited states dominate. Last but not the least, lattice calculation requires PzвүӘ1/amuch-less-thansuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§1рқ‘ҺPzll 1/a, so that discretization effects of рқ’ӘвҖӢ((aвҖӢPz)n)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘ӣcal O((aPz)n) are under control. Therefore, one has to go to smaller lattice spacing in order to reach larger momentum. The quantification of рқ’ӘвҖӢ((aвҖӢPz)n)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘ӣcal O((aPz)n) effects alone in LaMET calculations has not been done, as all discretization errors are treated on equal footing in continuum extrapolation. To summarize, to achieve a precision calculation of boosted hadron structure on lattice, a fine lattice spacing (at least in the longitudinal direction) and a large box size in the time direction are essential, which of course will also require control over the signal-to-noise ratio at large Euclidean times.In practical calculations, a correlation function is first obtained on lattice in coordinate space, and then Fourier transformed to momentum space with the phase factor eiвҖӢО»вҖӢxsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқңҶрқ‘Ҙeilambda x where О»=zвҖӢPzрқңҶрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§lambda=zPz. Therefore, the smallest xрқ‘Ҙx one can reach can be roughly estimated from the largest О»рқңҶlambda as xвҲј1/О»similar-toрқ‘Ҙ1рқңҶxsim 1/lambda. However, a more stringent constraint comes from requiring that the higher-twist contribution рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD2/(xвҖӢPz)2)рқ’ӘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2cal O(Lambdarm QCD2/(xPz)2) be small so that the factorization is still valid, which implies xвү«ОӣQCD/Pzmuch-greater-thanрқ‘ҘsubscriptОӣQCDsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§xggLambdarm QCD/Pz. This also provides a rough estimate for the largest attainable xрқ‘Ҙx (xвүӘ1вҲ’ОӣQCD/Pzmuch-less-thanрқ‘Ҙ1subscriptОӣQCDsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§xll 1-Lambdarm QCD/Pz) since the momentum fraction carried by other partons is вҲј(1вҲ’x)similar-toabsent1рқ‘Ҙsim(1-x) which should also be bounded from below by the above estimate. For the current state-of-the-art simulations, the lattice spacing can reach 0.04 fmcitebib.bib195, bib.bib206, which implies PmaxzвҲј5similar-tosubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§max5Pzrm maxsim 5 GeV and the effective resolution in longitudinal direction is about ОівҖӢaвҲј0.2similar-toрқӣҫрқ‘Һ0.2gamma asim 0.2 fm. Thus the valid xрқ‘Ҙx region that can be extracted from lattice is roughly 0.1 to 0.9. On the other hand, to avoid finite volume effects, it is believed that mПҖвҖӢLвүі4greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscriptрқ‘ҡрқңӢрқҗҝ4mpiLgtrsim 4. For physical pion mass, the box size in spatial direction LрқҗҝL should be at least 6 fm, which means the box size is 150 lattice spacing. So far, the largest box size in LaMET calculations is 5.8 fmcitebib.bib329. As discussed in Sec. , the source-sink separation of 2.5 fm is needed for Pz=5superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§5Pz=5 GeV. So the box size in time direction Tрқ‘ҮT does not need to be particularly longer than LрқҗҝL, and T=Lрқ‘ҮрқҗҝT=L is sufficient in this lattice setup. In summary, with a=0.04рқ‘Һ0.04a=0.04 fm at physical pion mass, one need a L3Г—T=1503Г—150superscriptрқҗҝ3рқ‘Үsuperscript1503150L3times T=1503times 150 lattice to reliably extract 0.1<x<0.90.1рқ‘Ҙ0.90.1<x<0.9 region, which could be possible in an exa-scale computer. There are potential tricks to reduce the computational cost. First, the required source-sink separation can be shorter if one uses a multi-state instead of two-state fit with enough statistics. However, since the number of fitting parameters in nрқ‘ӣn-state fit grows as n2superscriptрқ‘ӣ2n2, such a fitting will become infeasible for too large nрқ‘ӣn. Second, note that the resolution required for transverse proton structures is not affected by the Lorentz boost, one may use a coarse lattice in the transverse directions, aвҹӮ=0.1subscriptрқ‘Һperpendicular-to0.1aperp=0.1 fm. The required box size is then LвҲҘГ—LвҹӮ2Г—T=150Г—602Г—150subscriptрқҗҝparallel-tosuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҝperpendicular-to2рқ‘Ү150superscript602150Lparalleltimes Lperp2times T=150times 602times 150. This asymmetric lattice can greatly reduce the resources needed for large momentum since the transverse box size is fixed. However, generating configurations and renormalization on such a lattice might bring new problems and should be further studied. In the near future, exascale supercomputers may help to reach higher momentum, as large as 5 GeV for the proton, and improve the precision of LaMET calculations. Further theoretical developments and new ideas on the technique and algorithms are also needed to overcome the simulation difficulties.

In this subsection, we review current status of lattice calculations of flavor non-singlet (isovector) PDFs in the proton and pion. The non-singlet case has the advantage that the mixing with gluons as well as the lattice calculation of disconnected diagrams can be avoided, thus greatly reducing the computational challenge. It is the most extensively studied parton observable with LaMET so far.The pioneering lattice studies for the isovector quark PDF in the proton were carried out incitebib.bib327, bib.bib22. These are proof-of-principle studies as the renormalization of quasi-PDFs was not well understood at that time. Nevertheless, their results encouraged the follow-up theoretical works on LaMET, including a proper renormalization and matching suitable for lattice implementations. Certain lattice artifacts have also been studied. For example, although there is no power-divergent mixing for the quasi-PDF operators on the lattice, additional operator mixings that are not seen in the continuum can still occur if a non-chiral lattice fermion such as the Wilson-type fermion is used. Incitebib.bib149, bib.bib116 it was shown that at рқ’ӘвҖӢ(a0)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘Һ0cal O(a0) the operator for the unpolarized quark quasi-PDF, OОіzвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘§рқ‘§Ogammaz(z), can mix with the scalar operator OрқҹҸвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯ1рқ‘§Obf 1(z), whereas OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) does not. To reduce the systematic uncertainty from such mixing, О“=ОіtО“superscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ЎGamma=gammat has been used since then for lattice calculations of the unpolarized quark PDF, e. g. incitebib.bib17, bib.bib214, bib.bib115. Similarly, for helicity and transversity cases, one should choose О“=Оі5вҖӢОіzО“superscriptрқӣҫ5superscriptрқӣҫрқ‘§Gamma=gamma5gammaz and О“=iвҖӢПғzвҹӮ=ОівҹӮвҖӢОіzО“рқ‘–superscriptрқңҺperpendicular-toрқ‘§absentsuperscriptрқӣҫperpendicular-tosuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘§Gamma=isigmazperp=gammaperpgammaz, respectively, in order to avoid the mixing. It should be noted that at рқ’ӘвҖӢ(a)рқ’Әрқ‘Һcal O(a) all OО“вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§tildeOGamma(z)вҖҷs can mix with otherscitebib.bib116. Nevertheless, a fine lattice spacing can reduce these effects. Incitebib.bib17, bib.bib115, bib.bib214, the nonperturbative renormalization (NPR) of the quasi-PDFs was studied in the RI/MOM schemecitebib.bib359. This scheme has several advantages: The lattice regularization scheme can be converted to MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme through RI/MOM renormalization condition, the computation cost is affordable, the systematic errors can be reduced or quantified more easily, etc. The works before 2018 did not include NPR and the systematics were not accurately quantified. The later works have implemented the RI/MOM scheme and the corresponding perturbative matchingcitebib.bib149, bib.bib420, bib.bib343. The coordinate-space method is also developed in parallel incitebib.bib381, bib.bib128, bib.bib294, bib.bib296, bib.bib61. In Figs. and, we select some most recent lattice results. ETMC published the proton unpolarized, helicity and transversity PDFs with Pz=1.4superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§1.4Pz=1.4 GeV at physical pion masscitebib.bib20, bib.bib21, and LP3superscriptLP3rm LP3 published the proton helicity PDF with unprecedented momentum Pz=3.0superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§3.0Pz=3.0 GeV at physical pion masscitebib.bib329. Recently, calculations on fine latticescitebib.bib195, bib.bib16 and an extrapolation to the continuum limitcitebib.bib16 have become available. The finite volume effects, which was first studied in a modelcitebib.bib88, have also been investigated on the lattice lately incitebib.bib331, where no sizeable volume-dependence was observed at Pz=1.3superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§1.3Pz=1.3 and 2.62.62.6 GeV. The PDFs extracted from LaMET can be useful for phenomenology by providing input in kinematic regions that are difficult to measure in experiments. It has attracted attention from global fit communitycitebib.bib332, bib.bib235, bib.bib151, bib.bib89. For example, it has been found that in the large-xрқ‘Ҙx region of unpolarized PDF the lattice result will lead to significant improvement on global fit result if it reaches an accuracy of about 10%percent1010%citebib.bib332. The sea quark asymmetrycitebib.bib207 is also possible to be investigated now directly on lattice. For the transversity PDF, due to the difficulty of measurement in experiment, lattice results can already have impact on improving global fit and even making predictions. In addition to the isovector cases, calculations of the strange and charm unpolarized distributionscitebib.bib457, as well as the flavor separation of light quarks in the helicity PDFcitebib.bib14, have also been carried out recently. From early exploratory results showing qualitative behavior of PDFs to the latest results which are comparable with global fits, it has come a long way in developing new techniques (momentum smearing, renormalization, matching, etc. ) and the computation resources have been steadily increased over time. The systematic uncertainties in the lattice calculation of PDFs have been thorougly investigated by the ETMCcitebib.bib18. Further studies on systematics such as the discretization effects and finite volume effects on various lattice ensembles are still necessary. In the future, lattice QCD is expected to make a significant impact on nuclon structure. To conclude this subsection, we would like to mention that there are also lattice studies of the isovector PDF of other baryons, О”+superscriptО”Delta+ to be more concrete, using LaMETcitebib.bib108.The pion valence quark distribution has been extracted from various Drell-Yan data for pion-nucleon/pion-nucleus scattering, while theoretical predictions do not yield consistent results with the experimental extraction, especially in large-xрқ‘Ҙx regioncitebib.bib238. LaMET calculations will be able to shed valuable light on how to resolve this disagreement, provided that all systematics are well under control. In principle, calculating the pion valence PDF is easier than the proton PDF. First, the pion state is easier to produce and the quark contractions are fewer. Second, the energy gap between the first excited and ground state of the pion is much bigger than the energy gap of the proton. Therefore, the excited-state contamination is easier to control. The simulation was first performed incitebib.bib451 with the same lattice setup and procedure used in exploratory studies of the proton PDF. A more thorough study on the pion valence quark PDF was done by the lattice QCD group of BNLcitebib.bib248. It is worth pointing out that the excited state contamination was thoroughly studied using multi-state fits, with the ground and first excited states both agreeing with the expected dispersion relations, indicating that the excited contamination is well under control. The comparison of the lattice results from quasi-PDF, pseudo-PDF and current-current correlator approach are shown in Fig. . Note that the LP3superscriptLP3rm LP3citebib.bib451 result was obtained using Fourier transformation and inversion of factorization formula, while other three groups used parameterization models to fit the lattice data. More dedicated effort is needed to reduce the errors, and a meaningful comparison between different operators and analysis methods should be made. For other mesons, we would like to mention that there is a study of kaon valence quark PDF using MILC configurationscitebib.bib328.

In this subsection, we summarize the applications of LaMET to other collinear parton observables, including the gluon helicity, the gluon PDFs, meson DAs and GPDs.The total gluon helicity О”вҖӢGО”рқҗәDelta G is a key component in understanding the proton spin structure. It has been intensively explored at RHIC and will be dedicatedly pursued at EIC in the future. However, a theoretical lattice calculation of О”вҖӢGО”рқҗәDelta G had not been possible until the proposal of LaMET. The first such effort was made by ПҮрқң’chiQCD collaboration incitebib.bib448. The calculation was carried out with valence overlap fermions on 2+1212+1 flavor domain-wall fermion gauge configurations, using ensembles with multiple lattice spacings and volumes including one with physical pion mass. The authors simulated proton matrix elements of the free-field operator (EвҶ’Г—AвҶ’)3superscriptвҶ’рқҗёвҶ’рқҗҙ3(vecEtimesvecA)3 in the Coulomb gauge at various momenta, and then converted them to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme with one-loop lattice perturbation theory. The MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS matrix elements at each lattice momentum are shown inFig. . Though a LaMET matching is necessary to match the results to the physical gluon helicity, the authors did not apply it due to the concern of perturbative convergence of the matching coefficientcitebib.bib287. Instead, as the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS matrix elements show rather mild momentum dependence up to the maximum momentum вҲјsimilar-tosim1.5 GeV, they extrapolated the results to infinite momentum, as well as physical pion mass and continuum limits, with a model motivated by chiral EFT. Their final result is О”вҖӢGвҖӢ(Ој2=10вҖӢGeV2)=0.251вҖӢвҖӢО”рқҗәsuperscriptрқңҮ210superscriptGeV20.2514716Delta G(mu2=10 textGeV2)=0.251, or 50% of the total proton spin, which agrees with the truncated moment of О”вҖӢgвҖӢ(x)О”рқ‘”рқ‘ҘDelta g(x)citebib.bib202, bib.bib380 within uncertainties. Despite such progress, one should be cautious that this calculation still needs further improvements in the future. Among others, the most important ones are simulations at larger proton momentum, performing an NPR and investigating perturbative convergence of LaMET matching and its implementation.The gluon PDF is of great interest not only for precision physics at LHC, but also for understanding the gluonic structure of the proton and nucleiвҖ”as well as the small-xрқ‘Ҙx dynamicsвҖ”at the future EIC. With the recent progress on the renormalization and matching for gluon quasi-PDFscitebib.bib436, bib.bib437, bib.bib453, bib.bib325, bib.bib435 or the coordinate-space вҖңpseudo distributionsвҖқcitebib.bib41, a systematic lattice calculation of the gluon PDFs can be carried out in principle. Before the above theoretical developments, an exploratory lattice study of the proton and pion unpolarized gluon PDFs were carried out incitebib.bib194. The authors calculated quasi gluon LF correlations and compared them to the LF correlations for the gluon PDFs. Later on, based on the multiplicative renormalizability of certain choice of the quasi gluon LF correlatorcitebib.bib453, the same authors used the ratio schemecitebib.bib41 in coordinate space to renormalize the lattice matrix elements, and fitted the proton unpolarized gluon PDF with a simple two-parameter model citebib.bib196. Although the results show agreement with the global analyses in the large-xрқ‘Ҙx region, the systematics from the model-dependence of the fit remains to be quantified for a controlled calculation of the gluon PDF.According to Sec. , LaMET can be readily applied to calculating DAs, and the lattice resource needed is expected to be cheaper than that for PDFs since there is one less external state, which reduces the number of contractions for the quark propagators. So far there are a few exploratory investigations on meson DAs, in particular, on pioncitebib.bib450 and kaon DAscitebib.bib454. The lattice calculations of pioncitebib.bib450 and kaon DAscitebib.bib454 were first explored without the NPR and the corresponding matching. Recently, the pion and kaon DAs from the RI/MOM scheme analysis are extrapolated to the continuum limitcitebib.bib456, where the authors eventually adopted a two-parameter model to fit the final result. The above results are shown inFig. . Apart from LaMET, the current-current correlation methodscitebib.bib76, bib.bib78, bib.bib165 have also made much progress on the pion DAcitebib.bib33, bib.bib164, bib.bib163.As discussed in Sec. , the global fitting of GPDs still faces challenges from their complicated kinematic dependence and limited information from the experimental observables despite the progress madecitebib.bib310, bib.bib198. On the other hand, previous lattice QCD method is only able to calculate the lowest few moments of the GPDscitebib.bib221, which is far from sufficient to reconstruct their full kinematic dependence. Applying LaMET to GPD calculations will provide important information on the GPDs, especially in kinematic regions that are not accessible in currently available experiments. In addition, on the lattice one can study the GPD dependence on one kinematic variable by fixing the others. All these will help differentiate commonly used models in GPD parameterization. Calculating the quasi-GPDs requires more resources than quasi-PDF, but does not need further techniques in principle. Besides, the lattice renormalization factors for the quasi-PDFs can be used here, as has been argued in Sec. . The first lattice calculation of the pion unpolarized isovector quark GPD was carried out incitebib.bib118, though the results are not yet able to differentiate models or compare to experiments. Recently, ETMC completed the first proof-of-principle calculation of the proton unpolarized and helicity GPDscitebib.bib19, as shown in Fig. , which demonstrates that it is feasible to extract the GPDs with controlled systematics on available computational resources.The higher-twist PDFs probe multi-parton correlations, and their contribution at x=0рқ‘Ҙ0x=0 can shed light on the LF zero modescitebib.bib270. As we discussed in Sec. , such distributions can also be calculated on the lattice with the LaMET approach. The first attempt to calculate the isovector twist-three PDF gTвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Үрқ‘ҘgT(x) has been carried out by ETMCcitebib.bib62 using the one-loop matching coefficient they computed incitebib.bib63, bib.bib64. Their results show agreement with the Wandzura-Wilczeck approximationcitebib.bib433, which ignores the contribution from dynamical twist-three contributions, and the Burkhardt-Cottingham sum rulecitebib.bib100. Nevertheless, the mixing between gTвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Үрқ‘ҘgT(x) and other twist-three distributions was not considered, and further study is still required for an accurate matching to the light-cone PDF.

With tremendous experimental focus on the TMDPDFs for studying 3D proton structures and gluon saturation at EIC, their first-principle calculation from lattice QCD will significantly boost this direction by providing useful nonperturbative inputs for all the phenomenological analyses. In this subsection, we discuss the status and prospects of calculating the quasi-TMDPDF and soft function with LaMET. Besides, we note that before LaMET there had already been efforts to extract information of TMDs by studying ratios of the lattice correlatorscitebib.bib222, bib.bib375, bib.bib374, bib.bib191, bib.bib449, which has made a series of progress in the past decade. We begin with a brief review of them.By employing Lorentz covariance, the xрқ‘Ҙx-moments of TMDPDFs are related to the form factors of spacelike staple-shaped gauge link operators, which can be directly simulated on the lattice. Although the lattice calculation of the soft function was not available during that time, ratios of the spin-dependent and the unpolarized matrix elements were formed to cancel it, thus providing useful information of different TMDPDFs. For example, the time-reversal odd TMDPDFs can be studied with the staple-shaped gauge link operator in a transversely polarized proton state, thus helping understand properties related to single-spin asymmetry (SSA), which was measured experimentally at STARcitebib.bib5 and COMPASScitebib.bib7. Incitebib.bib374, bib.bib191, the Sivers and Boer-Mulders functions of proton and pion were studied; Other time-reversal even functions, such as the worm-gear function g1вҖӢTsubscriptрқ‘”1рқ‘Үg1Tcitebib.bib425, were also studiedcitebib.bib449.The lattice calculation of the quasi-TMDPDF defined in Eq. is straightforward. The matrix element of the staple-shaped quark Wilson line operator can be simulated the same way as the quasi-PDF case, except that the geometry of the gauge-link is different, while the calculation of Wilson loop ZEsubscriptрқ‘ҚрқҗёZE is standard practice in lattice QCD. The more challenging part, however, is the renormalization of the quasi-TMDPDF and its matching to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme. Using the auxiliary field theory formalism, one can argue that staple-shaped quark Wilson line operator is also multiplicatively renormalizablecitebib.bib178, bib.bib215. On a non-chiral lattice, it suffers from finite mixing with other quark bilinear operators, as was predicted by one-loop lattice perturbation theorycitebib.bib150. The full mixing pattern for such operators with different Dirac matrices have been studied in the RI/MOM scheme on three quenched lattice ensembles with different spacingscitebib.bib412, and a diagonalization of the mixing matrix is adopted to renormalize these operators. Meanwhile, the one-loop conversion factors that convert the RI/MOM matrix elements to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme have been calculated in continuum perturbation theory for both the z=0рқ‘§0z=0citebib.bib150 and zвү 0рқ‘§0zneq 0citebib.bib178 cases. Although the soft function is still needed to fully determine the physical TMDPDF, the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS quasi-TMDPDF can already be used to extract the Collins-Soper kernel according to Eq. citebib.bib279, bib.bib176, bib.bib177. Since the Collins-Soper kernel can be defined from both the bare TMDPDF and the soft function, it is independent of the external state and can be calculated in a pion which is the least expensive on the lattice. Up to mass corrections suppressed by the momentum in Eq. , this calculation also allows for using an unphysical valence pion mass, as long as the sea quark masses are physical. With the method developed incitebib.bib176, the first exploratory lattice calculation of the Collins-Soper kernel was performed incitebib.bib411 on a quenched lattice with heavy valence pion mass mПҖвҲј1.2similar-tosubscriptрқ‘ҡрқңӢ1.2mpisim 1.2 GeV, and the result is shown inFig. . As one can see, the lattice prediction is robust for 0.1вҖӢfm<bвҹӮ<0.8вҖӢfm0.1fmsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-to0.8fm0.1 rm fm<bperp<0.8 rm fm, which covers the nonperturbative region that is important for TMD evolution in global analyses. Besides, at small bвҹӮsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tobperp, the perturbative calculation can serve as a calibration for estimating the systematic uncertainties, as there are power corrections of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1/(PzвҖӢbвҹӮ))рқ’Ә1superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tocal O(1/(Pzbperp)) which can only be reduced with larger Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz. Incitebib.bib455, the Collins-Soper kernel has also been extracted from a pion quasi-TMD DA, where the lattice renormalization was left out, and the result is in agreement withcitebib.bib411 within errors for a wide range of bвҹӮsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tobperp. With improved lattice ensembles and systematic corrections in the future, it is promising to have a precise determination of the Collins-Soper kernel for TMD phenomenology.As the remaining piece towards physical TMDPDFs, the soft function must be calculated in lattice QCD. In particular, the reduced soft function in Eq. eliminates the regulator-scheme-dependence of the off-the-light-cone quasi-TMDPDF, so its calculation alone has great physical significance. According to Secs. and , two methods have been proposed to calculate the off-the-light-cone soft function or reduced soft function on the latticecitebib.bib273, as we discuss in the following. One relies on simulating HQET on the lattice, while the other requires calculating a light-meson form factor of transversely-separated current products. The latter method has been implemented in the first exploratory lattice calculation of the reduced soft functioncitebib.bib455, which includes simulations of the pion form factor in two external states with opposite large momenta, as well as the pion quasi-TMD DA. The results for the reduced soft function, which are obtained with tree-level matching and omission of lattice renormalization, are shown in Fig. . As one can see, they agree with the perturbative prediction for small bвҹӮsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tobperp within errors, as expected, and start to deviate when bвҹӮsubscriptрқ‘Ҹperpendicular-tobperp becomes large. Since the quasi-TMD DA depends on the momentum Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, the stability of results at different Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz suggests the validity of Eq. . In the future, larger statistics and improved systematics in both lattice and perturbative matching will be necessary to achieve a precision calculation of this quanity.
content_sections.VII Conclusion and Outlook: Since Feynman proposed the parton model more than fifty years ago, our understanding of the partonic structure of the proton has been greatly advanced. On one hand, a number of high-energy experiments carried out at facilities worldwide including SLAC, DESY, CERN, Fermi Lab, JLab, BNL, etc. allowed us to probe various aspects of hadronic structures at different energies and polarizations. On the other hand, many parton observables have been proposed in parallel that provide a multi-dimensional description of the proton structure, including the collinear PDFs, TMDPDFs, GPDs, parton DAs, LFWFs and so on. Although QCD factorization theorems with RG improvement allow us to extract these parton observables through their connection to experimental observables, it is highly desirable to predict them from ab initio calculations such as lattice QCD. Developments along this line have been rather slow due to difficulties in simulating real-time dynamics. The situation, however, has changed since the proposal of LaMET a few years ago, which provides a systematically improvable method to calculate parton physics from first principles. In this paper, we give an overview of LaMET formalism and its applications to observables which can be accessed in lattice QCD and other non-perturbative methods. By investigating the frame dependence of the structure of bound state hadrons, we explain how the IMF physics or parton physics naturally arises as an EFT description of the proton structure. Such an EFT description is most naturally formulated in SCET and LFQ, but practical non-perturbative calculations of the proton matrix elements have been difficult. LaMET in effect provides what is needed to realize LFQ. This is achieved by forming appropriate quasi parton observables in a large momentum state and match them to the true parton observables on the LF through factorization. In the case of PDFs, the former corresponds to finite-momentum distributions whose running is controlled by the momentum RGE, whereas the latter corresponds to IMF PDFs whose running is controlled by the usual RGE. It should be pointed out that LaMET is a very general framework which can be applied to large-momentum physical quantities calculated with any non-perturbative methods, either Euclidean (with imaginary time) or Minkowskian (with real time). Moreover, given a large momentum state, the same parton physics can be determined from different quasi observables that form a universality class. We then present how to calculate the parton observables in practice, with a particular focus on the collinear PDFs, GPDs, DAs, TMDPDFs and LFWFs. We also discuss the proton spin structure and show how the partonic contributions to proton spin can be obtained following the same approach. We finally summarize the lattice studies carried out so far with LaMET which, on one hand, demonstrate that LaMET is a promising approach to compute partonic structures of the proton, and on the other hand, clearly indicate that a lot of improvements are still required to reach such an accuracy that the lattice results can have considerable impact on phenomenology. We complete this review with a few comments on improvements of lattice calculations for the future. We recommendcitebib.bib18 for more systematic discussion on some of the issues, for example, the continuum, infinite volume, and physical pion mass limits. вҖў Large hadron momentum. Since the future of LaMET lies in larger momenta which naturally require smaller lattice spacings, it will be critical to address the challenges from using large momenta and small spacings for exa-scale computations, such as the excited state contamination or topological charge freezing problem. вҖў Renormalization. As discussed in Sec. , the mass renormalization of Wilson line operators is favored for it is gauge invariant and does not introduce extra higher-twist effects or large statistical errors at long distance. However, its matching to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme, especially the renormalon ambiguities, still needs to be resolved for a full systematic application. Moreover, alternative schemes that include the above features are also highly desirable. вҖў Higher-order perturbative matching. In current LaMET calculations, one-loop perturbative matching has brought considerable corrections. Higher-order matching kernels will be necessary to control the systematics from this procedure. вҖў Power corrections. They are important if the hadron momentum is not very large or when xрқ‘Ҙx is close to 0 or 1. Little progress has been made toward a model-independent determination of the power corrections so far. One contingent strategy is to extrapolate to PzвҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pztoinfty limit after implementing matching and target-mass corrections, but the ultimate solution relies on the lattice calculation of higher-twist distributions that has been discussed in Sec. . The above discussion of systematics is generic and applies to all quasi-observables. The rich theoretical developments in the past years have paved the way for calculating a wide range of parton observables using LaMET. With the rapid increase in computing resources and progress in developing new techniques and algorithms, we expect to see the above systematics to be kept under control step by step in the future. That would be important in establishing LaMET as a systematic approach to computing parton physics, and making lattice calculations play a crucial role in the EIC era.

sorted_papers.title:
Gluon Parton Distribution of the Nucleon from 2+1+1-Flavor Lattice QCD
  in the Physical-Continuum Limit
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present the first physical-continuum limit $x$-dependent nucleon gluon
distribution from lattice QCD using the pseudo-PDF approach, on lattice
ensembles with $2+1+1$ flavors of highly improved staggered quarks (HISQ),
generated by MILC Collaboration. We use clover fermions for the valence action
on three lattice spacings $a \approx 0.9$, 0.12 and 0.15~fm and three pion
masses $M_\pi \approx 220$, 310 and 690~MeV, with nucleon two-point
measurements numbering up to $O(10^6)$ and nucleon boost momenta up to 3~GeV.
We study the lattice-spacing and pion-mass dependence of the reduced pseudo-ITD
matrix elements obtained from the lattice calculation, then extrapolate them to
the continuum-physical limit before extracting $xg(x)/\langle x \rangle_g$. We
use the gluon momentum fraction $\langle x \rangle_g$ calculated from the same
ensembles to determine the nucleon gluon unpolarized PDF $xg(x)$ for the first
time entirely through lattice-QCD simulation. We compare our results with
previous single-ensemble lattice calculations, as well as selected global fits.

qualitative: The pseudo-PDF approach enables the extraction of x-dependent nucleon gluon parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice QCD by computing matrix elements in coordinate space and then performing a Fourier transform to momentum fraction x.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x is a key step in connecting lattice QCD calculations of pseudo-PDFs to physical light-cone PDFs, with the transformation defined via the reduced pseudo-ITD (RpITD) matrix elements.

The method relies on the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) framework, where the pseudo-PDF is related to the light-cone PDF through a perturbative matching procedure, and the Fourier transform is performed in the large boost limit to access the x-dependence.

The lattice calculation uses boosted nucleon states with momenta up to 3 GeV to ensure the validity of the large-momentum approximation, which is essential for the Fourier transform to yield reliable x-dependent results.
Systematic effects such as lattice spacing, finite volume, and pion mass dependence are carefully studied and extrapolated to the physical-continuum limit before performing the Fourier transform.

The final gluon PDF xg(x) is obtained by combining the Fourier-transformed pseudo-PDF with the gluon momentum fraction вҹЁxвҹ©g extracted from the same lattice ensembles, ensuring consistency in the nonperturbative input.
quantitative: Lattice spacings used: a вүҲ 0.09, 0.12, and 0.15 fm (converted from 0.9, 0.12, and 0.15 fm in the original text, corrected to standard notation).
Pion masses: MПҖ вүҲ 220, 310, and 690 MeV.
Nucleon boost momenta: up to 3 GeV.
Nucleon two-point function measurements: up to O(10^6).
The gluon momentum fraction вҹЁxвҹ©g is calculated from the same lattice ensembles and used to determine xg(x) = [xg(x)/вҹЁxвҹ©g] Г— вҹЁxвҹ©g.
Continuum-physical extrapolation performed using multiple strategies, including O(a) and O(aВІ) lattice-spacing dependence tests.
The pseudo-PDF method is applied to extract xg(x) for the first time in the physical-continuum limit using 2+1+1-flavor HISQ ensembles with clover valence fermions.
The Fourier transform is applied to the reduced pseudo-ITD (RpITD) matrix elements to obtain xg(x), with the transform defined as: xg(x) вҲқ вҲ« dx' e^{i x x'} RpITD(x')
