sorted_papers.title:
Reconstructing parton distribution functions from Ioffe time data: from
  Bayesian methods to Neural Networks
sorted_papers.abstract:
The computation of the parton distribution functions (PDF) or distribution
amplitudes (DA) of hadrons from first principles lattice QCD constitutes a
central open problem. In this study, we present and evaluate the efficiency of
a selection of methods for inverse problems to reconstruct the full
$x$-dependence of PDFs. Our starting point are the so called Ioffe time PDFs,
which are accessible from Euclidean time calculations in conjunction with a
matching procedure. Using realistic mock data tests, we find that the ill-posed
incomplete Fourier transform underlying the reconstruction requires careful
regularization, for which both the Bayesian approach as well as neural networks
are efficient and flexible choices.

sections.3: Feynman, Photon-hadron Interactions, Advanced Books Classics (Avalon Publishing, 1998), ISBN 9780201360745.
sections.19: Rojo, in 13th Conference on Quark Confinement and the Hadron Spectrum (Confinement XIII) Maynooth, Ireland, July 31-August 6, 2018 (2018), 1809.04392.

qualitative: The reconstruction of parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice QCD requires inverting an ill-posed incomplete Fourier transform from Ioffe time (ОҪ) space to momentum fraction (x) space.
The Ioffe time PDF, related to the matrix element of quark fields separated in space, is accessible via Euclidean lattice calculations and serves as input for PDF reconstruction.

The Fourier transform connecting the Ioffe time PDF to the valence quark PDF is given by рқ’¬R(ОҪ,ОјВІ) = вҲ«вӮҖВ№ cos(ОҪx) qv(x,ОјВІ) dx, which is an incomplete transform over the finite interval [0,1] in x and limited in ОҪ.

Due to the limited number of accessible Ioffe time points and the incomplete domain of integration, the inverse problem is ill-posed, requiring regularization to obtain stable and reliable PDF reconstructions.
Both Bayesian methods and neural networks are effective and flexible regularization strategies for handling the ill-posed nature of the inverse Fourier transform.

The functional form of the PDF near x=0 and x=1 is physically significant: experimentally determined PDFs diverge at x=0 and vanish at x=1 (a<0, b>0), while lattice results at low scales may show opposite behavior (a>0), motivating different mock data scenarios.

The reconstruction challenge is analogous for both the MSМ„ Ioffe time PDF and the quasi-PDF formalisms, though the latter involves a different Fourier transform with a different variable (y) and domain.
quantitative: The Ioffe time PDF is related to the valence quark PDF via the quasi-Fourier transform: рқ’¬R(ОҪ,ОјВІ) = вҲ«вӮҖВ№ cos(ОҪx) qv(x,ОјВІ) dx.
The mock data PDF is modeled using the functional form: p(x) = О“(a+b+2) / [О“(a+1)О“(b+1)] В· x^a В· (1вҲ’x)^b.
Scenario A uses a<0 (divergent at x=0, vanishing at x=1), consistent with phenomenological fits at Ој>2 GeV.
Scenario B uses a>0 (vanishing at x=0), mimicking low-scale lattice results and quasi-PDF behavior in the yвҶ’0 limit.
The quasi-PDF is related to the lattice-calculated matrix element via в„іR(ОҪ, ОҪВІ/pвӮғВІ) = вҲ«вӮҖ^вҲһ cos(ОҪy) qМғv(y,pвӮғ) dy.

content_sections.1 Introduction: Parton distributions form the core of our theoretical understanding of the inner workings of hadronscitebib.bib1. They encode how the momentum and angular momentum is distributed among quarks and gluons inside a hadron. As such there exists an intense high precision experimental program devoted to their determination and it is the concurrent goal of theoretical nuclear physics to compute these quantities from first principles QCD. As they constitute genuinely non-perturbative objects, lattice QCD calculations appear as a very promising route in achieving this goal. All current methods for extracting parton distribution functions (PDF) or distribution amplitudes (DA) from lattice QCD require a Fourier transform or a quasi-Fourier transform of a certain class of hadronic position space matrix elements. These novel methods allow lattice QCD calculations to go beyond the traditional computation of the lowest momentscitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5. Let us focus on two related methods, called the pseudo-PDFcitebib.bib6 and the quasi-PDFcitebib.bib7. Both involve the hadron matrix element of space-like separated quark fields connected with a Wilson line and a Оірқӣҫgamma matrix. For simplicity, we will use the example of the flavor iso-vector unpolarized quark PDFs. In this case the relevant matrix element has the following Lorentz decomposition вҹЁhвҖӢ(p)|ПҲВҜвҖӢ(z)вҖӢП„32вҖӢОіОұвҖӢWвҖӢ(z;0)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ|hвҖӢ(p)вҹ©=pОұвҖӢв„івҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)+zОұвҖӢрқ’©вҖӢ(ОҪ,z2),quantum-operator-productв„Һрқ‘қВҜрқң“рқ‘§superscriptрқңҸ32superscriptрқӣҫрқӣјрқ‘Ҡрқ‘§0рқң“0в„Һрқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘қрқӣјв„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2superscriptрқ‘§рқӣјрқ’©рқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2 h(p)|barpsi(z)fractau32gammaalphaW(z;0)psi|h(p)=palphaM(nu,z2)+zalphaN(nu,z2),, where pрқ‘қp is an arbitrary hadron momentum, zрқ‘§z is a space-like separation, П„3superscriptрқңҸ3tau3 denotes a flavor Pauli matrix, ОіОұsuperscriptрқӣҫрқӣјgammaalpha refers to a gamma matrix acting in spin space, WвҖӢ(z;0)рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§0W(z;0) to the 0вҶ’zвҶ’0рқ‘§0to z Wilson line, and ОҪ=pвӢ…zрқңҲвӢ…рқ‘қрқ‘§nu=pcdot z represents a Lorentz invariant quantity known as the Ioffe time. Through a choice of pрқ‘қp, zрқ‘§z, and Оұрқӣјalpha one is able to isolate the term containing leading twist contributions в„івҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)в„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2M(nu,z2)citebib.bib8. Using pseudo-PDF formalism discussed incitebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, the real component of this term, в„іRвҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)subscriptв„ірқ‘…рқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2MR(nu,z2), is matched to the MвҖӢSВҜВҜрқ‘Җрқ‘ҶoverlineMS Ioffe time PDF, рқ’¬RвҖӢ(ОҪ,Ој2)subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…рқңҲsuperscriptрқңҮ2QR(nu,mu2), via a perturbative kernel. The MвҖӢSВҜВҜрқ‘Җрқ‘ҶoverlineMS Ioffe time PDF on the other hand is related to the valence quark PDF, qvвҖӢ(x,Ој2)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2qv(x,mu2), through the quasi-Fourier transformation рқ’¬RвҖӢ(ОҪ,Ој2)вүЎвҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢcosвҒЎ(ОҪвҖӢx)вҖӢqvвҖӢ(x,Ој2),subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…рқңҲsuperscriptрқңҮ2superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘ҘрқңҲрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2QR(nu,mu2)equivint01dx,cos(nu x),qv(x,mu2),, as was shown incitebib.bib8, bib.bib12. Using the quasi-PDF formalism, originally proposed incitebib.bib7, the lattice calculated в„іRвҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)subscriptв„ірқ‘…рқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2MR(nu,z2) is related to the quasi-PDF, qvвҖӢ(y,p3)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘қ3tildeqv(y,p3), via the Fourier transformation в„іRвҖӢ(ОҪ,ОҪ2p32)вүЎвҲ«0вҲһрқ‘‘yвҖӢcosвҒЎ(ОҪвҖӢy)вҖӢqvвҖӢ(y,p3). subscriptв„ірқ‘…рқңҲsuperscriptрқңҲ2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қ32superscriptsubscript0differential-dрқ‘ҰрқңҲрқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘қ3MR(nu,fracnu2p32)equivint0inftydy,cos(nu y)tildeqv(y,p3),. The quasi-PDF can then be connected to the PDF using a perturbative kernel as discussed incitebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib11. Immediately after the presentation of the basic idea a plethora of works citebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21 explored the properties of the new methodologies as well as of other approachescitebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26. Possible doubts against this approach that emanate from the need to invert the Fourier transform in Eq. were raised incitebib.bib27, bib.bib28. These claims were refuted incitebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, however as we discuss in this paper, apart from the theoretical issues raised, the numerical inversion of the Fourier transform is not straight forward. We refer the reader tocitebib.bib32, bib.bib33 for two detailed reviews of the topic. This study will be concerned with the reconstruction of the PDF from the MвҖӢSВҜВҜрқ‘Җрқ‘ҶoverlineMS Ioffe time PDF, but many of the conclusions are also applicable to the reconstruction of quasi-PDFs from the lattice calculated matrix element. In inverting Eq. to obtain qvвҖӢ(x,Ој2)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2qv(x,mu2), there exist two challenges. First being that the required integral over ОҪрқңҲnu does not extend over the full Brillouin zone. The second is that in practice only a small number of points along ОҪрқңҲnu can be computed. Only the second challenge exists for the calculation of the quasi-PDF. As we will discuss in more detail below, taken together these issues render the extraction highly ill-posed and we explore several regularization strategies on how to nevertheless reliably estimate the PDF from the data at hand. To assess the viability of different reconstruction approaches in practice and to elucidate their systematic uncertainties, we will carry out tests based on two different mock data sets. The first test scenario is based on experimentally determined PDFвҖҷs for which it has been found that a simple ansatz is able to approximate their functional form quite well pвҖӢ(x)=О“вҖӢ(a+b+2)О“вҖӢ(a+1)вҖӢО“вҖӢ(b+1)вҖӢxaвҖӢ(1вҲ’x)b. рқ‘қрқ‘ҘО“рқ‘Һрқ‘Ҹ2О“рқ‘Һ1О“рқ‘Ҹ1superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Һsuperscript1рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҹp(x)=fracGamma(a+b+2)Gamma(a+1)Gamma(b+1)xa(1-x)b,. Based on phenomenological fits the expectation is that, for scales Ој>2рқңҮ2mu>2GeV, the physical PDF shows a divergence close to x=0рқ‘Ҙ0x=0, while vanishing at x=1рқ‘Ҙ1x=1. This requires that a0рқ‘Ҹ0b>0. In mock scenario A, we insert into Eq. such experimentally determined PDFвҖҷs, which in turn tests the case a<0рқ‘Һ0a<0. On the other hand at very low scales Ој2superscriptрқңҮ2mu2, lattice results in the quenched approximation and with heavy pionscitebib.bib8 suggest that aрқ‘Һa may become positive instead. Thus for mock scenario B we deform by hand the experimental PDF data so that it goes to zero at the origin. This scenario B with its different functional form serves as a gauge of the robustness of the methods we are testing. Also, scenario B is reminiscent of the quasi-PDF case which is known to converge in the limit of yрқ‘Ұy going to zero. This article starts out with the study of the direct inversion in section 2, followed by the recapitulation of the main ideas of advanced reconstructions in section 3. In section 4 we present extensive numerical experiments of all methods employing mock data and section 5 summarizes our conclusions.
content_sections.2 Direct Inversion: While Eq. is a Fourier transform and inverting may be considered a simple task, the facts that the range of ОҪрқңҲnu extends only over a finite interval different from the full Brillouin zone and that our data for рқ’¬Rsubscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…QR are discrete, makes its inversion highly ill-posed. Let us explore this issue in more detail by naively discretizing the integral in the interval xвҲҲ[0,1]рқ‘Ҙ01xin[0,1], considering Nx+1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘Ҙ1Nx+1 points in a trapezoid integration rule. However, Eq. may be badly conditioned rendering the computation of рқ”®рқ”®mathfrakq a difficult task. As a concrete example we take an idealized situation of having in our possession NОҪ=40subscriptрқ‘ҒрқңҲ40Nnu=40 data points for рқ’¬Rsubscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…QR over the four different intervals I0=[0,10]subscriptрқҗј0010I0=[0,10], I1=[0,20]subscriptрқҗј1020I1=[0,20], I2=[0,100]subscriptрқҗј20100I2=[0,100], I3=[0,40вҖӢПҖ]subscriptрқҗј3040рқңӢI3=[0,40pi]. Interval I3subscriptрқҗј3I3 is special in the sense that with a redefinition of xрқ‘Ҙx one may rewrite Eq. in the form of a genuine discrete Fourier transform, which we know is well conditioned. And indeed as shown in Fig. , for ОҪвҲҲI3рқңҲsubscriptрқҗј3nuin I3 the eigenvalues О»ksubscriptрқңҶрқ‘ҳlambdak of the matrix в„ӯв„ӯmathfrakC are all of the same order (рқ’ӘвҖӢ(0.1)рқ’Ә0.1cal O(0.1)). Once the resolved ОҪрқңҲnu region is shrunk below the full Brillouin zone, the cosinecosinerm cosine functions no longer constitute linearly independent basis functions and the columns of в„ӯв„ӯmathfrakC become linearly dependent, which manifest itself in eigenvalues that are exactly zero and eigenvalues that are exponentially suppressed. The exponential decay of the eigenvalues tells us that the inversion problem is ill-conditioned and that a direct inversion will become impractical once the ОҪрқңҲnu range is significantly smaller than the full Brillouin zone. To make this explicit we carry out a direct inversion of mock data. For this illustration we take Eq. with parameters a=вҲ’14рқ‘Һ14a=-frac14 and b=3рқ‘Ҹ3b=3. Ideal data for рқ’¬Rsubscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…QR are obtained based on the three different discretization intervals I1,I2subscriptрқҗј1subscriptрқҗј2I1,I2 and I3subscriptрқҗј3I3. The ideal data are then distorted by Gaussian noise corresponding to constant and uncorrelated relative errors on the averaged data of О”вҖӢрқ’¬R/рқ’¬R=const. О”subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…constDeltaQR/QR=rm const. The matrix inverse is computed via a singular value decomposition, where only singular values which are larger than 10вҲ’4superscript10410-4 are inverted. In Fig. the results of the direct inversion are shown, with the ideal data results depicted by red circles and the original qвҖӢ(x)рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙq(x) as gray dashed line. The leftmost panel corresponds to the well conditioned case of I3subscriptрқҗј3I3, where the reconstruction based on ideal data works flawlessly and even produces accurate results already for a relative large error on the input data of О”вҖӢрқ’¬R/рқ’¬R=10вҲ’2О”subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…superscript102DeltaQR/QR=10-2. One exception is the point at x=0рқ‘Ҙ0x=0, which formally would have to diverge. The shorter the ОҪрқңҲnu interval becomes the worse the reconstruction results, where even for ideal data having an error on the level of standard machine precision we obtain artificial fluctuations of the reconstructed PDF. At the same time, significantly smaller relative errors on the input data is required for a reasonably stable reconstruction to ensue. For ОҪ=[0,100]рқңҲ0100nu=[0,100] we already need О”вҖӢрқ’¬R/рқ’¬R=10вҲ’5О”subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…superscript105DeltaQR/QR=10-5, while for ОҪ=[0,20]рқңҲ020nu=[0,20] even the О”вҖӢрқ’¬R/рқ’¬R=10вҲ’6О”subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…superscript106DeltaQR/QR=10-6 result only gives an approximate PDF with significant unphysical fluctuations. One may attempt to improve the results of the direct inversion by considering a higher order integration scheme. At large values of ОҪрқңҲnu, the integrand is a highly oscillatory function due to the presence of cosвҒЎ(ОҪвҖӢx)рқңҲрқ‘Ҙcos(nu x). As a result, an integration algorithm that approximates the integrand by a low degree polynomial is bound to fail at large values of ОҪрқңҲnu. We can improve on this by designing a better integration rule that performs similarly for all values of ОҪрқңҲnu. We know that the oscillatory nature of the integrand is due to the cosвҒЎ(ОҪвҖӢx)рқңҲрқ‘Ҙcos(nu x) term and that the unknown function qvвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘Ҙqv(x) is slowly varying in the interval [0,1]01[0,1]. Therefore we will approximate qvвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘Ҙqv(x) with a linear interpolation and perform the integral exactly leaving the result to linearly depend on the unknown values of the function qvвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘Ҙqv(x) on the grid points xksubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳxk. It turns out that the relative integration error for typical functions qvвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘Ҙqv(x) with 10 interpolating points continuously grows with ОҪрқңҲnu for the trapezoid rule and reaches 100% at ОҪ=15рқңҲ15nu=15. On the other hand the improved integration, which performs similarly with the trapezoid rule at small ОҪрқңҲnu, at ОҪ=15рқңҲ15nu=15 has 65% relative error which remains almost unchanged up to ОҪ=100рқңҲ100nu=100. The improved integration achieves a constant relative error as a function of ОҪрқңҲnu at large ОҪрқңҲnu for any number of interpolating points. On the other hand the trapezoid rule has a relative error that always increases with ОҪрқңҲnu for any number of interpolating points. Therefore, this improved integration scheme achieves its design goal of having a constant integration error for all values of ОҪрқңҲnu. We could further improve the integration by using a second order interpolation formula therefore producing a generalized SimpsonвҖҷs rule. Perhaps this is needed because the number of points we have is very limited. However, it may be that the largest systematic in our problem is the truncation at relatively small values of ОҪрқңҲnu and further improvement of the integration scheme will not affect this systematic. The proposed integration scheme can prove valuable because it can significantly reduce the number of points required to discretize the integral resulting a smaller maximum value of ОҪрқңҲnu for which the problem is no longer ill-defined. It has been proposed in citebib.bib34, that the unphysical oscillations in the related quasi-PDF inverse problem can be controlled by considering the derivative of the integral equation with respect to ОҪрқңҲnu or z3subscriptрқ‘§3z3. Even if the derivative method results in a smoother curve, it does not alleviate the ill-posed nature of the problem. Using the same intervals of ОҪрқңҲnu and x mentioned above, one can find that the eigenvalues of this matrix, shown in Fig. , have the same pattern of exponential decay which characterizes an ill-conditioned inverse problem. Our numerical experiments indicate that for realistic scenarios encountered in lattice studies, for which we foresee ОҪвҲҲ[0,20]рқңҲ020nuin[0,20] and О”вҖӢрқ’¬R/рқ’¬R=10вҲ’2вҲ’10вҲ’3О”subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…superscript102superscript103DeltaQR/QR=10-2-10-3, a direct inversion is not feasible and more sophisticated regularization schemes of the ill-defined inversion problem are needed. In particular we have observed that the truncation in ОҪрқңҲnu, results in a reconstructed PDF with unphysical fluctuations. This is an effect similar to that observed incitebib.bib34. In addition we also see that any non-analytic behavior of qвҖӢ(x)рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙq(x), e. g. a divergence at x=0рқ‘Ҙ0x=0 will pose a difficulty for (direct) inversion methods and needs to be considered with care. Therefore in the main part of this paper we will explore several modern methods for treating inverse problems and compare their efficiency in dealing with the uncertainties introduced by the truncation of the integration regime.
content_sections.3 Advanced PDF reconstructions: The fundamental difficulty of solving an ill-posed inverse problem lies in the fact that the input data by themselves do not single out a unique answer. To give meaning to the PDF extraction the inversion needs to be regularized, i. e. we need to provide criteria on how to choose a single PDF from an infinite number of possible solutions reproducing the discrete and noisy input. NaГҜve methods, such as the direct inversion and the derivative method, introduce a regularization by removing small singular values of the kernel or by setting data to zero at large distances. However, these approaches introduce uncontrolled systematic errors. A straightforward example of a stable solution to the inverse problem, which was used incitebib.bib8, bib.bib12, is to parametrize the solution with a functional form, such as, containing a small number of parameters. One can then use this functional form to fit the data using a ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 minimization. This approach typically results in smaller statistical errors, however, it introduces a model systematic that one may control by varying the functional form used in the model. As the flexibility of the parametrization increases the model systematic is reduced and the ill-posed nature of the inverse problem becomes more apparent. More sophisticated methods often use some additional prior information to constrain the results in order to improve the robustness of the result and provide controlled systematic error estimates. In the following, we discuss three extraction methods that do not presuppose a functional form of the encoded PDFs or have a very flexible functional form parametrizing the solution. Each of these methods regulates the inverse problem in different ways. The first, the Backus-Gilbert method, regulates the problem by minimizing the statistical variance of the solution. The second, a neural network parametrization, that provides a flexible parametrization given by specific choices of network geometry and activation functions. The third class of methods, is based on Bayesian inference, that rely on Bayes theorem to systematically incorporate prior information, such as e. g. positivity or smoothness of the solution. In addition, the Bayesian inference methods introduce a default model, which represent the prior information in that this model is the correct solution in the absence of any data. Non Bayesian approaches, while not explicitly mentioning prior information often incorporate additional knowledge about the problem at hand in an indirect fashion. One such way is to apply a preconditioning to the inverse problem, in case that a relatively good guess of the form of the solution is available. In a Bayesian method such information would instead be supplied in the form of a default model. Let us discuss the preconditioning, which we will employ in order to improve the results of the Backus-Gilbert and neural network methods. The main ingredient is to define a rescaled kernel and rescaled PDF hвҖӢ(x)в„Һрқ‘Ҙh(x) KjвҖӢ(x)вүЎcвҖӢoвҖӢsвҖӢ(ОҪjвҖӢx)вҖӢpвҖӢ(x)вҖӢand,hвҖӢ(x)вүЎqvвҖӢ(x)pвҖӢ(x),formulae-sequencesubscriptрқҗҫрқ‘—рқ‘Ҙрқ‘җрқ‘ңрқ‘ subscriptрқңҲрқ‘—рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ҙandв„Һрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Јрқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘ҘKj(x)equiv cos(nujx)p(x);;rm and,,;h(x)equivfracqv(x)p(x),, where pвҖӢ(x)рқ‘қрқ‘Ҙp(x) corresponds to an appropriately chosen function that makes the problem easier to solve. In particular, we wish to incorporate into pвҖӢ(x)рқ‘қрқ‘Ҙp(x) most of the non-trivial structure of qвҖӢ(x)рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙq(x), such that hвҖӢ(x)в„Һрқ‘Ҙh(x) is a slowly varying function of xрқ‘Ҙx and contains only the deviation of qвҖӢ(x)рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙq(x) from pвҖӢ(x)рқ‘қрқ‘Ҙp(x). As discussed in the introduction, it has been found that the Ansatz Eq. describes phenomenological PDFs rather well. Integrated over the cosine, it yields the function рқ’¬pвҖӢ(ОҪ,a,b)subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘қрқңҲрқ‘Һрқ‘ҸdisplaystyleQp(nu,a,b) =ПҖвҖӢ2вҲ’aвҲ’bвҲ’1вҖӢО“вҖӢ(a+1)вҖӢО“вҖӢ(b+1)вҖӢF32вҖӢ(a+12,a+22;12,12вҖӢ(a+b+2),12вҖӢ(a+b+3);вҲ’ОҪ24)absentрқңӢsuperscript2рқ‘Һрқ‘Ҹ1О“рқ‘Һ1О“рқ‘Ҹ1subscriptsubscriptрқҗ№32рқ‘Һ12рқ‘Һ221212рқ‘Һрқ‘Ҹ212рқ‘Һрқ‘Ҹ3superscriptрқңҲ24displaystyle!=!!pi 2-a-b-1Gamma(a+1)Gamma(b+1)2tildeF3!left(fraca+12,fraca+22;frac12,frac12(a+b+2),frac12(a+b+3);-fracnu24right) where F32subscriptsubscriptрқҗ№322tildeF3 is the generalized hypergeometric function. One can find a good choice for the parameters aрқ‘Һa and bрқ‘Ҹb by first fitting the Ioffe-time PDF data with Eq. . The choice of preconditioning function or default model will influence the result of the reconstruction and it is therefore necessary to explore the stability of the end result on that choice.
content_sections.3 Advanced PDF reconstructions 3.1 Backu: One approach to inverse problems, which has been used in a number of engineering and physics applications, is the Backus-Gilbert methodcitebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38. Like many approaches to the inverse problem, the Backus-Gilbert method provides a unique solution to the ill posed problem given some condition. This method differs from other regularizations, which impose a smoothness criterion on the resulting function qвҖӢ(x)рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙq(x), by imposing a minimization condition of the variance of the resulting function. The Backus-Gilbert method has been studied previously incitebib.bib39 as a solution to an inverse problem for extracting PDFs from other types of lattice calculated hadronic matrix elements. Let us start from the preconditioned expression with a rescaled PDF hвҖӢ(x)в„Һрқ‘Ҙh(x) that is only a slowly varying function of xрқ‘Ҙx. Hence our inverse problem becomes djвүЎрқ’¬RвҖӢ(ОҪj)=вҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢKjвҖӢ(x)вҖӢhвҖӢ(x). subscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘—subscriptрқ’¬рқ‘…subscriptрқңҲрқ‘—superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқҗҫрқ‘—рқ‘Ҙв„Һрқ‘ҘdjequivQR(nuj)=int01dxKj(x)h(x),. The Backus-Gilbert method introduces a function О”вҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)О”рқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘ҘDelta(x-barx) that is written as О”вҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)=вҲ‘jajвҖӢ(xВҜ)вҖӢKjвҖӢ(x),О”рқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘—ВҜрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқҗҫрқ‘—рқ‘ҘDelta(x-barx)=sumjaj(barx)Kj(x),, where ajвҖӢ(xВҜ)subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘—ВҜрқ‘Ҙaj(barx) are unknown functions to be determined. It then estimates the unknown function as a linear combination of the data, hвҖӢ(xВҜ)=вҲ‘jajвҖӢ(xВҜ)вҖӢdj,orqvвҖӢ(xВҜ)=вҲ‘jajвҖӢ(xВҜ)вҖӢdjвҖӢpвҖӢ(xВҜ). formulae-sequenceв„ҺВҜрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘—ВҜрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘—orsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘ЈВҜрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘—subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘—ВҜрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘—рқ‘қВҜрқ‘Ҙhath(barx)=sumjaj(barx)dj,,quadrm orquad;hatqv(barx)=sumjaj(barx)djp(barx),. Given the above definitions, if О”вҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)О”рқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘ҘDelta(x-barx) were to be a Dirac Оҙрқӣҝdelta function then hвҖӢ(xВҜ)в„ҺВҜрқ‘Ҙhath(barx) would be equal to hвҖӢ(xВҜ)в„ҺВҜрқ‘Ҙh(barx). If О”вҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)О”рқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘ҘDelta(x-barx) approximates a Оҙрқӣҝdelta-function with a width ПғрқңҺsigma, then the smaller ПғрқңҺsigma is, the better the approximation of hвҖӢ(x)в„Һрқ‘Ҙhath(x) to hвҖӢ(x)в„Һрқ‘Ҙh(x). In other words, if hПғвҖӢ(x)subscriptв„ҺрқңҺрқ‘Ҙhathsigma(x) is the approximation resulting from О”вҖӢ(x)О”рқ‘ҘDelta(x) with a width ПғрқңҺsigma then limПғвҶ’0hПғвҖӢ(x)=hвҖӢ(x). subscriptвҶ’рқңҺ0subscriptв„ҺрқңҺрқ‘Ҙв„Һрқ‘Ҙlimsigmarightarrow 0hathsigma(x)=h(x),. With this in mind the Backus-Gilbert method minimizes the width ПғрқңҺsigma given by Пғ=вҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)2вҖӢО”вҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)2. рқңҺsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘Ҙ2О”superscriptрқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘Ҙ2sigma=int01dx(x-barx)2Delta(x-barx)2,. Note that other choices for the definition of the width can be used. This choice makes the resulting integrals easy to compute and the minimization problem becomes quadratic in the unknown values ajsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘—aj. Furthermore, if О”вҖӢ(x)О”рқ‘ҘDelta(x) is to approximate a Оҙрқӣҝdelta-function then one has to impose the constraint вҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢО”вҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)=1. superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘ҘО”рқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘Ҙ1int01dx,Delta(x-barx)=1,. Using a Lagrange multiplier О»рқңҶlambda one can minimize the width and impose the constraint by minimizing ПҮвҖӢ[a]=вҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢ(xвҲ’xВҜ)2вҖӢвҲ‘j,kajвҖӢ(xВҜ)вҖӢKjвҖӢ(x)вҖӢKkвҖӢ(x)вҖӢakвҖӢ(xВҜ)+О»вҖӢвҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢвҲ‘jKjвҖӢ(x)вҖӢajвҖӢ(xВҜ). рқң’delimited-рқ‘Һsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘Ҙ2subscriptрқ‘—рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘—ВҜрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқҗҫрқ‘—рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқҗҫрқ‘ҳрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ҳВҜрқ‘ҘрқңҶsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘—subscriptрқҗҫрқ‘—рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘—ВҜрқ‘Ҙchi[a]=int01dx(x-barx)2sumj,kaj(barx)Kj(x)Kk(x)ak(barx)+lambdaint01dxsumjKj(x)aj(barx),.
