We will discuss more on anomalous dimensions in Appendix . Given all above, it is much more convenient to redefine the hard kernel to be purely real, while assign the phase to the jet function: HвҖӢ(Lz)вүЎ|H|HLвҖӢ(Lz),рқҗ»subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§subscriptрқҗ»HLsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§displaystyle H(Lz)equiv|H|rm HLleft(Lzright) , JfвҖӢ(zвҖӢОј)вүЎJвҖӢ(z2вҖӢОј2)вҖӢexpвҒЎ[iвҖӢsignвҖӢ(z)вҖӢAвҖӢ(Lz)],subscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“рқ‘§рқңҮрқҗҪsuperscriptрқ‘§2superscriptрқңҮ2рқ‘–signрқ‘§рқҗҙsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§displaystyle Jf(zmu)equivtildeJ(z2mu2)expbigg[irm sign(z)A(Lz)bigg] , where we define the phase angle as signвҖӢ(z)вҖӢAвҖӢ(Lz)=ArgвҖӢ(HHLвҖӢ(О¶zОј2,signвҖӢ(z))). signрқ‘§рқҗҙsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§Argsubscriptрқҗ»HLsubscriptрқңҒрқ‘§superscriptрқңҮ2signрқ‘§displaystylerm sign(z)A(Lz)=rm Argbigg(Hrm HLleft(fraczetazmu2,rm sign(z)right)bigg). Notice that the phase angle is proportional to signвҖӢ(z)signрқ‘§rm sign(z) to all orders. After this redefinition, one can see that the real part of JfsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“Jf at one-loop remains the same as JрқҗҪtildeJ, while the imaginary part becomes ImвҖӢJfвҖӢ(zвҖӢОј)=ОұsвҖӢCF2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(LzвҲ’1)вҖӢiвҖӢПҖвҖӢsignвҖӢ(z). ImsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“рқ‘§рқңҮsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқңӢsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§1рқ‘–рқңӢsignрқ‘§displaystylerm ImJf(zmu)=fracalphasCF2pi(Lz-1)ipirm sign(z) . After Fourier transform, one simply has в„ұвҖӢ(iвҖӢПҖвҖӢsignвҖӢ(О»))вҖӢ(1вҲ’y)=рқ’«вҖӢ(11вҲ’y). в„ұрқ‘–рқңӢsignрқңҶ1рқ‘Ұрқ’«11рқ‘Ұdisplaystylecal Fleft(ipirm sign(lambda)right)(1-y)=cal Pbigg(frac11-ybigg) . Adding this to Eq. , one has the final NLO jet function pzвҖӢJfвҖӢ((1вҲ’y)вҖӢpzОј,4вҖӢpz2Ој2)=ОҙвҖӢ(1вҲ’y)вҖӢ[1+ОұsвҖӢCF2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(2+ПҖ24+Lz22вҲ’Lz)]superscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§subscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§рқңҮ4superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§2superscriptрқңҮ2рқӣҝ1рқ‘Ұdelimited-1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқңӢ2superscriptрқңӢ24superscriptsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§22subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§displaystyle pzJfleft(frac(1-y)pzmu,frac4pz2mu2right)=delta(1-y)bigg[1+fracalphasCF2pileft(2+fracpi24+fracLz22-Lzright)bigg] +ОұsвҖӢCF2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ[рқ’«вҖӢ(ln(1вҲ’y)2|1вҲ’y|)+(LzвҲ’1)вҖӢ(рқ’«вҖӢ(11вҲ’y)+рқ’«вҖӢ(1|1вҲ’y|))],displaystyle+fracalphasCF2pibigg[cal Pleft(fracln(1-y)2|1-y|right)+(Lz-1)bigg(cal Pleft(frac11-yright)+cal Pleft(frac1|1-y|right)bigg)bigg] , and the NLO hard kernel HвҖӢ(Lz)=1+ОұsвҖӢCF2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(вҲ’2+ПҖ212вҲ’Lz22+Lz). рқҗ»subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқңӢ2superscriptрқңӢ212superscriptsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§22subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§displaystyle H(Lz)=1+fracalphasCF2pibigg(-2+fracpi212-fracLz22+Lzbigg) . It is easy to check that the above hard kernel together with the jet function JfsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“Jf completely reproduces the one-loop quasi-PDF in MвҖӢSВҜВҜMрқ‘ҶoverlinetextMS schemecitebib.bib66, bib.bib95 at power (1вҲ’y)вҲ’1superscript1рқ‘Ұ1(1-y)-1 through the factorization formula Eq. , when the plus-functions are converted to the principle value using the rules in Appendix . Moreover, the hard kernel completely agrees with the hard kernel for quasi-TMDPDF/quasi-LFWF factorizationcitebib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib77.
content_sections.5 Threshold limit at NNLO: explicit resu: In this section, armed with the factorization formalism Eq. and all the ingredients defined in previous sections, we investigate the threshold factorization at NNLO to demonstrate the correctness of our formalism. Since our jet function agrees with the analytic continuation from the time-like version in Ref. citebib.bib70, we can completely predict the threshold limit of the MвҖӢSВҜВҜMрқ‘ҶoverlinetextMS matching kernel at power (1вҲ’y)вҲ’1superscript1рқ‘Ұ1(1-y)-1 up to two unknown constants casubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Һca and cHsubscriptрқ‘җрқҗ»cH. We show that our prediction completely agrees with the threshold limit extracted directly form the full two-loop matching kernelcitebib.bib68, bib.bib69. Furthermore, it allows us to extract the two unknown constants casubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Һca and cHsubscriptрқ‘җрқҗ»cH and completely determines the heavy-light Sudakov hard kernel at NNLO that also appears in the quasi-TMDPDF/TMDWF factorization.

Here we present the results of the quark quasi-PDF in the threshold limit, predicted by our factorization formula Eq. . Our notation are H=1+ОұsвҖӢH+Оұs2вҖӢHрқҗ»1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ superscriptрқҗ»1superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2superscriptрқҗ»2H=1+alphasH+alphas2H, J=1+ОұsвҖӢJ+Оұs2вҖӢJрқҗҪ1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ superscriptрқҗҪ1superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2superscriptрқҗҪ2tildeJ=1+alphastildeJ+alphas2tildeJ, Jf=1+ОұsвҖӢJf+Оұs2вҖӢJfsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ superscriptsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“1superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2superscriptsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“2Jf=1+alphasJf+alphas2Jf, Оі=ОівҖӢОұs+ОівҖӢОұs2рқӣҫsuperscriptрқӣҫ1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ superscriptрқӣҫ2superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2gamma=gammaalphas+gammaalphas2 and ArgвҖӢ(HHLвҖӢ(О¶zОј2,signвҖӢ(z)))=signвҖӢ(z)вҖӢA=signвҖӢ(z)вҖӢAвҖӢ(Lz)вҖӢОұs+signвҖӢ(z)вҖӢAвҖӢ(Lz)вҖӢОұs2Argsubscriptрқҗ»HLsubscriptрқңҒрқ‘§superscriptрқңҮ2signрқ‘§signрқ‘§рқҗҙsignрқ‘§superscriptрқҗҙ1subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ signрқ‘§superscriptрқҗҙ2subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2rm Argbigg(Hrm HLleft(fraczetazmu2,rm sign(z)right)bigg)=rm sign(z)A=rm sign(z)A(Lz)alphas+rm sign(z)A(Lz)alphas2. We first consider the space-like jet function. Using the RGE Eq. of the jet function, one has lnвҒЎJ=CFвҖӢОІ024вҖӢПҖвҖӢlz3+14вҖӢ(О“cusp+CFвҖӢОІ02вҖӢПҖ)вҖӢlz2+(CFвҖӢОІ04вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(2+ПҖ212)вҲ’ОіJ2)вҖӢlz+c1. superscriptрқҗҪ2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣҪ024рқңӢsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘§314subscriptsuperscriptО“2cuspsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣҪ02рқңӢsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘§2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣҪ04рқңӢ2superscriptрқңӢ212superscriptsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗҪ22subscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘җ1displaystylelntildeJ=fracCFbeta024pilz3+frac14left(Gammarm cusp+fracCFbeta02piright)lz2+bigg(fracCFbeta04pileft(2+fracpi212right)-fractildegammaJ2bigg)lz+c1 . Here О“cuspsuperscriptsubscriptО“cusp2Gammarm cusp is given in Eq. , and ОіJsuperscriptsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗҪ2tildegammaJ is given in Eq. . As we show in Appendix, the constant term c1subscriptрқ‘җ1c1 is the same as that in the time-like jet functioncitebib.bib70 c1=2вҖӢОІ0вҖӢCFПҖвҖӢ(281216+5вҖӢПҖ2576вҲ’О¶348)+CFвҖӢCAПҖ2вҖӢ(вҲ’1154+7вҖӢПҖ2144вҲ’17вҖӢПҖ42880вҲ’5вҖӢО¶38). subscriptрқ‘җ12subscriptрқӣҪ0subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№рқңӢ2812165superscriptрқңӢ2576subscriptрқңҒ348subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗҙsuperscriptрқңӢ211547superscriptрқңӢ214417superscriptрқңӢ428805subscriptрқңҒ38displaystyle c1=frac2beta0CFpibigg(frac281216+frac5pi2576-fraczeta348bigg)+fracCFCApi2bigg(-frac1154+frac7pi2144-frac17pi42880-frac5zeta38bigg) . Notice lzsubscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘§lz is defined in Eq. , and our convention for ОІ0subscriptрқӣҪ0beta0 is dвҖӢОұdвҖӢlnвҒЎОј=ОІ=вҲ’ОІ0вҖӢОұ2вҲ’ОІ1вҖӢОұ3+вҖҰ,ОІ0=11вҖӢCA6вҖӢПҖвҲ’nf3вҖӢПҖ. formulae-sequenceрқ‘‘рқӣјрқ‘‘рқңҮрқӣҪsubscriptрқӣҪ0superscriptрқӣј2subscriptрқӣҪ1superscriptрқӣј3вҖҰsubscriptрқӣҪ011subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗҙ6рқңӢsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“3рқңӢdisplaystylefracdalphadlnmu=beta=-beta0alpha2-beta1alpha3+. . . , beta0=frac11CA6pi-fracnf3pi . One also needs the two-loop value of the imaginary part AвҖӢ(Lz)=ПҖ2вҖӢ[О“cuspвҖӢLzвҲ’CFвҖӢОІ02вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(Lz22вҲ’Lz)+ca],superscriptрқҗҙ2subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§рқңӢ2delimited-subscriptsuperscriptО“2cuspsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣҪ02рқңӢsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§22subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Һdisplaystyle A(Lz)=fracpi2bigg[Gammarm cuspLz-fracCFbeta02pileft(fracLz22-Lzright)+cabigg] , where Lzsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§Lz is defined in Eq. . Combined together, one has Jf=12вҖӢ(Jf)2+lnвҒЎJ+iвҖӢsignвҖӢ(z)вҖӢAвҖӢ(Lz),superscriptsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“212superscriptsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“12superscriptрқҗҪ2рқ‘–signрқ‘§superscriptрқҗҙ2subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§displaystyle Jf=frac12(Jf)2+lntildeJ+irm sign(z)A(Lz) , Jf=CF2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(lz22+lz+ПҖ212+2)+iвҖӢsignвҖӢ(z)вҖӢAвҖӢ(Lz),superscriptsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“1subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқңӢsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘§22subscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘§superscriptрқңӢ2122рқ‘–signрқ‘§superscriptрқҗҙ1subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§displaystyle Jf=fracCF2pileft(fraclz22+lz+fracpi212+2right)+irm sign(z)A(Lz) , where AвҖӢ(Lz)=CF2вҖӢ(LzвҲ’1)superscriptрқҗҙ1subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§1A(Lz)=fracCF2(Lz-1) is given in Eq. . Finally, one also has the two-loop hard kernel lnвҒЎH=CFвҖӢОІ024вҖӢПҖвҖӢLz3вҲ’14вҖӢ(О“cusp+CFвҖӢОІ02вҖӢПҖ)вҖӢLz2вҲ’(CFвҖӢОІ04вҖӢПҖвҖӢ(вҲ’2+ПҖ212)+ОіH2)вҖӢLz+cH. superscriptрқҗ»2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣҪ024рқңӢsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§314subscriptsuperscriptО“2cuspsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣҪ02рқңӢsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣҪ04рқңӢ2superscriptрқңӢ212superscriptsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗ»22subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘җрқҗ»displaystyleln H=fracCFbeta024piLz3-frac14left(Gammarm cusp+fracCFbeta02piright)Lz2-bigg(fracCFbeta04pileft(-2+fracpi212right)+fractildegammaH2bigg)Lz+cH . The explicit expression for ОіHsuperscriptsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗ»2tildegammaH is given in Eq. . Given all the above, our two-loop prediction for the quasi-PDF in the threshold limit reads in coordinate space as fвҖӢ(z,Lz)вҶ’eвҲ’iвҖӢzвҖӢPzвҖӢfвҖӢ(lz,signвҖӢ(z),Lz),вҶ’superscriptрқ‘“2рқ‘§subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘“2subscriptрқ‘ҷрқ‘§signрқ‘§subscriptрқҗҝрқ‘§displaystyletildef(z,Lz)rightarrow e-izPzf(lz,rm sign(z),Lz) , where one has f=Jf+lnвҒЎH+HвҖӢJf+12вҖӢ(H)2. superscriptрқ‘“2superscriptsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“2superscriptрқҗ»2superscriptрқҗ»1superscriptsubscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“112superscriptsuperscriptрқҗ»12displaystyle f=Jf+ln H+HJf+frac12(H)2 . When Fourier-transforming (the same convention as Eq. ) it into momentum space, one obtains all the singular terms ОҙвҖӢ(1вҲ’y)рқӣҝ1рқ‘Ұdelta(1-y), 1yвҲ’11рқ‘Ұ1frac1y-1, lnвҒЎ(yвҲ’1)yвҲ’1рқ‘Ұ1рқ‘Ұ1fracln(y-1)y-1, ln2вҒЎ(yвҲ’1)yвҲ’1superscript2рқ‘Ұ1рқ‘Ұ1fracln2(y-1)y-1, ln3вҒЎ(yвҲ’1)yвҲ’1superscript3рқ‘Ұ1рқ‘Ұ1fracln3(y-1)y-1. There are 41 non-vanishing terms, all of them are consistent with the exact results. Detailed expressions are presented in Appendix . In particular, this confirms the correctness of our ОіJsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗҪtildegammaJ and ОіHsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗ»tildegammaH.

Through comparing our prediction for the 11вҲ’y11рқ‘Ұfrac11-y term based on threshold factorization in either y>1рқ‘Ұ1y>1 or y<1рқ‘Ұ1y<1 region with the exact calculationcitebib.bib68, bib.bib69, one confirms the correctness of ОіJsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗҪtildegammaJ, and extracts casubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Һca as ca=(вҲ’3вҖӢО¶3ПҖ2+712вҲ’12вҖӢПҖ2)вҖӢCF2+(11вҖӢО¶34вҖӢПҖ2вҲ’1124вҲ’475108вҖӢПҖ2)вҖӢCFвҖӢCA+(16+3827вҖӢПҖ2)вҖӢCFвҖӢnfвҖӢTFsubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Һ3subscriptрқңҒ3superscriptрқңӢ271212superscriptрқңӢ2superscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№211subscriptрқңҒ34superscriptрқңӢ21124475108superscriptрқңӢ2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗҙ163827superscriptрқңӢ2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Үрқҗ№displaystyle ca=left(-frac3zeta3pi2+frac712-frac12pi2right)CF2+left(frac11zeta34pi2-frac1124-frac475108pi2right)CFCA+left(frac16+frac3827pi2right)CFnfTF Through comparing the ОҙвҖӢ(1вҲ’y)рқӣҝ1рқ‘Ұdelta(1-y) term based on threshold factorization with the exact calculationcitebib.bib68, bib.bib69, one confirms the correctness of all the anomalous dimensions, and obtains the constant term in the two loop hard kernel lnвҒЎHsuperscriptрқҗ»2ln H as cH=subscriptрқ‘җрқҗ»absentdisplaystyle cH= (241вҖӢО¶3144вҖӢПҖ2+11вҖӢПҖ2320вҲ’5591728вҲ’971324вҖӢПҖ2)вҖӢCFвҖӢCA+(вҲ’45вҖӢО¶3вҲ’2вҖӢПҖ4+30вҖӢПҖ2вҲ’3024вҖӢПҖ2)вҖӢCF2241subscriptрқңҒ3144superscriptрқңӢ211superscriptрқңӢ23205591728971324superscriptрқңӢ2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗҙ45subscriptрқңҒ32superscriptрқңӢ430superscriptрқңӢ23024superscriptрқңӢ2superscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2displaystylebigg(frac241zeta3144pi2+frac11pi2320-frac5591728-frac971324pi2bigg)CFCA+bigg(frac-45zeta3-2pi4+30pi2-3024pi2bigg)CF2 +displaystyle+ (36вҖӢО¶3+51вҖӢПҖ2+13121296вҖӢПҖ2)вҖӢCFвҖӢnfвҖӢTF.36subscriptрқңҒ351superscriptрқңӢ213121296superscriptрқңӢ2subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Үрқҗ№displaystylebigg(frac36zeta3+51pi2+13121296pi2bigg)CFnfTF . Numerically, one has cH=0.0725вҖӢCF2вҲ’0.0840вҖӢCFвҖӢCA+0.1453вҖӢCFвҖӢnfвҖӢTF. subscriptрқ‘җрқҗ»0.0725superscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№20.0840subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗҙ0.1453subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“subscriptрқ‘Үрқҗ№displaystyle cH=0.0725,CF2-0.0840,CFCA+0.1453,CFnfTF . Details of the extraction of cHsubscriptрқ‘җрқҗ»cH are presented in Appendix and . Combining with casubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Һca, the above completely determines the heavy-light Sudakov form factor at two-loop, a universal object that also appears in TMD factorization for quasi-TMDPDFs and quasi-LFWFs.
content_sections.6 Evolution equations and RGE resummatio: To summarize, the perturbative quark quasi-PDF for an incoming collinear quark with momentum pрқ‘қp, in the threshold limit reads fвҖӢ(y,О¶zОј)=HвҖӢ(4вҖӢpz2Ој2)вҖӢpzвҖӢJfвҖӢ((1вҲ’y)вҖӢpzОј,4вҖӢpz2Ој2)вҖӢ(1+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1вҲ’y)),рқ‘“рқ‘ҰsubscriptрқңҒрқ‘§рқңҮрқҗ»4superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§2superscriptрқңҮ2superscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§subscriptрқҗҪрқ‘“1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§рқңҮ4superscriptsubscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§2superscriptрқңҮ21рқ’Ә1рқ‘Ұdisplaystyletildefleft(y,fraczetazmuright)=Hleft(frac4pz2mu2right)pzJfleft(frac(1-y)pzmu,frac4pz2mu2right)left(1+cal O(1-y)right) , to all orders in perturbation theory. The matching kernelcitebib.bib42, bib.bib66, bib.bib95, in the Оҫ=yxвҶ’1рқңүрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҘвҶ’1xi=fracyxrightarrow 1 limit can then be read directly by the substitution yвҶ’ОҫвҶ’рқ‘Ұрқңүyrightarrowxi and pzвҶ’|xвҖӢPz|вҶ’superscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§pzrightarrow|xPz|, leading to Eq. . Clearly, after using the RGE, the scale in the jet function, Ојi=|1вҲ’Оҫ|вҖӢ|xвҖӢPz|subscriptрқңҮрқ‘–1рқңүрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§mui=|1-xi||xPz| will be re-summed, leading to all the threshold logarithms. We now collect all the RGE and show that after using the relations of the anomalous dimensions, the matching kernel matches precisely with the threshold limit of the DGLAP kernel. This allows us to use the formalism in Ref. citebib.bib31 to obtain a fully RGE re-summed form of the matching kernel in momentum space, facilitating further applications to lattice calculation.

The formula is valid up to рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1вҲ’Оҫ)рқ’Ә1рқңүcal O(1-xi) corrections.
content_sections.7 Summary and outlook: In this paper we have presented all the necessary results on the threshold limit of quark quasi-PDF. We have shown that in the threshold limit, the quasi-PDF factorizes through a space-like jet function and a pair of heavy-light Sudakov form factors. By comparing the prediction based on our factorization formula with the exact calculation in Ref. citebib.bib68, bib.bib69, we are able to confirm the correctness of our formalism explicitly up to NNLO, and extract the universal heavy-light Sudakov form factor at NNLO. Using the RGE of the individual pieces, we obtain the resummed form of the matching kernel in momentum space that will be applied to numerical calculations. Finally, the results reveal a high level of universality across the threshold limit of quasi-PDF and the large momentum expansion of quasi-TMDPDFs and LFWFs. In a future work, we will study further the RGE re-summation of the threshold region of matching kernel based on our current formalism and apply it to numerical determination of pion PDF. Note: During the writing-up stage of this paper, we noticed that Ref. citebib.bib101 explicitly computed two-loop heavy-light Sudakov form factor in the context of quasi-TMDPDF large-momentum expansion, which agrees with the result we found here. Acknowledgements. We thank Iain Stewart for pointing out possible connection between time-like and space-like heavy-quark jet functions, and the former has been computed in Refcitebib.bib70. All calculations in the paper are cross checked between Y. L and Y. S. This research is supported by the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Nuclear Physics, under contract number DE-SC0020682. Y. L. is supported by the Priority Research Area SciMat and DigiWorlds under the program Excellence Initiative - Research University at the Jagiellonian University in KrakГіw. Y. S. is partially supported by the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Nuclear Physics, contract no. DE-AC02-06CH11357.

all_papers.title: Large-Momentum Effective Theory
all_papers.abstract:
Since the parton model was introduced by Feynman more than fifty years ago,
we have learned much about the partonic structure of the proton through a large
body of high-energy experimental data and dedicated global fits. However,
calculating the partonic observables such as parton distribution function
(PDFs) from the fundamental theory of strong interactions, QCD, has made
limited progress. Recently, the authors have advocated a formalism,
large-momentum effective theory (LaMET), through which one can extract parton
physics from the properties of the proton travelling at a moderate
boost-factor, e.g., $\gamma\sim (2-5)$. The key observation behind this
approach is that Lorentz symmetry allows the standard formalism of partons in
terms of light-front operators to be replaced by an equivalent one with
large-momentum states and time-independent operators of a universality class.
With LaMET, the PDFs, generalized PDFs or GPDs, transverse-momentum-dependent
PDFs, and light-front wave functions can all be extracted in principle from
lattice simulations of QCD (or other non-perturbative methods) through standard
effective field theory matching and running. Future lattice QCD calculations
with exa-scale computational facilities can help to understand the experimental
data related to the hadronic structure, including those from the upcoming
Electron-Ion Colliders dedicated to exploring the partonic landscape of the
proton. Here we review the progress made in the past few years in development
of the LaMET formalism and its applications, particularly on the demonstration
of its effectiveness from initial lattice QCD simulations.

qualitative: The large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) provides a framework to extract parton distribution functions (PDFs) and other partonic observables from lattice QCD simulations by studying the proton at large but finite momentum.
In LaMET, the light-front operators used in the standard parton model are replaced by time-independent operators in a large-momentum state, leveraging Lorentz symmetry to maintain universality.

The Fourier transform from coordinate space to momentum fraction x is a key step in connecting lattice QCD matrix elements to physical parton distributions, with the transformation involving the large momentum of the proton.

The momentum fraction x in LaMET is related to the Fourier conjugate of the spatial coordinate in the direction of the proton's boost, enabling the extraction of x-dependent observables from spatial correlations in the lattice.
The formalism allows for the systematic matching of lattice results to physical PDFs through effective field theory techniques, including perturbative matching and renormalization group evolution.
quantitative: The boost factor is typically in the range Оі вҲј (2вҖ“5), corresponding to large but moderate momentum for the proton in lattice simulations.

The momentum fraction x is defined via the Fourier transform: x = k / P^+ , where k is the momentum conjugate to the spatial coordinate in the boost direction, and P^+ is the large light-front momentum of the proton.

The matching between the lattice matrix element and the physical PDF involves a perturbative matching coefficient Z(x, Ој, P^+), which depends on the renormalization scale Ој and the large momentum P^+.
The leading-order matching coefficient in LaMET is given by Z(x, Ој, P^+) = Оҙ(x) + O(Оұ_s), with higher-order corrections involving logarithmic terms in P^+ and Ој.
The convergence of the LaMET expansion is controlled by the ratio Оӣ_QCD / P^+, where Оӣ_QCD is the QCD scale, and P^+ must be sufficiently large to suppress power corrections.

content_sections.I Introduction: The proton and neutron, collectively called the nucleon, are the basic building blocks of visible matter in the universe today. Ever since they were discovered in laboratories nearly a century agocitebib.bib404, bib.bib107, their fundamental properties have been vigorously explored: from the determination of the spin through the specific heat of liquid hydrogencitebib.bib162, to the measurement of the magnetic momentscitebib.bib192, and the extraction of their electromagnetic sizes through elastic electron scattering citebib.bib237. The most revealing discovery, however, came from the electron deep-inelastic scattering (DIS) on the proton and nuclei at Stanford Linear Accelerator Center (SLAC) in the late 1960s, in which the constituents of the proton and neutron, quarks (and later gluons), were discoveredcitebib.bib71. Soon after, quantum chromodynamics (QCD), a quantum field theory (QFT) based on вҖңcolorвҖқ SU gauge symmetry, was established as the fundamental theory of strong interactionscitebib.bib203, bib.bib217, bib.bib385, and of the internal structure of the nucleon as wellcitebib.bib426. During the last fifty years, significant progress has been made in understanding the nucleonвҖҷs internal structure in both experiment and theory. Multiple experimental facilities have been built to study high-energy collisions involving protons and nuclei, from which a large amount of experimental data has been accumulated. Based on the QCD factorization theoremscitebib.bib132, derived from perturbative QCD analyses beyond FeynmanвҖҷs parton modelcitebib.bib199, the parton distribution functions (PDFs), which characterize the longitudinal momentum distributions of quarks and gluons in hadrons moving at infinite momentum, have been obtained from global fits to these datacitebib.bib205, bib.bib244, bib.bib227, bib.bib44. A recent result of the phenomenological proton PDFs is shown in Fig. where xрқ‘Ҙx is the momentum fraction of the proton carried by partons. The PDFs provide a comprehensive description of the quark and gluon content of the nucleon. On the theoretical frontier, the Euclidean path-integral formalism of QCD, combined with the lattice regularization and Monte Carlo simulationscitebib.bib440, has offered a systematic way of performing ab initio calculations of non-perturbative strong interactions. The rapid rise in computational power and development of intelligent numerical algorithms have made such a lattice QCD approach extremely successful in computing hadron spectroscopy, the strong coupling, hadronic form factors, etc. , and even scattering phase shiftscitebib.bib424, bib.bib87, bib.bib24. Despite these impressive achievements, we have not been able to systematically explain the partonic structure of the proton from first principles, or more explicitly, we have not made fundamental progress in computing the quark and gluon distributions starting from the QCD Lagrangian (see Sec. for a brief summary). There is actually a good reason behind it: The standard formulation of parton physics in the textbookscitebib.bib419, bib.bib132 is accomplished through the dynamical correlators of quark and gluon fields on the light-front (LF) defined by tвҲ’z=const. рқ‘Ўрқ‘§constt-z=rm const. , which has the important feature of being independent of the protonвҖҷs momentum. On the other hand, lattice QCD is formulated in the Euclidean space with imaginary time, and cannot be used to directly calculate the dynamical correlations that depend on real time. The standard lattice approach to parton physics has been to calculate the lower moments of parton distributions, which are matrix elements of local operatorscitebib.bib332. However, the limitations to the first few moments prohibit practitioners from reproducing reliably the xрқ‘Ҙx-dependent structure as shown in Fig. , other than fitting model functional forms. Over the years, Hamiltonian diagonalization in LF quantization (LFQ)citebib.bib93 and Schwinger-Dyson equationscitebib.bib357 have been proposed to solve the nucleon structure as Minkowskian approaches. Although significant advances have been made phenomenologically, a systematic approximation to calculate the nucleon PDFs is still missing. A few years ago, some of the present authors proposed a general approach to calculate xрқ‘Ҙx-dependent parton distributions based on FeynmanвҖҷs original idea about partons: They are the infinite-momentum limit of static properties of the proton at large momentum, and therefore are intrinsically Euclidean quantities accessible through lattice QCDcitebib.bib267, bib.bib286, bib.bib268. According to this, parton physics in an intermediate range of xminвҲј0.1<x<xmaxвҲј0.9similar-tosubscriptрқ‘Ҙmin0.1рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ҙmaxsimilar-to0.9xrm minsim 0.1<x<xrm maxsim 0.9 can be calculated from the physical properties of the proton at a moderately-large momentum, e. g. , with a Lorentz boost factor Оі=2вҲ’5рқӣҫ25gamma=2-5. The theory has been named as large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) because a rigorous connection between the infinite-momentum frame (IMF) partons and quarks and gluons at a finite momentum requires proper account of the ultraviolet (UV) modes with large momentum in effective field theory (EFT) and systematic power counting. The basic principle for LaMET comes from an implicit observation in the naive parton model: The structure of the proton is approximately independent of its momentum so long as it is much larger than a typical strong-interaction scale ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD, or its mass. For example, the quark momentum distribution at moderate xрқ‘Ҙx in the proton at P=|PвҶ’|=5рқ‘ғвҶ’рқ‘ғ5P=|vecP|=5 GeV is not very different from that at P=50рқ‘ғ50P=50 GeV or P=5рқ‘ғ5P=5 TeV. One might call this phenomenon large-momentum symmetry, the nature of which is similar to that of the electronic structure of the hydrogen atom is not sensitive to the proton mass, so long as it is much larger than that of the electron. The asymptotic behavior of the proton structure might be controlled by an expansion in ОӣQCD/PsubscriptОӣQCDрқ‘ғLambdarm QCD/P, but a justification would require a better understanding of the underlying dynamics. Assuming this, Feynman replaced the protons probed at large but finite momenta in high-energy scattering with the one at infinite momentum P=вҲһрқ‘ғP=infty, corresponding to the leading term in the ОӣQCD/PsubscriptОӣQCDрқ‘ғLambdarm QCD/P expansion, and therefore the idealized concepts of the proton in the IMF and its constituentsвҖ”partonsвҖ”were born. In QFTs, however, the existence of the P=вҲһрқ‘ғP=infty limit depends on their UV behavior. In general, the infinite-momentum limit does not commute with the UV cut-off limit ОӣUVвҶ’вҲһвҶ’subscriptОӣUVLambdarm UVtoinfty. While the physical limit is (ОӣUVвү«P)вҶ’вҲһвҶ’much-greater-thansubscriptОӣUVрқ‘ғ(Lambdarm UVgg P)toinfty, the parton model and subsequent QCD factorization theorems use (Pвү«ОӣUV)вҶ’вҲһвҶ’much-greater-thanрқ‘ғsubscriptОӣUV(PggLambdarm UV)toinfty, keeping all PDFs with the finite support |x|вүӨ1рқ‘Ҙ1|x|leq 1 where negative xрқ‘Ҙx is for antiquarks. Thus partons are an idealized concept which does not exist in the real world. Fortunately, because of asymptotic freedom, the above differences can be calculated in perturbative QCD. Therefore, LaMET is an effective theory of partons, which uses the ordinary field theoretical calculations (ОӣUVвү«P)вҶ’вҲһвҶ’much-greater-thansubscriptОӣUVрқ‘ғ(Lambdarm UVgg P)toinfty and systematically takes into account non-commuting PвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ғPtoinfty limits through EFT matching and running and finite Pрқ‘ғP effects by power corrections. Thus, the PDFs defined in the IMF or on the LF can be accessed at moderate xрқ‘Ҙx from the structure calculations at PвҲјsimilar-toрқ‘ғabsentPsim a few GeVs. The first application of LaMET was to the total gluon helicity О”вҖӢGО”рқҗәDelta G in the polarized proton, a quantity of significant experimental interest at the polarized RHICcitebib.bib96, but not within theoretical reach for many years. Incitebib.bib286, we have shown that from a large-momentum matrix element of the gluon spin operator in a physical gauge, О”вҖӢGО”рқҗәDelta G can be obtained through an EFT matching. Following this success, LaMET was applied to the collinear quark PDFscitebib.bib267. This latter application has generated considerable theoretical as well as numerical activities, particularly for the flavor non-singlet uвҲ’dрқ‘ўрқ‘‘u-d distributions in the proton and other hadrons. A general LaMET framework was subsequently introduced incitebib.bib268. More recently, the approach has been extended to the gluons as wellcitebib.bib453, bib.bib325. Therefore, the PDFs can now be computed directly in lattice QCD at specific Feynman variable xрқ‘Ҙx, without using LFQ. Besides, the partonic landscape of the proton is extremely rich, and LaMET holds the promise of computing parton physics beyond the collinear PDFs. In recent years, tremendous progress has been made in formulating new parton observables for the proton. In particular, two parallel concepts have been developed in characterizing the transverse structure of the proton. The first is the generalized parton distributions (GPDs)citebib.bib365, bib.bib258, bib.bib398. The GPDs combine the features of the protonвҖҷs elastic form factors, which provide the transverse-space density of partonscitebib.bib364, and Feynman PDFs, and interpolate them. Given the joint longitudinal-momentum and transverse-space distributions, one can construct the orbital angular momentum (OAM) of partons, among otherscitebib.bib258. In general, the GPDs can be used to generate momentum-dissected transverse space images of the protoncitebib.bib99. A new class of experimental processes, deeply-virtual exclusive processes (DVEP), including deeply-virtual Compton scattering (DVCS) in which the final state is a diffractive real photon plus a recoiling proton, has been found to measure themcitebib.bib258, bib.bib257. The second concept is the transverse-momentum-dependent (TMD) PDFs (or TMDPDFs), in which the partonвҖҷs transverse momentum is explicitcitebib.bib141, bib.bib132. Much theoretical progress has been made in recent years regarding their proper definitions, factorizations, and spin correlationscitebib.bib139, bib.bib179, bib.bib134. TMDPDFs can be measured in experimental processes by observing the transverse momentum of the final-state particles. Over the years, it has gradually become clear that a dedicated experimental facility to fully explore the partonic landscape of the proton is required. To meet this requirement, the US nuclear science community has proposed, to build a high-energy, high-luminosity Electron-Ion Collider (EIC)citebib.bib26, which has been recently approved by the US Department of Energy. The new collider accelerates electrons to 10-30 GeV and ionsвҖ” including the proton and heavy nuclei all the way up to Pb or UвҖ” up to 100 GeV per nucleon, realizing the center-of-mass collision energy EcmsubscriptрқҗёcmErm cm from 40 to 170 GeV. The corresponding electron energy in fixed-target experiments would be 100 GeV to 10 TeV. The beams are polarized, with high-luminosity up to 1033вҲ’34superscript1033341033-34 collisions/(cm2вӢ…sвӢ…superscriptcm2srm cm2cdotrm s), which are critical for studying exclusive processes such as DVCS. The kinematic range of the collisions covers the Bjorken xBsubscriptрқ‘ҘрқҗөxB (which coincides with the parton momentum fraction xрқ‘Ҙx in the naive parton model to be discussed in the next section) down to sub-10вҲ’4superscript10410-4, and Q2superscriptрқ‘„2Q2 as high as 104superscript104104 GeV. Much of the EIC science has been discussed in a dedicated studycitebib.bib4. Of course, the EIC and lattice QCD efforts will not stop at the precision parton physics of the proton. More importantly, we need to develop ways or languages to describe the nucleon as a strongly-coupled relativistic quantum system, in much the same way as we understand, for example, the quantum Hall effects in condensed matter physics. Without a deep understanding of the mechanisms of strongly-coupled QCD physics, we cannot claim a fundamental understanding of the structure of the proton and neutron, in particular, the origin of their mass and spin. This is one of the most challenging goals facing the standard model of particle and nuclear physics today. This review is to systematically expose the idea, formalism, and results of the LaMET approach to parton physics. We do not claim to be entirely complete because the field is rapidly developing. References in the related fields are not meant to be complete either, and we apologize for any important omissions. Closely-related reviews on lattice parton physics can be found incitebib.bib127, bib.bib458. There have been studies on the effectiveness of LaMET in various modelscitebib.bib276, bib.bib204, bib.bib94, bib.bib446, bib.bib416, bib.bib351, bib.bib379, bib.bib66, bib.bib293, bib.bib236, bib.bib95, bib.bib396, bib.bib65, bib.bib302, bib.bib161, some of which we will mention in the following for illustrative purposes. There have been also papers questioning the validity of LaMET methodcitebib.bib104, bib.bib401, bib.bib402 and some got clarified later in the literaturecitebib.bib86, bib.bib288, bib.bib399, We will not discuss them here and interested readers may refer to the above references. We have used proton in most places in the text to emphasize its importance in nuclear and particle physics. However, the discussions apply equally to the neutron and other hadrons as well. The plan for the presentation of this review is as follows. In the remainder of the Introduction, we explain the nature of parton physics as an effective description of the internal structure of the proton at large momentum, as well as other existing methods in the literature for solving the parton structure. In Sec. , we introduce the LaMET method starting from momentum renormalization group equation (RGE) of physical observables in a moving hadron, followed by the matching between momentum distributions and PDFs. We then formulate an EFT expansion to compute parton physics from theoretical methods suitable for the structure of a large-momentum proton. In Sec. , we discuss some important details for collinear PDFs: renormalization of the nonlocal operators, particularly power divergences in lattice regularization, and matching to all orders in perturbation theory. Sec. is devoted to applications to general collinear parton observables including GPDs, parton distribution amplitudes and higher-order parton correlations. We also discuss applications for the OAM of the partons in a polarized proton. In Sec. , we consider the application to TMDPDFs, a new class of parton observables. We study matching of the quasi-TMDPDFs to the physical ones, and explore the lattice calculation of the soft function. Finally, Sec. summarizes the recent lattice calculations relevant to the LaMET applications, and the conclusion is given in Sec. . The review is completed with an Appendix with a list of acronyms and glossaries, as well as notations and conventions.
content_sections.I Introduction I.1 Partons through Infin: Although partons have become a ubiquitous language for high-energy scattering, their role as effective degrees of freedom of QCD for describing the internal structure of the nucleon is less emphasized in the literature. In applications within QCD factorization theorems, they areвҖ”following FeynmanвҖ”objects arising from the limit of infinite momentum, with the potential UV divergences regulated and renormalized after the limit. Thus, the partons are an idealized concept, referring to the quark and gluon Fock components of the nucleon or other hadrons only in the context of IMF and LF gauge A+=(A0+Az)/2=0superscriptрқҗҙsuperscriptрқҗҙ0superscriptрқҗҙрқ‘§20A+=(A0+Az)/sqrt2=0. They are in the same category of concepts as the infinitely-heavy quark in heavy-quark effective field theory (HQET)citebib.bib355. To motivate LaMET, it is important to understand this origin and nature of partons. Built from the knowledge of electron scattering in non-relativistic systems (atoms and molecules)citebib.bib439, Feynman introduced the naive parton model to describe deep-inelastic scattering (DIS) on the proton, and to explain the observed phenomenon of Bjorken scalingcitebib.bib200, bib.bib70, bib.bib199. Shown in Fig. is the DIS process in which a virtual photon with large momentum qОјsuperscriptрқ‘һрқңҮqmu is absorbed by a proton of momentum PОјsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқңҮPmu and mass Mрқ‘ҖM. The invariant variables are Q2=вҲ’qОјвҖӢqОјsuperscriptрқ‘„2superscriptрқ‘һрқңҮsubscriptрқ‘һрқңҮQ2=-qmuqmu and PвӢ…q=MвҖӢОҪвӢ…рқ‘ғрқ‘һрқ‘ҖрқңҲPcdot q=Mnu, and Bjorken xB=Q2/(2вҖӢPвӢ…q)subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗөsuperscriptрқ‘„2вӢ…2рқ‘ғрқ‘һxB=Q2/(2Pcdot q) fixed in the scaling (or Bjorken) limit Q2вҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘„2Q2toinfty, PвӢ…qвҶ’вҲһвҶ’вӢ…рқ‘ғрқ‘һPcdot qtoinfty. The inclusive DIS cross section can be factored into a product of leptonic and hadronic tensors, where the former is associated with the electromagnetic current of the lepton, while the latter contains all information about the electromagnetic interaction with the target proton. To learn about the proton structure, it is best to consider the scattering in the Breit frame where qОјsuperscriptрқ‘һрқңҮdisplaystyle qmu =(0,0,0,вҲ’Q),absent000рқ‘„displaystyle=(0,0,0,-Q),, PОјsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқңҮdisplaystyle Pmu =(Q24вҖӢxB2+M2,0,0,Q2вҖӢxB),absentsuperscriptрқ‘„24superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗө2superscriptрқ‘Җ200рқ‘„2subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗөdisplaystyle=left(sqrtfracQ24xB2+M2,0,0,fracQ2xBright),, and the virtual photon has zero energy. The probe is sensitive only to the spatial structure as in non-relativistic electron scattering. However, relativity now constrains the proton to move at a large momentum Pz=Q/(2вҖӢxB)superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘„2subscriptрқ‘ҘрқҗөPz=Q/(2xB) with boost factor Оі=Q/(2вҖӢxBвҖӢM)рқӣҫрқ‘„2subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗөрқ‘Җgamma=Q/(2xBM), which approaches Pz=вҲһsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz=infty in the Bjorken limit. Feynman made intuitive assumptions about the proton structure and scattering mechanism, without QFT subtletiescitebib.bib199: The proton structure at different large Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz should be similar, and can be approximated by that at Pz=вҲһsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz=infty, or in the IMF. The interactions between constituents (partons) are infinitely time-dilated, and the wave function configurations are frozen. The proton in high-energy scattering can be seen as being made of non-interacting partons, each with a longitudinal momentum xвҖӢPzрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§xPz with 0<x<10рқ‘Ҙ10<x<1. The internal structure of non-relativistic systems is independent of their overall momentum. However, relativistic systems is different as they least experience the Lorentz contraction. The structures of such systems are inextricably mixed with the overall motion, and their dependence on the external momentum is a dynamical problem. On the other hand, if the internal structure depends on a particular hadron scale ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD, the protons at all large-momentum with Pzвү«ОӣQCDmuch-greater-thansuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptОӣQCDPzggLambdarm QCD have a similar structure, corresponding to the PzвҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pztoinfty limit. This means that if fвҖӢ(kz,Pz)рқ‘“superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§f(kz,Pz) is the constituent momentum-kzsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§kz distribution in a proton of momentum Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, it might be analytical at Pz=вҲһsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz=infty and admits Taylor series expansions in 1/Pz1superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§1/Pz, fвҖӢ(kz,Pz)=fвҖӢ(x)+f2вҖӢ(x)вҖӢ(ОӣQCD/Pz)2+вҖҰ,рқ‘“superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘“рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘“2рқ‘ҘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCDsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2вҖҰdisplaystyle f(kz,Pz)=f(x)+f2(x)(Lambdarm QCD/Pz)2+. . . ,, where x=kz/Pzрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§x=kz/Pz. If so, one may find a large-momentum symmetry of the proton properties up to power corrections рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1/Pz)рқ’Ә1superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§cal O(1/Pz) (we omit the upper index zрқ‘§z sometimes for simplicity), and fвҖӢ(x)рқ‘“рқ‘Ҙf(x) is the parton distribution. The above picture can be shown to hold in certain simple QFT models, where the dynamical frame dependence of wave functions for composite systems can be studied straightforwardly. There are many interesting examples of two-dimensional systems, for which solutions can be found. One of the much studied cases is the large Ncsubscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җNc QCD, also called вҖҷt Hooft modelcitebib.bib241, in which the bound states have a well-defined large-momentum limit. The wave functions can be expanded in 1/P1рқ‘ғ1/P, with the corrections starting from (1/P)2superscript1рқ‘ғ2(1/P)2. The momenta of the constituents, kрқ‘ҳk and PвҲ’kрқ‘ғрқ‘ҳP-k, scale in this limit. When plotted as a function of x=k/Pрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘ғx=k/P, the change in the wave function with the magnitude of the momentum can be found in Figs. 8вҖ“11 incitebib.bib291. This is the type of example in which FeynmanвҖҷs intuition applies. However, such a intuition fails in many 3+1 dimensional QFTs, such as QCD. When a bound state travels at increasingly large momentum, more and more high-momentum modes of a field theory are needed to build up its internal structure. Lorentz contraction indicates that the range of constituent momentum important for the structure also increases. If these high-momentum modes do not decouple effectively from the low-momentum ones, large logarithms of the form lnвҒЎPрқ‘ғln P, will develop in the structural quantities. Hence a singularity (cut) at P=вҲһрқ‘ғP=infty can exist in these theories, making PвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ғPtoinfty limit ill-defined and the large momentum expansion impossible. This situation is intimately related to UV properties of the theories, for which the limits of taking the UV cut-off ОӣUVвҶ’вҲһвҶ’subscriptОӣUVLambdarm UVtoinfty and PвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ғPtoinfty do not commute. While the physically-relevant one is (ОӣUVвү«P)вҶ’вҲһвҶ’much-greater-thansubscriptОӣUVрқ‘ғ(Lambdarm UVgg P)toinfty, partons in QCD factorizations are obtained in the other limit (ОӣUVвүӘP)вҶ’вҲһвҶ’much-less-thansubscriptОӣUVрқ‘ғ(Lambdarm UVll P)toinfty when the UV divergences are ignored. Thus one can formally write the parton distribution as fвҖӢ(x)=вҲ«dвҖӢО»2вҖӢПҖвҖӢeiвҖӢxвҖӢО»вҖӢвҹЁPz=вҲһ|ПҲвҖ вҖӢ(z)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ|Pz=вҲһвҹ©,рқ‘“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘‘рқңҶ2рқңӢsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқ‘ҘрқңҶquantum-operator-productsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§superscriptрқң“вҖ рқ‘§рқң“0superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§displaystyle f(x)=intfracdlambda2pieixlambda Pz=infty|psidagger(z)psi|Pz=infty,, where О»=limPzвҶ’вҲһ,zвҶ’0(zвҖӢPz)рқңҶsubscriptformulae-sequenceвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§вҶ’рқ‘§0рқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§lambda=limPztoinfty,zto 0(zPz), and ПҲрқң“psi is a quantum field. Historically, the IMF limit of field theories has been studied first at the level of diagrammatic rules for perturbation theorycitebib.bib438. It was found that taking PвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ғPtoinfty by ignoring the UV divergences considerably simplifies the perturbation theory rules: Many time-ordered diagrams vanish and only few have finite contributions. Moreover, scattering in this limit resembles that in non-relativistic quantum mechanics, and the wave function description becomes useful. The Fock states define the partons which have the proper kinematic support (0<x<10рқ‘Ҙ10<x<1). After the limit is taken, all physical quantities are now independent of Pрқ‘ғP, and large-momentum symmetry is exact before UV divergences are regulated. Therefore, it is the вҖңnaiveвҖқ limit, ОӣUVвүӘPвҶ’вҲһmuch-less-thansubscriptОӣUVрқ‘ғвҶ’Lambdarm UVll Ptoinfty, that corresponds to FeynmanвҖҷs naive parton model. In the standard QCD study of high-energy scattering, the above concept of partons as effective degrees of freedom has been used implicitly. The PDFs are defined in terms of the naive P=вҲһрқ‘ғP=infty limit, and are used to match the experimental cross sections, resulting in QCD factorization theoremscitebib.bib132.
content_sections.I Introduction I.2 Partons through Light: In the literature and textbooks, parton distributions are not traditionally represented in terms of the Euclidean matrix elements as in Eq. . Rather, they are represented by the so-called LF correlators of quantum fields (вҖңoperator formalismвҖқ)citebib.bib93, bib.bib132. A more explicit formulation in terms of collinear quantum fields and effective lagrangian is made in the soft-collinear effective theory (SCET)citebib.bib48, bib.bib50, bib.bib49. There is a physical way to see that the parton description of high-energy scattering results in the light-front correlations. Consider DIS in the rest frame of the proton, where the virtual photon has momentum qОј=(ОҪ,0,0,вҲ’ОҪ2+2вҖӢxBвҖӢMвҖӢОҪ). superscriptрқ‘һрқңҮрқңҲ00superscriptрқңҲ22subscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗөрқ‘ҖрқңҲdisplaystyle qmu=(nu,0,0,-sqrtnu2+2xBMnu),. In the Bjorken limit ОҪвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқңҲnutoinfty, although the invariant mass Qрқ‘„Q of the photon goes to infinity, the photon momentum becomes actually light-like in the sense that it approaches the light front. Therefore, in inclusive DIS cross section, the separation of the two electromagnetic currents in the hadronic tensor, which is Fourier conjugate to the photon momentum, also approaches the light-cone direction. Thus, it appears natural that all the structural physics of the proton in the IMF can also be expressed in terms of time-dependent LF correlators or correlations of quantum fields on the LF. Formally, this is simple to see if one writes |PвҶ’вҲһвҹ©=UвҖӢ(ОӣвҲһ)вҖӢ|P=0вҹ©. ketвҶ’рқ‘ғрқ‘ҲsubscriptОӣketрқ‘ғ0displaystyle|Ptoinfty=U(Lambdainfty)|P=0,. The boost operator UвҖӢ(ОӣвҲһ)рқ‘ҲsubscriptОӣU(Lambdainfty) can be applied to the static nonlocal operators in the ordinary momentum distributions. In doing so, all static correlations become light-cone ones. The boost process is then similar to shifting the Hamiltonian evolution in quantum mechanics from SchrГ¶dinger to Heisenberg picture where time-dependence is now in the operators. To express light-cone correlations, it is convenient to introduce two conjugate light-like (or light-cone) vectors, pОј=(Оӣ,0,0,Оӣ)superscriptрқ‘қрқңҮОӣ00Оӣpmu=(Lambda,0,0,Lambda) and nОј=(1/2вҖӢОӣ,0,0,вҲ’1/2вҖӢОӣ)superscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮ12Оӣ0012Оӣnmu=(1/2Lambda,0,0,-1/2Lambda), with the following properties, n2=p2=0superscriptрқ‘ӣ2superscriptрқ‘қ20n2=p2=0, and nвӢ…p=1вӢ…рқ‘ӣрқ‘қ1ncdot p=1, where ОӣОӣLambda is a parameter. Then any four-vector can be expanded as, kОј=kвӢ…nвҖӢpОј+kвӢ…pвҖӢnОј+kвҹӮОј. superscriptрқ‘ҳрқңҮвӢ…рқ‘ҳрқ‘ӣsuperscriptрқ‘қрқңҮвӢ…рқ‘ҳрқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҳperpendicular-toрқңҮdisplaystyle kmu=kcdot npmu+kcdot pnmu+kperpmu . In particular, the momentum PОјsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқңҮPmu of a proton moving in the zрқ‘§z-direction can be expressed as PОј=pОј+(M2/2)вҖӢnОј,superscriptрқ‘ғрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘қрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘Җ22superscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮdisplaystyle Pmu=pmu+(M2/2),nmu,, where Mрқ‘ҖM is the proton mass. Using the above notation, one can express the unpolarized quark distribution in the proton ascitebib.bib132, qвҖӢ(x)=12вҖӢвҲ«dвҖӢО»2вҖӢПҖвҖӢeiвҖӢО»вҖӢxвҖӢвҹЁP|ПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢnМёвҖӢWвҖӢ(0,О»вҖӢn)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(О»вҖӢn)|Pвҹ©c,рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙ12рқ‘‘рқңҶ2рқңӢsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқңҶрқ‘Ҙsubscriptquantum-operator-productрқ‘ғВҜрқң“0italic-nМёрқ‘Ҡ0рқңҶрқ‘ӣрқң“рқңҶрқ‘ӣрқ‘ғрқ‘җdisplaystyle q(x)=frac12intfracdlambda2pieilambda x P|overlinepsinotnW(0,lambda n)psi(lambda n)|Pc , where ПҲрқң“psi is the quark field and Wрқ‘ҠW is a gauge-link defined as WвҖӢ(x2,x1)=рқ‘Ҡsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ2subscriptрқ‘Ҙ1absentdisplaystyle W(x2,x1)= рқ’«вҖӢexpвҒЎ[вҲ’iвҖӢgвҖӢвҲ«01рқ‘‘tвҖӢ(x2вҲ’x1)ОјвҖӢAОјвҖӢ(x1+(x2вҲ’x1)вҖӢt)]рқ’«рқ‘–рқ‘”superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ўsubscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ2subscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқңҮsuperscriptрқҗҙрқңҮsubscriptрқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқ‘Ҙ2subscriptрқ‘Ҙ1рқ‘Ўdisplaystylehskip 5.0ptcal Pexp[-igint01dt,(x2-x1)muAmu(x1+(x2-x1)t)] to ensure gauge invariance with рқ’«рқ’«cal P denoting the path ordering. cрқ‘җc indicates the connected contributions only, and will be suppressed in the rest of this work. It is a property of gauge theories in which the charge fields are not gauge-invariant, and the physical distributions must include a beam of collinear gauge particles. Note that the above expression is true for any momentum Pрқ‘ғP (a residual momentum symmetry), in particular, in the rest frame of the nucleon. The xрқ‘Ҙx-support of the above distribution is [вҲ’1,1]11[-1,1]. For negative xрқ‘Ҙx, one defines the antiquark distribution with вҲ’qвҖӢ(вҲ’x)вүЎqВҜвҖӢ(x)рқ‘һрқ‘ҘВҜрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙ-q(-x)equivbarq(x). The above expression has been more familiar in the literature than FeynmanвҖҷs original formulation of PDFs. In the single quark target, one finds qвҖӢ(x)=ОҙвҖӢ(xвҲ’1)рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙрқӣҝрқ‘Ҙ1q(x)=delta(x-1). To expose the partons in the above equation, one can follow the QCD light-front quantitzation citebib.bib112, bib.bib303, bib.bib174, suggested by Dirac in 1949citebib.bib168. In LFQcitebib.bib93, one defines the LF coordinates, ОҫВұ=(Оҫ0ВұОҫ3)/2,superscriptрқңүplus-or-minusplus-or-minussuperscriptрқңү0superscriptрқңү32displaystylexipm=(xi0pmxi3)/sqrt2 , where Оҫ+superscriptрқңүxi+ is the LF вҖңtimeвҖқ, and ОҫвҲ’superscriptрқңүxi- is the LF вҖңspatial coordinateвҖқ. And any four-vector AОјsuperscriptрқҗҙрқңҮAmu will be now written as (A+,AвҲ’,AвҶ’вҹӮ)superscriptрқҗҙsuperscriptрқҗҙsubscriptвҶ’рқҗҙperpendicular-to(A+,A-,vecAperp). Dynamical degrees of freedom are defined on the Оҫ+=0superscriptрқңү0xi+=0 plane with arbitrary ОҫвҲ’superscriptрқңүxi- and ОҫвҶ’вҹӮsubscriptвҶ’рқңүperpendicular-tovecxiperp, with conjugate momentum k+superscriptрқ‘ҳk+ and kвҶ’вҹӮsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toveckperp. Dynamics is generated by the light-cone Hamiltonian HLC=PвҲ’subscriptрқҗ»LCsuperscriptрқ‘ғHrm LC=P-. For a free particle with three-momentum (k+,kвҶ’вҹӮ)superscriptрқ‘ҳsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-to(k+,veckperp) and mass mрқ‘ҡm, the on-shell LF energy is kвҲ’=(kвҶ’вҹӮ2+m2)/(2вҖӢk+)superscriptрқ‘ҳsuperscriptsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-to2superscriptрқ‘ҡ22superscriptрқ‘ҳk-=(veckperp2+m2)/(2k+). For QCD, one can define the Dirac matrices ОіВұ=(Оі0ВұОі3)/2superscriptрқӣҫplus-or-minusplus-or-minussuperscriptрқӣҫ0superscriptрқӣҫ32gammapm=(gamma0pmgamma3)/sqrt2, and the projection operators for the quark fields as PВұ=(1/2)вҖӢОівҲ“вҖӢОіВұsubscriptрқ‘ғplus-or-minus12superscriptрқӣҫminus-or-plussuperscriptрқӣҫplus-or-minusPpm=(1/2)gammampgammapm, so that any ПҲрқң“psi can be decomposed into ПҲ=ПҲ++ПҲвҲ’рқң“subscriptрқң“subscriptрқң“psi=psi++psi- with ПҲВұ=PВұвҖӢПҲsubscriptрқң“plus-or-minussubscriptрқ‘ғplus-or-minusрқң“psipm=Ppmpsi, where ПҲ+subscriptрқң“psi+ is considered as a dynamical degree of freedom. For the gauge field, A+superscriptрқҗҙA+ is fixed by the LF gauge A+=0superscriptрқҗҙ0A+=0. AвҹӮsubscriptрқҗҙperpendicular-toAperp are dynamical degrees of freedom. ПҲвҲ’subscriptрқң“psi- and AвҲ’superscriptрқҗҙA- are dependent variables, which can be expressed in terms of ПҲ+subscriptрқң“psi+ and AвҹӮsubscriptрқҗҙperpendicular-toAperp using equations of motioncitebib.bib303. The physics of the LF correlations becomes manifest if one introduces the canonical expansion, ПҲ+(Оҫ+=0,ОҫвҲ’,ОҫвҶ’вҹӮ)=вҲ«d2вҖӢkвҹӮ(2вҖӢПҖ)3dвҖӢk+2вҖӢk+вҲ‘Пғ[bПғ(k)u(k,Пғ)displaystylepsi+(xi+=0,xi-,vecxiperp)=intfracd2kperp(2pi)3fracdk+2k+sumsigmaBig[bsigma(k)u(k,sigma) Г—eвҲ’iвҖӢ(k+вҖӢОҫвҲ’вҲ’kвҶ’вҹӮвӢ…ОҫвҶ’вҹӮ)+dПғвҖ (k)v(k,Пғ)eiвҖӢ(k+вҖӢОҫвҲ’вҲ’kвҶ’вҹӮвӢ…ОҫвҶ’вҹӮ)],displaystyletimes e-i(k+xi--veckperpcdotvecxiperp)+dsigmadagger(k)v(k,sigma)ei(k+xi--veckperpcdotvecxiperp)Big], where bвҖ вҖӢ(k)superscriptрқ‘ҸвҖ рқ‘ҳbdagger(k) and dвҖ вҖӢ(k)superscriptрқ‘‘вҖ рқ‘ҳddagger(k) (bвҖӢ(k)рқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳb(k) and dвҖӢ(k)рқ‘‘рқ‘ҳd(k)) are quark and antiquark creation (annihilation) operators, respectively.
