Substituting the above expansion into Eq. , one finds the quark distribution as qвҖӢ(x)=12вҖӢxвҖӢвҲ‘ПғвҲ«d2вҖӢkвҶ’вҹӮ(2вҖӢПҖ)3вҖӢвҹЁP|bПғвҖ вҖӢ(x,kвҶ’вҹӮ)вҖӢbПғвҖӢ(x,kвҶ’вҹӮ)|Pвҹ©/вҹЁP|Pвҹ©рқ‘һрқ‘Ҙ12рқ‘ҘsubscriptрқңҺsuperscriptрқ‘‘2subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-tosuperscript2рқңӢ3quantum-operator-productрқ‘ғsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҸрқңҺвҖ рқ‘ҘsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-tosubscriptрқ‘ҸрқңҺрқ‘ҘsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-toрқ‘ғinner-productрқ‘ғрқ‘ғdisplaystyle q(x)=frac12xsumsigmaintfracd2veckperp(2pi)3 P|bsigmadagger(x,veckperp)bsigma(x,veckperp)|P/ P|P for x>0рқ‘Ҙ0x>0, and similarly for x<0рқ‘Ҙ0x<0 for which one gets the antiquark distribution. The factor 1/x1рқ‘Ҙ1/x comes from the normalization of the creation and annihilation operators. The matrix element above should be interpreted as the matrix element in a wave packet state, in the limit of a state of definite momentumcitebib.bib132. This way, one recovers the physical meaning of PDFs in the LF correlator (operator) formalism.
content_sections.I Introduction I.3 Other Approaches to P: Calculating the partonic structure of the hadrons from QCD has always been an important goal in hadronic physics. There have been two main approaches apart from various phenomenology and models: light-front quantization and lattice QCD. Here the authors give a very brief review on LFQ and lattice approaches that are different from the main subject of this review. Although LFQ explicitly uses the parton degrees of freedom, it has not been very successful in practical calculations. First of all, LF perturbation theory, like the standard Hamiltonian perturbation theory, breaks Lorentz symmetry manifestly and requires a sophisticated renormalization scheme to restore it. A potential renormalization scheme must deal with the long-range correlations in the ОҫвҲ’superscriptрқңүxi- direction which require functional dependence on the renormalization countertermscitebib.bib441. Thus LF perturbation theory has not been used for any calculations beyond one loop, except for the two-loop anomalous magnetic moment in QEDcitebib.bib314. In fact, the common wisdom of using dimensional regularization (DR) for the transverse integrals and cut-off regularization for the longitudinal one has not been proven useful for multi-loop calculations, although it has been successfully used to derive the BFKL evolution by Mueller from the quarkonium wave functionscitebib.bib371. The enthusiasm for using LFQ in QCD is not about perturbation theory, but to solve the hadron states. Discretized LFQ was proposed incitebib.bib382 to make practical calculations for the bound state problems. This non-perturbative method turns out to be successful for models in 1+1 dimension, such as the Schwinger model citebib.bib361, bib.bib225, the 1+1 QCDcitebib.bib97, bib.bib417, the 1+1 П•4superscriptitalic-П•4phi4 theorycitebib.bib226 and the sine-Gordon modelcitebib.bib98. For 3+1 dimensional theories, simple approximations have been considered, like the Tamm-Dancoff approximationcitebib.bib384. For QCD itself, one again has to use severe truncations in the number of Fock states. Some recent works of this type includecitebib.bib428, bib.bib312, bib.bib290. However, to derive a fully-renormalized hamiltonian is difficult and moreover, there has been no demonstration so far that the Fock-space truncation actually convergescitebib.bib441. Therefore a systematic approximation for QCD bound states in LFQ has yet to be found. Given the rapid development in lattice QCD, it is natural to use it to compute parton physics. However, simulating real-time evolution directly is numerically challenging, which runs into the so-called sign problem or more generally NP-hard problem. Over the years, a number of methods have been proposed previously to indirectly calculate the PDFs, which includes well-studied moment methods, hadronic tensor and Compton amplitude method, coordinate space factorization, etc. These approaches calculate lattice observables that can be related to the PDFs/structure functions through OPE or the dispersion relation, and thus can be used to probe certain information on the partonic structure of hadrons. However, their aims are mainly to get the lower moments of PDFs and/or segments of certain coordinate correlations, not directly in parton degrees of freedom. The most-adopted approach on the lattice has been to calculate the moments of PDFs as the matrix elements of local operatorscitebib.bib309, bib.bib360. In the moments approach, one starts with the so-called twist-two operatorscitebib.bib126, OОј1вҖӢвҖҰвҖӢОјn=ПҲВҜвҖӢОі(Ој1вҖӢiвҖӢDОј2вҖӢвҖҰвҖӢiвҖӢDОјn)вҖӢПҲвҲ’tracedisplaystyle Omu1. . . mun=overlinepsigamma(mu1iDmu2. . . iDmun)psi-rm trace in the quark case, where (Ој1вҖӢвҖҰвҖӢОј2)subscriptрқңҮ1вҖҰsubscriptрқңҮ2(mu1. . . mu2) indicates that all the indices are symmetrized, the trace terms are those with at least one factor of the metric tensor gОјiвҖӢОјjsuperscriptрқ‘”subscriptрқңҮрқ‘–subscriptрқңҮрқ‘—gmuimuj multiplied by operators of dimension (n+2)рқ‘ӣ2(n+2) with nвҲ’2рқ‘ӣ2n-2 Lorentz indices, etc. Their matrix elements in the proton state are вҹЁP|OОј1вҖӢвҖҰвҖӢОјnвҖӢ(Ој)|Pвҹ©=2вҖӢanвҖӢ(Ој)вҖӢ(PОј1вҖӢвӢҜвҖӢPОјnвҲ’trace),quantum-operator-productрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘ӮsubscriptрқңҮ1вҖҰsubscriptрқңҮрқ‘ӣрқңҮрқ‘ғ2subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘ғsubscriptрқңҮ1вӢҜsuperscriptрқ‘ғsubscriptрқңҮрқ‘ӣtracedisplaystyle P|Omu1. . . mun(mu)|P=2an(mu)(Pmu1cdots Pmun!-!rm trace),, and the PDFs are related to the local matrix elements through anвҖӢ(Ој)subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқңҮdisplaystylean(mu) =вҲ«вҲ’11рқ‘‘xвҖӢxnвҲ’1вҖӢqвҖӢ(x,Ој2)absentsubscriptsuperscript11differential-dрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣ1рқ‘һрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2displaystyle=int1-1dxxn-1q(x,mu2) =вҲ«01xnвҲ’1вҖӢ[qвҖӢ(x,Ој2)+(вҲ’1)nвҖӢqВҜвҖӢ(x,Ој2)]absentsubscriptsuperscript10superscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ӣ1delimited-рқ‘һрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2superscript1рқ‘ӣВҜрқ‘һрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2displaystyle=int10xn-1big[q(x,mu2)+(-1)nbarq(x,mu2)big] with n=1,2,вҖҰрқ‘ӣ12вҖҰn=1,2,. . . . The time-dependent correlation for the PDF in Eq. is recovered by taking all the components as ++ in Eq. , вҹЁP|O+вҖҰ+вҖӢ(Ој)|Pвҹ©=2вҖӢanвҖӢ(Ој)вҖӢP+вҖӢвӢҜвҖӢP+,quantum-operator-productрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘Ӯlimit-fromвҖҰрқңҮрқ‘ғ2subscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘ғвӢҜsuperscriptрқ‘ғdisplaystyle P|O+. . . +(mu)|P=2an(mu)P+cdots P+,, and packaging all the moments into a distribution. Likewise, for the gluon PDF, its moments are again related to the matrix elements of local operators, OgОј1вҖӢвҖҰвҖӢОјn=вҲ’F(Ој1ОұвҖӢiвҖӢDОј2вҖӢвӢҜ. iвҖӢDОјnвҲ’1вҖӢFОұОјn),displaystyle Ogmu1. . . mun=-F(mu1alphaiDmu2cdots. iDmun-1Fmun) alpha , with n=2,4,6,вҖҰрқ‘ӣ246вҖҰn=2,4,6,. . . . A large number of lattice QCD calculations of PDF moments have been done so far with various degrees of control in systematicscitebib.bib332, which include discretization errors, physical pion mass, finite volume effects, excited state contaminations, and proper renormalization. Most of the lattice calculations have been focused on the first and second moments, вҹЁxвҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙ x citebib.bib213, bib.bib13, bib.bib35, and вҹЁx2вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқ‘Ҙ2 x2citebib.bib171, bib.bib159 for the unpolarized distributions, and the zero-th and first moments, вҹЁ1вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©1 1citebib.bib212, bib.bib13, bib.bib110, bib.bib12, and вҹЁxвҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙ xcitebib.bib25, bib.bib2 for the polarized distributions. However, it has been difficult to calculate higher moments, due to power divergences and rapid decay in signals. Nonetheless, moment calculations can provide a useful calibration for any comprehensive lattice approach to PDFs. To get more information about the PDFs, it was proposed to calculate the hadronic tensor of DIS in Euclidean space, and analytically continue the result to Minkowski spacecitebib.bib337, bib.bib338, bib.bib333, bib.bib334, bib.bib335, bib.bib336. Since numerical methods for analytical continuation are known to be difficult for precision control (similar to NP-hard or sign problem mentioned earlier), the approach is useful mainly for the nucleon low-lying excitations. It is very challenging to obtain parton physics this way. A similar approach called вҖңoperator product expansion (OPE) without OPEвҖқ was suggested incitebib.bib9, bib.bib358, see alsocitebib.bib158, bib.bib102, bib.bib103. The point is that the Compton amplitude in the non-dispersive region can be calculated in the Euclidean spacecitebib.bib262. Through dispersion relation and Taylor-expansion at ОҪ=PвӢ…q=0рқңҲвӢ…рқ‘ғрқ‘һ0nu=Pcdot q=0, one can extract the higher moments of structure functions from the lattice Compton amplitude. The recent works and references for parton structure from this approach can be found incitebib.bib109, bib.bib224, bib.bib243. A similar method has been adopted for Compton amplitude with heavy-light currentscitebib.bib165. This approach has been used to calculate the second moment of pion distribution amplitudecitebib.bib164, bib.bib163. The current-current correlators can also be studied through OPE in the coordinate space without momentum insertion into the currentscitebib.bib76. The spatial correlation at small distances can be used to calculate higher-moments of distribution amplitudes of the mesons. A number of lattice studies have been performed incitebib.bib78, bib.bib34, bib.bib33. Similar strategy has been suggested more recently by Qiu et al. citebib.bib349 for parton distributions, and has been used in lattice simulationscitebib.bib422, bib.bib421. The pseudo-PDF has been proposed based on the equal-time correlationвҖ”or the quasi-PDF in Fourier spaceвҖ”used in LaMETcitebib.bib390, bib.bib397, and uses a coordinate-space factorization or OPE at small distance as incitebib.bib76. Because of its close connection with the quasi-PDF, we will discuss comparisons of the pseudo-PDF data analysis method with that for the quasi-PDF in Sec. . There have been pioneering studies on moments of the вҖңquasiвҖқ quark TMDPDFs on lattice citebib.bib222, bib.bib375, bib.bib374, bib.bib191, bib.bib449. The staple-shaped gauge link operators have been used to connect the quark fields separated in the spatial direction to simulate the moments of TMDPDF. The ratios of these moments are presumed independent of the unknown soft function and may be compared with experimental data. However, a rigorous relation of these constructions to the physical moments of TMDPDFs had not been investigated before LaMET, particularly the relationship between large momentum limit and the rapidity cutoff which is an essential ingredient of TMD physics. Comparison of this approach and LaMET will be made in Sec. .
content_sections.II Large-Momentum Effective Theory: As has been explained in Sec. , FeynmanвҖҷs partons were motivated from describing the structure of a bound state travelling at large momentum Pрқ‘ғP. On the other hand, in QCD factorizations, they appear as effective degrees of freedoms arising in infinite momentum limit disregarding UV divergences. Reconciling these two pictures results in large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) for the parton structure of hadrons. In this section, we start by considering the structure of the proton at finite momentum. We define the ordinary momentum distributions of the constituents, and trying to illustrate their dependence on the proton momentum. We demonstrate that the large-Pрқ‘ғP momentum-dependence follow a RGE, similar to the well-known RGE for partons. In Sec. , we show that momentum distributions at large Pрқ‘ғP, are related to PDFs through a matching between different orders of PвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ғPtoinfty and UV cut-off limits. This matching process has a standard EFT explanation: Parton physics or observables can be obtained from an effective theory in which PвүӘОӣUVmuch-less-thanрқ‘ғsubscriptОӣUVPllLambdarm UV are calculated non-perturbatively in the so-called рқ’«рқ’«cal P spacecitebib.bib363, after вҖңintegrating outвҖқ degrees of freedom between Pрқ‘ғP and вҲһinfty (or рқ’¬=1вҲ’рқ’«рқ’¬1рқ’«cal Q=1-cal P space) through perturbation theory. Therefore, the LaMET approach to partons is in some sense similar to lattice QCD as an EFT approach for continuum field theories, in which all active degrees of freedom (рқ’«рқ’«cal P space) are bounded by |k|вүӨПҖ/aрқ‘ҳрқңӢрқ‘Һ|k|leqpi/a, where aрқ‘Һa is lattice spacing, whereas those at |k|вүҘПҖ/aрқ‘ҳрқңӢрқ‘Һ|k|geqpi/a (рқ’¬рқ’¬cal Q space) are taken into account through perturbative coefficients and higher dimensional operators. In Sec. , we outline the formalism of LaMET for a general parton observable. The method can in principle be used also to calculate any LF correlations in terms of large momentum external states (see in particular the application to soft function in Sec. ). The strategy is also applicable for the components of the LF wave functions. Thus, LaMET offers a practical and systematic way to carry out the program of LFQ. Instead of working with the LF coordinates directly, one uses the instant form of dynamics and large momentum or boost factor Оірқӣҫgamma as a regulator for the LF divergences. In a certain sense, the quantization using tilted light-cone coordinatescitebib.bib318 is similar to the spirit of the LaMET approach. At present, the only systematic approach to solve non-perturbative QCD is lattice field theorycitebib.bib440. Therefore, a practical implementation of LaMET can be done through lattice calculations. It can also be done with other bound-state methods using Euclidean approaches, such as the instanton liquid modelcitebib.bib407. While LFQ may provide an attractive physical picture for the proton, the Euclidean equal-time formulation is more practical for carrying out the calculations, and LaMET serves to bridge them.
content_sections.II Large-Momentum Effective Theory II.1: In relativistic theories, the internal structure of a composite system is frame-dependent (we always refer to the total momentum eigenstates), and we are interested in the properties of the proton at a momentum much larger than its rest mass. We start from the quark momentum density in a fast-moving proton, assuming that it moves in the zрқ‘§z-direction. A straightforward definition is NPвҖӢ(kвҶ’)=вҲ‘ПғвҹЁP|bПғвҖ вҖӢ(kвҶ’)вҖӢbПғвҖӢ(kвҶ’)|Pвҹ©/вҹЁP|Pвҹ©,subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғвҶ’рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқңҺquantum-operator-productрқ‘ғsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҸвҖ рқңҺвҶ’рқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘ҸрқңҺвҶ’рқ‘ҳрқ‘ғinner-productрқ‘ғрқ‘ғdisplaystyle NP(veck)=sumsigma P|bdaggersigma(veck)bsigma(veck)|P/ P|P , where the quark helicity, color, and other implicit indices are summed over. This equation should be compared with the parton density in Eq. . To make it gauge invariant, it is convenient to consider the definition from a coordinate-space correlator, NP,WвҖӢ(kвҶ’)=вҲ«d3вҖӢОҫ(2вҖӢПҖ)3вҖӢeвҲ’iвҖӢkвҶ’вӢ…ОҫвҶ’вҖӢвҹЁP|ПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢОі0вҖӢWвҖӢ(0,ОҫвҶ’)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(ОҫвҶ’)|Pвҹ©,subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғрқ‘ҠвҶ’рқ‘ҳsuperscriptрқ‘‘3рқңүsuperscript2рқңӢ3superscriptрқ‘’вӢ…рқ‘–вҶ’рқ‘ҳвҶ’рқңүquantum-operator-productрқ‘ғВҜрқң“0superscriptрқӣҫ0рқ‘Ҡ0вҶ’рқңүрқң“вҶ’рқңүрқ‘ғdisplaystyle NP,W(veck)=intd3xiover(2pi)3 e-iveckcdotvecxi P|overlinepsigamma0W(0,vecxi)psi(vecxi)|P,, where the Dirac matrix Оі0superscriptрқӣҫ0gamma0 ensures that it is a number density. Clearly, it is a static quantity without time-dependence and can be calculated in Euclidean field theories, in contrast to Eq. for partons. The gauge invariance is ensured by the Wilson line WвҖӢ(0,ОҫвҶ’)рқ‘Ҡ0вҶ’рқңүW(0,vecxi) between the quark fields separated by ОҫвҶ’вҶ’рқңүvecxi, which is defined in the fundamental representation of the color SU group. There are infinitely many choices for the Wilson line, generating infinitely many momentum densities. For example, one can choose a straight-line link between 00 and ОҫвҶ’вҶ’рқңүvecxi. One can also let the Wilson line run from the fields along the zрқ‘§z-direction for a long distance (if not infinity) before joining them together along the transverse direction (a staple). For its obvious connection to the PDFs, we consider a transverse-momentum integrated, longitudinal-momentum distribution, NPвҖӢ(kz)subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§displaystyle NP(kz) =вҲ«d2вҖӢkвҶ’вҹӮвҖӢNP,WвҖӢ(kвҶ’)absentsuperscriptрқ‘‘2subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-tosubscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғрқ‘ҠвҶ’рқ‘ҳdisplaystyle=int d2veckperp NP,W(veck) =вҲ«dвҖӢz2вҖӢПҖвҖӢeвҲ’iвҖӢkzвҖӢzвҖӢвҹЁP|ПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢОі0вҖӢWвҖӢ(0,z)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(z)|Pвҹ©,absentрқ‘‘рқ‘§2рқңӢsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§рқ‘§quantum-operator-productрқ‘ғВҜрқң“0superscriptрқӣҫ0рқ‘Ҡ0рқ‘§рқң“рқ‘§рқ‘ғdisplaystyle=intdzover2pie-ikzz P|overlinepsigamma0W(0,z)psi(z)|P, where we ignore the question of convergence at large |kвҶ’вҹӮ|subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҳperpendicular-to|veckperp|. Now the gauge-link WвҖӢ(0,z)рқ‘Ҡ0рқ‘§W(0,z) is naturally taken as a straight-line, WвҖӢ(0,z)рқ‘Ҡ0рқ‘§displaystyle W(0,z) =expвҒЎ(вҲ’iвҖӢвҲ«0zAzвҖІвҖӢ(zвҖІ)вҖӢрқ‘‘zвҖІ)absentрқ‘–subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘§0superscriptрқҗҙsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІdifferential-dsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІdisplaystyle=exp(-iintz0Azprime(zprime)dzprime) =expвҒЎ(iвҖӢвҲ«вҲһ0AzвҖІвҖӢ(zвҖІ)вҖӢрқ‘‘zвҖІ)вҖӢexpвҒЎ(iвҖӢвҲ«zвҲһAzвҖІвҖӢ(zвҖІ)вҖӢрқ‘‘zвҖІ)absentрқ‘–subscriptsuperscript0superscriptрқҗҙsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІdifferential-dsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІрқ‘–subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘§superscriptрқҗҙsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІdifferential-dsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІdisplaystyle=exp(iint0inftyAzprime(zprime)dzprime)exp(iintinftyzAzprime(zprime)dzprime) =WвҖ вҖӢ(вҲһ,0)вҖӢWвҖӢ(вҲһ,z),absentsuperscriptрқ‘ҠвҖ 0рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§displaystyle=Wdagger(infty,0)W(infty,z),, where in the second line we have split the gauge link into two, going from zрқ‘§z to the infinity and coming back from the infinity to zero. We can define a вҖңgauge-invariantвҖқ quark field ОЁвҖӢ(ОҫвҶ’)=WвҖӢ(вҲһ,ОҫвҶ’)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(ОҫвҶ’),ОЁвҶ’рқңүрқ‘ҠвҶ’рқңүрқң“вҶ’рқңүdisplaystylePsi(vecxi)=W(infty,vecxi)psi(vecxi) , and the above density becomes, NPвҖӢ(kz)=вҲ«dвҖӢz2вҖӢПҖвҖӢeвҲ’iвҖӢkzвҖӢzвҖӢвҹЁP|ОЁВҜвҖӢвҖӢОі0вҖӢОЁвҖӢ(z)|Pвҹ©,subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§рқ‘‘рқ‘§2рқңӢsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§рқ‘§quantum-operator-productрқ‘ғВҜОЁ0superscriptрқӣҫ0ОЁрқ‘§рқ‘ғdisplaystyle NP(kz)=intdzover2pie-ikzz P|overlinePsigamma0Psi(z)|P , where again we have not considered UV divergences. The momentum distribution defined above has been called quasi-PDF, but it is really a physical momentum distribution in a proton of momentum Pрқ‘ғP. In the rest frame of the proton, NP=0вҖӢ(kz)subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғ0superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§NP=0(kz) is symmetric in positive and negative kzsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§kz, probably peaks around kz=0superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§0kz=0 and decays away as kzвҶ’ВұвҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§plus-or-minuskztopminfty. Due to the perturbative QCD effects, it decays algebraically at large kzsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§kz, instead of exponentially. Because of this property, the high moments of the distribution, вҲ«рқ‘‘kzвҖӢ(kz)nвҖӢN0вҖӢ(kz)differential-dsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§рқ‘ӣsubscriptрқ‘Ғ0superscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§int dkz(kz)nN0(kz) with n>0рқ‘ӣ0n>0, have the standard QFT UV divergences. As Pрқ‘ғP becomes non-zero and large, the peak NPвҖӢ(kz)subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§NP(kz) will be around ОұвҖӢPzрқӣјsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§alpha Pz, where Оұрқӣјalpha is a constant of order one. The density at negative kzsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§kz becomes smaller, but not zero. This is due to the so-called backward-moving particles from the large momentum kick in perturbation theory. For the same reason, the density at kz>Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§kz>Pz is not zero either. NPвҖӢ(kz)subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘ғsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§NP(kz) has a renormalization scale dependence because the quark fields must be renormalized. One can choose DR and modified minimal subtraction (MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS) scheme. Any other regularization scheme can be converted into this one perturbatively. For zвү 0рқ‘§0zneq 0, the only renormalization necessary is the quark wave function (with anomalous dimension ОіFsubscriptрқӣҫрқҗ№gammaF) in the Az=0superscriptрқҗҙрқ‘§0Az=0 gauge, because the linear divergence associated with the gauge link vanishes in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme. More extensive discussions on the renormalization issue, particularly about non-perturbative renormalization, will be made in the following section. As an example showing how the parton momentum density depends on Pрқ‘ғP, we depict in Fig. the quark wave function amplitude of a meson in the вҖҷt Hooft model (1+1 dimensional QCD with NcвҶ’вҲһвҶ’subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җNctoinfty)citebib.bib241 , the square of which yields the quark momentum density. In this model, a meson of momentum PОјsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқңҮPmu can be built as |PnОјвҹ©ketsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘ӣрқңҮdisplaystyle|Pnmu =вҲ«dвҖӢk2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ|P|[M(kвҲ’P,k)П•n+(k,P)displaystyle=intfracdk2pi|P|big[M(k-P,k)phin+(k,P) +MвҖ (k,kвҲ’P)П•nвҲ’(k,P)]|0вҹ©,displaystyleqquadqquad+Mdagger(k,k-P)phin-(k,P)big]|0,, where MвҖӢ(p,k)=вҲ‘idвҲ’piвҖӢbki/Ncрқ‘Җрқ‘қрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘–subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘–рқ‘қsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җM(p,k)=sumidi-pbki/sqrtNc, and MвҖ вҖӢ(p,k)=вҲ‘ibkiвҒЈвҖ вҖӢdвҲ’piвҒЈвҖ /Ncsuperscriptрқ‘ҖвҖ рқ‘қрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘–superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҳрқ‘–вҖ superscriptsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘қрқ‘–вҖ subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җMdagger(p,k)=sumibkidaggerd-pidagger/sqrtNc are annihilation and creation operators for quark-antiquark pairs. The corresponding wave function amplitudes, П•n+вҖӢ(k,P)superscriptsubscriptitalic-П•рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳрқ‘ғphin+(k,P) and П•nвҲ’вҖӢ(k,P)superscriptsubscriptitalic-П•рқ‘ӣрқ‘ҳрқ‘ғphin-(k,P), satisfy a pair of equations first derived incitebib.bib46. The meson bound state defined above has a well-defined large-momentum limit. The wave functions can be expanded in 1/P1рқ‘ғ1/P, with the corrections starting from (1/P)2superscript1рқ‘ғ2(1/P)2. The momenta of the constituents, kрқ‘ҳk and PвҲ’kрқ‘ғрқ‘ҳP-k, scale in this limit. When plotted as a function of x=k/Pрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҳрқ‘ғx=k/P, the change in the wave function with the magnitude of the momentum is shown in Fig. .
content_sections.II Large-Momentum Effective Theory II.2: In this subsection, we consider how to calculate the external momentum Pрқ‘ғP dependence of physical observables discussed in the previous subsection. Clearly, the dependence is related to the boost properties of the operators under consideration, namely their commutation relations with the boost generators, Kisuperscriptрқҗҫрқ‘–hatKi. We argue that in the large momentum limit, one has a momentum RGE which is a differential equation relating properties of the system at different momenta. Momentum RGE will be, in the end, related to the renormalization properties of the observables on the LF. Consider a generic operator Oрқ‘ӮhatO, and its matrix element in a state with momentum Pрқ‘ғP, OвҖӢ(P)=вҹЁP|O|Pвҹ©. рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғquantum-operator-productрқ‘ғрқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғdisplaystyle O(P)= P|hatO|P . We calculate the momentum dependence by writing |Pвҹ©=expвҒЎ(вҲ’iвҖӢПүвҖӢ(P)вҖӢK)вҖӢ|P=0вҹ©ketрқ‘ғрқ‘–рқң”рқ‘ғрқҗҫketрқ‘ғ0|P=exp(-iomega(P)hatK)|P=0, where KрқҗҫhatK is the boost operator along the momentum direction and Пүрқң”omega is a boost parameter depending on Pрқ‘ғP. Taking a derivative with respect to the boost parameter gives dвҖӢOвҖӢ(P)dвҖӢP=iвҖӢdвҖӢПүвҖӢ(P)dвҖӢPвҖӢвҹЁP|[O,K]|Pвҹ©. рқ‘‘рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғрқ‘‘рқ‘ғрқ‘–рқ‘‘рқң”рқ‘ғрқ‘‘рқ‘ғquantum-operator-productрқ‘ғрқ‘Ӯрқҗҫрқ‘ғdisplaystylefracdO(P)dP=ifracdomega(P)dP P|[hatO,hatK]|P . The r. h. s. of the equation depends on the commutator [O,K]рқ‘Ӯрқҗҫ[hatO,hatK], i. e. , the boost properties of the operator. For a scalar operator, the commutation relation vanishes, and OвҖӢ(P)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғO(P) is frame independent. For a vector operator, the commutation relation resembles that of an energy-momentum four-vector, and the result is the standard Lorentz transformation of a four-vector. For nonlocal operators, the commutation relation requires the elementary formula, [JОјвҖӢОҪ,П•iвҖӢ(x)]=iвҖӢ[lОјвҖӢОҪвҖӢОҙiвҖӢj+SiвҖӢjОјвҖӢОҪ]вҖӢП•jвҖӢ(x),superscriptрқҗҪрқңҮрқңҲsubscriptitalic-П•рқ‘–рқ‘Ҙрқ‘–delimited-superscriptрқ‘ҷрқңҮрқңҲsubscriptрқӣҝрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҶрқңҮрқңҲрқ‘–рқ‘—subscriptitalic-П•рқ‘—рқ‘Ҙdisplaystyle[Jmunu,phii(x)]=ileft[lmunudeltaij+Smunuijright]phij(x) , where lОјвҖӢОҪ=вҲ’iвҖӢ(xОјвҖӢвҲӮОҪвҲ’xОҪвҖӢвҲӮОј)superscriptрқ‘ҷрқңҮрқңҲрқ‘–superscriptрқ‘ҘрқңҮsuperscriptрқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘ҘрқңҲsuperscriptрқңҮlmunu!=!-i(xmupartialnu!-!xnupartialmu) is the OAM operator and SОјвҖӢОҪsuperscriptрқ‘ҶрқңҮрқңҲSmunu is the intrinsic spin matrix. Thus one of the fields is now П•iвҖӢ(t=sinhвҒЎПүвҖӢz,0,0,coshвҒЎПүвҖӢz)subscriptitalic-П•рқ‘–рқ‘Ўрқң”рқ‘§00рқң”рқ‘§phii(t=sinhomega z,0,0,coshomega z) which generates a time-dependent correlation function. In the large-momentum limit, because of asymptotic freedom, the Pрқ‘ғP-dependence is calculable in perturbation theory, and Eq. simplifies. One obtains the momentum or boost RGEcitebib.bib268, dвҖӢOвҖӢ(P)dвҖӢPрқ‘‘рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғрқ‘‘рқ‘ғdisplaystylefracdO(P)dP =limО”вҖӢPвҶ’0[OвҖӢ(P+О”вҖӢP)вҲ’OвҖӢ(P)]/О”вҖӢPabsentsubscriptвҶ’О”рқ‘ғ0delimited-рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғО”рқ‘ғрқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғО”рқ‘ғdisplaystyle=limDelta Pto 0[O(P+Delta P)-O(P)]/Delta P вҶ’Pвү«MCвҖӢ(ОұsвҖӢ(P))вҠ—OвҖӢ(P)+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(M2/P2). much-greater-thanрқ‘ғрқ‘ҖвҶ’absenttensor-productрқҗ¶subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ‘ғрқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғрқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘Җ2superscriptрқ‘ғ2displaystylexrightarrowPgg MC(alphas(P))otimesO(P)+cal O(M2/P2) . where CвҖӢ(ОұsвҖӢ(P))рқҗ¶subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ‘ғC(alphas(P)) is a perturbative expansion in the strong coupling Оұssubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ alphas. The symbol вҖңвҠ—tensor-productotimesвҖқ can be a simple multiplication or certain form of convolution, depending on the observable OвҖӢ(P)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ғO(P) studied. The proof of the above equation is non-trivial, and it can be analyzed on a case-by-case basis. There can be mixings among a set of independent operators with the same quantum numbers. The momentum RGEs are very similar to those for scale transformation or that for the coarse graining of a Hamiltonian. That the two are connected in some cases may be traced to Lorentz symmetry. As an example of the momentum RGE, we calculate the quark momentum distribution in a perturbative quark state using Eq. . Since it is gauge invariant, we can calculate it in any gauge, for example, the Feynman gauge. The one-loop diagrams in QCD are shown in Fig. . There are two sources of UV divergences, one is the logarithmic divergences from the vertex and self-energy diagrams, and the other is the linear divergence in the self-energy of the Wilson line. For the moment, we will use transverse momentum cut-off, ОӣUVsubscriptОӣUVLambdarm UV, as the UV regulator. Using y=kz/Pzрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§y=kz/Pz, the one-loop result reads for a large momentum quarkcitebib.bib443, qвҖӢ(y,Pz,ОӣUV)=ОұsвҖӢCF2вҖӢПҖsuperscriptрқ‘һ1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptОӣUVsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқңӢdisplaystyletildeq(y,Pz,Lambdarm UV)=fracalphasCF2pi Г—1+y21вҲ’yвҖӢlnвҒЎyyвҲ’1+1+ОӣUV(1вҲ’y)2вҖӢPz,y>11+y21вҲ’yвҖӢlnвҒЎ(Pz)2m2+1+y21вҲ’yвҖӢlnвҒЎ4вҖӢy1вҲ’yвҲ’4вҖӢy1вҲ’y+1+ОӣUV(1вҲ’y)2вҖӢPz,01 frac1+y21-ylnfrac(Pz)2m2+frac1+y21-ylnfrac4y1-y ,,,,-frac4y1-y+1+fracLambdarm UV(1-y)2Pz ,01рқ‘Ұ1y>1. The same gluon can also carry a momentum larger than Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, making the active quark have y<0рқ‘Ұ0y<0. вҖў While the above effect is easy to understand perturbatively, it is surprising that a scaling contribution remains outside [0,1] in the IMF. As the proton travels faster, one might think any constituent has a momentum kzsuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§kz positive from Lorentz transformation. However, the order of limits matters because no matter how large the parent-quark momentum is, there are always quarks with much larger momentum, i. e. , kzвү«Pzвү«ОӣQCDmuch-greater-thansuperscriptрқ‘ҳрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§much-greater-thansubscriptОӣQCDkzgg PzggLambdarm QCD. In this sense, FeynmanвҖҷs parton model does not describe the exact properties of the momentum distribution in a large-momentum nucleon. вҖў The contribution outside [0,1]01[0,1] at one-loop is entirely perturbative because of the absence of any infrared (IR) divergence. This is no longer true at two-loop level, but the contribution depends only on the same one-loop IR physics in [0,1]01[0,1]. вҖў The distribution for yрқ‘Ұy in [0,1] has a term depending on lnвҒЎPzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§ln Pz. This dependence reflects that the quark substructure is resolved as a function of Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, an interesting feature of boost. This dependence is perturbative in the sense that the derivative is IR safe, PzвҖӢdвҖӢqвҖӢ(y,Pz,ОӣUV)dвҖӢPzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘‘рқ‘һрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptОӣUVрқ‘‘superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§displaystyle Pzfracdtildeq(y,Pz,Lambdarm UV)dPz =ОұsвҖӢCFПҖвҖӢ[(1+y21вҲ’y)+вҲ’32вҖӢОҙвҖӢ(1вҲ’y)]. absentsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№рқңӢdelimited-subscript1superscriptрқ‘Ұ21рқ‘Ұ32рқӣҝ1рқ‘Ұdisplaystyle=fracalphasCFpileft[left(frac1+y21-yright)+-frac32delta(1-y)right],. Apart from the Оҙрқӣҝdelta-function term, the r. h. s. is similar to the one-loop quark splitting function in DGLAP evolutioncitebib.bib169, bib.bib216, bib.bib23. Therefore one might suspect that the momentum dependence is closely related to the familiar renormalization scale evolution in the PDFs. In fact, the physics is just the other way around: It is the hadron-momentum dependence of the physical momentum distribution that generates the DGLAP evolution in the infinite-momentum limit. One can derive an all-order momentum RGE for the momentum distribution function. Momentum RGE also provides a method to sum over the large logarithms of the momentum. вҖў There is a singularity at y=1рқ‘Ұ1y=1. This singularity is generated from soft-gluon radiation. Fortunately, this singularity combined with the virtual contribution yields a finite result. вҖў There is a linear divergence in the cut-off regulator, leading to ОӣUV/PzsubscriptОӣUVsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Lambdarm UV/Pz term, which is absent in DR. Thus, to keep 1/(Pz)21superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§21/(Pz)2 power counting, it is important to work in a renormalization scheme where this term does not exist. We can also move on to study the hadron momentum RGEs of other structural properties considered in the previous subsection. In particular, the RGE for TMD distributions will lead to the familiar rapidity RGE in the literature. We reserve these discussions to Sec. .
content_sections.II Large-Momentum Effective Theory II.3: As we have seen in the previous subsection, the momentum distributions of the constituents (now called quasi-PDFs in the literature) in a proton at large Pрқ‘ғP are different from the PDFs or LF distributions in many ways. In particular, the momentum fraction yрқ‘Ұy in a physical momentum distribution is not limited to [0,1] due to backward moving particles, which is the case even in the PвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘ғPtoinfty limit. In fact, the infinite-momentum limit is not analytical due to the presence of lnвҒЎPрқ‘ғln P. However, partons are effective objects arising from a different limit ОӣUVвүӘPвҶ’вҲһmuch-less-thansubscriptОӣUVрқ‘ғвҶ’Lambdarm UVll Ptoinfty. There is also an important computational advantage in taking the naive limit Pвү«ОӣUVmuch-greater-thanрқ‘ғsubscriptОӣUVPggLambdarm UV in perturbative calculations: Feynman integrals have one fewer four-momentum. Therefore, this limit of QFTs serves as a reference system where the structure of the bound states is manifestly independent of the hadron momentum, and is similar to scale-invariant critical points at which second-order phase transitions occur in condensed matter systems. However, the theory in the naive IMF limit introduces additional UV divergences. Therefore, the partons in QCD are very similar to the infinitely heavy quarks in HQETcitebib.bib355. In certain QCD systems, heavy quarks such as the bottom quark are present, and their masses are much larger than the typical QCD scale ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. In this case, one might study the dependence on the heavy quark mass by expanding around mQ=вҲһsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘„mQ=infty. This expansion will generally produce a power series in 1/mQ1subscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘„1/mQ. However, the limits of taking ОӣUVвҶ’вҲһвҶ’subscriptОӣUVLambdarm UVtoinfty and infinite heavy-quark mass limits are not interchangeable, due to the presence of the large logarithms lnвҒЎmQsubscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘„ln mQ. In an EFT approach, one takes the mQвҶ’вҲһвҶ’subscriptрқ‘ҡрқ‘„mQtoinfty limit first, this will result in a new theory with different UV behavior, but without the heavy-quark mass, and symmetries among very different heavy-quark systems become manifest. The renormalization of the extra UV divergences yields a RGE which can be used to resum large quark-mass logarithms. Therefore, the momentum distribution at large-Pрқ‘ғP differs from the parton distributions only in the order of limits, their IR non-perturbative physics is the same. In asymptotically free theories such as QCD, differences (or discontinuities) in taking the limits of Pвү«ОӣUVmuch-greater-thanрқ‘ғsubscriptОӣUVPggLambdarm UV and ОӣUVвү«PвҶ’вҲһmuch-greater-thansubscriptОӣUVрқ‘ғвҶ’Lambdarm UVgg Ptoinfty are perturbatively calculable, as only the high-momentum modes matter. The differences are called matching coefficients. Therefore, one is able to write down a power expansion for the momentum-dependent distribution (quasi-PDF) in terms of the PDFcitebib.bib443, bib.bib350, bib.bib349, bib.bib247, qвҖӢ(y,Pz,Ој)рқ‘һрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқңҮdisplaystyletildeq(y,Pz,mu)! =вҲ«вҲ’11dвҖӢx|x|вҖӢCвҖӢ(yx,ОјxвҖӢPz)вҖӢqвҖӢ(x,Ој)absentsuperscriptsubscript11рқ‘‘рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙрқҗ¶рқ‘Ұрқ‘ҘрқңҮрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘һрқ‘ҘрқңҮdisplaystyle=!int-11fracdx|x|Cleft(fracyx,fracmuxPzright)q(x,mu) +рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD2(yвҖӢPz)2,ОӣQCD2((1вҲ’y)вҖӢPz)2),рқ’ӘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscript1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2displaystyleqquadqquad+cal Obigg(fracLambdatextQCD2(yPz)2,fracLambdatextQCD2((1-y)Pz)2bigg),, where the power correction is suppressed by the parton momentum yвҖӢPzрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§yPz and the spectator momentum (1вҲ’y)вҖӢPz1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§(1-y)Pzcitebib.bib275. This expansion may be also called a factorization formula, as the quasi-PDF contains all the IR physics in the PDF, and Cрқҗ¶C involves only UV physics. As we will discuss extensively in the next section, this factorization formula is true to all orders in perturbation theory. The above relation allows us to calculate the LF parton physics from the momentum distribution at large Pрқ‘ғP. Since the expansion parameter is ОӣQCD2/(yвҖӢPz)2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2Lambdarm QCD2/(yPz)2 and ОӣQCD2/((1вҲ’y)вҖӢPz)2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscript1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2Lambdarm QCD2/((1-y)Pz)2 , for intermediate yрқ‘Ұy one might not need very large Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz to neglect the power corrections. The above relation between the two quantities has a simple explanation in terms of the Lorentz boost: Consider the spatial correlation along zрқ‘§z shown in Fig. in a large momentum state. It can be seen as approaching the LF one in the rest frame of the proton. In other words, we are using a near-LF correlation to approximate a LF correlation. Accordingly, we can invert the above equation recursively to express the PDF in terms of quasi-PDF with their differences being taken care of through the perturbative matching Cрқҗ¶tildeC and power corrections, qвҖӢ(x,Ој)рқ‘һрқ‘ҘрқңҮdisplaystyle q(x,mu)! =вҲ«вҲ’вҲһвҲһdвҖӢy|y|вҖӢCвҖӢ(xy,ОјyвҖӢPz)вҖӢqвҖӢ(y,Pz,Ој)absentsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұрқҗ¶рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰрқңҮрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘һрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқңҮdisplaystyle=!int-inftyinftyfracdy|y|tildeCleft(fracxy,fracmuyPzright)tildeq(y,Pz,mu) +рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD2(xвҖӢPz)2,ОӣQCD2((1вҲ’x)вҖӢPz)2). рқ’ӘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscript1рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2displaystyleqquadqquad+cal Obigg(fracLambdatextQCD2(xPz)2,fracLambdatextQCD2((1-x)Pz)2bigg),. The above equation has an EFT interpretation: The parton physics is calculated in an effective field theory with physical momentum scale from 00 to Pрқ‘ғP, whereas the physics from degrees of freedom from Pрқ‘ғP to вҲһinfty can be integrated out to generate the perturbative coefficients Cрқҗ¶tildeC and the high-order terms in 1/(Pz)21superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§21/(Pz)2. In constrast to HQET, the full QCD degrees of freedom are used in LaMET calculations. In other words, the effective Lagrangian of LaMET is the standard QCD one, while the large momentum Pрқ‘ғP for expansions appears only in the external states.
content_sections.II Large-Momentum Effective Theory II.4: The principle of LaMET is to simulate the time-dependence of parton observables through external states at large momentum. Thus, we can generalize the discussions in the previous subsection to any type of physical observables for the large momentum proton, which will be generally called quasi-parton observables. Examples will be given in the later sections including transverse-momentum dependent distributions and LF wave functions. Consider any Euclidean quasi-observable Oрқ‘ӮO which depends on a large hadron momentum Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz and UV cut-off ОӣUVвү«Pzmuch-greater-thansubscriptОӣUVsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Lambdarm UVgg Pz. Using asymptotic freedom, we can systematically expand the Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz dependence, OвҖӢ(Pz,ОӣUV)=ZвҖӢ(PzОӣUV,PzОј)вҠ—oвҖӢ(Ој)+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD2(Pz)2)+вҖҰ,рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptОӣUVtensor-productрқ‘Қsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptОӣUVsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқңҮрқ‘ңрқңҮрқ’ӘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2вҖҰdisplaystyle OBig(Pz,Lambdarm UVBig)=ZBig(PzoverLambdarm UV,PzovermuBig)otimeso(mu)+cal OBig(Lambdarm QCD2over(Pz)2Big)+. . . , where вҠ—tensor-productotimes refers to a convolution if appropriate, and Zрқ‘ҚZ factorizes all the perturbative dependence on Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz and does not contain any IR divergence. The quantity oвҖӢ(Ој)рқ‘ңрқңҮo(mu) is defined in a theory with PzвҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pzrightarrowinfty, exactly as in FeynmanвҖҷs parton model. In fact, oвҖӢ(Ој)рқ‘ңрқңҮo(mu) is a LF correlation containing all the IR collinear (and soft) singularities. The important point of the expansion is that it may converge at moderately large Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, say a few GeV, allowing access to quantities needed for very large Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz (a few TeV). One can also use the large boost-factor Оі=Pz/Mрқӣҫsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘Җgamma=Pz/M as the expansion parameter 1/Оі1рқӣҫ1/gamma. Momentum dependence of the quasi-observables can be studied through momentum RGEs. Defining the anomalous dimension through ОіPвҖӢ(Оұs)=1ZвҖӢвҲӮZвҲӮlnвҒЎPz,subscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ғsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 1рқ‘Қрқ‘Қsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§displaystylegammaP(alphas)=frac1Zfracpartial Zpartialln Pz , it follows that вҲӮOвҖӢ(Pz)вҲӮlnвҒЎPz=ОіPвҖӢ(Оұs)вҠ—OвҖӢ(Pz),рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§tensor-productsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ғsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§displaystylefracpartial O(Pz)partialln Pz=gammaP(alphas)otimes O(Pz),, up to power corrections. One can resum large logarithms involving Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz using the above equation. When taking PzвҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pzrightarrowinfty first in OвҖӢ(Pz)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§O(Pz) before a UV regularization is imposed, one recovers from Oрқ‘ӮhatO the light-cone operator oрқ‘ңhato, by construction. On the other hand, the physical matrix element is calculated at a large Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, with UV regularization such as the lattice cut-off imposed first. Thus the difference between the matrix elements of oрқ‘ңhato and Oрқ‘ӮhatO is a matter of the order of limits. This is the standard set-up for an EFT. The different limits do not change the IR physics. In fact, the factorization in terms of Feynman diagrams can be proved order by order as in the renormalization program, as discussed in the following section. The parton physics can be calculated more directly by reversing Eq. to produce an EFT expansion, oвҖӢ(Ој)=ZвҖӢ(PzОӣUV,PzОј)вҠ—OвҖӢ(Pz,ОӣUV)+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD2(Pz)2)+вҖҰ. рқ‘ңрқңҮtensor-productрқ‘Қsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptОӣUVsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқңҮрқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptОӣUVрқ’ӘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2вҖҰdisplaystyle o(mu)=tildeZBig(PzoverLambdarm UV,PzovermuBig)otimesOBig(Pz,Lambdarm UVBig)+cal OBig(Lambdarm QCD2over(Pz)2Big)+. . . . Thus, to compute any parton observable defined by an operator made of LF dynamical fields, oрқ‘ңhato, one constructs a time-independent version Oрқ‘ӮhatO which, under an infinite Lorentz boost, approaches oрқ‘ңhato. Then, one calculates the matrix element of Oрқ‘ӮhatO in a hadron with large momentum Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz using whatever approach (lattice QCD is an obvious choice for the time-independent operator Oрқ‘ӮhatO) and then uses Eq. to systematically approximate the parton observable. Usually the matrix element of Oрқ‘ӮhatO depends on Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz as well as all the lattice UV artifacts. In principle, the latter does not affect the EFT expansion and will be cancelled by the matching coefficient Zрқ‘ҚtildeZ and higher order terms in the expansion. However, in practical applications such as the quasi-PDF calculations, a nonperturbative renormalization is still necessary to remove all the power divergences to ensure a continuum limit.
content_sections.II Large-Momentum Effective Theory II.5: LaMET provides a framework to systematically compute partonic observables on the LF from the properties of a large-momentum proton. However, the relationship is not one-to-one. There can be infinitely many possible Euclidean operators in the large-momentum proton which generate the same LF observable. This is because the large-momentum physical states have built-in collinear (as well as soft) parton modes, and upon acting on a Euclidean operator, they help to project out the leading LF physics. All operators projecting out the same LF physics form a universality class. Accordingly, in the operator formulation for parton physics such as SCET, one uses LF operators to project out parton physics off the external states of any momentum, including P=0рқ‘ғ0P=0. The concepts such as universality class have been used in critical phenomena in condensed matter physics, where systems with different microscopic Hamiltonians can have the same scaling properties near their critical points. Critical phenomena correspond to the IR fixed points of the scale transformation, and are dominated by physics at long-distance scales. In the present case, parton physics arises from the infinite-momentum limit, P=вҲһрқ‘ғP=infty, which is a UV fixed point of the momentum RGEs. It is the longitudinal short distance (or large momentum) physics that is relevant at the fixed point. However, the short distance here does not mean everything is perturbative. The part that is non-perturbative characterizes the partonic structure of the proton. The critical region near P=вҲһрқ‘ғP=infty acts as a filter to select only the physics that is relevant, so universality classes emerge. In the case of unpolarized PDFs, the initial proposal in LaMET starts from the matrix element of the following operatorcitebib.bib267, O1вҖӢ(z)=ПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢОіzвҖӢWвҖӢ(0,z)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(z). subscriptрқ‘Ӯ1рқ‘§ВҜрқң“0superscriptрқӣҫрқ‘§рқ‘Ҡ0рқ‘§рқң“рқ‘§displaystyle O1(z)=overlinepsigammazW(0,z)psi(z) . However, one can equally start fromcitebib.bib393, bib.bib149, O2вҖӢ(z)=ПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢОі0вҖӢWвҖӢ(0,z)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(z),subscriptрқ‘Ӯ2рқ‘§ВҜрқң“0superscriptрқӣҫ0рқ‘Ҡ0рқ‘§рқң“рқ‘§displaystyle O2(z)=overlinepsigamma0W(0,z)psi(z) , and the leading contributions in the large-momentum expansion are the same. One can also consider any linear combination of the two. Incitebib.bib292, the calculations have been done with these two operators in the вҖҷt Hooft model, and the results have been compared at different hadron momenta. For lattice simulations, an important issue is about the operator mixing, which depends on specific choices of the operators in the universality class. Another example of Euclidean operators for PDFs is the current-current correlators in a pure space separation, O3вҖӢ(z)=JОјвҖӢвҖӢJОҪвҖӢ(z),subscriptрқ‘Ӯ3рқ‘§superscriptрқҗҪрқңҮ0superscriptрқҗҪрқңҲрқ‘§displaystyle O3(z)=JmuJnu(z) , where JОјsuperscriptрқҗҪрқңҮJmu is, for example, an electromagnetic current. This type of correlator was first considered incitebib.bib76, bib.bib37 for calculating pion DA, and recently has been suggested to calculate PDFs with generalized bilocal вҖңcurrentsвҖқcitebib.bib349. When the matrix elements are calculated in the large-momentum states, OвҖӢ(z)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘§O(z) falls into the same universality class as the operators in Eqs. and . Instead of using light quarks as the intermediate propagator in OвҖӢ(z)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘§O(z), one can have a number of other choices for LaMET applications, including scalarscitebib.bib9, bib.bib1 and heavy-quarkscitebib.bib165. One can also similarly work with quark bilinear operators in any physical gauge which become the light-front one in the large momentum limitcitebib.bib220. Another important example of universality class is the gluon helicity contribution to the spin of the proton, as we will discuss in detail in Sec. . The gluon spin operator EвҶ’Г—AвҶ’вҶ’рқҗёвҶ’рқҗҙvecEtimesvecA is gauge-dependent. However, in physical gauges where the transverse degrees of freedom are dynamical, its matrix element is the same in the large-momentum limit. Therefore, one can potentially choose different gauges to perform calculations at finite momentum on lattice, such as Coulomb gauge вҲҮвҶ’вӢ…AвҶ’=0вӢ…вҶ’вҲҮвҶ’рқҗҙ0vecnablacdotvecA=0, axial gauge Az=0superscriptрқҗҙрқ‘§0Az=0 or temporal gauge A0=0superscriptрқҗҙ00A0=0. Different gauge choices will have different UV properties (lnвҒЎPрқ‘ғln P) and hence different matching conditions. However, the IR part of the matrix element is the samecitebib.bib230. At a practical level, it is very useful to find which operator has the fastest convergence in the LaMET expansion. The current-current correlators use the light-quark propagator to simulate the light-like Wilson line (sometimes called light-ray). The quasi-PDF approach not only starts from a quantity with clear physical meaning (a momentum distribution), but also generates the needed Wilson line simply by rotating a space-like one, shown in Fig. . Thus, it is plausible that the quasi-PDF will provide mathematically the fastest large-Pрқ‘ғP convergence than the other choices.
content_sections.III Renormalization and Matching for PDF: In this section, we consider the LaMET application to calculating the simplest collinear PDFs, which have been most extensively studied in the literature so far. Although universality allows one to extract the collinear PDFs from the matrix elements of a wide class of operators evaluated at large momentum, we will focus on physical observables closely resembling the collinear PDFs, i. e. , the quark and gluon momentum distributions or the quasi-PDFs. We also discuss the coordinate-space factorization approach in which the pseudo-PDF and current-current correlators have been studied. We mainly review the technical progress made in renormalization and matching using the quasi-PDFs. The matching can be done in principle at the bare matrix elements level, since the factorization formula like Eq. is valid for both bare and renormalized momentum distributions. All the UV divergences in the bare quasi-PDF can be factorized into the matching coefficient Cрқҗ¶C, and the latter automatically renormalizes the bare lattice matrix elements, so the continuum limit can be taken afterwards. However, such a matching coefficient then has to be calculated in lattice perturbation theory, which is computationally challenging and converges slowlycitebib.bib320. More importantly, the quasi-PDF contains linear power divergence under UV cutoff regularization due to the Wilson line self-energycitebib.bib267, bib.bib443, which makes it impossible to take the continuum limit with fixed-order calculations in lattice perturbation theory. Though the latter problem can be improved by resumming the linear and possibly logarithmic divergences, it is usually preferred to nonperturbatively renormalize the quasi-PDFs on the lattice, after which a continuum limit can be taken and a perturbative matching can be done in the continuum theory. To this end, a thorough understanding of the renormalizability of Wilson-line operators defining the quasi-PDFs is required. In addition to renormalization, the applications of LaMET rely on the validity of the large-momentum factorization formulaEq. , which can be proven in perturbation theory to all orders by showing that the collinear divergences are the same in the momentum distributions and light-cone PDFs. We begin in Sec. with the proof of multiplicative renormalizability of the Wilson-line operators that define the quasi-PDFs. We first work in the continuum theory with MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme, and then generalize the conclusion to lattice theory. Next, in Sec. we outline the factorization theorem for momentum distributions to all orders in perturbation theory, and state the form of convolution between the matching coefficient and the PDF. In Sec. we show that the factorization theorem has an equivalent form in coordinate space, which can be used as an alternate route to extract PDFs from lattice matrix elements. Finally, we discuss the nonperturbative renormalization of quasi-PDFs on the lattice and their matching to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS PDF in Sec. .

The momentum distributions of the proton are defined from equal-time nonlocal Wilson line operators of the form in Eq. . In this subsection, we review the renormalization of these spacelike nonlocal operators (the renormalization of lightlike nonlocal operators defining the PDFs can be found incitebib.bib143, bib.bib132). We first discuss their renormalization in DR using an auxiliary field approach, followed by the discussion on similar gluon operators. We then consider power divergences in the momentum cutoff type of UV regularization. The conclusion is that they are all multiplicatively renormalizable with a finite number of mixings with other operators.We are interested in operators of the following kind, OО“вҖӢ(z)=ПҲВҜвҖӢ(z2)вҖӢО“вҖӢWвҖӢ(z2,вҲ’z2)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(вҲ’z2). subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§ВҜрқң“рқ‘§2О“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§2рқ‘§2рқң“рқ‘§2OGamma(z)=barpsibig(zover 2big)Gamma Wbig(zover 2,-zover 2big)psibig(!-!zover 2big) . Since the Wilson line WвҖӢ(z1,z2)рқ‘Ҡsubscriptрқ‘§1subscriptрқ‘§2W(z1,z2) is a path-ordered integral of gauge fields, it is not obvious that such operators are multiplicatively renormalizable. The renormalization of non-lightlike Wilson loops and Wilson lines has been studied in early literaturecitebib.bib173, bib.bib153, and the all-order proof of their multiplicative renormalizability was first made using diagrammatic methods incitebib.bib173, bib.bib153 and then the functional formalism of gauge theories incitebib.bib172. The same conclusion was conjectured to hold also for the quark bilinear operator OО“вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§OGamma(z), whose renormalization takes the following formcitebib.bib375, bib.bib245, bib.bib117, OО“BвҖӢ(z,Оӣ)=ZПҲ,zвҖӢ(Оӣ,Ој)вҖӢeОҙвҖӢmвҖӢ(Оӣ)вҖӢ|z|вҖӢOО“RвҖӢ(z,Ој),subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ӮрқҗөО“рқ‘§Оӣsubscriptрқ‘Қрқң“рқ‘§ОӣрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝрқ‘ҡОӣрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ӯрқ‘…О“рқ‘§рқңҮdisplaystyle OBGamma(z,Lambda)=Zpsi,z(Lambda,mu)edelta m(Lambda)|z|ORGamma(z,mu),, where вҖңBрқҗөBвҖқ and вҖңRрқ‘…RвҖқ stand for bare and renormalized operators respectively, and all the fields and couplings in OО“BвҖӢ(z,Оӣ)subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ӮрқҗөО“рқ‘§ОӣOBGamma(z,Lambda) are bare ones which depend on the UV cutoff ОӣОӣLambda. ОҙвҖӢmвҖӢ(Оӣ)рқӣҝрқ‘ҡОӣdelta m(Lambda) is the вҖңmass correctionвҖқ of the Wilson line, which includes all the linear power divergences of its self-energy. ZПҲ,zвҖӢ(Оӣ,Ој)subscriptрқ‘Қрқң“рқ‘§ОӣрқңҮZpsi,z(Lambda,mu) includes all the logarithmic divergences from wavefunction and vertex renormalizations. An early two-loop study of the quasi-PDF in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme indeed indicated the multiplicative renormalizability of OО“вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§OGamma(z)citebib.bib285. The first rigorous proof of Eq. was given in the auxiliary вҖңheavy quarkвҖқ field formalismcitebib.bib289, bib.bib214 which was used to prove the renormalizability of Wilson linescitebib.bib405, bib.bib210, bib.bib27, bib.bib172. This formalism is defined by extending the QCD Lagrangian to include the auxiliary вҖңheavy quarkвҖқ fields Q,QВҜрқ‘„ВҜрқ‘„Q,barQ and their gauge interaction, в„’=в„’QCD+QВҜ0вҖӢiвҖӢnzвӢ…D0вҖӢQ0,в„’subscriptв„’QCDвӢ…subscriptВҜрқ‘„0рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§subscriptрқҗ·0subscriptрқ‘„0displaystylecal L=cal Lrm QCD+barQ0inzcdot D0Q0,, where the subscript вҖң0вҖқ denotes bare quantities. nzОј=(0,0,0,1)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқңҮ0001nzmu=(0,0,0,1) is the direction vector of the spacelike Wilson line WвҖӢ(z,0)рқ‘Ҡрқ‘§0W(z,0), D0Ој=вҲӮОј+iвҖӢg0вҖӢA0Ојsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ·0рқңҮsuperscriptрқңҮрқ‘–subscriptрқ‘”0superscriptsubscriptрқҗҙ0рқңҮD0mu=partialmu+ig0A0mu, and Q0subscriptрқ‘„0Q0 is a color-triplet scalar Grassmann field in the fundamental representation of SU. The renormalizability of this auxiliary field theory has been proven using the standard functional techniques for gauge theoriescitebib.bib172. After fixing the covariant gauge and introducing the ghost fields, the theory including the auxiliary вҖңheavy-quarkвҖқ has a residual BRST symmetry, from which one can derive the Ward-Takahashi identities to show that all the UV divergences of the GreenвҖҷs functions can be subtracted with a finite number of local counterterms. In analogy, the same method has also been used to prove the all-order renormalization of HQET in perturbation theorycitebib.bib31. The heavy-quark-gluon vertex renormalization constant Z1QвҖӢQвҖӢgsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Қ1рқ‘„рқ‘„рқ‘”Z1QQg is related to Zgsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘”Zg through the Slavnov-Taylor identities of the auxiliary field theorycitebib.bib172, Zg=Z1QвҖӢQвҖӢgвҖӢZAвҲ’12вҖӢZQвҲ’1. subscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘”superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Қ1рқ‘„рқ‘„рқ‘”superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Қрқҗҙ12superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘„1displaystyle Zg=Z1QQgZA-1over 2ZQ-1,. The iвҖӢОҙвҖӢmрқ‘–рқӣҝрқ‘ҡidelta m can be regarded as the mass correction of the вҖңheavy quarkвҖқ except that it is imaginary. For Dirac fermions, the mass correction is logarithmically divergent and proportional to the bare mass, as a result of chiral symmetry; for HQET, the mass correction of the heavy quark is proportional to the UV cutoff, i. e. linearly divergent, which is also expected for the auxiliary field here. Since the proof of renormalizability for this auxiliary field theory is carried out in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme with DR (d=4вҲ’2вҖӢПөрқ‘‘42italic-Пөd=4-2epsilon), all power divergences vanish, so does ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m. Nevertheless, ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m may include рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD)рқ’ӘsubscriptОӣQCDcal O(LambdatextQCD) contributions due to the renormalon ambiguities which are known to exist in HQETcitebib.bib69, bib.bib56. Since the auxiliary field theory is renormalizable, the renormalization of the operator product in Eq. amounts to the renormalizations of the two вҖңheavy-to-lightвҖқ currents. Using the standard techniques in quantum field theorycitebib.bib135, one can show recursively that the overall UV divergence of the insertion of JBsuperscriptрқҗҪрқҗөJB into GreenвҖҷs functions is absorbed into a renormalization factor ZJsubscriptрқ‘ҚрқҗҪZJ to all orders in perturbation theory, JBsuperscriptрқҗҪрқҗөdisplaystyle JB =ZJвҖӢJR=ZПҲ1/2вҖӢZQ1/2вҖӢZVвҖӢJR,absentsubscriptрқ‘ҚрқҗҪsuperscriptрқҗҪрқ‘…superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Қрқң“12superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘„12subscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘үsuperscriptрқҗҪрқ‘…displaystyle=ZJJR=Zpsi1/2ZQ1/2ZV JR,, where ZVsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘үZV is the vertex renormalization constant of the вҖңheavy-to-lightвҖқ current. The renormalization of heavy-to-light currents in HQET has been calculated up to three-loop order in perturbative QCDcitebib.bib413, bib.bib386, bib.bib265, bib.bib124, bib.bib90. More recently, it has been argued that the anomalous dimension of the вҖңheavy-to-lightвҖқ current is identical to that in HQET to all orderscitebib.bib85, which is also the case for the вҖңheavy-to-gluonвҖқ current that will be discussed below, so the renormalization factors for the spacelike and timelike Wilson line operators should be exactly the same. This completes the proof of renormalizability of the gluon Wilson-line operators.One-loop renormalization. Now we demonstrate the above result by an explicit one-loop example. For the nonlocal Wilson-line operators to be multiplicatively renormalizable, it is important that all linear divergences associated with diagrams other than the Wilson line self-energy cancel out among themselves. The key to LaMET applications for collinear parton physics is the factorization formula that relates the quasi-PDFs to light-cone PDFscitebib.bib267. Here we use the perturbative properties of the matching coefficients to write the factorization form in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme in a way consistent with a direct EFT calculations of PDFs at any given xрқ‘Ҙxcitebib.bib247, bib.bib435 qiвҖӢ(x,Ој)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘–рқ‘ҘрқңҮdisplaystyle qi(x,mu)! =вҲ«вҲ’вҲһвҲһdвҖӢy|y|[вҲ‘jCqiвҖӢqj(xy,ОјyвҖӢPz)qj(y,Pz,Ој)displaystyle=!int-inftyinftyfracdy|y| bigg[!sumjtildeCqiqj!left(fracxy,fracmuyPzright)tildeqj(y,Pz,mu) +CqвҖӢg(xy,ОјyвҖӢPz)g(x,Pz,Ој)]+вӢҜ,displaystyleqquad+tildeCqgleft(fracxy,fracmuyPzright)tildeg(x,Pz,mu)bigg]+cdots , gвҖӢ(x,Ој)рқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮdisplaystyle g(x,mu)! =вҲ«вҲ’вҲһвҲһdвҖӢy|y|[вҲ‘jCgвҖӢq(xy,ОјyвҖӢPz)qj(y,Pz,Ој)displaystyle=!int-inftyinftyfracdy|y| bigg[!sumjtildeCgqleft(fracxy,fracmuyPzright)tildeqj(y,Pz,mu) +CgвҖӢg(xy,ОјyвҖӢPz)g(y,Pz,Ој)]+вӢҜ,displaystyleqquad+tildeCggleft(fracxy,fracmuyPzright)tildeg(y,Pz,mu)bigg]+cdots,, where i,jрқ‘–рқ‘—i,j runs over quark and anti-quark flavors. The вҖңвӢҜвӢҜcdotsвҖқ term includes mass corrections whose anayltical forms have been derived to all orders of M2/(Pz)2superscriptрқ‘Җ2superscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2M2/(Pz)2citebib.bib114, and higher-twist contributions of order рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD2/(xвҖӢPz)2,ОӣQCD2/((1вҲ’x)вҖӢPz)2)рқ’ӘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscript1рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2cal Obig(LambdatextQCD2/(xPz)2,LambdatextQCD2/((1-x)Pz)2big) (see Eq. ). All Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz-dependence on the right hand side cancels out, just like a renormalization scale. As we have explained in the Sec. , the above factorization is guaranteed on the physics ground because the difference between quasi-PDFs and light-cone PDFs is the order of limits in PzвҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pztoinfty and ОӣUVвҶ’вҲһвҶ’subscriptОӣUVLambdarm UVtoinfty, and the IR physics in both quantities must be the same. An all-order factorization proof for the quark quasi-PDF in perturbation theory was first given with a diagrammatical approachcitebib.bib350. The formula has also been derived using the operator product expansion (OPE) of nonlocal Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib247, bib.bib349, bib.bib435. Here we outline the diagrammatic proof similar tocitebib.bib350, showing that the collinear divergences of the quasi-PDFs do factorize and are equal to those of the light-cone PDFs. Since the collinear divergence is a concept in perturbation theory, we will show the factorization using a massless external quark state with lightlike momentum PОј=(Pz,0,0,Pz)superscriptрқ‘ғрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§00superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pmu=(Pz,0,0,Pz). While the proof is only for perturbative free quark states, the factorization formulas are widely believed to be true nonperturbatively as well. We use DR to regulate both UV and collinear divergences and only consider bare quantities, since UV renormalization does not change the leading collinear divergences. Before the analysis, we should mention that all the soft divergences cancel between the real and virtual contributions to the quasi-PDFs, as discussed in Sec. , thus we only need to focus on the collinear divergences. To obtain an intuitive understanding of the structure for collinear divergences, we start from the one-loop diagram in Fig. in the Feynman gauge. One key feature of the diagram is that while the gauge link probes the zрқ‘§z-component of the gluon field Az=(A+вҲ’AвҲ’)/2superscriptрқҗҙрқ‘§superscriptрқҗҙsuperscriptрқҗҙ2Az=left(A+-A-right)/sqrt2, only the A+superscriptрқҗҙA+ component (longitudinal polarization) contributes to the leading collinear divergence. While attaching a new collinear gluon to the gauge-link induces a power suppression from the link propagator of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1/Pz)рқ’Ә1superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§cal O(1/Pz), the A+superscriptрқҗҙA+ component of the collinear gluon radiated from fast-moving color charges receives enhancement from Lorentz boost factor Оірқӣҫgamma that compensates for the suppression. The above result can be generalized to all orders. Similar to the one-loop diagrams, in the leading region of collinear divergence there are an arbitrary number of longitudinally polarized A+superscriptрқҗҙA+ gluons, which are emitted dynamically from the fast-moving state instead of being put in by hand using the lightlike gauge link, in contrast to the standard collinear PDF. The existence of the A+superscriptрқҗҙA+ gluons clearly increases the level of complication in showing the equivalence of collinear divergences between the quasi- and light-cone PDFs. For simplification, from now on we choose to work in the light-cone gauge A+=0superscriptрқҗҙ0A+=0 to eliminate all the A+superscriptрқҗҙA+ gluons. The hвҖӢ(z,Pz)в„Һрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§tildeh(z,Pz) is a function of two independent variables zрқ‘§z and Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, and in LaMET analysis the relevant combinations are quasi-LF distance О»рқңҶlambda (see Fig. ) and Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz, hence hвҖӢ(О»,Pz)в„ҺрқңҶsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§tildeh(lambda,Pz) will be called quasi-LF correlation, which is distinguished from the LF correlation hвҖӢ(О»,Ој)в„ҺрқңҶрқңҮh(lambda,mu) below. Since we are interested in the relation between the quasi-LF correlation with the matrix element of the light-ray operator OОі+вҖӢ(О»вҖӢn)subscriptрқ‘ӮsuperscriptрқӣҫрқңҶрқ‘ӣOgamma+(lambda n), Eq. can also be obtained by using the light-ray operator expansion incitebib.bib42, bib.bib75, bib.bib76. Using OPE or short-distance expansion, the exact factorization formula for the gluon and singlet quark quasi-PDFs, which includes their mixings, has also been derived in coordinate space and studied at one-loop ordercitebib.bib435, bib.bib41. It is easy to see that the limits PzвҶ’вҲһвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pztoinfty in LaMET expansion and zвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘§0zto 0 in coordinate-space factorization, keeping finite О»=zвҖӢPzрқңҶрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§lambda=zPz, are equivalent. However, in practical lattice QCD calculations, one is limited by the largest momentum Pmaxzsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§maxPzrm max in a specific setup, and the two approaches start to differ. In LaMET systematic approximation, one should calculate hвҖӢ(z,Pmaxz)в„Һрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§maxtildeh(z,Pzrm max) with all possible zрқ‘§z or О»рқңҶlambda, but in practice the largest О»max=zmaxвҖӢPmaxzsubscriptрқңҶmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§maxlambdarm max=zrm maxPzrm max is limited by the lattice volume as well as data quality at large zрқ‘§z. Due to QCD confinement, hвҖӢ(z,Pmaxz)в„Һрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§maxtildeh(z,Pzrm max) has a correlation length вҲј1/ОӣQCDsimilar-toabsent1subscriptОӣQCDsim 1/Lambdarm QCD, leading to an exponential decay at large zрқ‘§zcitebib.bib275. Therefore, if zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max is sufficiently large (e. g. , the proton size вҲј1similar-toabsent1sim 1 fm) for hвҖӢ(z,Pmaxz)в„Һрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§maxtildeh(z,Pzrm max) to fall to almost zero, then the truncated Fourier transform of hвҖӢ(z,Pmaxz)в„Һрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§maxtildeh(z,Pzrm max) should converge quickly, and the truncation effects mainly affect results at small xвүІ1/О»maxless-than-or-similar-toрқ‘Ҙ1subscriptрқңҶmaxxlesssim 1/lambdarm max. If hвҖӢ(z,Pmaxz)в„Һрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§maxtildeh(z,Pzrm max) exhibits exponential decay but still has a nonzero value at zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm max, then one can perform a physically motivated extrapolation beyond zmaxsubscriptрқ‘§maxzrm maxcitebib.bib275 to do the Fourier transform, which removes the unphysical oscillation from truncation and only affects the small-xрқ‘Ҙx region. In the momentum space, one can use LaMET expansion to calculate the PDF point by point in xрқ‘Ҙx with systematic error controlled by ОӣQCD2/(xвҖӢPmaxz)2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘Ҙsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§max2Lambdarm QCD2/(xPzrm max)2 and ОӣQCD2/((1вҲ’x)вҖӢPmaxz)2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscript1рқ‘Ҙsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§max2Lambdarm QCD2/((1-x)Pzrm max)2, which gives the prediction for a cetain region of xрқ‘Ҙx, [xmin,xmax]subscriptрқ‘Ҙminsubscriptрқ‘Ҙmax[xrm min,xrm max], with a target error. In coordinate-space factorization, one expands hвҖӢ(О»,z2)в„ҺрқңҶsuperscriptрқ‘§2tildeh(lambda,z2) in z2вҖӢОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘§2subscriptsuperscriptОӣ2QCDz2Lambda2rm QCD. For the factorization formula to be valid, zрқ‘§z must remain in the perturbative region. For example, an estimate incitebib.bib275 gives zmaxвҲј0.3similar-tosubscriptрқ‘§max0.3zrm maxsim 0.3вҖ“0.40.40.4 fm. Although there have been observations that forming ratios of hвҖӢ(О»,z2)в„ҺрқңҶsuperscriptрқ‘§2tildeh(lambda,z2) may cancel the higher-twist contributions at z>0.4рқ‘§0.4z>0.4 fmcitebib.bib381, this cancellation needs be quantified for precision calculations. With a finite range of quasi-LF correlations, the PDFs can be extracted through modelling the xрқ‘Ҙx-dependence or more advanced techniques such as Bayesian analysiscitebib.bib89 or neural networkcitebib.bib299, bib.bib128, bib.bib160, which is similar to extracting the PDFs from experimental datacitebib.bib349, although quantifying the systematic error from fitting can be challenging. The coordinate-space factorization can also provide the extraction of the lowest moments of PDFscitebib.bib300, bib.bib414, bib.bib296, bib.bib206, where the main systematic error is controlled by z2вҖӢОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘§2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2z2Lambdarm QCD2. So far, there have been very limited studies about the comparison between quasi- and pseudo-PDF analysis citebib.bib61, bib.bib16. It remains to be seen how systematic errors in the two strategies are compared and contrasted.

The multiplicative renormalizability of the nonlocal Wilson-line operators for quasi-PDFs allows a nonperturbative renormalization on the lattice, after which the continuum limit can be taken. This is an important step in the application of LaMET. One way of doing so is to perform a mass subtraction of the Wilson line firstcitebib.bib375, bib.bib245, bib.bib117, bib.bib450, bib.bib214, bib.bib215, and then renormalize the remnant UV divergences with lattice perturbation theory or other nonperturbative schemes. Another scheme which has gained more popularity in recent years is the regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) schemecitebib.bib149, bib.bib420, bib.bib17, bib.bib115, bib.bib343. In the coordinate space approach where |z|вүӘОӣQCDвҲ’1much-less-thanрқ‘§superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD1|z|llLambdarm QCD-1, the ratios of quasi-LF correlations in different statescitebib.bib390, bib.bib381, bib.bib84, bib.bib326 have also been proposed as a renormalization scheme. At large zрқ‘§z, the RI/MOM and ratio schemes introduce extra nonperturbative effects at different levels, which may distort the IR property of the original quasi-LF correlations. Due to the suppression of long-range contributions by large Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz in the Fourier transform, this nonperturbative contamination mainly affects the end-point region in xрқ‘Ҙx-space, while the existing LaMET calculations with RI/MOM scheme at moderate xрқ‘Ҙx, for example incitebib.bib21, bib.bib329, suffers less from such systematics. Nevertheless, the above complication can be avoided by switching to the hybrid schemecitebib.bib275 where one utilizes the advantages of different schemes at short and large distances. In the following, we discuss the above schemes in order, with a particular focus on the hybrid renormalization scheme. Before we proceed, it should be noted that the current-current correlators incitebib.bib165, bib.bib76, bib.bib349 do not need or have simple renormalization on the lattice, though it might be more costly to simulate them. Besides, there is another distinct method based on a redefinition of the quasi-PDF with smeared fermion and gauge fields via the gradient flowcitebib.bib367. The smeared quasi-PDF is free from UV divergences and remains finite in the continuum limit, which can be perturbatively matched onto the PDFcitebib.bib369. Nevertheless, this method awaits to be implemented on the lattice.Since the mass correction ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m includes all the linear UV divergences, it is highly favored to nonperturbatively subtract it from the quasi-PDFs. It is well known that the Wilson line renormalization is related to the additive renormalization of the static quark-antiquark potential, i. e. , ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m, especially in the context of finite temperature field theory. For a rectangle-shaped Wilson loop of dimension LГ—Tрқҗҝрқ‘ҮLtimes T in the spatial and temporal directions, its vacuum expectation value for large Tрқ‘ҮT scales as limTвҶ’вҲһWвҖӢ(L,T)=cвҖӢ(L)вҖӢeвҲ’VвҖӢ(L)вҖӢT. subscriptвҶ’рқ‘Үрқ‘Ҡрқҗҝрқ‘Үрқ‘җрқҗҝsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘үрқҗҝрқ‘ҮdisplaystylelimTtoinftyW(L,T)=c(L)e-V(L)T,. The renormalized static potential is VRвҖӢ(L)=VвҖӢ(L)+2вҖӢОҙвҖӢm,superscriptрқ‘үрқ‘…рқҗҝрқ‘үрқҗҝ2рқӣҝрқ‘ҡdisplaystyle VR(L)=V(L)+2delta m,, and ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m can be fixed by imposing the condition VRвҖӢ(L0)=0superscriptрқ‘үрқ‘…subscriptрқҗҝ00VR(L0)=0 for a particular value of L0subscriptрқҗҝ0L0. Alternatively, one can also fit ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m from the famous string potential model, VвҖӢ(L)=ПғвҖӢLвҲ’ПҖ12вҖӢLвҲ’2вҖӢОҙвҖӢm. рқ‘үрқҗҝрқңҺрқҗҝрқңӢ12рқҗҝ2рқӣҝрқ‘ҡdisplaystyle V(L)=sigma L-piover 12L-2delta m,. Apart from using the static potential to determine ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m, it was also proposed to calculate this quantity in the auxiliary вҖңheavy quarkвҖқ field theory with the following conditioncitebib.bib214, ОҙвҖӢm=ddвҖӢzвҖӢlnвҒЎTrвҖӢвҹЁQвҖӢ(x+zвҖӢnz)вҖӢQВҜвҖӢ(x)вҹ©QCD+Q|z=z0. рқӣҝрқ‘ҡevaluated-atрқ‘‘рқ‘‘рқ‘§Trsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘„рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§ВҜрқ‘„рқ‘ҘQCDрқ‘„рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘§0displaystyledelta m=dover dzlnrm TrbigQ(x+znz)barQ(x)bigrm QCD+QBig|z=z0,. Other suggestions have also been made for a nonperturbative calculation of ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta mcitebib.bib275. For example, one can investigate the asymptotic large-zрқ‘§z behavior of the hadron matrix element or the single quark GreenвҖҷs function, of the vacuum expectation value of OО“вҖӢ(z,a)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§рқ‘ҺOGamma(z,a) in a fixed gauge. The ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m calculated from all these matrix elements will have the following dependence on the lattice spacing aрқ‘Һa, ОҙвҖӢm=mвҲ’1вҖӢ(a)/a+m0,рқӣҝрқ‘ҡsubscriptрқ‘ҡ1рқ‘Һрқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҡ0delta m=m-1(a)/a+m0 , where mвҲ’1subscriptрқ‘ҡ1m-1(a) is the coefficient of the power divergence which is independent of the specific matrix element, while m0вҲјOвҖӢ(ОӣQCD)similar-tosubscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘ӮsubscriptОӣQCDm0sim O(Lambdarm QCD) is finite and depends on the external state. The determination of m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 can be rather nontrivial, and in practical calculations one could adopt a fine-tuning method, such as that for the Wilson-fermion mass, to find the critical value of m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 at which the final result converges fastest in the large Pzsuperscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz limit. After the Wilson-line mass subtraction, there are still logarithmic UV divergences in OО“вҖӢ(z,a)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§рқ‘ҺOGamma(z,a). One can use lattice perturbation theory to match ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m-subtracted OО“вҖӢ(z,a)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§рқ‘ҺOGamma(z,a) to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS schemecitebib.bib245, bib.bib444, bib.bib149, but the convergence still needs to be examined at higher orders. Incitebib.bib214, bib.bib215, the logarithmic divergences were nonperturbatively renormalized with RI/MOM-like schemes. The Wilson-line mass-subtraction has been implemented on the lattice incitebib.bib375, bib.bib450, bib.bib214, bib.bib454, bib.bib16.The RI/MOM scheme has been widely used in lattice QCD for the renormalization of local composite quark operators that are free from power-divergent mixings citebib.bib359. It is essentially a momentum subtraction scheme in QFT and can be nonperturbatively implemented on the lattice. For an arbitrary composite quark bilinear operator OBsuperscriptрқ‘ӮрқҗөOB that is multiplicatively renormalized as OB=ZOвҖӢORsuperscriptрқ‘Ӯрқҗөsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Ӯрқ‘…OB=ZOOR, the RI/MOM scheme is defined by imposing the following condition on its off-shell quark matrix element at a subtraction scale ОјRsubscriptрқңҮрқ‘…mutiny R, ZOвҲ’1вҖӢвҹЁp|OB|pвҹ©|p2=вҲ’ОјR2=вҹЁp|O|pвҹ©tree. evaluated-atsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Қ1рқ‘Ӯquantum-operator-productрқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘Ӯрқҗөрқ‘қsuperscriptрқ‘қ2superscriptsubscriptрқңҮрқ‘…2subscriptquantum-operator-productрқ‘қрқ‘Ӯрқ‘қtreedisplaystyle Z-1O p|OB|pBig|p2=-mutiny R2= p|O|prm tree,. where the subscript вҖңtreeвҖқ means the tree-level matrix element in perturbation theory. In this sense, ZOsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘ӮZO is not literally nonperturbative, but an all-order calculable quantity. Since the nonlocal quark bilinear operator OО“вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§OGamma(z) has been proven to be multiplicatively renormalizable in the coordinate space, one can also renormalize it in the RI/MOM scheme and then match the result to PDF in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS schemecitebib.bib149, bib.bib420.
