content_sections.I Introduction: Many precision phenomenology and theoretical predictions for hadron colliders rely on accurate estimates of the uncertainty in Standard-Model (SM) predictions. Among these predictions, the parton distribution functions (PDFs), the nonperturbative functions quantifying probabilities for finding quarks and gluons in hadrons with particular momentum fraction, are particularly important inputs in high-energy scatteringcitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11. The gluon PDF gвҖӢ(x)рқ‘”рқ‘Ҙg(x) needs to be known precisely to calculate the cross section for these processes in pвҖӢpрқ‘қрқ‘қpp collisions, such as the cross section for Higgs-boson production and jet production at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC)citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, and direct J/ПҲрқҗҪрқң“J/psi photoproduction at Jefferson Labcitebib.bib14. The future U. S. -based Electron-Ion Collider (EIC)citebib.bib15, planned to be built at Brookhaven National Lab, will further our knowledge of the gluon PDFcitebib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18. In Asia, the Electron-Ion Collider in China (EicC)citebib.bib19 is also planned to impact the gluon and sea-quark distributions. Although significant efforts to extract the gluon distribution gвҖӢ(x)рқ‘”рқ‘Ҙg(x) have been made in the last decade, there are still problems in obtaining a precise gвҖӢ(x)рқ‘”рқ‘Ҙg(x) in the large-xрқ‘Ҙx. Lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) is a nonperturbative theoretical method for calculating QCD quantities that has full systematic control. Calculations of xрқ‘Ҙx-dependent hadron structure in lattice QCD have multiplied since the proposal of Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22. Many lattice works have been done on nucleon and meson PDFs, and generalized parton distributions (GPDs) based on the quasi-PDF approachcitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55. Alternative approaches to lightcone PDFs in lattice QCD are the Compton-amplitude approach (or вҖңOPE without OPEвҖқ)citebib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, the вҖңhadronic-tensor approachвҖқcitebib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, the вҖңcurrent-current correlatorвҖқcitebib.bib63, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib48, bib.bib79, bib.bib80 and the pseudo-PDF approachcitebib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib90, bib.bib78, bib.bib91, bib.bib92, bib.bib93, bib.bib94, bib.bib95, bib.bib96, bib.bib97. A few works have started to include lattice-QCD systematics, such as finite-volume effects, in their calculationscitebib.bib39, bib.bib80. However, most these calculations are still, at the current stage, done with a single lattice spacing. Most lattice calculations of PDFs use next-to-leading-order (NLO) matching or, equivalently, NLO Wilson coefficientscitebib.bib98, bib.bib99, bib.bib22, bib.bib100, and some lattice calculations of the valence pion PDFcitebib.bib101 have incorporated NNLO matchingcitebib.bib102, bib.bib46. More work is needed to reduce high-twist systematics and improve the lattice determination of small-xрқ‘Ҙx and antiquark PDFs with very large boost momenta. Recently, progress has been made in the most-calculated isovector quark distribution of nucleon by MSULatcitebib.bib49, ETMCcitebib.bib51 and HadStruc Collaborationscitebib.bib103, who studied lattice-spacing dependence. MSULat studied three lattice spacings (0.09, 0.12 and 0.15fm) and pion masses (135, 220, 310MeV) and performed a simultaneous continuum-physical extrapolation using a third-order zрқ‘§z-expansion on renormalized LaMET matrix elementscitebib.bib49 with nucleon boost momenta around 2.2 and 2.6GeV. ETMC also uses three lattice spacings, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.09fm, but with heavier pion mass (370MeV) and investigated the continuum extrapolation of the data on renormalized LaMET matrix elements with boost momentum around 1.8GeVcitebib.bib51. HadStruc Collaboration studied three lattice spacings, 0.048, 0.065, and 0.075fm with two-flavor 440-MeV lattice ensembles using the continuum pseudoвҖ“Ioffe-time distribution (ITD)citebib.bib103. Most of the works above found mild nonzero dependence on lattice spacing (varying with the Wilson-link displacement) in the nucleon case for LaMET or pseudo-ITD matrix elements. In contrast with the quark PDFs, the gluon PDFs calculations are less calculated, due to their notoriously noisier matrix elements on the lattice. To date, there have only been a few exploratory gluon-PDF calculations for unpolarized nucleoncitebib.bib36, bib.bib92, bib.bib95, pioncitebib.bib96 and kaoncitebib.bib104, and polarized nucleoncitebib.bib97 using the pseudo-PDFcitebib.bib105 and quasi-PDFcitebib.bib106, bib.bib38 methods. Most of these calculations, like many exploratory lattice calculations, are done only using one lattice spacing at heavy pion mass. In this work, we report the first continuum-limit unpolarized gluon PDF of nucleon study using three lattice spacings: 0.09, 0.12 and 0.15fm with pion mass ranging from 220 to 700MeV using the pseudo-PDF method. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: In Sec. , we present the procedure for how the lattice correlators are calculated and analyzed to extract the ground-state matrix elements for the pseudo-PDF method. We then study the lattice-spacing dependence of matrix elements at 310 and 700-MeV pion mass in Sec. , checking both OвҖӢ(a)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҺO(a) and OвҖӢ(a2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2O(a2) forms using multiple continuum-extrapolation strategies. We perform a physical-continuum extrapolation to obtain continuum reduced pseudo-ITDs (RpITDs) matrix element, before final determination of the nucleon unpolarized gluon PDF xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) is obtained from the xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)/вҹЁxвҹ©gрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”xg(x)/ xg and вҹЁxвҹ©gsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘” xg results. Using the gluon momentum fraction calculated on the same ensemble, we obtain the gluon PDF and compare with the phenomenological global-fit PDF results. We consider the quark-mixing systematics, but they are found to be small. The final conclusion and future outlook can be found in Sec. .
content_sections.II Lattice Setup, Correlators and Matrix: This calculation is carried out using four ensembles with Nf=2+1+1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘“211Nf=2+1+1 highly improved staggered quarks (HISQ)citebib.bib107, generated by the MILC Collaborationcitebib.bib108, with three different lattice spacings (aвүҲ0.9рқ‘Һ0.9aapprox 0.9, 0.12 and 0.15fm) and three pion masses (220, 310, 690MeV); see Table for more details. We apply five steps of hypercubic (HYP) smearingcitebib.bib109 to the gauge links to reduce short-distance noise. Wilson-clover fermions are used in the valence sector, and the valence-quark masses are tuned to reproduce the lightest light and strange sea pseudoscalar meson masses (which correspond to pion masses 310 and 690MeV, respectively). A similar setup is used by PNDME collaborationcitebib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112, bib.bib113, bib.bib114, bib.bib115, bib.bib116, bib.bib117, bib.bib118, bib.bib119, bib.bib120, bib.bib121 with local operators, such as isovector and flavor-diagonal charges, form factors and moments; the results from this mixed-action setup are consistent with the same physical quantities calculated using different fermion actionscitebib.bib122, bib.bib123, bib.bib55, bib.bib124, bib.bib125, bib.bib126. On each lattice configuration, we calculate the nucleon two-point correlators using multiple sources: CN2ptвҖӢ(Pz;t)=вҹЁ0|О“вҖӢвҲ«d3вҖӢyвҖӢeвҲ’iвҖӢyвҖӢPzвҖӢПҮвҖӢ(yвҶ’,t)вҖӢПҮвҖӢ(0вҶ’,0)|0вҹ©,superscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқ‘Ғ2ptsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘Ўquantum-operator-product0О“superscriptрқ‘‘3рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқң’вҶ’рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ўрқң’вҶ’000CNtext2pt(Pz;t)= 0|Gammaint d3y,e-iyPzchi(vecy,t)chi(vec0,0)|0, with the nucleon interpolation operator ПҮрқң’chi as ПөlвҖӢmвҖӢnвҖӢ[uвҖӢ(y)lTвҖӢiвҖӢОі4вҖӢОі2вҖӢОі5вҖӢdmвҖӢ(y)]вҖӢunвҖӢ(y)superscriptitalic-Пөрқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡрқ‘ӣdelimited-рқ‘ўsuperscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ұрқ‘ҷрқ‘Үрқ‘–subscriptрқӣҫ4subscriptрқӣҫ2subscriptрқӣҫ5superscriptрқ‘‘рқ‘ҡрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ўрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ұepsilonlmn[u(y)lTigamma4gamma2gamma5dm(y)]un(y) (where l,m,nрқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡрқ‘ӣl,m,n are color indices, uвҖӢ(y)рқ‘ўрқ‘Ұu(y) and dвҖӢ(y)рқ‘‘рқ‘Ұd(y) are quark fields), the projection operator О“=12вҖӢ(1+Оі4)О“121subscriptрқӣҫ4Gamma=frac12(1+gamma4), tрқ‘Ўt is lattice Euclidean time, and Pzsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz is the nucleon boost momentum along the spatial zрқ‘§z-direction. We use Gaussian momentum smearingcitebib.bib127 on the quark field to improve the signal for nucleon boost momenta up to 3.0GeV. Hundreds of thousands of measurements are made, varying for different ensembles. Compared to our previous nucleon gluon PDF calculation on one a12m310 ensemble with 105superscript105105 measurementscitebib.bib92, this study uses more measurements and varies the lattice spacing. We then calculate the three-point gluon correlator by combining the gluon loop with nucleon two-point correlators, CN3ptвҖӢ(z,Pz;tsep,t)=вҹЁ0|О“вҖӢвҲ«d3вҖӢyвҖӢeвҲ’iвҖӢyвҖӢPzвҖӢПҮвҖӢ(yвҶ’,tsep)вҖӢрқ’ӘgвҖӢ(z,t)вҖӢПҮвҖӢ(0вҶ’,0)|0вҹ©,superscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқ‘Ғ3ptрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘Ўsepрқ‘Ўquantum-operator-product0О“superscriptрқ‘‘3рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘–рқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқң’вҶ’рқ‘Ұsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepsubscriptрқ’Әрқ‘”рқ‘§рқ‘Ўрқң’вҶ’000CNtext3pt(z,Pz;ttextsep,t)= 0|Gammaint d3y,e-iyPzchi(vecy,ttextsep)cal Og(z,t)chi(vec0,0)|0, where tрқ‘Ўt is the gluon-operator insertion time, tsepsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepttextsep is the source-sink time separation. рқ’ӘgвҖӢ(z,t)subscriptрқ’Әрқ‘”рқ‘§рқ‘Ўcal Og(z,t) is the gluon operator introduced in Ref. citebib.bib105: рқ’ӘвҖӢ(z)вүЎвҲ‘iвү z,tрқ’ӘвҖӢ(FtвҖӢi,FtвҖӢi;z)вҲ’14вҖӢвҲ‘i,jвү z,tрқ’ӘвҖӢ(FiвҖӢj,FiвҖӢj;z),рқ’Әрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘–рқ‘§рқ‘Ўрқ’Әsuperscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘–superscriptрқҗ№рқ‘Ўрқ‘–рқ‘§14subscriptformulae-sequenceрқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘§рқ‘Ўрқ’Әsuperscriptрқҗ№рқ‘–рқ‘—superscriptрқҗ№рқ‘–рқ‘—рқ‘§cal O(z)equivsumineq z,tcal O(Fti,Fti;z)-frac14sumi,jneq z,tcal O(Fij,Fij;z), where the operator рқ’ӘвҖӢ(FОјвҖӢОҪ,FОұвҖӢОІ;z)=FОҪОјвҖӢ(z)вҖӢUвҖӢ(z,0)вҖӢFОІОұвҖӢрқ’Әsuperscriptрқҗ№рқңҮрқңҲsuperscriptрқҗ№рқӣјрқӣҪрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptрқҗ№рқңҮрқңҲрқ‘§рқ‘Ҳрқ‘§0subscriptsuperscriptрқҗ№рқӣјрқӣҪ0cal O(Fmunu,Falphabeta;z)=Fmunu(z)U(z,0)Falphabeta, and zрқ‘§z is the Wilson link length. To extract the ground-state matrix element, we use a two-state fit on the two-point correlators and a two-sim fit on the three-point correlators: CN2ptвҖӢ(Pz,t)=|AN,0|2вҖӢeвҲ’EN,0вҖӢt+|AN,1|2вҖӢeвҲ’EN,1вҖӢt+вҖҰ,superscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқ‘Ғ2ptsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ02superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ0рқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ12superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ1рқ‘ЎвҖҰCNtext2pt(Pz,t)= |AN,0|2e-EN,0t+|AN,1|2e-EN,1t+ldots, CN3ptsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқ‘Ғ3ptdisplaystyle CNtext3pt (z,Pz,t,tsep)=рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepabsentdisplaystyle(z,Pz,t,ttextsep)= |AN,0|2вҖӢвҹЁ0|Og|0вҹ©вҖӢeвҲ’EN,0вҖӢtsepsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ02quantum-operator-product0subscriptрқ‘Ӯрқ‘”0superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ0subscriptрқ‘Ўsepdisplaystyle|AN,0|2 0|Og|0 e-EN,0ttextsep +displaystyle+ |AN,0|вҖӢ|AN,1|вҖӢвҹЁ0|Og|1вҹ©вҖӢeвҲ’EN,1вҖӢ(tsepвҲ’t)вҖӢeвҲ’EN,0вҖӢtsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ0subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ1quantum-operator-product0subscriptрқ‘Ӯрқ‘”1superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ1subscriptрқ‘Ўsepрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ0рқ‘Ўdisplaystyle|AN,0||AN,1| 0|Og|1 e-EN,1(ttextsep-t)e-EN,0t +displaystyle+ |AN,0|вҖӢ|AN,1|вҖӢвҹЁ1|Og|0вҹ©вҖӢeвҲ’EN,0вҖӢ(tsepвҲ’t)вҖӢeвҲ’EN,1вҖӢtsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ0subscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ1quantum-operator-product1subscriptрқ‘Ӯрқ‘”0superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ0subscriptрқ‘Ўsepрқ‘Ўsuperscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ1рқ‘Ўdisplaystyle|AN,0||AN,1| 1|Og|0 e-EN,0(ttextsep-t)e-EN,1t +displaystyle+ |AN,1|2вҖӢвҹЁ1|Og|1вҹ©вҖӢeвҲ’EN,1вҖӢtsepsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғ12quantum-operator-product1subscriptрқ‘Ӯрқ‘”1superscriptрқ‘’subscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғ1subscriptрқ‘Ўsepdisplaystyle|AN,1|2 1|Og|1 e-EN,1ttextsep +displaystyle+ вҖҰ,вҖҰdisplaystyleldots, where the |AN,i|2superscriptsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘Ғрқ‘–2|AN,i|2 and EN,isubscriptрқҗёрқ‘Ғрқ‘–EN,i are the ground-state (i=0рқ‘–0i=0) and first excited state (i=1рқ‘–1i=1) amplitude and energy, respectively. To visualize our fitted matrix-element extraction, we compare to ratios of the three-point to the two-point correlator RNвҖӢ(z,Pz,tsep,t)=CN3ptвҖӢ(z,Pz,t,tsep)CN2ptвҖӢ(Pz,t). subscriptрқ‘…рқ‘Ғрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘Ўsepрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқ‘Ғ3ptрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘Ўsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepsuperscriptsubscriptрқҗ¶рқ‘Ғ2ptsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§рқ‘ЎRN(z,Pz,ttextsep,t)=fracCNtext3pt(z,Pz,t,ttextsep)CNtext2pt(Pz,t). The left-hand side of Fig. shows example ratios for the gluon matrix elements from all four ensembles at pion masses MПҖвҲҲ220,310subscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢ220310Mpiin220,310MeV at selected momenta Pzsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz and Wilson-line length zрқ‘§z. The left column shows the ratio plots with data points of Rрқ‘…R from different source-sink separation, tsepsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepttextsep, along with the reconstructed bands from the fit, showing how well the fit describing the data in Eq. ; the final ground-state matrix elements are shown in grey bands. We observe that the ratios increase with increasing source-sink separation tsepsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepttextsep and continuously to approach the ground-state matrix elements obtained from the simultaneous two-state fit to three-point correlators with five inputs of tsepsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepttextsep. The middle and right columns of Fig. show how the ground-state matrix elements vary with tsepminsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepminttextseptextmin and tsepmaxsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepmaxttextseptextmax with the same tsepmaxsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepmaxttextseptextmax and tsepminsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepminttextseptextmin used in the left column, respectively. The grey bands in each plot of the middle and right columns are the same ground-state matrix elements extracted from the fit shown in the corresponding plots in left column; these demonstrate how stable our ground-state matrix element extractions are. We observe that overall the ground-state matrix elements are consistent with each other within one standard deviation with different tsepminsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepminttextseptextmin choices. There seems to be some hint of tsepmaxsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepmaxttextseptextmax-dependence in the ground-state matrix elements, but they do converge. Taking the a12m220 ensemble as an example, we observe larger fluctuations in the matrix-element extractions when small tsepmin=3superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepmin3ttextseptextmin=3, or small tsepmax=6superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepmax6ttextseptextmax=6 and 7, are used. The ground-state matrix element extracted from two-sim fits comes into reasonable agreement when tsepmin>5superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepmin5ttextseptextmin>5 and tsepmax>8superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ўsepmax8ttextseptextmax>8. Similar results are observed with the nucleon when calculated at strange-quark mass, as shown in Fig. .
content_sections.III Results and Discussions III.1 Lattic: Using the nucleon ground-state matrix elements, we can now compute the reduced Ioffe-time pseudo-distribution (RpITD)citebib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib106, bib.bib128 в„івҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)=в„івҖӢ(zвҖӢPz,z2)/в„івҖӢ(0вӢ…Pz,0)в„івҖӢ(zвӢ…0,z2)/в„івҖӢ(0вӢ…0,0),в„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2в„ірқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘§2в„івӢ…0subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§0в„івӢ…рқ‘§0superscriptрқ‘§2в„івӢ…000mathscrM(nu,z2)=fracM(zPz,z2)/M(0cdot Pz,0)M(zcdot 0,z2)/M(0cdot 0,0), where Ioffe time ОҪ=zвҖӢPzрқңҲрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§nu=zPz, and в„івҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)в„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2M(nu,z2) are the nucleon matrix elements at boost momentum Pzsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz and gluon operators with Wilson displacement zрқ‘§z. By construction, the renormalization of рқ’ӘвҖӢ(z)рқ’Әрқ‘§cal O(z) and kinematic factors are cancelled in the RpITDs, and the ultraviolet divergences are removed. The RpITD double ratios employed here are normalized to one at z=0рқ‘§0z=0, and the lattice systematics are reduced due to the double ratio. These RpITDs will be input into the pseudo-PDF framework detailed in Ref. citebib.bib81 to obtain the unpolarized nucleon gluon PDFs. We first examine the pion-mass and lattice-spacing dependence of the nucleon gluon RpITDs. The top panel of Fig. shows the RpITDs at boost momentum around 1.3GeV as functions of Ioffe time ОҪрқңҲnu for the a12m220, a09m310, a12m310, and a15m310 ensembles. Note that given the different size of the lattice dimensions and lattice spacings, there is no easy way to use the same boost momentum for all four ensembles. For the a12m310, a12m220, and a09m310 ensembles, we are able to find close momenta, but the boost momentum on a15m310 is not so close, 1.54GeV. Nevertheless, this allows us to study the lattice-spacing and pion-mass dependence without any interpolation; we find in both cases mild dependence. The a=0.12рқ‘Һ0.12a=0.12fm ensembles seem to prefer slightly higher central values for the RpITDs, but they are consistent with those from a09m310 and a15m310 within one standard deviation. We then choose a fixed pion mass and Ioffe time ОҪрқңҲnu to explore the lattice-spacing dependence in more detail. We examine the RpITDs at three values of zрқ‘§z and set nzвҲҲ4,3,2subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§432nzin4,3,2 in lattice units (Pz=2вҖӢПҖLвҖӢnzsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§2рқңӢрқҗҝsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§Pz=frac2piLnz) for the a09m310, a12m310 and a15m310 ensembles, respectively, to check the lattice-spacing dependence, where the Ioffe time ОҪ=zвҖӢPzрқңҲрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§nu=zPz are the same for all ensembles. We assume a linear OвҖӢ(a)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҺO(a) or OвҖӢ(a2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2O(a2) dependence and fit the RpITD data: в„івҖӢ(ОҪ,z2,a,MПҖ)=в„іcont+caвҖӢanв„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢsuperscriptв„іcontsubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ӣmathscrM(nu,z2,a,Mpi)=mathscrMtextcont+caan for n=1рқ‘ӣ1n=1 and 222, respectively. The fit results are shown in Fig. for both light and strange nucleons at ОҪ=ПҖ/4,ПҖ/2,ПҖрқңҲрқңӢ4рқңӢ2рқңӢnu=pi/4,pi/2,pi. All the plots show the RpITD points at a fixed ОҪрқңҲnu are consistent within one-sigma error; however, there seems to be a trend that the lattice-spacing dependence is stronger at larger ОҪрқңҲnu, making the continuum-extrapolated results larger. The ПҮ2/dofsuperscriptрқң’2dofchi2/textdof are all within one standard deviation of 0.5, showing that there is no preference in choice of ОҪрқңҲnu in this respect. In both OвҖӢ(a)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҺO(a) and OвҖӢ(a2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2O(a2) extrapolation, the light and strange nucleons appear to have opposite trends with lattice spacing; however, the slopes of the fits are consistent with zero within one standard deviation for each pion mass over all the selected ОҪрқңҲnu. Corresponding to a reading of Fig. left to right, top to bottom, the OвҖӢ(a)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҺO(a) в„іcontsuperscriptв„іcontmathscrMtextcont are 0.988, 0.95, 0.70, 0.994, 0.978, and 0.87. The OвҖӢ(a2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2O(a2) values are 0.987, 0.950, 0.75, 0.9997, 0.959, and 0.837. The deviation of the continuum-limit RpITD increases as ОҪрқңҲnu increases, with the continuum-limit error at ОҪ=ПҖрқңҲрқңӢnu=pi consistently being about a factor of ten larger than those at ОҪ=ПҖ/4рқңҲрқңӢ4nu=pi/4. The continuum-limit RpITDs using aрқ‘Һa and a2superscriptрқ‘Һ2a2 extrapolation are consistent with each other, but the former has larger error, due to extrapolating over a larger distance. The error in the light-nucleon extrapolated RpITD values is consistently double that of the strange nucleon.
content_sections.III Results and Discussions III.2 Contin: We extrapolate the nucleon gluon PDF at the physical pion mass and continuum limit based on the RpITDs for the a12m220, a09m310, a12m310 and a15m310 ensembles. Given that we observe small differences between aрқ‘Һa and a2superscriptрқ‘Һ2a2 extrapolation at fixed MПҖsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢMpi and ОҪрқңҲnu in the previous subsection, we focus on a2superscriptрқ‘Һ2a2 extrapolation here. We use the following ansatz, linear in pion mass and lattice-spacing squared, to extrapolate to the continuum-physical limit: в„івҖӢ(ОҪ,z2,a,MПҖ)=в„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢabsentdisplaystylemathscrM(nu,z2,a,Mpi)= (вҲ‘k=0kmaxО»kвҖӢ(a,MПҖ)вҖӢОҪk+czвҖӢ(a,MПҖ)вҖӢz2)superscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҳ0subscriptрқ‘ҳmaxsubscriptрқңҶрқ‘ҳрқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢsuperscriptрқңҲрқ‘ҳsubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘§рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢsuperscriptрқ‘§2displaystyleleft(sumk=0ktextmaxlambdak(a,Mpi)nuk+cz(a,Mpi)z2right) Г—displaystyletimes (1+caa2+cM(MПҖ2вҲ’(MПҖphys)2),displaystyle(1+caa2+cM(Mpi2-(Mpitextphys)2), Given the noisiness of the gluon RpITD data, kmax=2subscriptрқ‘ҳmax2ktextmax=2 is used in this study. We found that in all the ensembles in our calculation, the z2superscriptрқ‘§2z2-dependence, czвҖӢ(a,MПҖ)subscriptрқ‘җрқ‘§рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢcz(a,Mpi) is consistent with zero within two standard deviations. The example reconstructed fitted bands for a09m310 and a12m220 are shown in the top plot of Fig. . We drop data points at a ОҪрқңҲnu value if they have errors more than twice as large another data point at that ОҪрқңҲnu; this improves the clarity of the diagram showing the extrapolation effects. Our fit ansatz is able to describe our data reasonably with ОҪрқңҲnu up to about 7; in the future, when larger Pzsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz is used to reach larger ОҪрқңҲnu, a better interpolation form will likely be needed to describe the small- and large-ОҪрқңҲnu regions well. We show the continuum-physical RpITD band on the top plot of Fig. with all the data points from the four ensembles and a2superscriptрқ‘Һ2a2 extrapolation to the continuum-physical band. The open symbols indicate the strange-mass nucleon calculation from the ensemble. With the a15m310, a12m310 and a09m310 ensembles, since we have the same number of measurements for both strange and light nucleons, within each ensemble we bootstrap the light and strange renormalized matrix elements to keep the correlations. Across the ensembles, the data are independent and the typical bootstrap treatment is used. For comparison purposes, we also replace the a2superscriptрқ‘Һ2a2 term in Eq. with aрқ‘Һa; its physical-continuum results are shown as the band with dashed center line in Fig. . Similar to what we observe in Fig. , both extrapolations give us a consistent continuum-physical limit (within one standard deviation) with Ioffe time ОҪрқңҲnu up to 7, but the OвҖӢ(a)рқ‘Ӯрқ‘ҺO(a)-extrapolated continuum-physical RpITD has larger errors, especially in the larger-ОҪрқңҲnu region.
content_sections.III Results and Discussions III.3 Gluon: With the physical-continuum RpITD obtained in the previous section, we can now extract the gluon PDF distribution using the pseudo-PDF matching conditioncitebib.bib105 that connects the RpITD в„ів„іmathscrM to the lightcone gluon PDF gвҖӢ(x,Ој2)рқ‘”рқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2g(x,mu2): в„івҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)=вҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢxвҖӢgвҖӢ(x,Ој2)вҹЁxвҹ©gвҖӢRgвҖӢgвҖӢ(xвҖӢОҪ,z2вҖӢОј2),в„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2subscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”subscriptрқ‘…рқ‘”рқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2superscriptрқңҮ2mathscrM(nu,z2)=int01dxfracxg(x,mu2) xgRgg(xnu,z2mu2), where ОјрқңҮmu is the renormalization scale in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextMS scheme and вҹЁxвҹ©g=вҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢxвҖӢgвҖӢ(x,Ој2)subscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2 xg=int01dx,xg(x,mu2) is the gluon momentum fraction of the nucleon. RgвҖӢgsubscriptрқ‘…рқ‘”рқ‘”Rgg is the gluon-in-gluon matching kernel originally derived in Ref. citebib.bib105 and has been used in previous gluon PDF lattice workscitebib.bib92, bib.bib96, bib.bib104, bib.bib95. We use the RpITD extrapolated using the a2superscriptрқ‘Һ2a2 term (in Eq. ) at physical pion mass and continuum limit with a fit range of ОҪвҲҲ[0,7]рқңҲ07nuin[0,7], corresponding to the region where we have data in all ensembles. We ignore the quark-PDF contribution to the RpITDs in this calculation; it is likely to be small, based on our past study of the pion gluon PDFcitebib.bib96. We will later estimate the quark contribution as systematic effect. One can obtain the gluon PDF gвҖӢ(x,Ој2)рқ‘”рқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2g(x,mu2) by fitting the RpITD through the matching condition in Eq. . We adopt the phenomenologically motivated form commonly used in the global analysis fgвҖӢ(x,Ој)=xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x,Ој)вҹЁxвҹ©gвҖӢ(Ој)=xAвҖӢ(1вҲ’x)CBвҖӢ(A+1,C+1),subscriptрқ‘“рқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗҙsuperscript1рқ‘Ҙрқҗ¶рқҗөрқҗҙ1рқҗ¶1fg(x,mu)=fracxg(x,mu) xg(mu)=fracxA(1-x)CB(A+1,C+1), for xвҲҲ[0,1]рқ‘Ҙ01xin[0,1] and zero elsewhere. The beta function BвҖӢ(A+1,C+1)=вҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢxAвҖӢ(1вҲ’x)Cрқҗөрқҗҙ1рқҗ¶1superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗҙsuperscript1рқ‘Ҙрқҗ¶B(A+1,C+1)=int01dx,xA(1-x)C is used to normalize the area to unity. Such a form is also used in global fits to obtain the global PDF fits, such as nucleon gluon PDF by CT18citebib.bib7 and the nucleon and pion gluon PDF by JAMcitebib.bib129, bib.bib130, bib.bib131. We then fit the lattice physical-continuum RpITDs в„іlatвҖӢ(ОҪ,z2,a,MПҖ)superscriptв„іlatрқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢmathscrMtextlat(nu,z2,a,Mpi) obtained in Eq. to the parametrization form в„іfitвҖӢ(ОҪ,Ој,z2,a,MПҖ)superscriptв„іfitрқңҲрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘§2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢmathscrMtextfit(nu,mu,z2,a,Mpi) in Eq. by minimizing the ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 function, ПҮ2вҖӢ(Ој,a,MПҖ)=вҲ‘ОҪ,z(в„іfitвҖӢ(ОҪ,Ој,z2,a,MПҖ)вҲ’в„іlatвҖӢ(ОҪ,z2,a,MПҖ))2Пғв„і2вҖӢ(ОҪ,z2,a,MПҖ). superscriptрқң’2рқңҮрқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢsubscriptрқңҲрқ‘§superscriptsuperscriptв„іfitрқңҲрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘§2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢsuperscriptв„іlatрқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢ2subscriptsuperscriptрқңҺ2в„ірқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢchi2(mu,a,Mpi)= sumnu,zfrac(mathscrMtextfit(nu,mu,z2,a,Mpi)-mathscrMtextlat(nu,z2,a,Mpi))2sigma2mathscrM(nu,z2,a,Mpi). Our results for the continuum-physical unpolarized gluon PDF xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x,Ој)/вҹЁxвҹ©gрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”xg(x,mu)/ xg are shown in Fig. , along with the same determination from the smallest lattice-spacing ensemble obtained in this work, and selected global-fit gluon PDFs from CT18citebib.bib7 and NNPDF3.1citebib.bib6 NNLO analysis. The gluon distribution in continuum-physical limit has much larger errors by a factor of 3вҖ“5 than those obtained from singleвҖ“lattice-spacing analysis, due to the continuum extrapolation. Overall, the results from single-ensemble calculations on a09m310 are consistent with the continuum-physical one (which has much larger uncertainties). To reduce the errors in the continuum-physical distribution will be difficult, since it requires reduced errors in all ensembles, increasing the calculation cost by at least another order of magnitude. Both of our lattice distributions agree with the global-fit gluon distribution at mid to large xрқ‘Ҙx but deviate for x<0.3рқ‘Ҙ0.3x<0.3. This is likely due to lack of large-ОҪрқңҲnu lattice data in the input, which has higher sensitivity to the smaller-xрқ‘Ҙx data. Future calculations to push for even larger Pzsubscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§Pz will be needed to improve the small-xрқ‘Ҙx gluon distribution. We now consider the systematic uncertainty coming from neglecting the contribution of the quark term, PzP0вҖӢвҲ«01рқ‘‘xвҖӢxвҖӢqSвҖӢ(x,Ој2)вҹЁxвҹ©gвҖӢRgвҖӢqвҖӢ(xвҖӢОҪ,z2вҖӢОј2)subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғ0superscriptsubscript01differential-dрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҘsuperscriptрқңҮ2subscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”subscriptрқ‘…рқ‘”рқ‘һрқ‘ҘрқңҲsuperscriptрқ‘§2superscriptрқңҮ2fracPzP0int01dxfracxqS(x,mu2) xgRgq(xnu,z2mu2) in Eq. . We ignored this contribution initially based on the assumption (motivated by global fits) that the nucleon total quark PDF qSвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҘqS(x) is smaller than the gluon PDF. We can estimate the systematic due to omitting the qSвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘һрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҘqS(x) contribution by using the nucleon flavor-dependent quark PDFs from CT18 at NNLOcitebib.bib7. Following a similar procedure to Ref. citebib.bib96, we add the quark-gluon mixing term to the extraction of xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)/вҹЁxвҹ©gрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”xg(x)/ xg from the RpITD in Eq. . The central value of the updated xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)/вҹЁxвҹ©gрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”xg(x)/ xg including both gluon-in-gluon (gg) and gluon-in-quark (gq) contributions is shown in Fig. (black solid line). The difference is much smaller than the current statistical errors, so we will ignore this quark contribution in this calculation. However, in the future, when the continuum-physical gluon PDF precision are improved, we should re-examine this contribution more carefully. We investigate the systematic uncertainty introduced by the choice of parametrization form used for fgвҖӢ(x,Ој)subscriptрқ‘“рқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮfg(x,mu). We consider a three-parameter form used in PDF global analysis and some lattice calculations, fg,3вҖӢ(x,Ој)=xAвҖӢ(1вҲ’x)CвҖӢ(1+DвҖӢx)BвҖӢ(A+1,C+1)+DвҖӢBвҖӢ(A+2,C+1). subscriptрқ‘“рқ‘”3рқ‘ҘрқңҮsuperscriptрқ‘Ҙрқҗҙsuperscript1рқ‘Ҙрқҗ¶1рқҗ·рқ‘Ҙрқҗөрқҗҙ1рқҗ¶1рқҗ·рқҗөрқҗҙ2рқҗ¶1fg,3(x,mu)=fracxA(1-x)C(1+Dx)B(A+1,C+1)+DB(A+2,C+1). We fit our continuum-physicalвҖ“limit RpITDs to this form up to maximal Ioffe time ОҪmax=7subscriptрқңҲmax7nutextmax=7. A comparison of the fit-form choice is shown in Fig. . We find the goodness-of-fit improves slightly due to the introduction of a new free parameter in the fit form, but the gluon PDF results are noisier and are consistent with the two-parameter fit. We will use xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x,Ој)/вҹЁxвҹ©gрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”xg(x,mu)/ xg from the two-parameter fit as our main result for this work. The unpolarized nucleon gluon PDF xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) can be extracted by taking the ratio of fgвҖӢ(x,Ој)=xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x,Ој)/вҹЁxвҹ©gвҖӢ(Ој)subscriptрқ‘“рқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘ҘрқңҮsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқңҮfg(x,mu)=xg(x,mu)/ xg(mu) and the gluon momentum fraction вҹЁxвҹ©gвҖӢ(Ој)subscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқңҮ xg(mu) obtained in Ref. citebib.bib132. Referencecitebib.bib132 calculated the gluon momentum fraction using valence clover fermion action on 0.09-, 0.12-, and 0.15-fm HISQ 2+1+1-flavor lattice ensembles with three pion masses, 220220220, 310310310 and 690690690MeV. The renormalization was done using RI/MOM nonperturbative renormalization in MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextMS scheme at 2GeV and using cluster-decomposition error reduction (CDER) to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio of the renormalization constantcitebib.bib133, bib.bib134. The gluon momentum fraction was extrapolated to the continuum-physical limit and found to be consistent with other recent lattice-QCD results at physical pion mass. Our final unpolarized nucleon gluon PDF xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) extrapolated to physical pion mass MПҖ=135subscriptрқ‘ҖрқңӢ135Mpi=135MeV and the continuum limit aвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘Һ0ato 0 is shown as green bands in Fig. ; once again, we found reasonable agreement with the global fits from CT18citebib.bib7 and NNPDF3.1citebib.bib6 NNLO analysis for xвҲҲ[0.25,1]рқ‘Ҙ0.251xin[0.25,1], even thought the the gluon momentum fraction obtained from the global fits is about two-sigmas lower than the lattice calculations. We do observe tension with gluon PDF from JAM20citebib.bib129 analysis for x<0.6рқ‘Ҙ0.6x<0.6 regions but its gluon PDF also behave quite different from the the CT18 and NNPDF results, even with smaller errors; we look forward to updates on the global-fit community on resolving these discrepancy. We also compare our results with other previous lattice-QCD calculation on xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x). The light cyan bands in Fig. shown the first pseudo-PDF calculation done using clover-on-HISQ with 0.12-fm lattice spacing and 310- and 700-MeV pion mass using 898 lattice configuration with 32 sources per configuration for nucleon two-point correlatorscitebib.bib92. The results are extrapolated to physical pion mass using naive two valence pion mass extrapolation with xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) reconstructed by multiplying the gluon momentum fraction taken from Ref. citebib.bib55. The blue bands in Fig. show a followup calculation performed by HadStruc collaboration using 2+1 dynamical flavors of clover fermions with stout-link smearing on the gauge fields, 0.09-fm lattice spacing, 358-MeV pion mass, and 64 source measurements on 349 lattice configurations with gradient-flow improved gluonic operatorscitebib.bib95. They used multiple nucleon interpolating fields, allowing them to use generalized eigenvalue method to determine the best overlap with ground-state nucleon gluonic matrix elements. They used the gluon momentum fraction obtained from an independent lattice work (2+1+1-flavor at physical pion mass) to determine xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x). The outer blue bands indicate their uncertainty estimated from вҹЁxвҹ©gsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”left xrightg. We also show our result on the ensemble with 0.09-fm lattice-spacing and 310-MeV pion mass as a purple band in Fig. ; it used about 300k measurements spread out over 1000 lattice configurations. Our single-ensemble results have errors comparable to (in some regions, smaller than) CT18 and NNPDF. The lattice-spacing and pion-mass here is similar to those used in the HadStruc calculationcitebib.bib95 but without the additional uncertainties due to continuum-physical extrapolation (shown as a green band). There are noticeable deviations from the HadStruc results, especially in the larger-xрқ‘Ҙx region; their large-xрқ‘Ҙx gluon PDF is much smaller than ours. However, given that multiple methodological aspects are done quite differently (for example, we used the momentum fraction from the same lattice ensemble and different gluon-operator smearing), it may require the full calculation, including continuum-physical extrapolation, to have meaningfully compare them. All the prior single-ensemble lattice results (without the systematics from lattice discretization) agree with our continuum-physical xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) due to the larger total errors from the continuum-physical extrapolation. Future work to include finer lattice-spacing and 220-MeV or lighter pion masses in the extrapolation will help to improve the continuum-physical determination of the lattice gluon PDF.
content_sections.IV Summary and Outlook: We extracted the nucleon xрқ‘Ҙx-dependent gluon PDFs xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) using clover fermions as valence action and 310-MeV 2+1+1 HISQ configurations generated by the MILC Collaboration at three pion masses and three lattice spacings. We found their dependence to be weak at the current statistics of hundreds of thousands of nucleon two-point correlator measurements. We removed the excited-state contributions to the ground-state matrix elements using a two-state fitting strategy and studied the stability of the extraction of the ground-state matrix elements with various fit ranges. We then calculated the reduced pseudo-ITD using the fitted matrix elements and studied their pion-mass and lattice-spacing dependence, which are also mild. We then extrapolated the reduced pseudo-ITDs to physical-continuum limit before extracting the gluon parton distribution xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)/вҹЁxвҹ©gрқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”xg(x)/ xg in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextMS scheme at 2GeV. Using the nonperturbatively renormalized nucleon momentum fraction calculated on clover-on-HISQ ensembles, we were able to compare our single-ensemble xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) calculations at lattice spacing 0.09fm with prior lattice calculations. We found both our 0.09-fm and continuum-physical limit xвҖӢgвҖӢ(x)рқ‘Ҙрқ‘”рқ‘Ҙxg(x) to be in good agreement with CT18 and NNPDF3.1 NNLO global-fit results in the range xвҲҲ[0.2,1]рқ‘Ҙ0.21xin[0.2,1] in MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextMS scheme at 2GeV. We have used the CT18 quark PDFs to estimate the quark-gluon mixing and checked the gluon PDF fit-form dependence, but these errors are not included in our continuum-physical results, since the statistical errors are large in comparison. Future work should include finer lattice spacings, even higher statistics to improve the signal and larger boost momentum to expand the range of ОҪрқңҲnu, which will improve the reliability of the results at small xрқ‘Ҙx.

all_papers.title:
A simple non-parametric reconstruction of parton distributions from
  limited Fourier information
all_papers.abstract:
Some calculations of parton distributions from first principles only give
access to a limited range of Fourier modes of the function to reconstruct. We
present a physically motivated procedure to regularize the inverse integral
problem using a Gaussian process as a Bayesian prior. We propose to fix the
hyperparameters of the prior in a meaningful physical fashion, offering a
simple implementation, great numerical efficiency, and allowing us to
understand and keep control easily of the uncertainty of the reconstruction.

sections.2: Constantinou etВ al.,В Parton distributions and lattice-QCD calculations: Toward 3D structure,В Prog
sections.3: Ji,В Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice,В Phys
sections.21: Ji,В Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice,В Phys

qualitative: Parton distributions are fundamental to understanding hadronic structure and are reconstructed from lattice QCD matrix elements via a Fourier transform.
The reconstruction of parton distributions from lattice data is an ill-posed inverse problem due to limited access to Fourier modes in Ioffe time ОҪ.
A Gaussian process is used as a Bayesian prior to regularize the inverse problem, offering flexibility and a mathematically sound treatment of uncertainty.
The method proposed fixes hyperparameters of the Gaussian process prior using physical constraints rather than inferring them from data, enhancing physical interpretability and control.
This physically motivated approach is especially advantageous when the accessible range in Ioffe time ОҪ is small and data is limited.
The method preserves the normal distribution of the reconstruction, enabling efficient and exact sharing of results.
The approach is applicable beyond lattice QCD, extending to other inverse problems in parton distribution determinations.
quantitative: M(ОҪ) = вҲ«_{-1}^{1} dx e^{i x ОҪ} f_q(x)
The accessible range in Ioffe time ОҪ in state-of-the-art lattice calculations barely reaches 10.

The reconstruction uses a Gaussian process prior with hyperparameters fixed by physical constraints (e.g., smoothness, support, scale), though specific values are not provided in the abstract or introduction.

authors: Herv\'e Dutrieux, Joseph Karpie, Kostas Orginos, Savvas Zafeiropoulos

content_sections.I Introduction: Parton distributions are fundamental quantities in our understanding of hadronic structure, one of the most active topics of particle physics for the past 50 years. They are notoriously difficult to compute because of the strongly coupled interaction of quarks and gluons at low energy. In recent years, they have been thoroughly studied in lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) from non-local matrix elements of the type citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4: вҹЁP|ПҲВҜвҖӢ(вҲ’z2)вҖӢОіОјвҖӢWвҖӢ[вҲ’z2,z2;A]вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(z2)|Pвҹ©displaystyle P|barpsileft(-fracz2right)gammamuhatWleft[-fracz2,fracz2;Aright]psileft(fracz2right)|P =PОјвҖӢMвҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)+zОјвҖӢNвҖӢ(ОҪ,z2),displaystylehskip 40.0pt=PmuM(nu,z2)+zmuN(nu,z2),, where zz is a space-like separation, PP the hadron momentum and ОҪ=PвӢ…znu=Pcdot z a Lorentz invariant often called Ioffe time. Assuming a proper removal of UV divergences, MвҖӢ(ОҪ)M(nu) can be related in the short-distance factorization scheme to the parton distribution fqвҖӢ(x)fq(x) through the Fourier transform: MвҖӢ(ОҪ)=вҲ«вҲ’11dxвҖӢeiвҖӢxвҖӢОҪвҖӢfqвҖӢ(x). M(nu)=int-11dx,eixnufq(x),. We ignore the matching procedure which bears no impact on the inverse problem we are concerned with. In state-of-the-art calculations, the range in ОҪnu accessible with controlled higher twist uncertainties barely reaches 10. See citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7 for reviews on the state of lattice computations. The full reconstruction of fqвҖӢ(x)fq(x) therefore suffers from an ill-posed inverse problem. It is typically regularized by parametrizing fqвҖӢ(x)fq(x) with a few free parameters вҖ“ at the risk of excessive model dependence вҖ“ or a neural network citebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12 whose behavior may be difficult to explain in a physically motivated way. Other methods, such as the Backus-Gilbert algorithm citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, maximum entropy method citebib.bib15, Bayesian reconstruction citebib.bib16 or polynomial expansions citebib.bib17 have been studied. Early comparisons of various xx-dependent reconstruction methods can be found in citebib.bib9, bib.bib18. We will use in this paper the formalism of Gaussian processes, already explored in the context of lattice parton distributions in citebib.bib19. We follow the idea of a non-parametric reconstruction using a Gaussian process as Bayesian prior in xx-space advocated in citebib.bib20, extending on the previous works citebib.bib21, bib.bib22. Gaussian processes have already been used in lattice QCD computations for other inverse problems, such as in citebib.bib23, bib.bib24, as well as in non-perturbative studies in continuum QCD citebib.bib25. They offer many advantages in terms of flexibility and proper mathematical treatment of the uncertainty. However, as with any regularization of an inverse problem, this technique introduces a bias, here in the form of a choice of kernel and hyperparameters. In citebib.bib20 it is proposed to infer the hyperparameters from the fitted dataset, however, we find it advantageous for the specific inverse problem at hand to propose a physically motivated variant of this idea where the hyperparameters are entirely fixed by easily tuned physical constraints. Especially when the range in ОҪnu is small and the available fitted information is very limited, we believe that it is useful and more meaningful to keep direct physical control over the hyperparameters and therefore the uncertainty. In a subsequent publication, we will study the effects of hyperparameter optimizations and compare these approaches with the simple and intuitive method proposed here. The approach presented here is also highly efficient from a numerical point of view and has the advantage of preserving the normal distribution of the reconstruction. Therefore, the results of the reconstruction can be shared in an efficient and exact fashion. Although we present our results in the context of the short-distance factorization of lattice matrix elements, many elements of the discussion presented here apply to a variety of other inverse problems both in lattice and phenomenological determinations of parton distributions. The article is organized in the following way. We first remind the principles of using a Gaussian process as Bayesian prior to regularize a functional inverse problem. Then we present our procedure and propose a closure test, detailing notably the effect of the range in ОҪnu on the uncertainty. We then apply the method to actual lattice QCD data, comparing its results to a usual parametric fit. We end up with a discussion of how other important inverse problems of parton distributions in lattice QCD and phenomenology may or may not benefit straightforwardly from our physically motivated procedure.
content_sections.II Gaussian processes as Bayesian priors: Let us assume that we possess experimental information on the function fqfq through some linear operators parametrized by ОҪnu: MвҖӢ(ОҪ)=BвҖӢ(ОҪ)вҲҳfq. M(nu)=B(nu)circ fq,. Our canonical example will be that MвҖӢ(ОҪ)M(nu) is a set of discrete Fourier modes of fqfq and BвҖӢ(ОҪ)B(nu) the linear operator вҲ«dxвҖӢeiвҖӢxвҖӢОҪintdx,eixnu. Other examples of linear inverse problems relevant for our physics are evoked in the final section of the document. If fqfq is just any function and the set (ОҪ)(nu) is finite, there is obviously an infinite number of possible reconstructions. A large number of regularization techniques rely on Bayesian priors. One assumes prior information II on the credibility of various models, represented by the prior probability distribution в„ҷвҖӢ(fq|I)P(fq|I). Then BayesвҖҷ theorem gives, в„ҷвҖӢ(fq|M,I)=в„ҷвҖӢ(M|fq)вҖӢв„ҷвҖӢ(fq|I)в„ҷвҖӢ(M|I),P(fq|M,I)=fracP(M|fq)P(fq|I)P(M|I),, where в„ҷвҖӢ(M|fq)P(M|fq) is the data likelihood, в„ҷвҖӢ(M|I)P(M|I) is the evidence, and в„ҷвҖӢ(fq|M,I)P(fq|M,I) is the posterior knowledge of fqfq given the experimental data MM and the prior information II. For instance, the expectation value вҹЁfqвҖӢ(x)вҹ© fq(x) is obtained by: вҹЁfqвҖӢ(x)вҹ©=вҲ«dfqвҖӢfqвҖӢ(x)вҖӢв„ҷвҖӢ(fq|M,I). fq(x)=intdfq,fq(x)P(fq|M,I),. A Gaussian process citebib.bib26 is an infinite collection of random variables indexed by a continuous parameter, such that any finite subset follows a multivariate normal distribution. Gaussian processes are fully characterized by their mean gвҖӢ(x)g(x) and covariance function KвҖӢ(x,xвҖІ)K(x,xprime). They are the natural extension of the normal distribution to continuous degrees of freedom, and form therefore an ideal Bayesian prior for a functional problem: в„ҷ(fq|I)=(det(2ПҖK))вҲ’1/2exp(вҲ’12Г—displaystyleP(fq|I)=(det(2pi K))-1/2expbigg(-frac12times вҲ«dxdxвҖІ[fq(x)вҲ’g(x)]KвҲ’1(x,xвҖІ)[fq(xвҖІ)вҲ’g(xвҖІ)]),displaystylehskip 10.0ptintdxdxprime [fq(x)-g(x)]K-1(x,xprime)[fq(xprime)-g(xprime)]bigg),, where KвҲ’1K-1 is the inverse covariance function. The rigorous definition of the functional determinant det(2вҖӢПҖвҖӢK)det(2pi K) requires an extension of the notion of matrix determinant, performed either with the zeta operator or a discretized path integral formulation citebib.bib27. For all practical purposes, since numerical computations require a finite number of degrees of freedom, we will evaluate the Gaussian process on a grid of points (xi)(xi). It then becomes an ordinary multivariate normal distribution. The covariance function KвҖӢ(x,xвҖІ)K(x,xprime) is replaced by a covariance matrix KiвҖӢj=KвҖӢ(xi,xj)Kij=K(xi,xj) whose determinant can be evaluated without issues, and the linear operator BвҖӢ(ОҪ)B(nu) becomes a matrix BkвҖӢiBki that maps (xi)(xi) into (ОҪk)(nuk). The values of the posterior outside of the grid are obtained by a linear interpolation from the fqвҖӢ(xi)fq(xi). If one considers the traditional Gaussian likelihood of the data given the model в„ҷвҖӢ(M|fq)P(M|fq), then the posterior distribution on the (xi)(xi) grid is also a multivariate normal distribution that can be computed analytically. With these conventions, the posterior mean is citebib.bib21, bib.bib20: вҹЁfqвҖӢ(xi)вҹ©=gвҖӢ(xi)+KвҖӢBTвҖӢ[C+BвҖӢKвҖӢBT]вҲ’1вҖӢ[MвҲ’BвҖӢg] fq(xi)=g(xi)+KBT[C+BKBT]-1[M-Bg] where CC is the covariance of the data MM, and the covariance matrix of the posterior is: Hdisplaystyle H =(KвҲ’1+BTвҖӢCвҲ’1вҖӢB)вҲ’1,displaystyle=(K-1+BTC-1B)-1,, =KвҲ’KвҖӢBTвҖӢ[C+BвҖӢKвҖӢBT]вҲ’1вҖӢBвҖӢK. displaystyle=K-KBT[C+BKBT]-1BK,.
content_sections.III The method: First, we start by separating the even and odd parts of the xx-dependence (which effectively corresponds to selecting different combinations of quark and anti-quark distributions), so that our functions can be modeled only in the xвҲҲ[0,1]xin[0,1] interval. Since we are using a Gaussian process as Bayesian prior, we then merely need to specify a mean gвҖӢ(x)g(x) and a covariance function KвҖӢ(x,xвҖІ)K(x,xprime). Many proposals are available in the literature. A successful parametric modeling of parton distributions is produced by variants of: xОұвҖӢ(1вҲ’x)ОІГ—smooth function ofвҖӢx,xalpha(1-x)betatimestextrm smooth function of x,, where Оұalpha is typically slightly negative, producing a power divergence when xвҶ’0xrightarrow 0, and ОІ=2вҲј3beta=2sim 3. See citebib.bib28 for a critical review of this parametric model in the context of global fits, where the authors warn against potentially unrealistic uncertainty in extrapolation regions. In our inverse problem, especially when the range in ОҪnu is small, this parametric form introduces a considerable bias in the reconstruction at endpoints. Sensitivity to point-like features, such as the exponent of the behavior at x=0x=0 or x=1x=1, is only achieved in the limit of large Fourier frequencies. The low frequencies only characterize low-resolution information on the function. To fulfill our premise of non-parametric reconstruction, we will abstain from making any reference to that model in our reconstruction. Instead, we choose a covariance function based only on general physical considerations: KвҖӢ(x,xвҖІ)=Пғ2вҖӢexpвҒЎ(вҲ’(lnвҒЎ(x)вҲ’lnвҒЎ(xвҖІ))22вҖӢl2),K(x,xprime)=sigma2expleft(-frac(ln(x)-ln(xprime))22l2right),, where Пғ2sigma2 controls the variance of our prior and ll is a logarithmic correlation length in xx space which enforces the smoothness of the reconstruction. By using lnвҒЎ(x)ln(x) in the numerator, we have introduced the prior expectation that the physics at xвүӘ1xll 1 and xx close to 1 are fundamentally different and should be decorrelated. This also enforces a requirement of smoothness of the reconstruction if the ratio x/xвҖІx/xprime lies typically within [eвҲ’l,el][e-l,el]. This choice of kernel is therefore imprinting the first physics-driven regularization of our inverse problem. But how should we choose the values of the priorвҖҷs hyperparameters (Пғ2,l)(sigma2,l) and mean gвҖӢ(x)g(x) which we have yet to decide upon? A proposal explored in citebib.bib20 is to sample the hyperparameters according to the evidence в„ҷвҖӢ(M|I)P(M|I). In practice, it means that e. g. the correlation length and variance of the prior in xx-space would be inferred from the dataset itself. However, in the case of our inverse problem, we have no data constraint in xx-space, only low-order Fourier frequencies. It is not obvious why information of smoothness derived from the low-order Fourier frequencies provides a good guide to reconstructing the higher frequencies. From a theoretical point of view, it has been long argued citebib.bib29 that different Ioffe times ОҪnu correspond to a different physics of hadronic structure. Empirically, we know (and will verify again in this document) that the low-order frequencies constrain well the reconstruction at intermediate values of xx, and the high-frequencies mostly constrain the behavior in the limit xвҶ’0xrightarrow 0. Therefore, using a smoothness information obtained at low frequency to constrain the high frequency may effectively go against our goal of decorrelating small and large xx regions of the reconstructed function. Various empirical tests of hyperparameter sampling lead, especially when the range in ОҪnu is small, to larger prior correlation lengths and smaller prior variances than physical insights would lead us to, as will be elaborated in more detail in a subsequent publication. Already in the study presented in citebib.bib20 with synthetic lattice data spanning in the range ОҪвҲҲ[0,8]nuin[0,8], it appears that the correlation length is very poorly informed by the data and is therefore free to err to as large values as allowed by the prior put on the hyperparameter. Direct comparison is however made difficult by the use of a different kernel in that study. Such features may give the wrong intuition that a reduced range in ОҪnu produces overall good xx reconstruction. Finally, the procedure demonstrated in this work is extremely numerically efficient compared for instance to sampling the posterior of GP hyperparameters, or even the traditional non-linear minimization of parameterized functions. By fixing the hyperparameters, the posterior is simply a Gaussian whose mean and covariance are analytically derived through eqs. -. This setting is a specific case of sampling hyperparameters with delta functions for the hyperparameter priors. The advantage of loosening those hyperparameter priors is to relax some of the choices made by the scientist, at the increased computational cost of Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling the non-trivial posterior. Even so, some choice of hyperparameter priors must be made and those biases can never be completely removed. In this work, we propose a simple set of data-driven choices for analyzing the Ioffe time distributions which will give efficient results without requiring sampling. If one wishes to augment the dataset with other observables such as those discussed in Sec. , a single good set of physical data-driven choices may not be clear and sampling of the parameters may be necessary.
content_sections.III The method Procedure: We suggest: 1. Set the logarithmic correlation length to l=lnвҒЎl=ln. 2. Always keep gвҖӢ(x)=Пғg(x)=sigma. 3. Fix Пғ2sigma2 such that the maximal standard deviation of the Fourier transform of the posterior for any ОҪвҲҲ[ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx,+вҲһ]nuin[numax,+infty] is exactly maxвҖӢ(1.3вҖӢО”вҖӢMвҖӢ(ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx),0.3вҖӢ|MвҖӢ(ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx)|)textrmmax(1.3Delta M(numax),0.3|M(numax)|), where ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxnumax is the maximal value of ОҪnu in the dataset1Since the parton distributions we are concerned with are absolutely integrable, their Fourier transform tends towards 0 as ОҪвҶ’вҲһnurightarrowinfty. Therefore, in practice, the criterion can be checked on a fairly narrow range in ОҪnu. For the application to real data, [ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx,50][numax,50] proved plenty enough. For the closure test with extended range in Ioffe time, [ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx,150][numax,150] was enough. .
content_sections.III The method Explanations: 1. A logarithmic correlation length of lnвҒЎln corresponds to starting to decorrelate x=1x=1 from x=0.5x=0.5, x=0.5x=0.5 from x=0.25x=0.25, etc. This is a physics-driven prescription on the degree of flexibility that one expects in ordinary parton distributions. The dataset itself may have features that enforce a stronger smoothness than what this baseline correlation length requires, through the data likelihood term in addition to the effects of the prior. 2. By picking gвҖӢ(x)=Пғg(x)=sigma, we introduce by definition a prior which is uniformly one standard deviation above 0 for all xx. Because our data is not informative on the behavior at the point x=0x=0 and the prior covariance kernel enforces no correlation in this limit, the posterior is exactly equal to the prior when x=0x=0. Therefore, the posterior will be just one standard deviation above 0 at x=0x=0. This allows us to enforce a loose positivity prior on the reconstruction and to reflect the large uncertainty at small xx, which is fixed by design at 100% when x=0x=0. 3. We want the uncertainty to increase in the extrapolation region away from ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxnumax. One has typically no control over this feature when sampling over (Пғ2,l)(sigma2,l) according to the evidence в„ҷвҖӢ(M|I)P(M|I). Yet, a visual inspection of the uncertainty in the extrapolation region is a powerful empirical criterion to evaluate whether an xx-space reconstruction is too optimistic or pessimistic in its uncertainty quantification. Deciding on what is a reasonable degree of increase in uncertainty in an extrapolation does not admit a simple good answer. We have chosen to require the maximum uncertainty in the extrapolation region [ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx,+вҲһ][numax,+infty] to be 30%30% larger than the uncertainty of the point MвҖӢ(ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx)M(numax). This gives the constraint 1.3вҖӢО”вҖӢMвҖӢ(ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx)1.3Delta M(numax). Since the uncertainty tends to 0 when ОҪвҶ’вҲһnurightarrowinfty, a point in the extrapolation region with maximal uncertainty always exists, and it is reached for practical purposes fairly quickly in ОҪnu. To satisfy the criterion, we suggest starting from a small value of Пғsigma and increasing it gradually until the criterion is satisfied. Since one reconstruction takes typically of the order of one second, a correct result is found extremely easily. A visual depiction of this targeted uncertainty in the extrapolation region is given in the upper plot of Fig. . However, the dataset may end while there is still a non-vanishing signal in the Fourier space that lies within the extrapolation region. The lower plot of Fig. highlights our request in uncertainty in that case, of 0.3вҖӢ|MвҖӢ(ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx)|0.3|M(numax)|. We summarize our physical regularization in Table. Of course, some of the choices that we have made here may not be consensual for some readers. The most problematic is likely the choice of Пғ2sigma2 which is dictated by a perception of what a good extrapolation uncertainty is. We have found our parameters to give a satisfactory uncertainty reconstruction without requiring any tuning in the cases where we have tried them. But if one is unsatisfied by the extent of the extrapolation uncertainty, for instance, it is very easy to act on the hyperparameters accordingly. Let us stress again the important philosophy underlying this reconstruction procedure: when performing an extrapolation in a region where there is no data, and where we have a physical intuition that the physics is fairly different compared to the region where we have data, then there is no objective choice of reconstruction procedure and choices must be made. The alternative strategy of allowing hyperparameters to be sampled in priors beyond the fixed (delta function) prescription we suggest only mildly relaxes the arbitrariness of these choices. Since some hyperparameters are very poorly controlled by the data, the choice of the hyperparameter priors may become itself a source of systematic uncertainty. On the other hand, the hyperparameters that are controlled by the data and affect significantly the extrapolation region (high-frequency region), are imprinting low-frequency information on the high-frequency domain, mixing up two physically different regimes. As a result, we believe that it is more difficult to control exactly the impact of the physical assumptions on the uncertainty when sampling is performed, compared to a direct fixing procedure as we propose. Our upcoming work will make direct comparisons of these two approaches to better understand the issues involved. Finally, let us mention that we could easily enforce the requirement that the distribution vanishes at x=1x=1. This can be performed for instance by adding a row to the BB matrix which only selects fqвҖӢfq, and enforcing its value to be 0. In the following tests, we have chosen to let it free to see what constraining power the data has on the behavior when xвҶ’1xrightarrow 1.
content_sections.IV Closure test: To test the effect of our procedure in a controlled environment, we generate 1000 functional samples of the input distribution fqвҖӢ(x)=NвҖӢxОұвҖӢ(1вҲ’x)ОІfq(x)=Nxalpha(1-x)beta where Оұalpha and ОІbeta follow normal distributions of respective (mean, standard deviation): (вҲ’0.2,0.1)(-0.2,0.1) and (2.5,0.5)(2.5,0.5). NN is fixed by the constraint вҲ«01dxвҖӢfqвҖӢ(x)=1int01dx,fq(x)=1. We produce three datasets of the real part of the Fourier modes to study the impact of the range in ОҪnu on the reconstruction: ОҪвҲҲ[0,ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx]nuin[0,numax] with ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx=4,10,25numax=4,10,25. The grid (xi)(xi) is made of 100 points evenly spaced between 10вҲ’610-6 and 1. The lower bound is non-zero to allow the evaluation of the prior covariance kernel eq. . The interpolation between grid nodes is performed by a cubic spline. To prevent floating precision errors, we cut the eigenspectrum of the covariance prior KK at 10вҲ’1310-13, and of the dataset sample covariance matrix CC at 10вҲ’13,10вҲ’12,10вҲ’1110-13,10-12,10-11 for ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx=4,10,25numax=4,10,25. The results for the three datasets are presented in Fig. where they are normalized to the input distribution to appreciate the quality of the reconstruction. A more global picture including the extrapolation in ОҪnu space is provided in Figs. - . Visually, the goodness-of-fit of the data is excellent. It is interesting to notice that quoting a sensible ПҮ2chi2 value requires additional work that is not needed to construct the posterior. Indeed, ПҮ2=rTвҖӢCвҲ’1вҖӢrchi2=rTC-1r where rr is the vector of residuals. One will notice that in our example, CC is in principle non-invertible since вҖ“ up to the floating precision regulator вҖ“ the data at ОҪ=0nu=0 has zero uncertainty due to the fixed normalization of the input function. Even if one excluded this point from the ПҮ2chi2 definition, there would still remain eigenvalues on the verge of floating point precision in the covariance of the data that we are fitting in this closure test. Furthermore, small eigenvalues of the covariance matrix may be poorly estimated due to the limited number of samples used to construct the dataset. This problem is nonexistent in the posterior distribution construction, since eqs. and do not involve directly CвҲ’1C-1, but always [C+BвҖӢKвҖӢBT]вҲ’1[C+BKBT]-1 where the prior eliminates the issue.2To understand the regularization effect of adding BвҖӢKвҖӢBTBKBT, we can consider the highly degenerate case where the dataset is only made of a single measurement at ОҪ=0nu=0, with 0 uncertainty. The covariance matrix is then C=C=, obviously strictly non-invertible. Using a simple integration rule, if the xx-grid contains NN elements, B is a line vector of NN components (1N,1N,вҖҰ,1N)(frac1N,frac1N,. . . ,frac1N), and BвҖӢKвҖӢBT=1N2вҖӢвҲ‘i,jKi,jBKBT=frac1N2sumi,jKi,j. This quantity is clearly non-zero since each Ki,jKi,j is strictly positive. Therefore C+BвҖӢKвҖӢBTC+BKBT is invertible. Therefore, while we could simply use an extremely tiny floating precision cutoff to construct the posterior, quoting a sensible goodness-of-fit estimate requires a stronger cutoff on the eigenvalues of CC. A cutoff of 10вҲ’610-6 leads to values of the absolute ПҮ2chi2 significantly smaller than 1. When ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxnumax increases, the extrapolation region shrinks and the reconstruction in xx becomes faithful to the input distribution up to increasingly small values of xx. We quote in Table the value of xx at which our reconstruction has a standard deviation 50% larger than the input distribution depending on the value of ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxnumax. We find approximately xвүҲ2/ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx,xapprox 2/numax,, which follows the usual known heuristic of the relationship between ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxnumax and the resolution of the reconstruction in xx. It is important to remember that this uncertainty is obtained within the procedure that we have detailed before, and tuning the hyperparameters differently вҖ“ especially (Пғ2,l)(sigma2,l) вҖ“ would lead to a different factor in front of 1/ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢx1/numax. We refer to an upcoming further work where a more general study of various kernels and strategies to handle the hyperparameters are explored. At xвүҲ0xapprox 0, as we have discussed previously, the posterior becomes equal to the prior. It is quite remarkable that the prior, which is selected dynamically by our request of uncertainty in the extrapolation region, ends up being fairly identical over all cases despite the notable increase in the range in ОҪnu and decrease of the posterior uncertainty for non-zero xx. Because of the stability of the prior, the uncertainty at x=0x=0 remains globally identical regardless of the value of ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxnumax. This may however be directly linked to the fact that the uncertainty in ОҪnu of the input distribution is itself very slowly varying. Considering the behavior of the input distribution in ОҪnu, it is clear that if we had not forced the uncertainty to increase in the extrapolation region, we could have produced a much more faithful xx reconstruction at smaller values of ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxnumax. However, it seems unwarranted to assume that the first principles parton distribution would have the simple features of this two-parameter input distribution. This exercise points out again how large ОҪnu and small xx go hand-in-hand. We must therefore exercise caution to avoid bias introduced by using models that infer large ОҪnu behavior from small ОҪnu data.
content_sections.V Application to real data: We apply our procedure to the unpolarized PDF data published in citebib.bib30 with a lattice spacing of a=0.094a=0.094 fm, a pion mass of 358 MeV and a maximal proton momentum of P=2.47P=2.47 GeV. We present results for z=3вҖӢaz=3a (0.28 fm), 6вҖӢa6a (0.56 fm), and 9вҖӢa9a (0.85 fm) for the real parts in Figs. - , and the imaginary parts in Figs. - . To isolate the effect of the Fourier transform uncertainty and not introduce the question of the perturbative matching which comes with its own separate uncertainty, we Fourier transform directly the object MвҖӢ(ОҪ,z2)M(nu,z2) defined in eq. . Therefore, the various reconstructions at different values of zz exist at different scales. For larger zz, this translates into less peaked distributions small xx, as can be inferred from the effect of the DGLAP evolution equation. As zz increases while the largest momentum PP is kept fixed, the maximal value of ОҪ=PвӢ…znu=Pcdot z in the fitted dataset increases likewise. This time, we apply a floating point precision cutoff on the eigenvalues of the prior covariance KK and the sample data covariance CC of 10вҲ’1310-13 in all cases. We compare our reconstruction to the still largely used simple Ansatz: fqвҖӢ(x)=NвҖӢxОұвҖӢ(1вҲ’x)ОІ. fq(x)=Nxalpha(1-x)beta,. Because it is a non-linear function of its free parameters, fitting this simple Ansatz is in fact considerably more challenging numerically than the procedure we advocate here. It is both lengthier and suffers from numerical instabilities, as explained in citebib.bib31. In the latter, the authors were forced to restrict the range of allowed values of NN with a direct noticeable effect on the fit result. In our case, for the real parts, we opt for a stronger cutoff of 10вҲ’710-7 on the eigenvalues of the data covariance matrix compared to the 10вҲ’1310-13 used in our non-parametric procedure. On the other hand, the smallest eigenvalues in the imaginary part are already larger than 10вҲ’710-7, so we do not apply any cutoff. Whereas the result of the parametric fits are generally approximately normally distributed, it is noticeably not the case for the imaginary part at z=3вҖӢaz=3a, a situation we will discuss further. The parametric reconstructions present a well-known issue of strong tightening of the uncertainty at some вҖңnodesвҖқ, especially worrying at small xx. It is very visible for xвҲј0.05xsim 0.05 in Fig. where the parametric uncertainty becomes considerably smaller than the non-parametric one. Whereas it is well understood that in either formalism, the uncertainty for xвҲј0xsim 0 is entirely model or prior-driven, it seems that вҖ“ especially when the range in Fourier frequencies is small вҖ“ our non-parametric procedure provides a much more valuable uncertainty quantification down to smaller values of xx than the parametric form. The situation for z=9вҖӢaz=9a in Fig. is particularly interesting. Whereas the parametric uncertainty is narrow and comparable to the one achieved for z=6вҖӢaz=6a, the non-parametric one has considerably worsened. This may sound paradoxical when the range in ОҪnu has increased. But the uncertainty of the last datapoint for z=9вҖӢaz=9a is significantly larger than for z=6вҖӢaz=6a. In the parametric fit, due to its large uncertainty which suppresses its contribution to the ПҮ2chi2, this point matters very little. On the other hand, the Пғ2sigma2 hyperparameter of the non-parametric reconstruction is set so that the reconstruction spreads out in the extrapolation region depending on the uncertainty of the last point. Since the uncertainty of the last point is likely excessive, due to a poor lattice signal-to-noise at this large PP and zz values, we may want to not base our extrapolation on this point. We show in Fig. the result if the prescription on Пғ2sigma2 is applied using the former-to-last datapoint instead. Both the parametric and non-parametric then coincide well. However, the reconstruction of Fig. is in principle data-driven, as it reproduces the sharp uncertainty increase present in the dataset. When x=1x=1, in the absence of any constraint requiring that the non-parametric reconstruction goes to 0, the uncertainty tends to increase. Indeed, the information at x=1x=1 is contained in the large Fourier frequencies that are less constrained by the data. Let us note that the mock dataset of the previous section did in fact constrain surprisingly well the large xx region. As we have mentioned previously, one can easily enforce this additional condition if desired. The case of the imaginary part for z=3вҖӢaz=3a is also noteworthy in Fig. . The parametric reconstruction is extremely unstable: several non-linear minimizers regularly failed to converge due to the extremely bad conditioning of the fitting. It is the only case in this study where the distribution of best-fit parameters we obtain by sampling replicas of the dataset deviates strongly from a normal distribution. It is noticeably bimodal around the modes (Оұ=1.5,ОІ=9)(alpha=1.5,beta=9) and (Оұ=вҲ’0.5,ОІ=4)(alpha=-0.5,beta=4). The latter clearly corresponds to a better phenomenological picture but is less frequent in the replicas. The two modes of the distribution can be distinguished in the replicas presented on Fig. by a branching effect for xвҲј0.3xsim 0.3 and a different trend at large ОҪnu. Overall, the parametric fit is simply extremely unreliable from a numerical point of view on such a small range in ОҪnu, where the Fourier data mostly presents a simple monotonical growth. The non-parametric reconstruction does not suffer from any of those issues. There again, it is easy to tune the value of Пғ2sigma2 if the uncertainty of the reconstruction in ОҪnu is deemed not quite large enough for instance. In the imaginary part at z=6вҖӢaz=6a and 9вҖӢa9a, we find good compatibility between both methods, with once again an unphysical tightening of the parametric uncertainty at small xx. Here as well, it seems that our non-parametric reconstruction provides much more meaningful uncertainty quantification down to much smaller values of xx than the parametric one, and also avoids serious numerical issues as the one in Fig. .
content_sections.VI Applicability of this procedure to ot: Although we have focused so far on the xx-dependence of one-dimensional parton distributions from the short-distance factorization of non-local matrix elements computed on the lattice, our procedure can be applied in other contexts. A first obvious application is the computation of parton distribution functions from the same matrix elements in the LaMET formalism citebib.bib1, bib.bib2. Constructing the quasi-PDF also requires a reconstruction of xx-dependent objects from limited Fourier information. It has become customary in many recent works to complete the tail of missing Fourier modes by a simple parametric model citebib.bib32, inspired by general expectations on the behavior of the non-local matrix element for large separations in zz. A popular simple model with 3 free parameters is sometimes applied as early as ОҪвүҲ5nuapprox 5 e. g. citebib.bib33, bib.bib34. The non-parametric procedure advocated here could help reduce the model-dependence introduced in such calculations. Since quasi-PDFs are known to have formally support in the interval [вҲ’вҲһ,вҲһ][-infty,infty] for finite hadron momentum, one needs to extend the support beyond xвҲҲ[0,1]xin[0,1]. However, at reasonably large momentum, a much smaller support is likely sufficient. We reanalyze the unpolarized PDF data of citebib.bib30 used in the previous section in the quasi-PDF formalism, namely performing the inverse Fourier transform at fixed hadron momentum PzPz instead of fixed non-local separation zz. We apply exactly the procedure advertised before, with the only modification that we allow the support in xx to extend to [0,2][0,2]. We present the results for four momenta Pz=1.23Pz=1.23GeV up to 2.47 GeV in Fig. . For all three largest momenta, our data in Ioffe time extends roughly up to the same ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxвүҲ9numaxapprox 9. For the smallest momentum, it only extends up to ОҪmвҖӢaвҖӢxвүҲ6numaxapprox 6 which explains in part the larger uncertainty. We are mostly interested to see how the reconstruction behaves outside of the range [0,1][0,1]. As observed in Fig. , the contribution outside of [0,1][0,1] is very small in magnitude (the quasi-PDF reaches a peak of the order of 3 when xвҲј0.15xsim 0.15). Furthermore, we retrieve the expected feature that it decreases when PzPz increases. Another extension of the proposed procedure concerns multi-dimensional parton distributions. For instance, the short-distance factorization of non-forward non-local matrix elements allows to reconstruct the three-dimensional generalized parton distributions (GPDs) citebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37. Sparing the reader the introduction of lengthy new notations, the problem presents itself in the double distribution representation citebib.bib38, bib.bib39 under the form of a two-dimensional Fourier transform with an additional external kinematic variable: MвҖӢ(ОҪ,ОҪВҜ,t)=вҲ«О©dОұвҖӢdОІвҖӢeiвҖӢ(ОІвҖӢОҪ+ОұвҖӢОҪВҜ)вҖӢfqвҖӢ(ОІ,Оұ,t),M(nu,barnu,t)=intOmegadalphadbeta,ei(betanu+alphabarnu)fq(beta,alpha,t),, where the 2D support О©Omega is defined by (Оұ,ОІ)||Оұ|+|ОІ|<1(alpha,beta),|,|alpha|+|beta|<1. The problem of reconstructing fqвҖӢ(ОІ,Оұ,t)fq(beta,alpha,t) shares a lot of similarities with the one we have addressed before. The increase in dimension in not really an issue per se as eq. can still be written as a simple linear transform similar to the one we have dealt with so far. However, more thought needs to be devoted to the physics-driven choice of prior kernels and hyperparameters. In the one-dimensional case, we know from our understanding of partonic physics that the small-x and large-x regions are uncorrelated and thus we have chosen a kernel that has these properties. This feature translates directly into the ОІbeta-space. But in terms of the two-dimensional (ОІ,Оұ)(beta,alpha) plane, should we allow more complicated physically motivated correlations? For instance, rather than decoupling the large ОІbeta from small ОІbeta, could it be more appropriate to decouple (ОІвүҲ0,ОұвүҲ0)(betaapprox 0,alphaapprox 0) from the rest of the kinematic domain? To imprint an appropriate behavior in the extrapolation in tt also requires tests that go beyond this short article, and which are of general interest for the phenomenology of GPDs. Another important source of first-principle information on parton distributions is obtained from Mellin moments: вҹЁxsвҲ’1вҹ©q=вҲ«вҲ’11dxвҖӢxsвҲ’1вҖӢfqвҖӢ(x). xs-1q=int-11dx,xs-1fq(x),. On the lattice, whether using local or non-local operators, a variable number of integer values of sвҲҲ1,2,3,вҖҰsin1,2,3,. . . are usually accessed. Although this problem shares deep connections with the one we have addressed before (Mellin moments are derivatives of the Fourier transform at ОҪ=0nu=0), the extrapolation strategy must be revised. If one considers the Mellin space with continuous values of the variable ss, then the bulk of the uncertainty at small xx is now contained in the extrapolation region at small s<1s<1. The physics-driven prescription we have developed in this paper is not directly applicable in this context. Further examination of this important channel for lattice calculations is necessary. Finally, the main source of information on parton distributions remains in many cases the QCD factorization of some experimental processes. The problem presents itself differently in that case, because вҖ“ to some approximation вҖ“ the measured data exists in the same space as the desired construction. Measurement of deeply inelastic structure functions gives access to the following integrals of the function to reconstruct: FвҖӢ(Оҫ)=вҲ«вҲ’11dвҖӢx|Оҫ|вҖӢCвҖӢ(xОҫ)вҖӢfqвҖӢ(x). F(xi)=int-11fracdx|xi|,Cleft(fracxxiright)fq(x),. To leading order in perturbation theory, CвҖӢ(Оұ)=ОҙвҖӢ(1вҲ’Оұ)C(alpha)=delta(1-alpha), meaning that the measured data approximately determines the xx-dependence of the function to reconstruct. At higher order in perturbation theory, the strict equivalence between Оҫxi and xx ceases to be valid. However, in the range of Оҫxi covered by experimental data, properties such as the correlation length of the prior can be informed by the data themselves more directly than what Fourier (or Mellin) modes allow to do. We believe sampling over hyperparameters, as studied in citebib.bib20 with synthetic structure function data, is probably the most meaningful strategy.
content_sections.VII Conclusion: We have presented a simple non-parametric method with a physical prescription to reconstruct parton distributions from limited Fourier modes. The effect of our physical prescription on the reconstruction uncertainty is easily understood and tunable if the results are unsatisfactory. We have found that our procedure gives satisfactory results, whether with simulated or real data. Especially when the range in Fourier frequencies is reduced, we have found our uncertainty to be much more in tune with physical expectations than a popular parametric model, which produces artifacts and frequently unreasonably optimistic uncertainties at small xx. Our method is also very efficient numerically, and its results can be communicated easily and exactly since the reconstruction is a multivariate normal distribution. We have discussed the relevant features of other related inverse problems in hadronic structure and paved the way for a more systematic study of those cases.
